38.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett

46.subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins

75.subject: Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner)

100.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith

135.subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Family History and Migration
clips.summary: Dixon traces his maternal lineage from Mississippi through his great-great-grandmother Emma, who worked in the laundry under Jim Crow, and his paternal lineage from Kentucky, where ancestors served in the Civil War on the Union side and founded the town of Brooksville. Both families participated in the Great Migration to Chicago. His father became a technical illustrator after graduating from the Art Institute.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Father's Military Service
clips.summary: Dixon recounts his father Elmer Dixon's drafted Army service during World War II, including discriminatory treatment at a Mississippi base, the burning of a racist farmer's barn, witnessing a Marine mutilating a dead Japanese woman in Okinawa, and going AWOL. His father's mother wrote Eleanor Roosevelt about conditions. Post-war, Elmer Dixon became involved with Paul Robeson's youth brigade and flirted with the Communist Party.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Growing Up in Madrona
clips.summary: The Dixon family relocated to Seattle in 1957-58, settling in the Madrona neighborhood after his father took a Boeing job. Dixon describes a multiracial neighborhood of Black, Chinese, Japanese, Filipino, and remaining white residents, his mother facing employment discrimination at Frederick & Nelson, his father's folk-dancing group and exposure to opera, classical music, jazz, Shakespeare, and Paul Robeson recordings, and the family expectation that all four children play instruments.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Political Awakening
clips.summary: Dixon describes early exposure to racism through oral family histories, his parents' refusal to let him become a policeman at 13 or enlist for Vietnam at 16, marching with Martin Luther King Jr. in Seattle at around 12-13, demonstrating against George Wallace, and a transformative 1967 visit by Stokely Carmichael that introduced him to Black Power. He details quitting his job after a supervisor told him to remove his Ray-Bans.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: BSU and Franklin High School
clips.summary: Dixon enters the University of Washington through an Urban League program founded by Mrs. Richmond, helps found the first Black Student Union there with Ron Dellums and San Francisco State organizers visiting, and supports BSU formation at Garfield High School. The chapter culminates in the Franklin High School demonstration over a racially disparate disciplinary incident, leading to his arrest on April 4, 1968, the day Walter Cronkite announced King's assassination.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Founding the Seattle Chapter
clips.summary: Dixon describes attending the Black Student Union conference in San Francisco, attending Little Bobby Hutton's funeral with Marlon Brando present, meeting Bobby Seale, and requesting a Seattle BPP chapter. Bobby Seale and George Murray traveled to Seattle, met with 20 prospective members at the Dixon family home, and Dixon was named first captain of what became the first BPP office outside California. He recounts being summoned to Oakland and a near-shootout at Seventh and Wood with Robert Bay, Landon and Randy Williams.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Armed Confrontation Summer 1968
clips.summary: Dixon details the Seattle chapter's first year: 300 membership applications, community response calls for domestic violence and landlord disputes, the Rainier Beach High School armed intervention against white-student attacks, a brief return from a U.N. delegation trip with Bobby Seale that ended SNCC-BPP coalition after a confrontation with James Forman, and Dixon's July 1968 arrest on a setup typewriter charge that triggered Seattle's first major riot. The summer became a sustained fire-bombing and sniping campaign with Madrona Hill nicknamed Pork Chop Hill after the Korean battle.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: First Casualties and FBI Targeting
clips.summary: Dixon describes the first Seattle BPP casualty, a 17-year-old killed by Seattle police while defending his mother, a December 1968 casualty during a directed grocery store robbery, and his typewriter-charge acquittal when the secret witness failed to appear. J. Edgar Hoover targeted Chicago, Los Angeles, and Seattle chapters for destruction; Bunchy Carter and John Huggins were assassinated in January 1969, Fred Hampton in December 1969. A Black Justice Department official tipped off the Seattle chapter, who fortified their office with sandbags. Mayor Wes Uhlman refused the ATF's planned Seattle raid.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Survival Programs
clips.summary: Dixon documents the Seattle chapter's pivot to community survival programs: five breakfast for children program locations starting at Madrona Presbyterian Church, the first free medical clinic, the first food bank program in Seattle, a free legal aid program, the bussing-to-prisons program with a donated 35-passenger bus, the first mass sickle-cell anemia testing at Walla Walla State Penitentiary, and chapter funding through newspaper sales, hustled donations from Japanese-Americans tied to internment memory, and wealthy liberal contributions.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Assassination Attempt and Party Split
clips.summary: Dixon recounts a 1971 shotgun explosion that nearly killed him when the gunpowder had been replaced with high explosives, his return to work within three days with a cast, Huey Newton's release from prison and his subsequent Algeria-mediated split with Eldridge Cleaver fostered by FBI letters, the New York Panthers' alignment with the Weather Underground, Huey's expulsion of the East Coast chapters, and the resulting street violence. Dixon's brother Elmer led a campaign that won a Washington gubernatorial pardon after Elmer's Oregon prison sentence.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Oakland Electoral Campaign
clips.summary: Dixon relocated to Oakland for the centralization campaign, working in the Bobby Seale for Mayor of Oakland and Elaine Brown for City Council races. Five campaign offices distributed 10,000 grocery bags with a chicken in each at the Oakland Auditorium kickoff. They lost but built a political machine. Subsequent support for Jerry Brown's gubernatorial campaign yielded six judgeship appointments and ultimately the election of Lionel Wilson as Oakland's first Black mayor. Dixon discusses women's prominent BPP leadership, naming Ericka Huggins, Audrea Jones, Elaine Brown, Kathleen Cleaver, and Bertha Knox Alexander.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Elaine Brown's Tenure and Newton's Return
clips.summary: Dixon details the Elaine Brown chairpersonship years: the Oakland Community School ranked among the country's best private schools, a martial arts program featured on Black Belt Magazine's cover, the first Bay Area gang intervention program funded by a CETA Grant, a seniors-against-fearful-environment program, the Lamp Post restaurant, music promotion bringing Marvin Gaye and Chaka Khan to Oakland, and Brown's Oakland Redevelopment Corporation presidency. Brown was forced out by Newton after his return from Cuban exile; Newton restarted cocaine use, extorted after-hours clubs, ordered the killing of a trial witness that went wrong, killing a Panther instead.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Departure and Post-BPP Life
clips.summary: Dixon describes leaving the BPP after a confrontation with Bill Cosby over Newton's behavior, being warned that Newton planned to have him killed, his subsequent year selling cocaine and committing embezzlement that earned $100,000 and a year on the run from the FBI, conviction and approximately one year incarcerated, work as a substance-abuse counselor at the West Oakland Health Center until walking off the job after a confrontation with the executive director, time in Northern California on a marijuana farm, witnessing the crack cocaine epidemic devastate Oakland.

clips.subject: Aaron Dixon
clips.title: Reflections and Legacy
clips.summary: Dixon discusses his return to Seattle, founding a nonprofit ten years prior including transition housing for 18-24 year-olds, a youth leadership project across five high schools, the first media literacy program in a Seattle high school, and his September 2012 book release. He attributes contemporary worsening of police brutality and community conditions to loss of working-class unity, Reagan-era neoliberalism and deregulation, the Reagan-Bush cocaine pipeline into Black communities, mass incarceration of 2.5 million Americans (60 percent Black and Latino men), and breakdown of family and cultural value systems.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Mississippi Roots and Slave Lineage
clips.summary: Brown describes being born in Jackson, Mississippi on February 20, 1941, and traces his great-great-grandfather Patrick Brown to slavery in Franklin County's Hamburg community, near the Forks of the Road slave market east of Natchez. He references the cholera epidemic of 1830 that quarantined incoming slaves at that site and notes his father's pastoral work in the Mississippi Delta succeeding the murdered Reverend George Lee.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Eddie Noel and Childhood Awakening
clips.summary: Brown recounts the 1955 case of Eddie Noel, a World War II veteran sharpshooter from Holmes County who killed three white men and evaded a 300-400 person posse. Arenia Mallory, first principal of the Saints Industrial School and vice president of the National Council of Negro Women, persuaded Noel to surrender. Brown notes Noel was committed to Whitfield Mental Institution rather than tried and was a descendant of Governor Edmund Noel.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Medgar Evers and Youth Council Organizing
clips.summary: Brown describes Miss Essie Collins, treasurer of the Jackson NAACP branch, naming him after the prophet Amos at age eight at Farish Street Baptist Church. He learned of Channing H. Tobias's 1954 "Free by '63" declaration through his sister Gloria's report after she attended the Dallas NAACP meeting with Medgar Evers. Brown organized the first NAACP Youth Council in Mississippi in 1955 and traveled with Evers to the 1956 San Francisco National Convention in the Blue Goose Oldsmobile.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Third Baptist and Movement Churches
clips.summary: Brown recounts worshipping at Third Baptist Church on his 1956 San Francisco trip and later being called as its pastor in 1976 to succeed Dr. Leon Howard Sullivan. He places the church's 1852 founding by Eliza Davis and her husband William alongside Harriet Beecher Stowe's Uncle Tom's Cabin and the abolition of Caribbean slavery. He names the four Jackson churches that opened their doors to civil rights activity: Farish Street Baptist, Pearl Street AME, College Hill Baptist, and New Mount Zion Baptist.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Sovereignty Commission Surveillance
clips.summary: Brown details the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission's 10-page file on him, opened in the 1980s, including reports that a deposit guaranteed bank vice president named LATAM leaked his account information to Mayor Thompson and Jackson police. He describes the Commission's practice of recording license plates at mass meetings and pressuring employers to fire civil rights workers, naming Mr. Kirby P. Walker as the white superintendent who attempted to bar him from re-enrolling at Jim Hill High School after his 1958 Cleveland Plain Dealer interview.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Oklahoma City and the Sit-In Origin Claim
clips.summary: Brown identifies the August 28, 1958 Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Council sit-ins led by Clara Luper as the true origin of the student sit-down movement, predating Greensboro by 17 months. He recounts presiding at the 1959 NAACP New York meeting where Ezell Blair Jr. heard Luper's report and carried the model to A&T College, sparking the February 1, 1960 Greensboro action. Brown notes the credentials committee record at the Library of Congress as documentation.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Morehouse and the Atlanta Movement
clips.summary: Brown describes his Morehouse years, including studying with Martin Luther King Jr., desegregating First Baptist Church Atlanta where he met his wife at Spelman, and authoring with Lonnie King, Charles Black, Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Marian Wright Edelman the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights document published in the Atlanta Journal Constitution. He recalls Drs. Rufus Clement and Benjamin Mays funding the publication and his subsequent jailing with King, A.D. King, Otis Moss, and David Satcher.
keywords: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: International Exposure and Crozer Pipeline
clips.summary: Brown describes meeting Rajmohan Gandhi in 1957 through the Moral Rearmament movement and attending Frank Buchman's gatherings at Mackinac Island. He details his 1958 Quaker world affairs camp at Cornell University and his 1964 Operation Crossroads Africa trip to Liberia, Sierra Leone, Senegal, and Ghana. He places Crozer Theological Seminary in the lineage of Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, J. Pius Barber, Samuel D. Proctor, Harold Carter, William Augustus Jones, Charles Booth, Jesse Wendell Mapson, and Archie LeMone.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Personalism and LGBTQ Rights Advocacy
clips.summary: Brown recounts King's Morehouse seminar on personalism, the Brightman-derived doctrine that every human being possesses dignity and worth. He applies this to his ordination of women preachers since 1984 and his opposition to Proposition 8 on California's marriage equality ballot, citing John Mercer Langston's 1866 St. Louis speech and Charles Satchel, the founding 1852 pastor of Third Baptist. He recalls a young congregant attempting to take his pulpit when he refused to support Proposition 8.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Human Rights and Africa Work
clips.summary: Brown extends his framework from civil rights to global human rights, citing King's Birmingham jail letter and Leakey's Rift Valley paleontology. He recounts Third Baptist's resettlement of approximately 3,000 Ethiopian, Eritrean, and Caribbean refugees beginning in his own family room during the 1970s. He co-founded the African-American Summit with Dr. Leon Sullivan in 1990, first held in Abidjan, Ivory Coast in 1991. He describes mentoring Ghanaian musician Kofi who he met at Ghana's 50th independence anniversary.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: 1961 Freedom Rides and Evers Assassination
clips.summary: Brown recounts his 1961 arrest at the Livingston Park Zoo in Jackson for protesting segregated benches and his subsequent arrest after taking an 85-year-old neighbor to the University of Mississippi Hospital where the doctor called him "boy." He describes learning of Medgar Evers's June 1963 assassination while in summer school at Morehouse, attending the Jackson funeral, and witnessing police on horseback beating mourners with billy clubs as the procession marched before Evers's burial at Arlington National Cemetery.

clips.subject: Amos C. Brown
clips.title: Gun Violence and Mississippi's Unsung Heroes
clips.summary: Brown discusses his recent meeting at the Biden vice presidential office with Franklin Graham and Richard Land on gun violence following the Newtown shooting, citing Anna Green from the 2011 Tucson shooting. He concludes by naming Mississippi's unsung organizers: C.C. Bryant of McComb, Amzie Moore of Clarksdale, Samuel Bailey, Joseph Broadwater, Carsie Hall, Reverend William Albert Bender of Tougaloo who fought for equal teacher pay in 1946, and his daughter Lou Ella Bender. He references Andrew Delbanco's The Real American Dream and the 2012 Django Unchained film.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Birmingham Birth and Pittsburgh Childhood
clips.summary: Avery describes her birth in Birmingham, Alabama, her father being 54 at her birth, and her mother sending her to live with a great-aunt in Hazelwood, Pittsburgh. She attended Gladstone Elementary, lived on Sylvan Avenue, and was exposed to integrated schools and Northern Black children who corrected her Southern speech patterns.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Emmett Till and Mack Charles Parker
clips.summary: At age nine or ten in Pittsburgh, Avery overheard adults discussing Emmett Till's 1955 murder and bought a newspaper at a neighborhood store to read about it. At fourteen, back in Birmingham, she followed the 1959 lynching of Mack Charles Parker, who was taken from jail and killed in Poplarville, Mississippi.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Movement Stirrings in Birmingham
clips.summary: Avery names Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth, Reverend Charles Billups, Reverend Oliver, and Reverend J.L. Phifer as ministers working to desegregate Birmingham schools, including Phillips High School. She references the Montgomery bus boycott and Autherine Lucy's attempt to enter the University of Alabama.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Freedom Rides and the Knife
clips.summary: At sixteen, Avery bought a Trailways ticket from Birmingham to Montgomery to join the Freedom Rides, and purchased a knife to protect herself. After the bus was attacked in Anniston, Alabama, she met Wilson Brown at a welcoming committee. Brown told her she could not ride because she did not accept nonviolence, and she returned the ticket.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Nation of Islam and Direct Action
clips.summary: Avery describes her brief engagement with the Nation of Islam through Muhammad Speaks coverage of the Mack Charles Parker case, but found the organization not visibly active enough. She turned toward the sit-ins and Freedom Rides as more direct action.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: First SNCC Meeting in Atlanta
clips.summary: Wilson Brown brought Avery to her first Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee meeting in Atlanta, held in the second-floor SNCC office on Auburn Avenue above the Atlanta Daily World. She met Ms. Ella Baker, James Forman, Ruby Doris Smith, Julian Bond, Anne Braden, Bob Zellner, Dottie Miller Zellner, Penny Patch, and Bill Hansen.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Marietta Bus Station Standoff
clips.summary: Returning from Atlanta with Wilson Brown, Nathaniel Lee, and a white woman, the group got lost in Marietta, Georgia. Police arrested Wilson, impounded the car, and an increasingly hostile white crowd surrounded the trio at the Greyhound station. James Forman, Julian Bond, Howard Moore, and a Justice Department representative arrived after midnight to extract them.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: First Birmingham Sit-In and Albany Arrest
clips.summary: Avery returned to Birmingham, met Shuttlesworth at his home, and participated in what she calls the first Birmingham sit-in at a Woolworth's lunch counter, where a white man spit and poured ketchup on the demonstrators. She traveled to Albany, Georgia, was arrested, and met attorney C.B. King, Rutha Mae Harris, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Charles Sherrod, Bob Mants, and Prathia Hall.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Gadsden March and Tutwiler Prison
clips.summary: After postal worker William Moore was killed during his solo march, Avery picked up a sign and continued his march alone into Gadsden, Alabama. She was arrested with Diane Nash, Madeleine Sherwood, and two other Birmingham women, refused to give any information to interrogators, drew a sentence, and was transferred to Tutwiler prison where the women were held in a hole and then in the prison hospital ward.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Danville Sentencing and 16th Street Bombing
clips.summary: In Danville, Virginia, Avery and Freedom Singer Matthew Jones went limp in court when they found the judge asleep, drawing a 90-day sentence for contempt served in solitary confinement. Released by Avon Rollins to take a break in Birmingham, Avery was awakened the next morning by the September 1963 bombing of the 16th Street Baptist Church. She walked sixteen blocks to the A.G. Gaston Motel, where Ella Baker, King, Abernathy, Forman, and Andrew Young assembled.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Hale County Project Director
clips.summary: Avery served as project director for voter registration in Hale County, Alabama. A Black Army sergeant who said he did not accept nonviolence stood guard outside the first mass meeting with his Army rifle, and the community provided a ten-gauge shotgun that St. Clair taught Avery to use behind their rented house, which she frames as self-defense rather than violence.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge
clips.summary: After Jimmie Lee Jackson's killing, the night before the Selma to Montgomery march a call from Attorney General Katzenbach's office warned that Dr. King's life was in danger. Hosea Williams and Andrew Young flipped a coin to determine SCLC's representative, and Williams led the march alongside John Lewis, Amelia Boynton, and Marie Foster. On March 7, 1965, Avery was the only person arrested on the bridge after a physical disagreement with a state trooper. Birmingham attorney Peter Hall bonded her out.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Women in SNCC and Jail Discipline
clips.summary: Avery reflects on being called a modern-day Harriet Tubman for her role in keeping morale up in jails by stopping crying jags that would invite jailer retaliation. She names friends including Janet Moses, Ruby Sales, Gwen Patton, Bob Mants, Ruby Doris Smith, Prathia Hall, Sam Block, Willie Peacock, James Peacock, Frank Smith, Charles Sherrod, Charles Jones, Dottie Zellner, Bob Zellner, and Judy Richardson.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Klan Siege in Clarksdale
clips.summary: Avery describes her most frightening night in the movement in Clarksdale, Mississippi, when the Klan surrounded the SNCC office after the citywide curfew on Black people. The workers, including Lafayette Surney who headed the project, could only see headlights and hear shouting and slurs, and hid under desks through the night.

clips.subject: Annie Pearl Avery
clips.title: Black Power and Post-Movement Reflections
clips.summary: Avery moved to New York around 1967 and worked from the New York SNCC office, then returned to Atlanta to run a tax service. She frames Black Power as cultural and self-defensive lifting rather than overt violence, recounts her arrest in Atlanta trying to see an African dignitary, and describes finally meeting Nelson Mandela in Selma in 1990, introduced by Rose Sanders. She closes by naming her continued work with Bridge Crossing Jubilee, the National Voting Rights Museum and Institute, and the Mississippi Civil Rights Veterans.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: St. Augustine Childhood and Movement Entry
clips.summary: Hamilton describes growing up in St. Augustine with a vibrant Black business district on Washington Street, accompanying her mother to Woolworth's and McCrory's where they could buy school supplies but not drink from segregated fountains. She and other youth began gathering at St. Paul AME Church under the encouragement of Dr. Robert B. Hayling, Mr. Clyde Jenkins, Mrs. Lucille Plummer, and Mrs. Fannie Fuller Wood, who supplied them with picket signs.
related_events: NAACP Youth Council formation in St. Augustine

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: July 18, 1963 Sit-In at Woolworth's
clips.summary: On July 18, 1963 the youth decided to escalate from picketing to a sit-in. After consulting at St. Paul AME Church and receiving money from barber and NAACP member Ernest Wells, sixteen teenagers walked downtown in pairs. Hamilton, Ulmer, Willie Carl Singleton, and Samuel White entered Woolworth's; Goldie and others split between McCrory's and the service drug store. They sat at the counter and refused to leave.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: JoeAnn Ulmer's Path into the Movement
clips.summary: Ulmer describes growing up on the west side of town "across the track," where Black children had no recreational activities. With her mother's permission she went to stay with her sister near St. Paul AME Church to join the youth council. She recalls Dr. Hayling's piano-accompanied singing meetings and the leadership of "Doc" and Janelle.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Arrest and County Jail
clips.summary: Refused service with the words "we do not serve Niggas here," the youth were ordered out by Sheriff L.O. Davis and arrested by Black deputy DJ Johnson, whom Hamilton had known as a neighbor and schoolmate's father. Sixteen youth were taken to jail; the four girls and three boys were separated from the others. They sang in their cells expecting to be released, but were held overnight.
related_events: Arrest of the St. Augustine Four

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Judge Mathis and the Parents' Refusal
clips.summary: In court Judge Mathis demanded that the youths' parents sign a petition pledging no further demonstration until age 21. Twelve youth went home when parents signed; Hamilton, Ulmer, Singleton, and White stayed because their mothers refused. NAACP representative Mr. Shaw stood up to Judge Mathis vowing to appeal. Mrs. Singleton (Miss Nora), who suffered from a bad heart, fainted in court but never signed; Mrs. White (Miss Price) likewise held firm. The four were sentenced to reform school.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Dr. Hayling's Leadership
clips.summary: Both speakers describe Dr. Robert B. Hayling as their youth advisor who motivated rather than restrained them, who "never left our side" and stood by the youth even when other Black leaders counseled caution. They credit him as "the inspiration and the father of St. Augustine" civil rights movement, noting his work began months before Dr. King arrived and that he sacrificed greatly for the cause.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Reform School: 77 Days and 52 Days Isolation
clips.summary: Hamilton and Ulmer were held 77 days in the county jail, then 52 days in isolation at the Florida reform school for girls at Ocala (after Marianna), where they scrubbed floors on their knees until they could see their faces and prayed alone with a Bible. Singleton and White were taken in the middle of the night to the Marianna boys' reform school; both later refused to speak of what happened there and "took it to the grave." Hamilton frames the violence inflicted upon them as something the girls did not return: "the violence was afflicted on us but we did not afflict violence back."

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Meeting Dr. King and the SCLC Campaign
clips.summary: After release on January 23, 1964, Dr. Hayling brought the four youth to meet Dr. Martin Luther King and Ralph Abernathy at a private room before the June 1964 SCLC campaign in St. Augustine. King embraced them saying they "went beyond the call of duty." Going to jail in 1964 under SCLC (rather than 1963 under NAACP), Hamilton was personally chosen by King at St. Mary's Missionary Baptist Church to join his group to test the Monson Motor Lodge alongside Reverend Fred Shuttlesworth, Mary Peabody, and a young white woman. Owner Jimmy Brock refused service, police arrested Dr. King and the group, and a police dog memorably licked Dr. King.
keywords: I Woke Up This Morning with My Mind Stayed on Freedom

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Jackie Robinson Hosts Them in Stamford
clips.summary: After Hamilton's release that night, Jackie Robinson came to St. Augustine speaking at the Catholic church and embraced her in front of the crowd, flying her and Ulmer first class to his home in Stamford, Connecticut. The Robinson family hosted them, took them to museums and a sandal workshop, and Robinson's daughter conducted interviews with them for radio stations. Photographs from the visit are property of the Jackie Robinson Foundation. Hamilton remembers Robinson's young son David following her around with his horse.
related_events: Jackie Robinson's support of St. Augustine movement

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Post-Movement Lives: College and Family
clips.summary: After the Civil Rights Act's 1964 passage, all four enrolled in Florida Memorial College. Hamilton dropped out for lack of funds when the college relocated; Samuel White completed three or more years. White was "very smart" and "all about the brotherhood"; he died in Germany in 1995, his body shipped home by his mother. Ulmer moved to Jacksonville, married, raised four children there, and reflects that she wanted her children to have privileges she did not, including swimming pools.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Activist Record, FBI Surveillance, and Reflection
clips.summary: Hamilton reflects that her activism record, while limiting some employment, was vindicated when historian David Nolan presented her with her FBI file at her Habitat for Humanity house dedication in 2008, revealing that she, Ulmer, Singleton, and White had been monitored by the FBI as 14-, 15-, and 16-year-olds. Both speakers describe being publicly acknowledged as the St. Augustine Four in 2004 when Earl Johnson Jr., son of their original lawyer Earl Johnson Sr., spoke at a commemoration alongside Mr. Dews.

clips.subject: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer
clips.title: Erasure, ACCORD, and the Foot Soldiers Monument
clips.summary: Hamilton concludes that St. Augustine "swept the whole movement up under the rug," demolishing civil rights sites including the Ponce de Leon site on US 1 and the Monson Motor Lodge and attempting to alter the Slave Market. The ACCORD organization led by Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, Dr. Hayling, and former city commissioner Henry Twine with Katherine Twine placed downtown markers next to Woolworth's and led to the Foot Soldiers Monument in the plaza. Hamilton describes referring Duncan to Dr. Hayling for proper historical guidance.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Interview Opening and Setting
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier introduces the interview at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine on September 13, 2011, for the Civil Rights History Project, jointly produced by the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. Dr. Robert B. Hayling is present as an observer and will be interviewed separately in the series.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project oral history series, 2011

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Childhood and Family in St. Augustine
clips.summary: Vickers describes growing up in St. Augustine, attending St. Benedict the Moor Catholic School through eighth grade and graduating from Excelsior High School in 1942. Her mother worked as a waitress at the Monson Motor Lodge; her grandmother and aunt also worked there. Her father was the head waiter (matre d') at the Bennett Hotel a few doors down. She had two younger sisters, Gwendolyn and Charlotte.
keywords: St. Benedict the Moor Catholic School

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Leaving the Jim Crow South
clips.summary: Vickers describes her determination to leave St. Augustine after high school graduation in 1942, citing daily indignities such as having to wait at the back of grocery store lines when white customers entered and being forced off sidewalks. She moved to New York and worked in a shipyard during the war, learning to tack weld at company-sponsored school. She then relocated to Seattle to work at Boeing Aircraft, where she was reassigned as a mechanic helper rather than a welder.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Return to St. Augustine and Marriage
clips.summary: Vickers describes returning to St. Augustine in the late 1950s after marrying Adell Vickers, who was a bellhop in New Jersey but refused to settle anywhere except his hometown. She opened a home beauty salon. Adell Vickers worked at a drive-in movie theater, supplemented by gambling, and later became recreation director at the center where Solomon Calhoun (Vickers's high school basketball coach, later city commissioner) had connected him.
related_events: Post-WWII return migration to the South

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Meeting Dr. Hayling and Dr. King
clips.summary: Vickers describes Dr. Robert B. Hayling as a neighbor who lived across the street and led the local civil rights effort. She attended rallies and meetings, where Hayling asked for volunteers for direct-action tactics. The night Dr. King visited a St. Augustine church, Hayling asked for people to integrate white churches, and King personally pointed to Vickers and asked if she would go. She agreed, then immediately wondered what she had committed herself to.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Scott Street Network and Klan Threats
clips.summary: Vickers describes the Scott Street network of eleven houses where movement participants lived. Julia Conway organized residents to protect Hayling, with the West Side Boys hiding in trees around his house. Vickers details the network's protocol for Klan night-rides: phone alerts, instructions to crawl below window level because the Klan was shooting low, and the practice of crawling between refrigerator and other appliances for cover. She recounts being pulled over by a police officer who tried to charge her tire iron as a weapon.
related_events: 1963 Klan attacks on St. Augustine movement

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Shooting at Hayling's Home
clips.summary: Vickers describes the September 1963 Klan shooting attack on Dr. Hayling's home. The protective network of men guarding the house had fallen asleep. When Vickers arrived home that night, an officer was blocking Scott Street; he allowed her through after she explained she lived there. Julia Conway reported having given the heavyset officer a chair. Hayling's dogs Madonna and a boxer were killed; his wife, pregnant with their third child, narrowly escaped after just having gotten up from a sofa struck by bullets. Hayling sent his children to Tallahassee for safety.
related_events: September 18, 1963 Klan attack on Hayling residence

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Petitioning the Governor and Daily Movement Work
clips.summary: Vickers describes traveling to Tallahassee with Hayling and others, representing the Catholic community, to petition the governor. She recounts the fear of Klan cars running them off the road, as had happened on trips to Butler's Beach. She describes managing her beauty salon during the movement years (covering windows with blankets, losing customers afraid to be seen patronizing a movement participant) and her family's reactions: her grandmother begged her to stop, predicting she would be killed; her sisters declined to participate, one being away at Bethune-Cookman College in Daytona.
related_events: St. Augustine movement appeals to state government

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Kneel-Ins at White Churches
clips.summary: Vickers describes attempting to integrate Grace United Methodist Church, where a white Metropolitan Life insurance agent who collected her premium each Monday stood in front of her kneeling form with his foot positioned as if to kick. She confronted him the next day at her door and dropped the policy. She frames the kneel-ins as part of a broader demand for full citizenship rights, not specifically for the right to eat with white people, rejecting her grandmother's resignation to the Jim Crow status quo.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Encounters with Dr. King and Mrs. Hayling
clips.summary: Vickers recounts meeting Dr. King at First Baptist Church (after arriving late to St. Paul AME Church) and shaking his hand, recalling his cotton-soft hands and reputation for remembering names. She missed an earlier meeting with King because she was serving as president of the beauticians' association at a Daytona convention, sending Queenie Mitchell McCall in her stead. She describes Mrs. Hayling as quiet and reserved, and recounts a later encounter in Fort Lauderdale where Mrs. Hayling, traumatized, kept crossing the street to avoid contact until Vickers identified herself.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Founding the Foot Soldiers Monument Campaign
clips.summary: Vickers describes conceiving the Foot Soldiers Monument roughly a decade before the interview. Working through the Council on Aging under director Kathy Brown's Mayor's Senior Advisory Committee, she became president of that committee after the prior president resigned. Brown initially urged her to pursue a Martin Luther King monument for easier fundraising, but Vickers insisted on commemorating the rank-and-file foot soldiers. The committee secured 501(c) nonprofit status and began fundraising.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Fundraising the Monument
clips.summary: Vickers describes the fundraising sequence: a donor named Nina Verilam contributed $20,000 to retain the sculptor; her grandson secured a UPS grant of approximately $7,400; Phil McDaniel arranged a $100-per-head dinner at attorney Patrick Canan's office that raised $24,000, featuring Vickers, musician Del Kahn, and foot soldier Freddie Thompson (beaten alongside Andrew Young in 1964). Hank Thomas of the Freedom Rides also gave a sizable donation. The total budget reached approximately $70,000. Vickers expresses disappointment that only one Black church in St. Augustine donated.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: City Government and Monument Installation
clips.summary: Vickers describes navigating the City Commission process, including departing from a feuding rival monument group to form her own committee. African American City Commissioner Earl Jones earmarked $5,000 of city money for prep work, and the city later paid for the surrounding walkway with foot-prints. She praises the mayor, city manager, and city attorney for legal cooperation. At the installation ceremony she was overcome and could not speak; Hayling waved her forward and the crowd applauded. Hayling subsequently credited her with the organizational drive that made the monument possible.

clips.subject: Barbara Edna Vickers
clips.title: Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Vickers reflects on how the monument campaign changed her perspective on white people by introducing her to committed white allies. She invokes her grandmother's stories of Klan home invasions and lynchings as the historical context against which she measures her own movement participation. At 88 she affirms she is still ready to keep working on civil rights, motivated by reaching young people who can understand what the foot soldiers achieved.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Howard, Bernice Reagon, Lomax
clips.summary: Caldwell opens by recalling his three years teaching at Howard University, working at the Smithsonian with Bernice Johnson Reagon, meeting Alan Lomax through Dr. William Barlow ('Bill Barlow, the doctor of the blues'), and encountering Worth W. Long at the Robert Flaherty Film Seminar in Philadelphia where Lomax's The Land Where the Blues Began deeply moved him.
keywords: The Land Where the Blues Began

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Migrant Family, Deming Border History
clips.summary: Caldwell describes his Black-Hispanic migrant farming family across Texas, Oklahoma, New Mexico, and Arizona, and his grandfather who could 'pass as Latino,' acquired property, met the mayor, and projected films in segregated theaters (Latino, white, Black-balcony). He locates Deming, New Mexico at the Guadalupe Hidalgo border zone with Pancho Villa, Geronimo, Cochise, the Butterfield Overland Mail, and Pershing's first wartime use of airplanes.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Childhood, Schooling, Pride
clips.summary: Caldwell recounts colored-team semi-pro baseball circuits across the Southwest, his foreman father born in Des Moines who attended Marquette, storytellers performing the Signifying Monkey, and being placed by one-block geography in the all-white Smith school, where rock-throwing peers taught him to fight back. He frames the family ethic his father transmitted: stand up for yourself, do not cow-tail, and require equal address.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Vietnam, Pacifism, Malcolm X
clips.summary: Drafted out of Arizona School of Art at 21, Caldwell describes refusing exploratory knee surgery, going to Japan, dining with the U.S. ambassador and the theater general (Westmoreland's predecessor), then returning to Vietnam for the Tet Offensive. He recounts shooting wide of an old man to spare his village from napalm, reading Malcolm X, recognizing Vietnamese people and the Montagnards as 'the other' just as he was the other in the United States, and learning to be 'a pacifist in a war zone.'

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Phoenix College, Photography, UCLA
clips.summary: Returning home, Caldwell briefly played football at Phoenix College and Arizona State before quitting over racial nicknaming and his concern about long-term knee damage. He studied economics until its predictive failures regarding the Depression alienated him, then committed to photographing his family and the Arizona landscape, observing the tonal continuity between Black skin and desert earth. That series won him a full scholarship to UCLA's MFA program, where he encountered a defected KGB instructor in a revolutionary-philosophy course.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Watts, Outterbridge, L.A. Arts Fusion
clips.summary: Coming to Los Angeles in 1971, Caldwell worked at the Watts Towers under John Outterbridge at the Communicative Arts Academy, the first media-arts-fusion organization in the area. He describes the holistic L.A. arts movement of theater, film, painting, music, and poetry, places like The Jua collective, drum classes with Nigerian masters, food co-ops, and the concentration of Motown-era performers (Miracles, Temptations, Richard Pryor) in the Crenshaw neighborhood. He encountered Calle de los Negros and the Black-Hispanic colonization of California, co-teaching with Norman Klein on 'California, the history of forgetting.'

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: L.A. Rebellion, Haile Gerima
clips.summary: Caldwell describes UCLA's segregation of ethnographic-film and regular-film tracks, his cohort being the first students of color admitted to the regular program, and how they gravitated around Haile Gerima as TA. He frames the L.A. Rebellion (initially about 13, growing to 22 over a decade and producing 55 films) as a rebellion against blaxploitation, distinguishing the original Black-authored Sweetback, Shaft, and Superfly from the white-imitator exploitation cycle that followed. He cites his films Medea (1971), I & I, Babylon Is Falling: A Visual Ritual for Peace (1980), and The Nubian.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: KAOS Network, Hip-Hop, I-Phresh
clips.summary: Caldwell founded Video 3333 (later KAOS Network) in Leimert Park to operate as an artist-advocate practice for 'the poor and the vulnerable' outside Hollywood. Working with Charles Burnett, he targeted 11-25-year-olds as the cultural-generative cohort, documented the emerging hip-hop lexicon, and made the 1989 short I-Phresh fusing youth-movement and Rastafarian framings. He describes hip-hop as Sankofa-principles remix and archival research, James Brown drum-break sampling as homage, capoeira as the encoded martial art behind break dancing, and quilombos / Rastafarian hill-resistance as African diasporic continuity.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Cuba, Babalawos, Yogi Bhajan
clips.summary: In 1999 Caldwell joined a Yogi Bhajan-sponsored delegation of Black yoga teachers to Cuba to study how Yoruba culture and Babalawo priesthood preserved African religious science across the Middle Passage. He describes Cuban physicists studying tribal markings as acupuncture immunization points, the Cuban state's deployment of healers and 300,000 soldiers against apartheid in Angola / South Africa, and bureaucratic encounters with state-owned nightclubs and DJs. He frames hip-hop exchange with Cuban hip-hop heads, and notes Los Muñequitos de Matanzas's New Orleans connection.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: 1992 Uprising, Rodney King
clips.summary: Caldwell recounts witnessing the 1992 Los Angeles uprising from Leimert Park: the shock of the Rodney King verdict, a white kid apologizing on the corner before driving south, multi-ethnic anarchy including Central American señoritas hauling crates from looted stores, neighbors physically rescuing a Black gallery's African sculptures, and the Korean grocer-family being protected by Black neighbors after the broader Korean-Black tension. He invokes the Charles R. Drew Postgraduate Medical School / Martin Luther King doctors' research linking gang infrastructure to organized covert distribution, citing the CIA / Dark Alliance crack allegations as connecting context.

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Sankofa, Payphones, Phantom Galleries
clips.summary: Caldwell describes his current Sankofa-principles project with USC School of Innovation graduate students to repurpose payphones as Wi-Fi totems and hyper-local cultural-distribution nodes, partnering with LA Metro and Pacific Bell on virtual-space anti-gentrification design. He details the Phantom Galleries program (Norton Family Foundation anchor) that filled nine derelict Crenshaw buildings with art, the Electronic Café International project he ran at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics connecting five disparate communities via proto-internet teleconferencing, and the mapping of cultural density showing every major Black musical figure lived or worked within a five-mile radius (Miles Davis, Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Hampton Hawes, The Chambers Brothers).

clips.subject: Ben Caldwell
clips.title: Art Versus Politics, Closing
clips.summary: Caldwell closes by articulating his lifelong refusal to align with political camps in favor of an artist's cross-cultural communicability, citing his teaching of Nazis at CalArts, his admiration for Leni Riefenstahl's craft despite her ideology, and his rejection of communism after reading The Communist Manifesto's framing of the lumpenproletariat ('that's my mama'). He frames his work as 'making flowers out of compost' and credits Egyptian-encoded musical cosmology as the holographic carrier of African historical memory through enslavement.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Family and Childhood Formation
clips.summary: Robinson describes her 1939 birth in New York City to a physicist father from a Pennsylvania-German farm family and a working-class New York mother. She recounts wartime years in Cambridge, Massachusetts, where her father worked at MIT's Radiation Lab on radar during the atomic bomb era, and her father's tortured experience at General Precision Equipment in suburban Pleasantville, New York, which shaped her rejection of corporate America.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Mother's Prejudice and Race Awakening
clips.summary: Robinson recounts confronting her mother's racism after hosting an interracial YMCA-camp counselor party at their home, including her mother's slur upon discovering an African American Vassar student had been a guest. She describes her Mexican godmother Lucy Droz, her mother's anti-immigrant and anti-communist views, and her own gradual recognition that the absence of Black people from her high school and community was itself a structural fact she had not noticed.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Skidmore and the National Student Association
clips.summary: Robinson enters Skidmore College in 1956 and is recruited to run for National Student Association (NSA) campus secretary, leading her to the 1958 NSA Student Congress at Ohio Wesleyan, a transformative encounter with students discussing South African apartheid, Southern segregation, and political organizing. She switches her major to political science and is elected NSA campus chair, then engages in the campaign against the National Defense Education Act loyalty oath, which she had studied in a civil-liberties course.
related_events: National Defense Education Act loyalty oath campaign

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Saratoga Sit-In Solidarity 1960
clips.summary: Following the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Robinson organizes a Skidmore mass meeting that decides to march 200 women students around the Saratoga Springs Woolworth's and to picket the next day. The first four pickets are taken to the Saratoga police station; the local paper editorializes that the students were "unladylike." Robinson considers attending the SNCC founding conference at Shaw University over spring break but is deterred by her mother's anger over her recent letter to the Hastings-on-Hudson newspaper.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: ISRS and CIA Covert Funding
clips.summary: After graduating in 1960, Robinson attends the International Student Relations Seminar (ISRS) in Philadelphia, which she later learned was the training ground for NSA's CIA-funded international program. She describes the recruitment of "witting" officers, the practice of student travelers gathering intelligence on emerging foreign student leaders, and the maneuvering that kept Tim Jenkins (a Black student leader) out of the international vice presidency. She references Karen Paget's book Patriotic Betrayal as documenting this history.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: SDS, Helsinki, and the Turn to SNCC
clips.summary: Robinson serves on the SDS executive committee and travels to the 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students in Helsinki under CIA-funded auspices alongside Gloria Steinem and Harold Bakken. She witnesses Helsinki police beating no-testing East-West demonstrators. After the LID-SDS split meeting in New York, she concludes she is a wallflower in SDS's male intellectual culture and accepts James Forman and Casey Hayden's affirmation, moving toward SNCC where she can take concrete initiative.
keywords: World Festival of Youth and Students
related_events: 1962 World Festival of Youth and Students (Helsinki)

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: SNCC Atlanta Office and Northern Coordinator
clips.summary: Robinson joins SNCC's Atlanta office on Raymond Street in March 1964 and becomes Northern Coordinator in fall 1964 after Dinky Romilly returns to New York. She describes the daily WATS-line calls to all SNCC projects, the use of operator numbers to dodge phone-company charges, fundraising letter campaigns (rejecting paternalistic appeals in favor of democratic-ideals framing), and her interracial housing on West Anthony Street with Joyce Brown, Carol Merritt, and Shirley Cooks. She earned $9.64 weekly after taxes in the field, raised to $40 in Atlanta.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Freedom Summer and Federal Programs
clips.summary: Robinson serves as lead Northern Coordinator in the Greenwood, Mississippi SNCC office during Freedom Summer 1964. Her parents send an air conditioner while also pressuring her to come home. She canvasses with white volunteer Peter Orris and witnesses the MFDP convention challenge fallout. In fall 1965, she transitions to federal programs work, documenting discriminatory administration of ASCS agricultural subsidies, food stamps, and Civil Rights Act of 1964 school desegregation plans across Arkansas and DC. With Marion Barry she audits the US Office of Education's files and publishes a report exposing dispensations granted to Southern school districts.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Selma, Waveland, and Women in SNCC
clips.summary: Robinson describes chartering a plane from Atlanta to Selma after the Edmund Pettus Bridge attack, sending Ivanhoe Donaldson, Courtland Cox, Prathia Hall, and a fourth staffer to back up John Lewis and John Love. She narrates SNCC's purchase of the Sojourner Motor Fleet at UAW-discounted prices and SNCC's internal soul-searching about strategy after the 1964 convention. She recalls the Waveland meetings of fall 1964 and the position paper on women by Mary King and Casey Hayden, noting her own non-engagement with women's liberation until the early 1970s because she felt affirmed within SNCC.
related_events: Bloody Sunday at the Edmund Pettus Bridge (March 7, 1965)

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Washington DC SNCC and Free DC
clips.summary: Robinson moves to the DC SNCC office on Rhode Island Avenue in late 1965, working alongside Marion Barry as he becomes head of the office. She participates in the Free DC voting-representation campaign, which pressures Woodward & Lothrop, Hecht Company, and other businesses to display the Free DC decal. She describes the divisive influence of L.D. Pratt, an older white advisor close to Barry, and a 1966 bus boycott on Benning Road organized via SNCC's basement phone bank. She resigns from SNCC by letter, citing the loss of participatory decision-making in the DC office.

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: Race, Gender, and Post-SNCC Years
clips.summary: Robinson reflects on SNCC's interracial dynamics, including Black-only social spaces, the backsliding conversations at Waveland targeting Black men in interracial relationships (including her own with Marion Barry and Stokely Carmichael's), and being struck in the face by a Black youth in DC after Dr. King's 1968 assassination. She narrates her post-SNCC work: a Black community arts program in DC, anti-war organizing, consciousness-raising groups in the early 1970s, and her 1972 move to Baltimore to work in a factory (eight years), marriage to a Black factory worker, and birth of biracial daughters.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. (April 1968)

clips.subject: Betty Garman Robinson
clips.title: SURJ, Black Lives Matter, and Movement Continuity
clips.summary: Robinson narrates her late-career organizing: HRSA-funded medical-student factory tours, 14 years at Johns Hopkins School of Public Health in injury prevention and HIV research, and lead-organizer work with Citizens Planning and Housing Association in Baltimore. She joined SURJ (Showing Up for Racial Justice) after the 2015 Baltimore uprising following Freddie Gray's death, working in accountability relationships with Black Lives Matter organizations. She closes with reflections on movement timing, the centrality of Black liberation as the foundation for all other liberation struggles, and the importance of scholarship by Emilye Crosby and Hasan Kwame Jeffries.
related_events: Killing of Michael Brown in Ferguson (August 2014)

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Family Origins and Mr. Charlie
clips.summary: Russell describes his parents Katie and Charles "Mr. Charlie" Russell, his grandfather's confrontation with the Ku Klux Klan in Louisiana using a shotgun loaded with birdshot, and the family's purchase of lumber to build a Black schoolhouse in defiance of a white lumber-yard owner. He recounts his mother's death from kidney failure when he was twelve and his father's promise to raise the boys himself, taking a foundry job at lower pay so he could be home each night.
related_events: Ku Klux Klan night attack in rural Louisiana

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Great Migration to Oakland
clips.summary: Russell describes his father's decision to leave Louisiana after a foundry boss refused him a raise and used a racial slur, the draft-board retaliation that followed, the move first to Detroit's Ford plants and then to Oakland's wartime shipyards, and his father's subsequent trucking business hauling fruit pickers to the Central Valley. He notes that he bought his own school clothes by picking fruit from age twelve through college.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: USF and the Vertical Game
clips.summary: Russell describes being cut from the USF junior varsity, being recruited to the varsity by coach Phil Woolpert, losing his athletic scholarship after a difficult freshman year and receiving a business-school academic scholarship instead, and how four of his teammates told the coach they did not appreciate playing with Black players. He describes inventing the vertical defensive game and shot-blocking, leading USF to a 55-game win streak and back-to-back NCAA championships in 1955 and 1956. He recounts the McClymonds High coach who taught him not to fight on the basketball court because "if Black kids fight, it's a riot."

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Olympics and Joining the Celtics
clips.summary: Russell describes the 1956 Olympic gold medal in Melbourne (beating the Soviet Union by 35 points), the 13-month run from college championship to Olympic gold to NBA championship in his rookie year. He recounts being met at the Boston airport by owner Walter A. Brown, Red Auerbach, and Bill Sharman, and the franchise's early integration milestones: Chuck Cooper from Duquesne (the first Black NBA draftee, 1950), the first all-Black starting five, and Sam Jones drafted in the first round from a historically Black college.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: First Black NBA Head Coach
clips.summary: Russell describes Red Auerbach's promise to hire no successor without Russell's full approval, the lists they exchanged with no overlap, and Auerbach offering Russell the player-coach role in 1966. He explains why he refused to take an assistant coach (the press would frame any assistant as the "real" coach) and how Auerbach's tradition of fighting referees to defend his players shaped Russell's own approach. He references Red Auerbach being thrown out of 22 games his last year as coach.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Teammates and the Celtic Way
clips.summary: Russell sketches his teammates: Bill Sharman as the most thoughtful player; Bob Cousy as a poor defender whom Russell covered by inviting drives into the lane; Sam Jones who could have been all-time great but consciously dialed back his effort; Tom Heinsohn whom Auerbach pulled at six minutes regardless of performance; Frank Ramsey who pioneered the sixth-man role; and John Havlicek who initially passed up open shots before adjusting to the pro game. He describes the Celtics' practice of icing Havlicek's shoulder after games and the team philosophy that "kindness is an act of strength."

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Boston, Race, and Walter A. Brown
clips.summary: Russell describes the racial climate in Boston: sportswriters who would not book him for speaking engagements, a Herald reporter questioning his coaching the morning after a championship parade, and his decision to retire in 1969 without telling anyone so as not to overshadow Sam Jones's retirement parade. He recounts Walter A. Brown calling him in for a candid acknowledgement that the Celtics could not afford to pay him what he was worth, leading to an eight-year no-cut contract from Auerbach after Brown's death, which Russell did not collect because he could not take money he had not earned.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Philosophy of Sport and Other People's Kids
clips.summary: Russell discusses his philosophical approach: "It is far more important to understand than to be understood." He describes a Texas speech where he argued for redirecting Vietnam War spending into education, met by a man asking why he should pay taxes for other people's kids. Russell's reply, that there are no other people's kids, only the next generation of Americans, became a recurring theme. He describes his role-model encounter with Minneapolis Lakers center George Mikan, who treated him with respect at age fourteen.
related_events: Vietnam War-era speech in Fort Worth

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Civil Rights: Mississippi, Ali, March on Washington
clips.summary: Russell describes his June 1964 trip to Jackson, Mississippi (during Freedom Summer when Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner were murdered) to run integrated basketball clinics at Charles Evers's request after Medgar Evers's assassination, with armed but invisible security. He recounts meeting Dr. King at the 1963 March on Washington and declining an invitation to the stage because he had not done the years of organizing work. He describes joining the 1967 Cleveland summit organized by Jim Brown to support Muhammad Ali's draft refusal after Ali's classification was arbitrarily changed to 1-A, and refusing a Boston demand to "disavow Muhammad Ali."

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Autograph Policy and the Red Sox
clips.summary: Russell describes his refusal to sign autographs as a matter of policy dating from 1964, his preference for conversation with kids instead, and his teammates Heinsohn, Ramsey, Sanders, and sometimes K.C. Jones forging his signature for fans. He recounts his refusal to throw out the Red Sox's first pitch and his demand that the team owners join his Mentoring USA board before he would reconcile. He describes the eventual outcome: an owner joined the board, the Red Sox host twice-yearly Mentoring nights at Fenway Park, and Russell threw out a first pitch about six years before the 2013 interview.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Presidential Medal and the Boston Statue
clips.summary: Russell describes receiving the Presidential Medal of Freedom from Barack Obama at the dedication of the Martin Luther King monument in Washington (Obama called him "the reason I can be President"), and ranks it as a strong second to his father at age seventy-five telling him "this is my hero." He recounts Mayor Tom Menino's request to dedicate a statue in Boston at City Hall Plaza, his initial resistance ("it sounds a lot like a tombstone"), and Menino's argument that it would be for the city's community and racial relations. He recounts the legacy foundation and Mentoring fun-runs around the statue.

clips.subject: Bill Russell (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Jackie Robinson and Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Russell describes Rachel Robinson asking him to serve as the only non-Brooklyn Dodger pall-bearer at Jackie Robinson's funeral, ranking this third in personal honors. He explains his view that Robinson took Black athletes from point A to point B, and that Russell wanted to take them from B to C. He references his conversation with Nelson Mandela on the role of violence in anti-apartheid struggle, declines to compare players across eras ("I would never ask a player to play against ghosts"), and reflects on his refusal to return to West Monroe, Louisiana, after his mother's burial.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Entry into Journalism
clips.summary: Booker traces his career from the Baltimore Afro-American in 1942 through the Cleveland Call & Post, a Nieman Fellowship at Harvard, and the founding of Jet magazine's Washington bureau under publisher John H. Johnson. Newson describes his Navy service, undergraduate years at Storer College in West Virginia, journalism studies at Lincoln University in Missouri, and his 1952 start at the Tri-State Defender in Memphis.
related_events: John H. Johnson's founding of Jet

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Hoxie and Clinton School Desegregation
clips.summary: Newson recalls covering early school desegregation efforts in Hoxie, Arkansas (1955), where media were unusually welcomed into classrooms, and in Clinton, Tennessee (1956), where outside agitators including John Kasper and the KKK fired up white residents against the small Black community on Foley Hill. National Guard intervention was eventually required.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Emmett Till Case
clips.summary: Booker describes Jet's coverage of the September 1955 murder of Emmett Till in Money, Mississippi, including David Jackson's open-casket funeral photographs at Mamie Till's insistence. Newson recounts the trial in Tallahatchie County, including the segregated press table and his work with NAACP teams and driver Medgar Evers to find Black witnesses on Delta plantations.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Black Press Reporting Strategies
clips.summary: Both journalists describe how Black newspapers built coverage networks: Jet's nationwide stringer system that turned rural readers into informants and local guides, the necessity of changing clothes to interview witnesses on Mississippi plantations, segregated courthouses, and the constant pressure on Black sources whose livelihoods were tied to white landowners.
related_events: Charles C. Diggs Jr observer at Till trial

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Little Rock 1957
clips.summary: Newson describes covering the Little Rock Nine, including being ordered out of the area by a National Guard colonel, the September 1957 mob attack on Black reporters including L. Alex Wilson (who refused to run after a prior KKK incident in Leesburg, Florida), and James L. Hicks of the Amsterdam News. Both discuss Elizabeth Eckford's isolated walk to Central High and President Eisenhower's decision to send in federal troops.
related_events: Little Rock Central High desegregation September 1957

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Race Relations and the Early 1960s
clips.summary: The journalists assess the political climate around Kennedy's 1960 election, contrasting Eisenhower's reluctance to speak about civil rights with Kennedy's openness to the press. Newson describes a Mississippi church meeting where a sharecropper, evicted for trying to register, asked the audience to keep registering rather than help him. The chapter touches on the Harry and Harriette Moore Christmas 1951 bombing in Florida.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Freedom Rides 1961
clips.summary: Newson describes the May 14, 1961 firebombing of the Greyhound bus outside Anniston, Alabama, including state trooper E.L. Cowling drawing his pistol to keep the mob off the bus, and the legal foundation laid by CORE's 1947 ride, the 1946 Morgan v. Virginia case, the four-women interstate-travel case argued by Attorney Fred Gray, and Boynton v. Virginia. Booker recounts arriving in Birmingham on the Trailways bus, ducking out the back, and being driven by a Black cab driver to Rev. Shuttlesworth, who organized a rescue caravan.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: March on Washington and King
clips.summary: Newson describes attending the 1963 March on Washington and the coalition of labor and church leaders. Booker recalls his final extended interview with Dr. King during Poor People's Campaign planning, conducted in a car en route to the airport with Andrew Young, Harry Belafonte, and Coretta Scott King present. King's response to charges of failure: 'if I'm failing, then... Jesus Christ failed because He got himself hung on the cross.'

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Black Press and White Press Relations
clips.summary: Both journalists reflect on the divide between Black and white newspapers during the Movement years. Newson describes working as the only Black reporter at major events, unable to stay at the same hotels as white colleagues, but maintaining professional working relationships. Booker covered multiple Republican and Democratic conventions; Newson did not cover the 1964 Atlantic City convention.
related_events: Republican conventions Miami New York Kansas City

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Television and the Movement
clips.summary: Booker argues that white press involvement, particularly television networks broadcasting images of police dogs, fire hoses, and beatings, accelerated the Movement in ways the Black press alone could not. He raises critical questions about contemporary mainstream coverage, citing the 2009 Henry Louis Gates Jr. arrest as an example of incomplete reporting.

clips.subject: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson
clips.title: Robert Kennedy and the Late 1960s
clips.summary: Booker reflects on Robert Kennedy as the first federal official genuinely responsive to racial matters, his 1968 assassination, and the political shift under Lyndon Johnson. The interview closes with Booker's assessment that the political coalition disintegrated after 1968 and was only partially rebuilt through Obama's election.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Interview Opening
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program opens the recording at the Luper household in Oklahoma City on May 24, 2011, with filmmaker John Bishop, framing the interview within the Library of Congress and Smithsonian NMAAHC Civil Rights History Project. Cal Luper greets Mosnier and references storm weather outside.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project interview series

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Brother President and the Youth Council
clips.summary: Cal recalls his sister Marilyn Luper and the 1958 NAACP Youth Council trip to New York City with the play Brother President. He describes a surviving film of the performance held at a local OKC photo shop and introduces other family members involved in the Youth Council, including his cousins Betty Germany and Richard Brown, an original sit-in participant.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: The Sit-In Sofa
clips.summary: Cal describes the Luper family living-room sofa where Youth Council meetings were held, recovered in the 1980s after surviving the sit-in movement, including scorching from a Molotov cocktail thrown through the front door of the house. He frames the recovered sofa as a physical relic of the campaign.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Downtown OKC Segregation
clips.summary: Cal describes the segregated geography of downtown Oklahoma City, the Skirvin and Huckins hotels were closed to Black patrons except as workers, and most lunch counters refused service. He recalls the Hudson hotel elevators and the contrast of being able to walk a New York lobby without being told to leave. The Katz Drug Store stood at Main and Robinson where the Ford Theater now stands.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Grandmother's Commute and Carverdale
clips.summary: Cal describes his grandmother's daily two-bus commute from the Carverdale neighborhood, a predominantly Black addition near Northeast Tenth and what is now Martin Luther King Avenue, to downtown Oklahoma City, with transfers near Johnny Browns. He gives her address as 1236 Windermere and notes she was the family's willing babysitter.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Youth Council Discipline and the Commanders
clips.summary: Cal describes the self-discipline structure of the Oklahoma City Youth Council, including a unit called the Commanders responsible for protecting members from outside harassment and from internal misconduct. Clara Luper required participants to carry schoolbooks to sit-ins at Katz Drug Store and Bishop's Restaurant, where Cal studied first-year Spanish while seated at the counter. Bishop's locked its doors against demonstrators.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Clara Luper's Public Bearing and Radio Show
clips.summary: Cal describes how his mother conducted herself when negotiating with restaurant owners, mayors, governors, and police chiefs, looking directly at them rather than performing the deferential posture expected of Black Oklahomans. He recalls working with her on the Clara Luper radio show on Southeast 44th and Bryant for over thirty years, and the comedic call-and-response format they developed.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Cal's Education and Electrical Trade
clips.summary: Cal describes his time at the University of Oklahoma as a history major and how his attention was divided between school and his mother's organizing. He left to attend trade school for electrical work, earned a license, and worked alongside his father, also an electrician, for a couple of years before his father's death.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Nonviolence Versus Black Power
clips.summary: Cal reflects on returning from military service with weapons proficiency and considering Black Power organizing as a path forward. He argues against the strategic logic of armed confrontation, that nonviolence reaches a person's heart while a fist cannot beat a gun, and observes that even nonviolent activists studied judo because there is always somebody tougher. He concludes he would rather concede a point than be beaten to prove one.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Family Cost and Morning Discipline
clips.summary: Cal reflects on whether the family paid too high a price for his mother's organizing, recalling how the household's morning rhythm, meet at the church by nine, walk downtown to the sit-in target, set a lifelong pattern. He describes meeting at Calvary Baptist under Reverend Kerry (Morris Kerry), close enough to walk to Katz Drug Store, Bishop's Restaurant, or Johnny Browns.

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Allies of Clara Luper
clips.summary: Cal names key allies of his mother: Reverend Bratzinger of Fifth Street Baptist; Reverend Alexander of the AME church at 13th and Kelham; Dr. Charles N. Atkins, a Bahamian-born physician who debated strategy with Clara, brought babies into the world, and treated arthritis on the East Side; Hannah Atkins, his wife, later an Oklahoma state representative; and their son Ed, Cal's close friend.
related_events: Oklahoma City civil rights ministerial network

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: Melvin B. Porter and Sam Cornelius
clips.summary: Cal describes meeting Melvin B. Porter on the return trip from the Brother President performance in New York, Porter, then at Vanderbilt Law School, boarded the bus when he saw the Oklahoma banner and committed to opening a law firm in Oklahoma City. Porter became an Oklahoma state senator, and his sons Melvin Jr. and Joel continue the firm on Northwest 23rd and Hudson. Cal also recalls Sam Cornelius, director of the East Side YMCA where Monday-night Youth Council meetings were held before the Freedom Center opened, and Cornelius's children Bruce and Charlene (Cal's Douglass High School classmate).

clips.subject: Calvin "Cal" Luper
clips.title: The Board of Education and Closing
clips.summary: Cal explains the family joke of the Board of Education, a wooden board carved by Alexander Jones, mechanical arts teacher at Dunjee School and Clara Luper's commute partner from the 1500 block of NE Park Place to the 1800 block where Clara lived. Cal closes by referencing Professor McClure of the OU desegregation effort. The interview ends with the standard LoC/NMAAHC sign-off.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Meeting at Highlander, April 1960
clips.summary: Candie describes the April 1, 1960 Highlander gathering at Monteagle, Tennessee, where sit-in students convened weeks after Greensboro. Guy taught freedom songs including "I'm Gonna Sit at the Welcome Table," "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize," and "We Shall Overcome." Myles Horton led breakout discussions on non-violence as tactic versus philosophy and on jail-no-bail strategy. James Bevel and Bernard Lafayette participated in the singing breakouts when sessions grew tense.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Septima Clark and Myles Horton
clips.summary: Guy recalls driving Septima Clark on Highlander business in the late 1950s, including instances when police stopped them because Clark sat in the front seat. Candie describes Clark as a sensible neighbor on Johns Island, South Carolina, and emphasizes her toughness against the assumption of a sweet demeanor. Both characterize Myles Horton's educational method as drawing on participants' own experiences without intimidation.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Pomona to Fisk Exchange
clips.summary: Candie describes her California upbringing in a Unitarian fellowship, her interest awakened by Montgomery bus boycott coverage and high-school study of Gandhi, and her path to Pomona College where a returning student introduced her to the Fisk Exchange Program. She arrived in Nashville in January 1960, timed to coincide with the sit-in mobilization that semester.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Nashville Sit-ins and Mass Meeting Music
clips.summary: Candie recalls non-violent training under Reverend James Lawson at Fisk University and the electric reaction when Greensboro broke. She contrasts the camp songs and rock-and-roll she knew with the community singing of Nashville mass meetings, where religious songs were adapted with a few changed words into freedom songs. The Nashville Quartet became important to this work.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Guy's Musical Formation
clips.summary: Candie traces Guy's California background: people-songs work in Los Angeles, the Oliver family who introduced him to political music, his master's in sociology at UCLA where he absorbed Library of Congress recordings and the Lomaxes, and folklorist Wayland D. Hand's warning to let folk music remain politically neutral. After meeting Pete Seeger, Guy traveled the South in summer 1953 with Rambling Jack Elliott and Frank Hamilton, ending at Highlander.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Traveling Movement Concerts
clips.summary: The Carawans married in 1961. Candie describes their travel to civil rights communities across the South, including a Clarksdale, Mississippi joint concert with opera student Mary Jane Pigee and her mother Vera Mae Pigee's NAACP youth organizing. Police escorted them out of the black part of Clarksdale and ultimately jailed them. She was pregnant at the time.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Sung Verses and Jamila Jones
clips.summary: Candie and Guy perform verses of "Keep Your Eyes on the Prize" and "We Shall Overcome." Candie identifies Jamila Jones, a thirteen-year-old in an adult Montgomery delegation, as the person who first hummed and inserted the "We Are Not Afraid" verse during the 1959 raid on Highlander, in the dark with police inside the building.
keywords: Keep Your Eyes on the Prize

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Documenting Movement Music
