clips.summary: Candie describes Guy's 1961 decision after the Freedom Rider release in Jackson, Mississippi (following imprisonment at Parchman) to shift from song-leading to documentation. He purchased an Ampex tape recorder and built a relationship with Moe Asch at Folkways Records and Irwin Silber at Sing Out! magazine. The first Oak Publications songbook of freedom songs followed. Guy was stabbed by police at an SCLC annual conference and had his mimeographed materials confiscated.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Music as Non-Violent Tool
clips.summary: Candie recounts John Lewis's line that the movement without its music would be a bird without wings. She describes the We Shall Overcome Fund's distribution of commercial royalties to southern communities and quotes Bernice Johnson Reagon on the aggressive, non-passive power of movement singing. She discusses the shift from "We Shall Overcome" to "We'll Never Turn Back" when the original came to feel too optimistic or co-opted.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Tree of Life and Ed Cabbell
clips.summary: Candie and Guy perform "Ain't You Got a Right to the Tree of Life," a Johns Island song they collected. Candie tells the story of Ed Cabbell, a West Virginia African American friend and historian of Appalachian Black communities, who attended an early Highlander workshop. The performances bracket Candie's reflection on carrying fear in retrospect that she did not feel at the time.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Highlander Shifts to Appalachia
clips.summary: Candie describes Highlander's 1965 pivot in response to SNCC's challenge to white participants. Myles Horton accepted that Black people led the civil rights movement and that white organizers should build allies in white communities. Septima Clark moved to SCLC's Citizenship Education Program; Bernice Robinson followed. Highlander brought Appalachian staff onboard and began listening tours in mountain communities, looking for places with both anger and hope.

clips.subject: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
clips.title: Pitzer College and Field Study
clips.summary: After two years in Pittsfield, New York, the Carawans took a Highlander assignment in Pike County, Kentucky. A Pitzer College political-science faculty member named Lucien recruited Guy to teach there. The family moved to Claremont, California for their son's kindergarten year. After one semester Guy created an Appalachian Field Study Program, taking Pitzer and Pomona College students into the mountains. Candie performs Joan Boyd's "Come Home," a song from Big Stone Gap, Virginia.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Births and Claiborne County Origins
clips.summary: James Miller recounts being born at the Alcorn State University infirmary in 1953 because his mother had a difficult pregnancy and midwives like Mrs. Jessie Dismuke delivered most black babies in the county. Carolyn Miller describes her upbringing in Hermanville with her paternal grandparents, where her grandmother taught second grade and her grandfather farmed several hundred acres of land.
related_events: Black family life in 1950s rural Mississippi

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Class and Cross-Racial Relationships
clips.summary: Both speakers describe the layered class structure of Claiborne County's black community, sharecroppers and field workers, the black middle class of teachers and business owners, and the paternalistic relationships powerful white men maintained with both groups. James details how Sheriff Dan McCay advanced his father money toward James's Alcorn tuition while at the same time the family was active in the movement.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Veteran Fathers and Family Trauma
clips.summary: Both speakers' fathers served in the military, James's father in the European theater of World War II, Carolyn's father in the Korean War. Carolyn recounts how her father returned with schizophrenia and shell shock that the VA failed to treat adequately, and how attorney Alvin Binder helped the family arrange VA mental-health interventions when her father went off his medication.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Hermanville School and Black Educators
clips.summary: Carolyn describes her all-black elementary school in Hermanville with six teachers including her grandmother Lulu Bell Jennings Turnipseed. James reflects on the rich tradition of Addison High School educators, the Reeves family, the Martins, the Shafers, Mr. Dodd, Mr. Holtz, and on what was lost when integration drew the black community away from its own institutions.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fair Street and the Black Commercial District
clips.summary: James describes Fair Street as a one-block black commercial and music district that on weekends drew packed crowds, black businesses, juke joints, music from Bobby "Blue" Bland, John Lee Hooker, and Muddy Waters, the Allen Motor Company across the way, and Mr. Gladio's fish market as the lone white business on the street. He recalls his near-fatal childhood encounter with a white family on Main Street when he accidentally knocked down a white girl, an incident that brought Emmett Till's killing into immediate personal scale.
related_events: Black commercial culture in segregated South

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Movement Arrival and NAACP Youth Chapter
clips.summary: Carolyn describes how her maternal grandmother brought her to early NAACP meetings, walking from Highway 552 to town, and how she was trained by the youth chapter under George Henry Walker to canvass and register voters. James locates the movement's local arrival around 1966 and identifies Rudy Shields as the principal outside organizer working alongside George Henry Walker.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Deacons for Defense and the Boycott
clips.summary: James recounts how the Deacons for Defense patrolled the community with weapons, enforced the boycott of white merchants, and publicly whipped a black minister who broke the boycott behind a Fair Street alley. Carolyn explains the Deacons' role guarding mass meetings and how Sheriff Dan McCay negotiated the de-escalation of open carry once both sides agreed to put weapons down.

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bi-Racial Committee and Claiborne Hardware Lawsuit
clips.summary: James and Carolyn describe the Bi-Racial Committee as a sophisticated white-installed counter-strategy, a handpicked set of black moderates intended to keep separate-but-equal in place, and contrast it with the Mississippi white-citizens-council institutional approach (Alvin Binder, the Chamberlain-Hunt Academy white-flight school). They locate the showdown meeting between Rudy Shields, Charles Evers, and the Bi-Racial Committee at First A.M.E. Church, leading into the white merchants' 1969 lawsuit that became NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. (458 U.S. 886).
related_events: NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. 458 U.S. 886

clips.subject: Carolyn Miller and James Miller (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Mass Meetings and Rudy Shields's Legacy
clips.summary: James describes mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church Port Gibson, overflow crowds standing outside, windows opened to let the freedom songs reach those who couldn't get in, Deacons standing as guard. He closes by arguing that Rudy Shields deserves a more prominent place in the historical record as the master organizer who empowered local people rather than centralizing his own role.
related_events: Mass meetings at First A.M.E. Church Port Gibson

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Family and Lamar Genealogy
clips.summary: Young describes her parents Lazarus and Lottie Lamar, her mother's work as a midwife who could turn breach babies, and her father's literacy at a third-grade level with strong mathematical ability. She traces the Lamar family's name change from "Raymond" (their plantation name) made by her great-great-grandfather, and names her maternal uncles James and George Bell of Barton and West Helena, Arkansas, and her aunt Bessie Lipsy of LaGrange.
related_events: Post-Reconstruction family-name changes from plantation names

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Sharecropping and Cotton Labor
clips.summary: Young details the daily labor of sharecropping in Lexa, Arkansas, picking and chopping cotton from sun-up to sundown, raising hogs and cleaning chitterlings, canning over 300 jars of food per year. She names Homer Tiles, a white farmer who protected her from her mother's whippings at the cotton-weighing scale, and recalls earning $3.50 per day chopping cotton and around $3 per hundred pounds picked.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Church, School, and Jim Crow Awareness
clips.summary: Young recalls First Baptist Church Lexa, Reverend Devaro, and superintendent Gus Wilburn, and her early exposure to gospel singing at revivals. She describes books with covers torn off and pages missing arriving from white schools, her mother's emphasis on reading despite her own inability to read or write, and an incident in which her father stood armed guard with other Black men to protect a young Black man falsely accused by a white merchant's daughter.
related_events: Local extralegal racial violence and Black community self-protection

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Phillips County and the Elaine Massacre
clips.summary: Young recalls being told as a teenager working the Phillips County fields that the unusual hardness of the cracked earth came from the blood in it, her introduction to the 1919 Elaine Massacre. She notes the NAACP had no visible presence in West Helena, Arkansas in her childhood, that news of the 1957 Little Rock Nine never reached her family, and that her home had no electricity until 1958.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: SNCC Arrives in West Helena, Arkansas
clips.summary: Young describes SNCC field organizers Howard Himmelbaum, Jim Jones, Bill Hansen, and Jerry Casey arriving in West Helena, Arkansas around 1965, holding meetings in local churches focused on voter education and poll tax abolition. She describes her father's fear of white retaliation and Molotov-cocktail attacks on civil rights workers' housing. She names Julian Bond, Andrew Young, Stokely Carmichael, Eldridge Cleaver, and Marion Barry as visitors to West Helena, Arkansas, and recounts her own work sitting on a storefront porch collecting poll-tax-abolition signatures.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Integrating the Bowling Alley
clips.summary: Young recounts SNCC organizer Stanley Tillman taking her to the segregated Midland Heights bowling alley between Helena, Arkansas and West Helena, Arkansas before the Civil Rights Act passed. They were denied rental shoes but bowled in their socks until police arrived; her father's reputation got her sent home rather than arrested. Young's first bowling-game score was 98.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Building Relationships with the Organizers
clips.summary: Young recounts how she came to know the SNCC organizers (most in their early twenties when she was about 15), the Ferndale, Arkansas workshops at a Presbyterian village, sit-ins at the Arkansas state capitol cafeteria where demonstrators were thrown out and mustard-gas-bombed, and the cotton-field assault by five young men at Snow Lake, Arkansas that left her hospitalized for three weeks. She also discusses the small mixed-race solidarity she experienced in Lexa from the Muscalino family and Homer Tiles.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Marriage to Howard Himmelbaum and Community Reaction
clips.summary: Young describes her secret engagement to SNCC organizer Howard Himmelbaum, her departure for Washington, D.C. and Head Start training in May 1966, and their March 1967 wedding in New York. She recounts her parents' reactions, her mother loved Howard, her father feared for her safety, and the hostility she received from members of the Black community for marrying a white man, including a confrontation with her father's friend who made a derogatory comment as she walked to church.
related_events: Interracial marriages in the post-Loving v. Virginia South

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: SNCC Tensions and the Helena Judge
clips.summary: Young recalls Judge Hester of Helena, Arkansas threatening James Heel in open court, telling the 21-year-old engineer with a master's degree to leave Helena, Arkansas "before you find yourself floating up." She describes the welcome SNCC organizers received from some Black pastors but not all, the freedom house in West Helena, Arkansas, Myrtle Glasgow's arrival from Oakland in 1964 and her becoming like an older sister to Young after the death of Young's biological sister. She also discusses the emerging tensions within SNCC between nonviolence and armed self-defense.
related_events: SNCC nonviolence vs. armed self-defense debate

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Memphis 1968 and Cornell BLF
clips.summary: Young describes traveling to Memphis in 1968 to hear and protect Dr. King, sharing rooms with Stokely Carmichael and Eldridge Cleaver. She recounts the rise of the Black Panther Party and the Cornell University Black Liberation Front armed-occupation photograph, including Ed Whitfield and "Eric" from Little Rock, Arkansas (the three young Cornell BLF members on the magazine cover). She argues armed-self-defense formations existed to protect leaders, not to attack white people.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: OIC, Class-Action Lawsuits, and Rockefeller
clips.summary: Young describes training at the Opportunities Industrialization Center as a key-punch operator, winning class-action discrimination lawsuits in Pulaski County against AMF Inc and National Old Line Life Insurance Company. She recounts being hired and then fired at the Arkansas Motor Vehicle Department by supervisor Lozano after he discovered she was married to a white man; attorney John W. Walker took the case to Governor Winthrop Rockefeller, who fired Lozano in the conference room. She credits the Black community's role in electing Rockefeller as Arkansas's first Republican governor of the era.
related_events: Young v. Lozano employment discrimination case

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Black United Youth and Earle, Arkansas
clips.summary: Young describes co-founding the Black United Youth in Little Rock, Arkansas around 1968-1971 with Bobby Brown, Bob Broadway (a Philander Smith College graduate and KOKY-AM news announcer working under Larry Hayes), and others. BUY focused on Black student representation in newly desegregated colleges and high schools. Young recounts being in Earle, Arkansas the night school authorities closed the Black high school and forced students into the white one, a state trooper terrorized residents and beat an elderly woman with a walking cane; Young drove her group back to Little Rock, Arkansas in a Mustang and John W. Walker called out the National Guard.
related_events: Black student-union organizing in Arkansas universities

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Political Analysis and Obama Reflections
clips.summary: Young reflects in 2011 on what changed and what did not after the 1960s, economic access for Black businesses remained limited; she perceived a shift in white drug-law treatment after Vietnam veterans returned addicted; she cites the September 2011 execution of Troy Davis as evidence the country is not progressing. She discusses her support for President Obama, the unprecedented demand that he produce a resume and birth certificate, and what she perceived as muted Black-Little-Rock enthusiasm during the 2008 primary.

clips.subject: Carrie Lamar Young (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Malcolm X Liberation University and Closing
clips.summary: Young describes her 1971 year at Malcolm X Liberation University in Durham, North Carolina, studying African ethnic groups and martial arts, and leaving after community members objected to her white husband's holiday visit. She closes by characterizing Worth Long as a sweet, gentle man who "danced to the sound of a different beat" and visited her parents whenever in West Helena, Arkansas, and by thanking her Modern Woodmen of America director Albert T. Hurst Jr. for giving her time off to do the interview.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Orangeburg Childhood Under Jim Crow
clips.summary: Williams describes growing up on Quick Street in Orangeburg, South Carolina, as the third child of Ethel and Cecil J. Williams Sr., a self-employed tailor and an educator. He details early experiences of segregated buses, schools, theaters, and lunch counters, and the parallel Black and white societies of 1940s Orangeburg under state-sanctioned Jim Crow.
related_events: segregated bus and movie theater system

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: First Camera and E.C. Jones Apprenticeship
clips.summary: Williams receives a Kodak Baby Brownie from his older brother around age 10 and begins photographing weddings and Sunday gatherings. Through his high school principal Robert Howard, he is introduced to Sumter photographer E.C. Jones of Majestic Studio, who pulls him into photographing Briggs petitioner meetings in Clarendon County and church gatherings of the DeLaine and Pearson families.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Thurgood Marshall and the Briggs Photograph
clips.summary: At age 11, Williams photographs Thurgood Marshall arriving by train in Charleston, South Carolina, for the Briggs v. Elliott hearings. He explains how he became an official Jet magazine correspondent at age 15, paid $35 per photograph, and the void in local press coverage of African American news that made his work necessary for the Afro-American, the Pittsburgh Courier, and Jet.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Brown Decision and Clandestine Discussion
clips.summary: Williams reflects on the muted response in the Black community to the 1954 Brown v. Board of Education ruling, given the dangers Briggs and DeLaine petitioners had already faced from the Ku Klux Klan and from economic retaliation by white employers. He notes that his mother taught under Rev. J.A. DeLaine, giving the family direct ties to the petitioners.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision May 1954

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Photographing the Klan and Meeting Kennedy
clips.summary: Williams recounts attending a Ku Klux Klan rally on Belleville Road as a teenage news photographer for the Orangeburg Times and Democrat, where the Grand Dragon and a state highway patrolman re-erected a burned cross to pose for him. He also describes traveling to New York during a Claflin semester break in January 1960 and being seated by Senator John F. Kennedy next to Chet Huntley and David Brinkley at the press conference where Kennedy announced his presidential candidacy.
related_events: Kennedy 1960 candidacy announcement at the Roosevelt Hotel

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fred H. Moore and the 1955 Boycott
clips.summary: Williams describes the 1955 Orangeburg counter-boycott after Black parents petitioned to integrate Orangeburg School District 5. SC State student government president Fred H. Moore led student support for the boycott and was expelled by president B.C. Turner. Williams's Jet magazine photographs of Moore and the student demonstrations cause Turner to rescind Williams's state scholarship, which Dr. H.V. Manning of Claflin University then matches.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Elloree Teachers and Arthur Rose
clips.summary: Williams photographs the Elloree School teachers who in April 1956 resigned rather than sign a state-mandated declaration disavowing NAACP membership. Their picture spans two pages of Jet magazine. Williams credits Claflin art professor Arthur Rose, a Briggs petition signer from Charleston, with shaping his political consciousness in art education classes.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Opening a Studio on Boulevard
clips.summary: After graduating from Claflin in 1960, Williams opens a photography studio on Boulevard in Orangeburg, directly opposite SC State College and Claflin University, giving him a literal front-row vantage for student marches. He earns income from college yearbook contracts and student portrait work.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Harvey Gantt at Clemson and Kennedy's Assassination
clips.summary: Williams covers Harvey Gantt's January 1963 admission to Clemson University, represented in court by Matthew J. Perry Jr. and Constance Baker Motley. As one of only three African Americans on the closed campus that morning, Williams takes exclusive photographs for Jet magazine and the Black press. He reflects on the community shock at President Kennedy's November 1963 assassination.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: St. Paul Methodist and the Selective Service
clips.summary: On Easter Sunday 1961, Williams photographs Mayor Clyde Fair turning away two African American students, Dorothy Vann and Emmanuel Hixson, from St. Paul Methodist Church in downtown Orangeburg. The Associated Press purchases the photograph for $35 and it runs on the front pages of newspapers worldwide. A month later, the Selective Service summons Williams to its Doyle Street office and drafts him into the Air Force, where he is injured in training.
related_events: 1961 St. Paul Methodist Church integration attempt

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bowling Alley Protests and Orangeburg Massacre
clips.summary: Williams describes the February 6, 1968 All-Star Bowling Alley protests led by SC State student John Stroman over the bowling alley's whites-only policy. On the night of February 8, 1968, nine SC Highway Patrolmen fired on SC State students gathered around a bonfire, killing three and wounding twenty-seven. Williams arrives the next morning, photographs the recovered shotgun shells, and is later approached by the FBI to become an agent at the request of J. Edgar Hoover, which he refuses.

clips.subject: Cecil J. Williams (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Pink Palace Arrests and the Books
clips.summary: Williams reviews photographs from his books Out of the Box in Dixie, Freedom & Justice, and The Orangeburg Massacre, including images of mass arrests at the Pink Palace stockade where 388 demonstrators were jailed, of 3,000-person 1963 downtown Orangeburg prayer demonstrations including his own mother, and of SC State student John Stroman, whom prosecutors tried to scapegoat. Cleveland Sellers, the only person jailed in connection with the massacre, later became president of Voorhees College.
keywords: Out of the Box in Dixie

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Hawaii to New York
clips.summary: Marshall describes growing up in Hawaii with no awareness of American racial hierarchy, attending school with Japanese, Filipino, and Chinese classmates. She arrives in New York, enrolls at Columbia University for stenographic study, and is sent by the employment office to the NAACP National Office, which she frames as a guardian-angel placement.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: NAACP National Office
clips.summary: Marshall recounts joining the branch department under Gloster B. Current, meeting executive secretary Walter White and Roy Wilkins, and taking minutes at annual conventions. She describes the office culture under Walter White (whom she calls Pops) and the controversy surrounding his divorce and remarriage to a white woman.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Marriage to Thurgood Marshall
clips.summary: Marshall describes her initial refusal of Thurgood Marshall's proposal out of concern that an interracial marriage would split the organization following the Walter White precedent. She recounts Thurgood's insistence and the family-like reception she received from NAACP colleagues who already knew her from branch and convention work.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Local Branches and Cases
clips.summary: Marshall recalls the cases that came through the National Office during her tenure, including the Groveland Four and Emmett Till, and the role of branches in carrying litigation forward. She quotes Thurgood's description of NAACP lawyers as cowards who could leave town while local people who put their names on briefs remained to face hostility.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Brown v. Board of Education
clips.summary: Marshall describes being pulled in to help with stenographic work on Brown v. Board of Education when the legal team was short-handed. She recounts Thurgood's response after the decision: that the celebration should end because the work was just beginning, framing him as a realist about post-Brown resistance.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision (1954)

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Dining Car Integration Test
clips.summary: Marshall recounts traveling to Chattanooga roughly a week after the Henderson v. United States ruling with attorney Franklin H. Williams. Gloster Current and a membership secretary declined to integrate the dining car; Williams (light-skinned) and Marshall (with long hair and a flower) tested the head waiter's ability to classify them and were seated in the previously segregated section.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Stepping Back and Looking Forward
clips.summary: Marshall notes that she stopped working at the NAACP in 1955 but kept in close contact. She names Robert L. Carter and Mildred Bond Roxborough among the colleagues she remembers, and comments on Shelby County v. Holder as a recent court reversal in the longer arc of progress.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Washington, D.C. and Faith
clips.summary: Marshall describes the move from New York to Washington, D.C., the constraints on her social life because of her husband's legal and judicial work, and the role of religious faith and the church as sustaining structure during that period.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Morningside Gardens
clips.summary: Marshall describes the family's New York residences at 409 Edgecombe Avenue and then at the Morningside Gardens cooperative on West 123rd Street. She names neighbors Edward R. Dudley, Robert L. Carter, Richard Parrish, dentist George Dockery, and artist Louis Jefferson, and recalls the Dockery and Parrish wives competing to leave food for her sons.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Lake Barcroft Integration
clips.summary: Marshall describes the move from D.C. to Lake Barcroft, Virginia at the urging of Attorney General Ramsey Clark, who told Thurgood the family needed to integrate the community. She reports the family remained the only Black household there and that the neighborhood kids welcomed her sons on arrival.

clips.subject: Cecilia Suyat Marshall
clips.title: Boards and Reflection
clips.summary: Marshall lists her board service: the National Capitol Auxiliary of Supreme Court wives, Travelers Aid, and the NAACP Legal Defense Fund. She names Brown v. Board of Education as the case that stands out, describes herself as an ordinary person doing ordinary things, and closes with her son John W. Marshall, who served at the United States Marshals Service.
related_events: NAACP Legal Defense Fund board service

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Massillon Roots and Steel-Football Town
clips.summary: McDew describes his birth on June 23, 1938 in Massillon, Ohio, a town of about 38,000 known for steel production and football. His father and grandfather played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers, and the Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton sits eight miles away. His father worked for Republic Steel.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Union Heritage and Integrated Schools
clips.summary: McDew recalls growing up active in United Steel Workers Local 1124, where his grandfather, uncles, and father all worked the steel mills. He describes Massillon's integrated neighborhood schools, his sister becoming one of the first Black teachers in Massillon public schools, and his father's training as a teacher that could not be used in the North before the 1954 Brown decision.
related_events: 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Early Exposure to Race and Civil Rights
clips.summary: McDew describes pre-South civil rights awareness: traveling from Massillon to Pittsburgh as a child to support Bobby Grier of the Pitt football team when bowl organizers refused to play against a team with a Black player; writing letters to James Lawson, minister at his mother's St. James A.M.E. Church and a conscientious objector in jail; and his uncle Horace Gillom playing for the Cleveland Browns.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Massillon as Immigrant America
clips.summary: McDew frames Massillon as an expression of the American dream built by worker-immigrants from Greece, Spain, Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, and the American South, all coming for decent jobs, schools, and health care. He recounts hearing General Jacob Coxey himself at the barbershop, alongside grandfathers who had marched in Coxey's Army of the Poor.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Steel Economics and Going South to College
clips.summary: McDew describes learning supply and demand from the rhythms of Mesabi iron ore arriving in Massillon, becoming steel, going to Detroit auto plants, then layoffs. He recounts his father's decision to send him to South Carolina State College in Orangeburg in fall 1959, against his own plan to play football at Michigan and turn pro, to expose him to a Southern Black professional class.
related_events: McDew's matriculation at South Carolina State, fall 1959

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: First Encounter with Segregation
clips.summary: McDew describes the drive from Massillon to Orangeburg on Route 21, his father preparing him for bathroom and food segregation once they crossed into West Virginia, packed lunches because no places would feed Black travelers, and his initial paradise at South Carolina State surrounded by Black female classmates he was not related to.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Thanksgiving 1959: Three Arrests in Two Days
clips.summary: McDew recounts being radicalized over Thanksgiving 1959 in Sumter, South Carolina, where his roommate Charles Gatson had taken him home. He fought back when a police officer struck him for not saying "yes sir" and was beaten bloody with broken arm and jaw, arrested for disturbing the peace, re-arrested the same evening for refusing the segregated baggage car on the train, and arrested a third time for cutting through Edisto Gardens park on a forbidden day.
related_events: McDew's Thanksgiving 1959 arrests in Sumter, SC

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Greensboro Sit-In and Orangeburg Spokesman
clips.summary: McDew describes being asked by South Carolina State students to be their spokesman after the February 1, 1960 sit-in by North Carolina A&T students in Greensboro. He became head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement, was arrested delivering a petition to Governor Ernest "Fritz" Hollings in Columbia, and was arrested with roughly 800 others in Orangeburg demanding desegregation of public places.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Shaw Conference and SNCC Founding
clips.summary: McDew narrates the April 1960 Shaw University conference convened by Dr. King and SCLC. He refused King's proposal that students join SCLC and accept nonviolence as a lifelong creed, arguing nonviolence was a tactic, not a way of life, and would not work against an amoral society. He and others met down the hall to form the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, with the "nonviolent" word retained at the urging of James Lawson's Nashville students.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: SNCC Chair and Rock Hill Jail-No-Bail
clips.summary: McDew describes electing Marion Barry as SNCC's first chairman to take the students' concerns to the 1960 political conventions, then succeeding Barry as chair in the fall. He recounts SNCC's development of the jail-no-bail tactic and its first deployment in Rock Hill, South Carolina, supported by Rev. C.A. Ivory and NAACP state head I. DeQuincey Newman.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Mississippi and the Black Belt Strategy
clips.summary: McDew narrates SNCC's 1961 entry into McComb, Mississippi for voter registration, and the strategic logic: power in Congress sat with white Southern committee chairs from heavily Black counties where Black people did not vote. SNCC targeted Dougherty, Terrell, and Baker counties in Southwest Georgia and Amite, Pike, and Walthall counties in Southwest Mississippi to dislodge senators like John Stennis, James Eastland, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge.

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Blood Price and the Pledge
clips.summary: McDew describes SNCC's internal pledge that violence would be the price of voter-registration work and that beatings, bombings, or killings would not turn them away. He frames this as the key SNCC commitment that distinguished the organization from prior generations who had been scared off by violence.
related_events: SNCC voter-registration violence in Mississippi and Georgia

clips.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
clips.title: Education and the Unfinished Struggle
clips.summary: McDew closes with reflections on education as the key to the future, his speech at Colby College for the Posse Foundation, President Obama's donation of his Nobel Peace Prize money to the Posse Foundation, the Mississippi public-school requirement to study civil rights, the forthcoming Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, and the continuing struggle for democracy.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Childhood in Segregated Jackson
clips.summary: McLaurin describes being born December 25, 1939 in Jackson, Mississippi to John and Connie McLaurin, his grandmother's role as schoolteacher and her introduction of Booker T. Washington, W.E.B. Du Bois, and George Washington Carver. He attended Smith-Robertson Elementary, Marisey Jones Elementary, and Lanier High School on Farish Street.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Early Racial Lessons
clips.summary: McLaurin recounts three childhood incidents that taught him about white supremacy: a train station encounter where a white man accused him of peeping at white women, a store job where white men berated him for writing a phone order in front of a white woman, and a Rankin County furniture auction where a constable threatened him for wearing an Army shirt with a PFC stripe.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Army Service and Awakening
clips.summary: McLaurin describes joining the US Army Reserve in 1955 at age 15, his six-month active-duty training at Fort Jackson, South Carolina under the newly desegregated military, his letter to Congressman John Bell Williams seeking discharge, and his return to Lanier High School having seen "beyond Jackson" for the first time.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Meeting Medgar Evers and the NAACP
clips.summary: McLaurin describes hitchhiking from Georgetown into Jackson and being picked up by Medgar Evers, learning Evers's identity from newspaper coverage, visiting the NAACP youth chapter office, and the inter-school rivalry between Lanier and Jim Hill that drew him toward retaliating against white drive-by attackers before NAACP organizing redirected him.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: James Bevel and the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: McLaurin recounts Rev. James Bevel coming to the Georgetown pool hall in 1961 and challenging the youth gang to ride to New Orleans rather than fight each other. His friends James Jones, Ralph Floyd, Levin Brown, and Charles Cox accepted the challenge and were jailed at Parchman, where Jesse Harris was "stretched" for refusing to count off as "nigger four."

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: State Fair Arrest and Medgar's Bail
clips.summary: McLaurin describes his arrest at the Mississippi State Fair after attempting to buy a ticket to the previously segregated fair, Medgar Evers posting his bond, and Evers drawing a circle around the Mississippi Delta on a state map to explain how voter registration would transform legislative apportionment.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Bob Moses and Entry to the Delta
clips.summary: McLaurin describes meeting Lawrence Guyot at the SNCC Freedom House at 714 Rose Street, his expectations of Bob Moses as a "Paul Bunyan type," and the August 1962 night drive to Greenwood with Moses, James Jones, and Landy McNair. They met Sam Block and Wazir Peacock in Greenwood after the White Citizens Council had run them out of Barnes Studio.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Amzie Moore and the Ruleville Assignment
clips.summary: McLaurin describes arriving at Amzie Moore's house at 614 Chrisman Avenue in Cleveland, learning Moore's biography of NAACP organizing under cover of his choir's church visits and Aaron Henry's banking work via Tri-State Bank of Memphis. The next morning at Mt. Galilee Baptist Church, Moses introduced McLaurin, Charles Cobb, and Landy McNair to Joe and Rebecca McDonald, who became their host family in Ruleville.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: First Voter Registration Trips
clips.summary: McLaurin describes the Ruleville mayor (Charles Dorrough) arresting him, Cobb, and McNair shortly after arrival, escorting the three to city hall and back, and the first voter registration trip to Indianola with two older women driven by Tommy Johnson. He describes his transformative moment at the Indianola courthouse confronting the fear of lynching.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Fannie Lou Hamer Joins the Movement
clips.summary: McLaurin describes the August 31, 1962 bus trip from Ruleville to Indianola with 18 registrants including Fannie Lou Hamer, the bus driver's arrest for the bus being "too yellow," and Hamer's confrontation with plantation owner W.D. Marlow where she told him "I didn't go for you. I went for myself."

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Night Riders and Joe McDonald's Stand
clips.summary: McLaurin recounts the September 1962 night-rider attack on Ruleville that shot up the McDonald home, the Sissle home (wounding two Jackson State students), and Mary Tucker's home where Hamer was staying. The Ruleville mayor confiscated Joe McDonald's gun the next day; McDonald, with Charles Cobb's assistance citing the Second Amendment, retrieved it from city hall in defiance.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Finding Fannie Lou Hamer in Tallahatchie
clips.summary: McLaurin describes Bob Moses assigning him to locate Hamer in Tallahatchie County (Cascilla) where she had taken refuge. After getting lost, getting his car stuck in a ditch, and being pulled out by a white farmer, he found Hamer in a plantation shack and drove her to Tougaloo College, where she joined a SNCC delegation traveling to Fisk University in Nashville.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Freedom Summer 1964
clips.summary: McLaurin describes initial opposition to bringing northern white volunteers to the Delta, his change of view after seeing the relationships between volunteers like Tracy Sugarman and host families like the Williams family in Sunflower County, the canary-yellow car incident with Linda Davis driving Black children, and the discovery that white people and Black sharecroppers could form genuine bonds.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Mock Election and MFDP Congressional Run
clips.summary: McLaurin describes the Freedom Vote with Aaron Henry for governor, Rev. Ed King for lieutenant governor, and Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine for Congress. He recounts being summoned to take Hamer to the Mississippi Secretary of State's office to legally qualify for MS-2 Congress, paying the $500 filing fee via COFO, and reluctantly signing as her campaign manager when no one else arrived.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Greenville Courtroom and Supreme Court Case
clips.summary: McLaurin describes desegregating the Greenville municipal courtroom by sitting on the empty white side, his arrest for "making statements calculated to provoke a breach of the peace," and his case's three trips to the US Supreme Court between 1963 and 1966-67. Police Chief Burnley arranged his quiet release on condition of one-year absence; R. Jess Brown was his attorney.
related_events: Burnley as later mayor of Greenville

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Atlantic City Compromise Rejection
clips.summary: McLaurin describes the MFDP delegation's rejection of the two-at-large-seats compromise at the 1964 Atlantic City Democratic Convention, the Lyndon B. Johnson administration's orchestration via Hubert H. Humphrey, Walter Mondale, Joseph Rauh Jr., Walter Reuther, Aaron Henry, and Rev. Ed King, and the post-1964 realignment of the white South into the Republican Party.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Burying Fannie Lou Hamer
clips.summary: McLaurin describes his hospital promise to Hamer not to bury her on a plantation, his negotiation with Maxwell Funeral Home in Greenwood and switch to Henry Espy's funeral home, Cleve McDowell's legal work to establish the burial site within Ruleville city limits, Owen Brooks of the Delta Ministry coordinating the funeral, and MACE/Delta Foundation under Charles Bannerman paying the nearly $10,000 cost.

clips.subject: Charles McLaurin (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Meredith March and Black Power
clips.summary: McLaurin describes James Meredith's June 1966 March Against Fear from the Tennessee state line, Meredith's shooting in ambush, the continuation by King, Floyd McKissick, and Stokely Carmichael, and the Greenwood encampment at the Black park after Mississippi Valley State College President J.H. White refused the campus. McLaurin was at Carmichael's side when Carmichael delivered the "Black Power" speech, which Willie Ricks had been chanting earlier.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Arrival in Albany and Romans 8
clips.summary: Sherrod describes arriving in Albany, Georgia around October 1961 with the chain-gang experience from Rock Hill, South Carolina behind him. He explains how the eighth chapter of Romans grounded his conviction that nothing but death could stop the mission of developing his people.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Reading the Community's Fear
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts learning how to reach the elder generation who would not lift their eyes to whites, the indignity of Black women required to wear stocking caps before trying on hats in segregated stores, and the fear of confronting the county sheriff. He describes the prior civic work of the Criterion Club petitioning the City Commission for paved streets and lighting.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Building the Youth Base
clips.summary: Sherrod describes obtaining permission from Albany's adults alongside Charles A. Jones and Cordell Reagon, recruiting young people first into small church meetings (the Presbyterian church opened first), and bringing in the east, west, north, and south side neighborhood gangs as nonviolent marchers. He emphasizes the weekly nonviolence preparation and the rule against switchblade knives in the church before marches.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: First Marches and Jail Strategy
clips.summary: Sherrod recounts the bus-station integration test with Jones and Cordell Reagon, the first mass march of 255 people that filled Shiloh Baptist Church and Mount Zion Baptist Church, and Laurie Pritchett's strategy of distributing arrestees to jails in Americus, Terrell County, Baker County, and Mitchell County, Georgia. He notes the picketing campaign against downtown stores and the Smith Grocery closure after two days.

clips.subject: Rev. Charles Melvin Sherrod
clips.title: Baker County Beating and the Reunion
clips.summary: Sherrod describes the Baker County, Georgia voter-registration march of approximately 150 people from the church to the courthouse, the white men with ax handles who beat him to the ground, and the elder workshop participant whose shielding action ended the assault. He closes by noting the Southwest Georgia 53rd Reunion week's signing of a radio-station license and a New Communities Inc. land contract.
related_events: Southwest Georgia Civil Rights Movement 53rd Reunion 2011

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Baton Rouge Family and Early Influences
clips.summary: Siler describes his upbringing in Baton Rouge by a beautician mother, piano-teacher grandmother, and railroad-janitor grandfather who rode a bicycle to work to avoid paying segregated bus fare. He details a family of six maternal uncles, several World War II and Korean War veterans, who ran independent businesses including a Lafayette cleaners, tailoring shop, insurance sales, and catering. His grandmother encouraged his early drawing; his father played baseball with Satchel Paige and started a Compton, California Black baseball league.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: First Protests at McKinley High
clips.summary: Siler recounts his first protest, quitting the Boy Scouts as a 12-year-old after Black scouts were placed behind horses in the Baton Rouge parade, then forming an explorer post at his school. At McKinley High School in spring 1960, he and classmates including Moses Edwards, Betty Jones, and E. Nola Price organized a picket of the segregated state library after final examinations. The Southern University demonstration of March 1960 had locked students on campus to prevent them joining.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Family Models of Standing Up
clips.summary: Siler describes how his uncles modeled resistance to white violence: his uncle Willie Jr., shot by another American soldier and carrying shrapnel until his death; his uncle Gene, who ran the first Black cleaners in Lafayette and kept guns hidden throughout his property; his uncle Ralph, who beat an assailant in an Army barracks and returned to his prayers. The grandfather rode a bicycle to the railroad rather than ride a segregated bus.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Southern University Sit-ins and Jail
clips.summary: As a sophomore art major at Southern University in 1961, Siler trained in nonviolent protest under CORE's Dave Dennis Sr. and Jerome Smith and was arrested December 12, 1961 picketing the Bon Marché Center with Willie Bradford and Thomas Peete. He spent the Christmas holidays in the East Baton Rouge Parish jail, where Baton Rouge police tear-gassed an estimated 5,000 protesters who had assembled outside singing "We Shall Overcome." Lolis Elie, Robert F. Collins, and Nils R. Douglas served as defense counsel.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Voter Registration and NAACP Atlanta Convention
clips.summary: Expelled from Southern after the December 1961 arrests, Siler was hired by Rev. T.J. Jemison and Rev. Robert Walker to run an NAACP voter registration project in South Baton Rouge, walking door-to-door to teach residents to pass voter registration forms. At the 1962 NAACP convention in Atlanta, he confronted a Klan picket line, joined a sit-in at the Krystal restaurant with Al Jones, met Medgar Evers three days before his June 1963 assassination, and ran for the NAACP Youth Commission. Clara Luper, Daisy Bates, and Ella Baker mentored him.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Federal Injunction and Return to Southern
clips.summary: In fall 1962 Siler took children to Fuqua elementary school and was threatened with prosecution for violating a federal injunction directed at CORE. Rev. Jemison brought in NAACP attorney Gloster Current, who negotiated with East Baton Rouge District Attorney Sargent Pitcher; the case did not proceed. Siler returned to Southern, became newspaper editor and a cartoonist for the student paper, and graduated in January 1966.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: California, the Draft, and Vietnam
clips.summary: Siler moved briefly to California to study at Frank Silvera's American Theatre, worked with the Hollywood NAACP, and was drafted in 1967. After nuclear weapons school and light vehicle driver training, he was assigned at Fort Huachuca, Arizona, where he was on charge of quarters the night Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated and worked to defuse tensions among Black soldiers. He deployed to Vietnam as a senior clerk in an AG section at Qui Nhon.

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Vietnam Information Office and Black Soldiers
clips.summary: At First Logistical Command's information office at Qui Nhon, Siler wrote hometown news releases on Black soldiers, work that previously accounted for only about five percent of the unit's output. He documented an orphanage where Vietnamese children had lost limbs and recounts witnessing race-related fights between Black and white American troops in Vietnam cities. He worked with Major (later General) Sherian Cadoria, a fellow Southern University alumna.
related_events: Race riots among U.S. troops in Vietnam

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Southern Sports, Black Culture, and the State Museum
clips.summary: After Vietnam, Siler became sports information director at Southern University, working with Mel Blount and Harold Carmichael. He joined Black Associated Sports Enterprise, an early attempt at a Black sports television network that preceded BET, and proposed films on Robert Smalls. Returning to Louisiana, he served at the Louisiana Office of Black Culture under director Ruth Wesley, the first state-level Black culture division, created by Governor Edwin Edwards. He then joined the Louisiana State Museum, where his 1994 Mardi Gras Indians exhibit drew on African-American photographers.
related_events: Louisiana Office of Black Culture founding

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Culture, Politics, and Teaching
clips.summary: Siler reflects on the inseparability of politics and culture and his work teaching children about jazz, the blues, and Mardi Gras Indian beadwork at museum workshops. He discusses being an editorial cartoonist for the Louisiana Weekly and the Black Commentator, his friendships with comics including Sally Marr at the Comedy Store and Improv in the 1970s and 1980s, and his self-definition as an "American African."

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Mentors, the Pen, and Mrs. Williams's Vote
clips.summary: Siler credits Southern University humanities professor Huel Perkins, who closed his classes reciting Cyrano de Bergerac, and art faculty Gene Paul Hubbard, Frank Hayden, and Harold Cureau as formative mentors. He describes accepting the Library of Congress and NMAAHC invitation after the birth of his grandson Ethan, so the family record would survive. He closes with the story of Mrs. Williams, an 84-year-old Baton Rouge woman who succeeded on her third attempt to register to vote in 1962; Siler carried a hidden gun in case of attack.
related_events: John Hope Franklin and Lonnie Bunch interview invitation

clips.subject: Charles "Chuck" Siler
clips.title: Faith, Dialogue, and Closing Reflection
clips.summary: Siler discusses his independent political stance, his friendships with a Black Republican, a Libertarian media operative, and two Muslim imams, and his appreciation for the American Friends Service Committee, which provided meeting space to the civil rights movement. He criticizes Black ministers who refused to let voter-registration workshops meet in their churches out of fear and concludes that artists' motivation comes from the audience, not money.
related_events: American Friends Service Committee civil rights support

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Birth and Catholic Boarding School
clips.summary: Jones describes being born in 1931 in Philadelphia to domestic-servant parents employed by the J.B. Lippincott family. From age six until nearly fourteen, he was placed at a Catholic boarding school for indigent Negro and Native American boys run by the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament. He recounts the Irish nuns' discipline, the Latin instruction that exempted him from the requirement in high school, and developing a serious stutter during this period.
related_events: Placement at Catholic boarding school for indigent Negro and Native American boys

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Palmyra High School and Columbia
clips.summary: Jones attended Palmyra High School in Palmyra, New Jersey, graduating in 1949 as most likely to succeed. Teachers paid his college application fees out of pocket. He attended Columbia College on a scholarship requiring a B-minus average, taking classes from Nobel laureates Linus Pauling and Polykarp Kusch.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Mother's Death and Clarinet
clips.summary: While taking midterm exams in January 1952, Jones learned that his mother had been operated on for terminal abdominal-rectal cancer. He set up a study desk at the foot of her bed and finished the semester. She died May 4, 1952, leaving a handwritten letter under her mattress. Jones also recounts his parallel life as a clarinetist, attending Juilliard summer programs and meeting Charlie Parker at Manny's Music in Manhattan.
related_events: Charlie Parker encounter at Manny's Music

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Paul Robeson and Political Awakening
clips.summary: Jones describes meeting Paul Robeson through his uncle, who had acted with Robeson on Broadway. Robeson's televised confrontation with Senator James Eastland before the House Un-American Activities Committee, in which he challenged Eastland's constitutional authority based on Mississippi's exclusion of Black voters, influenced Jones's political consciousness. He recounts Robeson's lesson on the racial significance of a touchdown by a Negro player at Baker's Field.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Draft and Loyalty Oath Refusal
clips.summary: After his student deferment lapsed in 1953, Jones was drafted at Mt. Holly, New Jersey. The night before induction he wrote a handwritten statement citing the First, Fifth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Amendments, stating he would not serve while denied first-class citizenship. He refused to raise his hand at induction and refused to sign the attorney general's loyalty oath listing 400 subversive organizations. He was assigned to Fort Dix Company D, 47th Regiment.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Undesirable Discharge and Appeal
clips.summary: After 21 months at Fort Dix as a soldier of the month and good-conduct-medal recipient, Jones was charged as a security risk on allegations including the unsigned loyalty oath and friendships with leftists. A board hearing issued an undesirable discharge August 14, 1955. ACLU attorney Stanley Faulkner filed a writ of mandamus and won an honorable discharge through the Armed Services Discharge Review Board at the Pentagon, qualifying Jones for the Korean Conflict GI Bill.
related_events: Pentagon Armed Services Discharge Review Board appeal

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Marriage to Ann Norton
clips.summary: Jones recounts meeting Ann Norton, daughter of the W.W. Norton publishing family, at the New York Public Library shortly after his discharge. They married, and his stepfather-in-law Daniel H. Crena de Iongh, a Dutch banker and early World Bank treasurer, was personally close to South African apartheid architect Hendrik Verwoerd. Jones describes the eventual reconciliation through chamber music when his mother-in-law heard him play the Mozart clarinet concerto.
related_events: Chamber music sessions in Wilton, Connecticut

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Boston University Law and Entertainment Practice
clips.summary: At Boston University Law School, Jones rented an apartment at 857 Beacon Street through Irish-American superintendent goodwill rooted in his boarding-school nun upbringing. His copyright-law paper won honorable mention in the Nathan Burkan Memorial Copyright Competition, leading professor Donald Shapiro to refer him to Leslie Sharlow, general counsel of Revue Productions in California, where Jones briefly worked alongside Bobby Darin and Sandy Dennis before Sharlow's sudden death.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Recruitment by King
clips.summary: In February 1960, Judge Hubert T. Delany asked Jones to clerk on King's Alabama state perjury defense. Jones declined due to family obligations. King flew to Los Angeles for a World Affairs Council engagement and visited Jones's Altadena home with Bernard Lee. The next day at a Los Angeles church, King preached about the responsibility of Negro professionals, weaving Jones's biography and the Langston Hughes poem Mother to Son into his sermon. Jones approached the pulpit and asked when to leave for Montgomery.
related_events: Jones's recruitment to King's legal team

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: March on Washington Planning
clips.summary: Jones recounts the post-Birmingham wave of more than 1,300 demonstrations across 200 cities and 36 states between May and August 1963. While King was advised against vacationing in the Bahamas or Jamaica, Stanley Levison and Bayard Rustin arranged for King's family to stay at Jones's home. The June 22, 1963 White House meeting in which President Kennedy demanded King sever ties with Levison and Jack O'Dell on FBI Communist Party allegations led to Jones becoming the operational conduit between King and Levison.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: I Have a Dream Speech
clips.summary: Jones describes drafting opening paragraphs at the Willard Hotel, including the promissory note metaphor inspired by raising bail money at Chase Manhattan Bank. King delivered the first six or seven paragraphs verbatim, then moved the written text aside when Mahalia Jackson called out for the dream and continued extemporaneously. Jones characterizes the closing as completely spontaneous except for the Free at Last spiritual quotation. He had argued before the march committee that conservative clergy should not impose eight-minute speaking limits or place King in the middle of the program; A. Philip Randolph backed his argument.

clips.subject: Clarence B. Jones (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Reflections on King's Legacy
clips.summary: Jones interprets King's political genius as summoning the conscience of the 88 percent majority to recognize the moral contradiction in denying equal protection to the 12 percent. He extends the analysis to the 2013 commemoration of the I Have a Dream speech, the Newtown school shooting, the Memphis sanitation workers' strike, Beyond Vietnam, Occupy Wall Street, the Omega Boys Club work of Dr. Joseph Marshall Jr., and the question of what King would have said about same-sex marriage.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Marion County Sharecropping Childhood
clips.summary: Magee describes being born May 1932 in Columbia, Marion County, Mississippi, the oldest of ten siblings. His father was a sharecropper who vowed his children would attend school and eventually purchased four acres. The family raised chickens, cows, hogs, and corn, drew water from a hillside spring, and studied by firelight without electricity or indoor plumbing.
related_events: Family migration from sharecropping to land ownership

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Family, Discipline, and Cotton
clips.summary: Magee recalls his mother's death during childbirth thirty days into his Alcorn semester, his father's diabetic coma the following month, and his grandmother raising the younger siblings. He describes his father as a 'trainer' who paid the children a penny per pound of cotton picked, taught them to weigh their own loads, and motivated him through a scolding that led Magee to outpick his father from that day forward.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Segregated Schools and Alcorn College
clips.summary: Magee contrasts his walk to a homemade bus owned by his uncle with the white students' real bus that drove past his house, and recalls books with previous students' names written inside them. He attended Alcorn A&M College (now Alcorn State University) starting in 1950, worked through PES control and the model home program to pay room and board, and received the nickname KOTAKT for carrying garbage from the female dormitory.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Army Service and Move to Hattiesburg
clips.summary: Magee was drafted after his deferred Alcorn graduation, served two years including a posting at Fort Eustis, Virginia, and was sent to Germany for fourteen months. He married Carey Bradley while in service. After discharge in 1956 he taught at Wiggins for one year, Prentiss Institute for two years coaching the college basketball team, then elementary grades in Hattiesburg before joining Lillie Burney School and later Rowan High School in 1969.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Voter Registration and U.S. v. Lynd
clips.summary: After purchasing his Hattiesburg home in 1956, Magee began paying the two-dollar poll tax and attempting to register with the Forrest County circuit clerk. He recounts being called 'boy' by registrar Theron Lynd, being told the registration was 'not quite satisfactory' without explanation, and his wife Carey Bradley McGee taking the test three times. He was subpoenaed when the federal Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals case proceeded, with John Doar prosecuting using his wife's application against that of an illiterate white truck driver who passed.
related_events: U.S. v. Lynd federal voter registration case
Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals proceedings

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: NAACP and Freedom Summer Support
clips.summary: Magee joined the NAACP through his church St. James CME, where branch president J.C. Fairley was a member. After Freedom Summer began, his church provided housing and meals for incoming volunteers, and Magee conducted sensitivity training preparing Northern students for Mississippi conditions, including advising against calling police 'pigs.' He worked closely with Dr. C.E. Smith, who succeeded Fairley as branch president, and served on the NAACP education committee.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Biracial Education Committee
clips.summary: Magee served on the biracial committee working with the Hattiesburg school district on desegregation strategy. He describes the committee's pre-meeting role-play preparations, witnessing Earline Boas (NAACP education committee chair) confront D.R. Patrick when he pointed his finger in her face, and meetings with Superintendent Dr. Spinks. Magee says the immediate goal was better supplies and equipment rather than 'rubbing shoulders with other races.'

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Clyde Kennard and Vernon Dahmer
clips.summary: In December 1962, Magee, his wife, and T.F. Boyne were subpoenaed to Aberdeen, Mississippi to witness that Clyde Kennard was still alive at Parchman Farm, with attorney R. Jess Brown representing the case. Magee knew Vernon Dahmer through his two sons who were Alcorn classmates. After the January 1966 firebombing, Magee and Charles Phillip drove out at night to stand watch at the Dahmer property. He wrote a letter to Governor William Waller protesting the pardon of one of Dahmer's killers, and was present for the eventual Sam Bowers conviction.

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Education, Legacy, and Community Policing
clips.summary: Magee identifies education three times as the answer to the country's ills, and credits N.R. Burger, principal of the city school, with shielding city teachers from the NAACP-retaliation firings that hit county teachers. He describes his current work with the Hattiesburg Association for Civic Improvement on the Pinebelt complex housing project that cost $121 million, the Forrest County community action agency for Head Start, and ongoing community policing dialogue between NAACP, clergy, judges, and the University of Southern Mississippi to prevent a Ferguson, Missouri-style incident.
related_events: Forrest County community action agency formation

clips.subject: Clarence Magee
clips.title: Great-Uncle's Lynching and Closing
clips.summary: Magee shares that his great-uncle was lynched on Richburg Road in Forrest County in 1929, near where the Pinebelt complex stands today off Hardy Street and Broad Street. No one was ever brought to trial. Magee learned of it after moving to the area, has spoken with one researcher about it, and reflects that 'just like it was them, it could have been me.'

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Growing Up in Denmark
clips.summary: Sellers describes Denmark, South Carolina, the cultural centrality of Voorhees College in his childhood, and the rigid segregation of the rural community. He explains how the Voorhees campus served as the area's high school under state funding, blending the Booker T. Washington industrial-education model with Du Bois's influence.
related_events: Founding of Voorhees College by a Tuskegee graduate

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Emmett Till and the African Connection
clips.summary: Sellers, eleven years old when Emmett Till was murdered, describes classroom discussions of the Jet magazine cover and the determination among Voorhees-area students to find remedies rather than revenge. He links this to the simultaneous independence movements of Kwame Nkrumah, Patrice Lumumba, and Jomo Kenyatta that African students at Voorhees brought into campus conversation.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Family and Church
clips.summary: Sellers describes his father, a Denmark entrepreneur who built a motel and served in France during World War II, and his mother, a South Carolina State and Hampton graduate who taught at Voorhees for twenty years and ran the Denmark Recreation Center. He traces his family's religious journey across AME, Baptist, and Episcopal affiliations and the Episcopal Church's later support of his SNCC work.
related_events: World War II service in France

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: First Sit-Ins and NAACP Youth
clips.summary: Sellers, then a high school student working in the Voorhees student center, describes how the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins drew nightly news audiences and prompted Voorhees students to plan their own demonstration. He recounts the college president withdrawing the bus under board pressure, the seven-student march downtown, and his organizing a Denmark NAACP youth chapter against his father's objections, which prompted him to apply unsuccessfully to Staunton Military Academy.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Howard and NAG
clips.summary: Sellers arrives at Howard in fall 1962 expecting a hotbed and instead finds a bourgeois campus. He joins the Nonviolent Action Group through Mike Thelwell, Stanley Wise, and Charlie Cobb, and describes NAG's picketing of the Justice Department, FBI, and White House over voter rights enforcement, plus local Washington D.C. home-rule and bus-desegregation campaigns. NAG members targeted leadership posts on the Hilltop newspaper, the Lyceum program, and student government.
related_events: NAG picketing of Justice Department and FBI

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: March on Washington
clips.summary: Sellers recounts NAG securing Bayard Rustin and then Malcolm X as Howard Lyceum speakers, and Rustin recruiting NAG members to handle March on Washington logistics. He describes the controversy over John Lewis's speech draft, Cardinal Patrick O'Boyle's objection to the Sherman-burning-the-South metaphor, A. Philip Randolph's personal appeal to SNCC, and the post-march euphoria shattered by the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Cambridge, Maryland
clips.summary: Sellers describes the Cambridge, Maryland movement under Gloria Richardson, the imposition of martial law, and the night George C. Wallace was invited to speak. He, Stokely Carmichael, Stanley Wise, and Gloria were arrested and held overnight in the National Guard armory. He recounts the National Guard's tear-gas-and-nausea-agent mixture and the bayonet wounding of SNCC photographer Cliff Vaughs as the demonstration tried to march to Wallace's venue.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Freedom Summer and the Search
clips.summary: Sellers recounts Bob Moses assigning NAG to recruit Freedom Summer volunteers from Maryland and D.C. campuses, the Oxford, Ohio orientation, and Moses's announcement that Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney were missing and presumed dead. He describes Jean Wheeler's song breaking the silence, then a small group including Stokely Carmichael, Charlie Cobb, Ralph Featherstone, Dona Richards, and Gwen Robinson entering Mississippi from four directions to search for bodies at night from a Neshoba County barn loft over two nights before pulling back.
related_events: Disappearance of Goodman Schwerner and Chaney
Mt. Zion church burning Philadelphia Mississippi

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Holly Springs Project
clips.summary: Sellers describes the Holly Springs project covering five or six northern Mississippi counties, with three Boston master teachers, a nurse, a freedom school, a community center, and the largest MFDP application count of any project. Ivanhoe Donaldson directed it through summer; Sellers took over in fall 1964 and mentored youth including Roy DeBerry and SNCC volunteers including Larry Rubin and Peter Cummings of the Harvard Crimson. Records were later archived at Rust College.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Atlantic City and the MFDP Compromise
clips.summary: Sellers describes hauling the burned Mt. Zion church bell and the burned Goodman-Schwerner-Chaney station wagon to Atlantic City as visual evidence, the Johnson administration delegating containment to Humphrey and Walter Mondale, and the two-seats-at-large compromise. He recounts Johnson cutting away national TV coverage during Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony with an impromptu press conference, the delegation's rejection of the compromise, and SNCC's loss of faith in the morality of Democratic Party coalition politics.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Selma and Bloody Sunday
clips.summary: Sellers describes the SNCC staff debate over whether to participate in the SCLC-led Selma campaign, John Lewis's insistence on marching personally, and the rapid SNCC mobilization from a Mississippi MFDP meeting after Bloody Sunday left Lewis with a concussion. He took over logistics with Hosea Williams and brought in Willie "Mukasa" Ricks; SNCC was frustrated that King's turnaround march was not communicated to them. Jim Forman shifted demonstrations to Montgomery.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Lowndes County and the Black Panther
clips.summary: Sellers describes Stokely Carmichael's organizing in Lowndes County, Alabama after Selma, and Jack Minnis's research department discovery of a Civil War era Alabama statute permitting independent county parties. SNCC organized the Lowndes County Freedom Organization, which the press dubbed the Black Panther Party. Young men brought down from Philadelphia, Chicago, New York, and California as poll watchers carried the symbol home, seeding Bobby Seale and Huey Newton's Oakland party.
related_events: Black Panther Party of Oakland formation

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Black Power and Dr. King
clips.summary: Sellers reflects on SNCC's transformation after Samuel Younge Jr's January 1966 killing and SNCC's anti-war statement, the membership shift from majority-black to majority-white, COINTELPRO disruption, and his own three-month imprisonment for draft refusal. He recounts a private four-to-five-hour Atlanta basement meeting after Cicero, Illinois where he, Stokely Carmichael, and Willie Ricks explained Black Power to Dr. King, reaching a mutual-non-condemnation agreement that David Garrow could not corroborate with other King lieutenants.

clips.subject: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
clips.title: Reflection and Career
clips.summary: Sellers, asked to summarize his last forty years, describes movement work as a university-equivalent education through contact with Archibald Cox, Dr. King, Whitney Young, Malcolm X, Ella Baker, Fannie Lou Hamer, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. He earned a master's at Harvard and a doctorate at UNC, returning to Voorhees as president in 2008. The interviewer references Sellers's autobiography The River of No Return as the principal complementary source.
related_events: Publication of The River of No Return

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Family Origins in Terrell County
clips.summary: Browner introduces his birth in Sasser, Georgia, his parents Clifford Sr. (from Webster County) and Johnny Lou (from Terrell County), and the family courtship story: his mother praying at the woodpile after her own mother's death, then meeting his father at school the next day. He describes his father's transition from sharecropping to work at a fertilizer plant in Albany.
related_events: family migration from farm to Albany wage labor

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Segregated Childhood and Community
clips.summary: Browner describes his father's shielding of the family from direct racial confrontation in Albany, the all-Black community of dirt streets and three-room houses in which he grew up, and the lessons of deference in front of whites picked up at home and through observation. He recounts that his parents raised four children plus an extended-family cousin in their household.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Dunbar and Carver Schools
clips.summary: Browner attended Dunbar Elementary School, a faded white wooden building with a pot-bellied stove, no indoor plumbing, and outdoor coal-fetching duties, until 1955 when a new building was constructed. He then transferred to George Washington Carver High School, seven miles away by bus from Sasser. His mother worked first in the white elementary school cafeteria in Sasser, then in the Dunbar lunchroom where she eventually became head cook.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Movement Comes to Sasser
clips.summary: Browner first learned about the Albany Movement through his father and older sister, who were active. Mass meetings had been held at Mt. Zion Baptist Church near Dawson but moved to Mt. Olive Baptist Church in Sasser to reach the population center. Browner attended the meetings, where SNCC organizers reported on voter registration drives, beatings, arrests, and confrontations with the Sheriff's Department in Terrell, Lee, and Dougherty counties.
related_events: SNCC voter registration in southwest Georgia

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: The Textbook Letter
clips.summary: Browner and classmate Clovis Jones wrote a letter to the Terrell County school superintendent requesting new books because Black students were receiving hand-me-downs with missing pages and written-in notes. The principal, E.E.S.S., called Browner and Jones into his office, said they should be expelled, and they were subsequently surveilled at recess. Browner was forced to resign as senior class president for his role in the letter.
related_events: Carver High School textbook letter incident

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Mt. Olive Church Burned
clips.summary: Browner recounts Sheriff Z.T. Matthews and other armed white men, dressed in regular clothes but carrying pistols, walking into a mass meeting at Mt. Olive Baptist Church to intimidate attendees. SNCC organizer Charles Sherrod was present that night. The church was burned to the ground later, and Browner went with his father to see the smoldering remains. He recalls rumors that whites discussed burning out his family's house but spared it because elderly non-involved neighbors lived nearby.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: March on Washington 1963
clips.summary: Browner traveled by train from Albany to Washington, D.C. for the August 1963 March on Washington, his first time away from his mother's home overnight. He had earned the spending money rebuilding burned churches at $1.40 an hour. He recalls arriving in DC underdressed for the colder weather, hearing Dr. King's speech, seeing Peter, Paul and Mary perform, and noticing John Lewis. He took the train home the same night.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Army Service and Germany
clips.summary: Unable to afford college, Browner enlisted in July 1964, completed basic training at Fort Gordon, Georgia, advanced communications training at Fort Knox, Kentucky, and was stationed at Hubbard Barracks in Kitzingen, West Germany for 26 months. He describes the army as his first integrated environment, traveling briefly to Frankfurt and Hamburg, being introduced to jazz by Black servicemen from northern cities, and being mentored by an older white squad leader who compared Browner favorably to his own 17-year-old brother.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Return Home and Albany State
clips.summary: Browner returned home and was shocked that little had changed economically despite legal integration. He enrolled at Albany State College because Tennessee State would not start him until January. He majored in sociology while working two part-time jobs, lived first at home then in an apartment. He briefly visited his father at the Albany fertilizer plant where dust coated the workers.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Reidsville and Flint
clips.summary: Browner took a counselor job in an ex-offender program at Reidsville State Prison, recruited by the Georgia labor department, but the warden, uncomfortable with Browner being the first Black non-maintenance employee there, promised to get rid of him. Browner soon left for Flint, Michigan to join boyhood friend Doug Settle.
