He worked at the Chevrolet Sheffield Truck Assembly Plant, then in computer operations, then sold insurance for Equitable Life Insurance Company.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: Doraville and Forced Retirement
clips.summary: Browner transferred back to General Motors at the Doraville plant north of Atlanta in DeKalb County, Georgia, working there for 20 years until a head-on highway collision forced his retirement. He returned to Terrell County and observed that white farmers had shifted from sharecropping to business farming with tractors and combines after running off the Black tenants who had asked to register to vote.

clips.subject: Clifford D. Browner Jr.
clips.title: North and South Reflections
clips.summary: Browner closes with reflection on the difference between Southern and Northern racism, the South's explicit position-knowing versus the North's perceived freedom that masks structural separation. He observes that gains since the movement appear surface-level: tax base flight to suburbs has hollowed out cities with Black mayors, replacing racial separation with economic separation while leaving Black political leaders blamed for conditions they have no resources to change.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Trinidad and Bronx Childhood
clips.summary: Cox describes being born in Harlem in 1941, moving to Trinidad with his mother and sister Lorraine in 1945, and returning to New York in 1952 after his grandmother's death. He recounts attending St. Helena's and St. Aloysius Catholic schools in the Bronx, the family expectation of college from his Trinidadian cousins with PhDs, and hiding his books walking home through housing-project peer groups affected by drugs.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Howard Admission and Early Awareness
clips.summary: Cox follows his cousin Erskine to Howard University, taking a Greyhound bus to Washington to sit the entrance exam at a time when tuition was $7.50 per semester credit. He describes his pre-Howard political awareness shaped by Emmett Till and Montgomery and the older jazz-scene mentors who introduced him to Black culture in the Bronx.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: NAG and Project Awareness
clips.summary: Cox describes encountering Washington's segregation and forming the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) at Howard with Stokely Carmichael and others. He details NAG's Glen Echo Park, Eastern Shore, RFK Stadium, and Route 40 actions, work as Julius W. Hobson's CORE shock troops, and Project Awareness programming including the Bayard Rustin-Malcolm X debate, the Norman Thomas thermonuclear-warfare debate, and the James Baldwin-Ossie Davis-John Oliver Killens symposium moderated by Sterling A. Brown with Sidney Poitier flying in for the after-party. Tom Kahn and Mike Thelwell are named as student-council and newspaper leaders.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: SNCC and Howard vs Nashville
clips.summary: Cox describes NAG joining SNCC as one of several campus coordinating groups, his role on SNCC's coordinating council, the long Atlanta drives down Highway 29, and the SNCC discussion culture that moved from 'why' to 'why not.' He distinguishes Howard NAG's view of nonviolence as tactic from the Nashville group (Diane Nash, James Lawson, John Lewis) view of nonviolence as a way of life, arguing that movement participants engaged in nonviolence because the other side had overwhelming force, not because power could be reasoned with.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Rustin-Malcolm Debate and Federal Limits
clips.summary: Cox describes the 1961 Cramton Auditorium debate between Bayard Rustin and Malcolm X, moderated by Howard government professor Emmett Dorsey after the government department chair refused. He recounts Rustin yielding fifteen minutes to Malcolm and the discipline of Malcolm's followers. Cox then describes the McComb FBI agent who told SNCC organizers the bureau was there to protect evidence, not people, and admitted having two guns and being scared.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Robert Kennedy Office Sit-In
clips.summary: Cox describes the 1962 sit-in at Robert Kennedy's office with Stokely Carmichael and Tom Kahn. He recounts Kahn calling Drum and Spear Bookstore + Drum and Spear Press contacts and the Soviet press from Kennedy's phone, the dragging-out of limp protesters down the hall, and Stokely going back to retrieve forgotten items and sitting down again. Cox frames the Kennedy administration's posture toward Martin Luther King and toward SNCC as opportunistic.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: March on Washington and John Lewis Speech
clips.summary: Cox, the SNCC representative on the March on Washington steering committee, describes circulating John Lewis's original draft speech, Archbishop O'Boyle's threat to withdraw over criticism of the Kennedy administration and the 'march through the South like Sherman' line, and the late-night meeting behind the Lincoln Memorial with Jim Forman, John Lewis, and others where A. Philip Randolph appealed to the coalition he had worked since 1941 to build. Cox and Forman were criticized within SNCC for caving.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Atlantic City and the MFDP Betrayal
clips.summary: Cox describes the 1964 MFDP delegation arriving at Atlantic City having played by the Democratic Party rules and finding the rules changed. He recounts Lyndon Johnson calling a press conference to cut off Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, his and Bob Moses's work with Rep. Edith Green to build credentials-committee support, Johnson's threat to deny Hubert Humphrey the vice presidency, and an unnamed congressman taking the supporters list from Bob Moses and using it to break the minority. The two-seat compromise was rejected; the liberal establishment including the National Council of Churches, AFL-CIO, Martin Luther King, NAACP, and Joseph L. Rauh Jr. urged acceptance.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Lowndes County and Independent Power
clips.summary: Cox describes Lowndes County, Alabama as 80% Black with four registered voters when SNCC arrived after Viola Liuzzo's murder. He recounts Jack Minnis's research uncovering the Alabama law permitting county-level political parties with the rooster as the Democratic Party symbol, the comic books designed to communicate the roles and responsibilities of county offices to people who did not read, and the 'Mr. Black Man' propaganda piece he developed with Jennifer Lawson using a Mississippi woman's phrase about Black people using their mouths to eat and say yes. John Hulett ran for sheriff; Mr. Jackson housed the SNCC organizers and stood guard at night with his gun.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Violence and Living in the County
clips.summary: Cox describes the SNCC organizers' rationalizations for managing fear in Lowndes County, the Collie Leroy Wilkins Jr. trial for Viola Liuzzo's murder where Cox was the only person of color in the courtroom, the chase down dirt roads at 70-80 miles per hour with John Jackson (Mr. Jackson's son), and the house with no running water and a hole in the roof. SNCC's trademark, Cox argues, was that organizers lived where they worked rather than dropping in and out.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Black Power and Whites in SNCC
clips.summary: Cox describes the 1966 transition from John Lewis to Stokely Carmichael, the Meredith March Against Fear and the Black Power slogan, and the Peg Leg Bates Country Club staff meeting decision about white organizers. He recounts Jim Forman's framing that the problem was not in the Black community but in the white community, and that white organizers should organize in white communities. Theodore Bikel's public reaction cut SNCC funding tremendously.
related_events: Peg Leg Bates Country Club staff meeting

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Stockholm and the Russell Tribunal
clips.summary: Cox describes traveling to London and Stockholm in 1967 as SNCC's representative on Bertrand Russell's International War Crimes Tribunal. He sits at the dinner with Russell, Jean-Paul Sartre, Simone de Beauvoir, and Isaac Deutscher, observes Sartre's reverence in the proceedings, and questions Colonel Ha Van Lau about cluster bombs and guerrilla warfare, arguing that an industrialized power could only fight a war of national liberation by committing genocide. Cox details the resulting FBI, CIA, and IRS surveillance and his 1970 two-night detention at Heathrow en route to Africa.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Sixth Pan-African Congress
clips.summary: Cox describes meeting C.L.R. James and Paulo (Carmody) and the conception of the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania to connect African-descended people across the continent and diaspora around the technical-skills-for-resources exchange and support for liberation movements. As Secretary-General at age 32, Cox traveled with C.L.R. James and Fletcher Robinson to meet Ahmed Sekou Toure of Guinea, Forbes Burnham of Guyana, and Julius Nyerere of Tanzania. State-versus-insurgent tensions limited Caribbean and Eritrean participation; Charles J. Ogletree Jr. attended. The conference lacked post-event infrastructure.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Drum and Spear and 1968
clips.summary: Cox describes settling in Washington after the Russell Tribunal and founding Drum and Spear Bookstore + Drum and Spear Press at 14th and Fairmont Street with Charlie Cobb, Judy Richardson, and Anne and Marvin Holloway, alongside the Center for Black Education. He recounts hearing of Martin Luther King's assassination in a Volkswagen en route to Pennsylvania, the 14th Street rebellion, the internal solidarity among Black participants versus external hostility, and Mayor Walter E. Washington declaring martial law with tanks on Columbia Road. Cox closes by reflecting on Bobby Kennedy's voice after King's death and the Chicago Convention.

clips.subject: Courtland Cox
clips.title: Economic Continuum and Lessons
clips.summary: Cox frames the civil-rights era from 1955 to 2008 as the political era, with three accomplishments: ending public-accommodations segregation, securing voting rights, and building electoral political organization. He argues that what was radical was not the tactics but who was included, and that the unresolved task is the economic dimension. The information economy requires literacy in English, math, and computers; the inclusion now must extend to poor whites and Native Americans, with intellectual property as the substrate for wealth and institution-building.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Bogalusa Setting and Cross-Burning
clips.summary: Joe Mosnier opens the interview with Fletcher Anderson and Cynthia Baker Anderson in Bogalusa, Louisiana. Fletcher describes the 1963 Ku Klux Klan cross-burning that drew the couple into civil rights activity, the segregated conditions at Crown Zellerbach when he began work there in 1962, and the lawsuits filed alongside Robert "Bob" Hicks that integrated facilities, opened jobs to Black employees and Black women, and produced the first Black supervisors at the plant.
related_events: 1963 Bogalusa cross-burning at Robert Hicks's home

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: City Hall and Federal Court
clips.summary: Fletcher recounts pressing the City of Bogalusa to hire Black workers in city government and on garbage trucks, the marches on the courthouse, federal court testimony that produced an injunction against the Bogalusa police department, and his role on the negotiating committee with an AFL-CIO mediator that produced the city's first two Black police officers from his 1959 high school class at Crown Zellerbach.
related_events: first Black Bogalusa police officers hired

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Marches to Franklinton and Baton Rouge
clips.summary: Fletcher describes three marches to Franklinton, Louisiana that drew no response, followed by the longer march to Baton Rouge to demand a meeting with Governor John McKeithen. The governor declined a meeting at the capitol but later flew to Bogalusa for an airport meeting with movement leaders, including Fletcher. He recounts sustained picketing, demonstrations, and economic boycotts that opened retail employment.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Family Backgrounds
clips.summary: Both speakers describe family histories. Fletcher's grandparents George and Maggie Anderson came from Mississippi to Bogalusa for the paper mill, became property owners, and his grandfather was among the first Black registered voters in Washington Parish. Cynthia describes her father Lewis Baker and mother Esther V. Watson, growing up in the middle quarters of Bogalusa, learning to shoot at age four, and her childhood response to the 1954 Brown v. Board desegregation ruling.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Work at Crown Zellerbach
clips.summary: Fletcher traces his work history at the Crown Zellerbach paper mill from boxcar loader to truck driver, takeoff person, slitter operator, first helper, and finally overhead crane operator, the position on which he was injured for lack of training and from which he retired. He describes the difficulty of being trained by white workers he would replace and the experience of picketing his own employer before reporting to work the same shift.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Meredith March and Community Defense
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the Bogalusa Deacons going to Mississippi to protect the Meredith March Against Fear after James Meredith was shot, and how the community organized walkie-talkie networks, block captains, and street blockades to defend Black neighborhoods from drive-by shootings. Cynthia describes the night Fletcher was beaten and the car attacked, retrieving the car from a fire station holding bay by climbing the rope to swing open the bay door.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Chartering the Bogalusa Deacons
clips.summary: Fletcher and Cynthia recall the February 1965 meeting where Ernest "Chilly Willy" Thomas and Rev. Frederick D. Kirkpatrick came from Jonesboro, Louisiana to explain how they had organized the Deacons for Defense and Justice. From that meeting the Bogalusa chapter chartered, with Charles R. Sims elected president, Robert "Bob" Hicks vice president, and Fletcher Anderson appointed lieutenant. Cynthia recalls suspecting one attendee was an informant.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Deacons Operations and Funding
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the weapons the Deacons acquired with their own funds and with donations raised on trips to California with Lewis Lomax and others: carbines, thirty-thirty rifles, and handguns. Veterans including Willie Jane Preston who would not formally join the Deacons quietly taught the members organizational and tactical skills. Fletcher names key members: Sam Barnes, Reese Perkins, Albert Davis, and others.
related_events: California fundraising trips for Bogalusa Deacons

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Summer 1965 and the Highway Arrest
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the heightened tension of summer 1965, repeated traffic stops and tickets that drove up insurance rates, and the march to Baton Rouge that produced the first Black state troopers. He recounts a shooting incident during a July 1965 march in which a Klansman attacking the marchers had to be shot, attorneys Lolis Elie and Robert Collins defending the attempted-murder charges that resulted, and Cynthia's separate arrest on Highway 21 for refusing to say "yes sir" to a state trooper, with a $4 bond.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Protecting CORE and Visitors
clips.summary: Fletcher describes the Deacons' protective detail for CORE leader James Farmer and other visiting civil rights workers, with Canadian Griffin and Fletcher himself assigned to escort them. He recounts Griffin's beating on Columbia Street in front of the Winn-Dixie, the shooting of military veteran Captain Seymour in a phone booth, and the Deacons' overnight protection of marchers in a park in Hammond, Louisiana near Southeastern Louisiana University on the way to Baton Rouge.
related_events: Bogalusa-to-Baton Rouge march overnight in Hammond

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: School Integration and Aftermath
clips.summary: Cynthia and Fletcher describe school integration in Bogalusa: an initial 60-40 Black majority enrollment without Black control of principals or administration, the flight of white students to private schools, the retirement or methodological retrenchment of white teachers unwilling to teach Black students, and the disproportionate referral of Black students to special education. Fletcher reflects on Klan surveillance from supervisors at his workplace.

clips.subject: Cynthia and Fletcher Anderson
clips.title: Reflections on Legacy
clips.summary: Cynthia and Fletcher close with reflections on the Bogalusa movement's lasting effects: pride in working alongside A.Z. Young, Robert "Bob" Hicks, Charles R. Sims, Mrs. Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and the women who escorted the first Black children into integrated schools; concern about young people's disengagement from civil rights work and drugs in the community; and the assertion that the Bogalusa movement could not have stood without the Deacons' armed self-defense capacity. Fletcher notes Bogalusa was, at the 2011 recording, finally merging its segregated YMCAs.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Activist Versus Radical
clips.summary: Bailey opens the interview by distinguishing activists from radicals: the activist accepts institutional parameters; the radical pushes against the outer walls knowing the agenda will not be fully achieved. He discusses the subtitle of his autobiography ("The Education of a Black Radical") and his reluctance to accept labels because they make a person easier to discount and pigeonhole. He also resisted joining CORE during his Baton Rouge years for the same reason.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Defining the Movement
clips.summary: Bailey describes how he conceives the civil rights movement at the National Civil Rights Museum, beginning with slavery and the abolitionist movement (a framing he credits to designer Benjamin Lawless), and grounded in his own movement experience. He names the Lorraine Motel as the site, references encounters with Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville, the Deacons for Defense in Bogalusa, SNCC freedom singers, Pete Seeger, Carl Braden, and Will D. Campbell. He locates the movement's ignition in the lynching of Emmett Till, the Little Rock Nine, and Daisy Bates with the NAACP rather than in the Brown v. Board decision alone.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Memphis Family and Crump Politics
clips.summary: Bailey describes his parents, his mother a housekeeper and later licensed practical nurse at St. Joseph's Hospital who became one of the first Black female barbers in Memphis, his father a Pullman porter on Illinois Central from New Orleans to Chicago. He locates his political education in the Shelby County Democratic Club, where as a teenager he worked sound trucks for white moderates and Black candidates including Russell Sugarmon (1959 commissioner race) and Ross Bass. Memphis Black voting was sustained by the residue of the E.H. "Boss" Crump machine even after its mid-1950s decline.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Southern University and Cox v. Louisiana
clips.summary: Bailey arrives at Southern University in Baton Rouge in 1959, elected Freshman Class President. Following the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Southern students stage lunch counter sit-ins; the resulting case Garner v. Louisiana (1961) establishes that peaceful demonstrators cannot be convicted of disturbing the peace. In December 1961 Bailey marches with Reverend B. Elton Cox around the old state capital; Cox is arrested and the Supreme Court ultimately reverses his conviction in Cox v. Louisiana. Bailey is expelled from Southern in his junior year under Rule 18 ("lack of university adjustment") for speaking at a rally despite warning.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Nonviolence, SNCC, and CORE
clips.summary: Bailey explains that nonviolence was strategic necessity rather than belief, facing tear gas and dogs, violent response was not viable. After expulsion, Bailey stays at a Black hotel in downtown Baton Rouge, paid for by CORE's national office. SNCC field secretaries Chuck McDew and Bob Zellner arrive to visit jailed organizer Dion Diamond and are themselves arrested for criminal anarchy and sedition. Bailey begins traveling to SNCC staff meetings in Atlanta, meeting John Lewis, Julian Bond, and Jim Forman, while CORE field secretary Dave Dennis grows suspicious of his contact with SNCC.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Clark University and Worcester Student Movement
clips.summary: Through Walter Williams (a Jackson State student also in the 1961 Wisconsin Seminar), Bailey is offered a scholarship from Clark University in Worcester, Massachusetts, raised by Clark students through car washes and bake sales. He rooms with Fred Jealous (later father of NAACP president Ben Jealous). Bailey founds a Worcester chapter of the Northern Student Movement, picketing Wyman-Gordon, the largest local industrial employer, whose chairman Robert W. Stoddard was national secretary of the John Birch Society. Massachusetts Commission Against Discrimination and Defense Department complaints force employment changes. Abbie Hoffman, then in Worcester after Brandeis, publishes the chapter's newsletter The Drum.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Malcolm X and the Deacons for Defense
clips.summary: Bailey calls Malcolm X at his New York home and persuades him to speak at Clark; when board members demand a counterweight, Malcolm declines to debate Bayard Rustin (whom he calls "a lackey for King"), insisting on debating King directly. Bailey escorts Malcolm through a WTAG radio interview with Julie Chase Fuller. Bailey describes traveling with two priests to Bogalusa in summer 1965, escorted by armed Deacons for Defense and Justice carrying shotguns alongside the march. Bailey distinguishes self-defense (which he accepts) from proactive violence (which he opposes but does not categorically condemn).

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Legal Defense Fund and LSCRRC
clips.summary: Summer 1965 between his first and second years of law school, Bailey works for the NAACP Legal Defense Fund out of Ernest "Dutch" Morial's New Orleans office, filing the first Title VII complaints against Crown Zellerbach in Bogalusa. He transfers to Yale Law School after Dean Jack B. Tate finds him on the waitlist. After Yale, Bailey becomes National Director of the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council (LSCRRC), housed at ACLU's national office, placing 200+ law students with Southern civil rights attorneys at $30/week stipends. Funding flowed from Taconic Foundation, Rockefeller Brothers Fund, Norman Foundation, New York Foundation, Cummins Engine Foundation, and Stern Family Fund.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: King Assassination and Move to California
clips.summary: On April 4, 1968, Bailey is working late in his LSCRRC New York office when his secretary reports the radio bulletin that King has been shot, then dead. The Memphis sanitation strike was the reason ten LSCRRC law students Bailey had placed in Memphis were on the ground, including Alex Hurder, briefly held as a suspect for being a white person near the Lorraine Motel. Bailey flies to Memphis and on to Atlanta for the funeral, then accepts a job at San Francisco Legal Services through former LSCRRC director Steve Antler. Two months in Europe with Europe on $5 a Day precede his October 1968 arrival in California, where he is later fired for challenging the director.
related_events: Martin Luther King Jr. assassination April 4 1968

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Field Foundation and Movement Funding
clips.summary: Bailey details the foundation network sustaining the movement: Leslie W. Dunbar at Field Foundation supports Marian Wright Edelman's Children's Defense Fund and chooses John Lewis over Bailey for the Voter Education Project. Field hires Bailey as a consultant and funds his 1972 black people's community inquiry into Southern University student shootings with $5,000, joined by John Lewis, Owusu Sadaukai, and W. Haywood Burns. A private benefactor establishes the Bread Limited Trust at Bank of America with Bailey controlling $1 million in corpus (up to $100,000 discretionary), with a directive to fund non-tax-exempt projects unable to receive foundation money.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Berkeley April Coalition and Recall
clips.summary: After Ron Dellums's congressional election opens a Berkeley council seat, Bailey and Ira Simmons declare candidacy through the Berkeley Black Caucus, forcing the April Coalition to accept them alongside Loni Hancock and Rick Brown. Bailey wins on a platform centering Black community interests. Council activity included filibustering meetings, supporting Black Panther Free Breakfast and daycare programs (while refusing Huey Newton's $25,000 funding request), and securing accelerated wage increases for garbage workers during a citywide strike. Mike Wallace interviews Bailey on 60 Minutes pressing him to disclose campaign funding sources; Bailey refuses. After two and a half years, conservatives organize a recall election Bailey loses.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Voting Rights, Mass Incarceration, Obama
clips.summary: Bailey reflects on Lyndon B. Johnson's leadership securing the Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act, recalling Johnson saying "we shall overcome" in his Southern accent after Selma, Viola Liuzzo, and Reverend James Reeb. Bailey doubts the vote alone produces fundamental change, Memphis Blacks voted under Crump without gaining power. He critiques the Bush v. Gore decision as judicial coup, Citizens United v. FEC as opening corporate ownership of electoral machinery, and Justice Antonin Scalia's "get over it" posture. On mass incarceration, Bailey ties Black imprisonment rates to three centuries of denial that destroyed family structure and self-worth, with gangs filling a vacuum of recognition.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Buying the Lorraine Motel
clips.summary: Returning to Memphis in 1974, Bailey practices law and writes a weekly Press-Scimitar column for seven years. He meets Walter L. Bailey Sr (no relation), owner of the deteriorating Lorraine Motel, around 1979. After Mr. Bailey's wife dies the night of the assassination, the motel survives on prostitution income and a small Dr. King shrine. Foreclosure proceeds December 13, 1982; Bailey arrives at the courthouse with $65,000, secures $25,000 from union leader Jim Smith minutes before bidding, then secures Tri-State Bank guarantees from Jesse H. Turner Sr through Paul Shapiro and Smith for another $50,000. Bidding against mortgage-holder Raymond Sauer, Bailey wins at $144,000. Jack Belz raises the final $4,000 overnight.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Building the National Civil Rights Museum
clips.summary: Coretta Scott King's lawyer demands removal of King's name; Bailey complies and renames the foundation the Lorraine Civil Rights Museum Foundation. Designer Benjamin Lawless's $8.8 million proposal, featuring a burned Freedom Riders bus, tear gas effects, and barking dog sounds in the Birmingham exhibit, becomes the museum's enduring design. Bailey secures $4.4 million from the Tennessee legislature (passing 83-17 House, 33-0 Senate after Black Memphis legislators make it their top priority), plus $2.2 million each from Memphis and Shelby County. The McKissack & McKissack Nashville firm is named architect; state architect Mike Fitts manages construction. The museum opens in 1991.

clips.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Corporate Capture and Resignation
clips.summary: Six months after the 1991 opening, Benjamin L. Hooks accepts the board presidency in a 9-6 vote; J.R. "Pitt" Hyde III becomes executive committee chairman. Bailey resigns the same night, having brought Bob Moses, Julian Bond, and John Lewis to opening events with the intent that the museum become a center for renewed activism. He contrasts mid-century foundation funders (Taconic, Field, Rockefeller Brothers), who funded without extracting integrity, with current corporate funders (FedEx, International Paper, AutoZone Inc, First Tennessee Bank). He cites the NAACP's defense of fast food against Michael Bloomberg's portion limits as evidence of corporate-funded movement compromise. Institutions, Bailey concludes, become obsolete or obstacles once they can no longer be used for change.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Petersburg and First Sit-Ins
clips.summary: Diamond describes growing up in segregated Petersburg, Virginia, attending segregated schools, and conducting spontaneous solo sit-ins at five-and-dime lunch counters and the local library's colored entrance before sit-ins had a name. He locates the source of his resistance as instinct rather than influence, since Petersburg offered no integrated activity to model.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Monticello and First Integrated Contact
clips.summary: At sixteen, Diamond traveled to Monticello, New York to work in the Catskills resort-laundry economy. Unable to find laundry work, he talked his way into a short-order cook job at the Frontier Restaurant by fabricating references through his aunt Janet Morgan in New Brunswick. This was his first sustained contact with white and Jewish communities outside the South.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Howard NAG and Arlington Drug Fair
clips.summary: Diamond entered Howard University in September 1959 as a physics major. After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, students at Howard organized the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) and crossed the Potomac to conduct their first sit-in at the Drug Fair lunch counter in Cherrydale, Arlington, Virginia. The chain was desegregated within two weeks, during which George Lincoln Rockwell and the American Nazi Party surrounded the demonstrators.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Nonviolence as Tactic
clips.summary: Diamond articulates his view that nonviolence is a tactic, not a philosophy or religious path, grounded in the practical assessment that the police and army held the firepower. He contrasts his position with co-presenters who hold nonviolence as a way of life or religious commitment, and acknowledges that off-camera and outside sit-ins he would not turn the other cheek.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Glen Echo Park Picket Line
clips.summary: NAG turned next to Glen Echo Park, a Maryland amusement park that bused in white children from the city but barred Black patrons. Unable to sit-in inside the park, the group picketed its perimeter through the summer of 1960. Diamond describes Hyman Bookbinder, then a labor-movement figure, arranging a black limousine, white chauffeur, and white interpreter so Diamond could approach the entrance in African dress, attempting in mock-pidgin to gain admission.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Freedom Rides to Jackson
clips.summary: After the Anniston, Alabama bus burning nearly ended the Freedom Rides, Diamond and others traveled to Montgomery to continue the ride from Montgomery to Jackson, Mississippi. He describes the National Guard escort transfer at the Alabama-Mississippi state line, the immediate arrest upon stepping off the bus toward the white waiting room, and the breach-of-peace charge. He links the arrest-rather-than-beat outcome to a closed-door agreement between the Mississippi governor and Robert F. Kennedy during Cold War tensions with Khrushchev.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Hinds County Jail to Parchman
clips.summary: Arrested at the Jackson bus terminal, Diamond was held in the Hinds County jail, then moved to the county prison farm, then to the state penitentiary at Parchman Farm as later busloads of Riders filled the system. Segregated by race and sex inside the jails, he shared a Parchman cell with CORE director James Farmer. Diamond recalls being humbled by Farmer's monosyllabic reserve in conversation.
related_events: Mass Freedom Rider arrests Jackson Mississippi 1961

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: McComb and the Shotgun Blast
clips.summary: After Parchman, Diamond flew to Atlanta and accepted a SNCC assignment in Pike County, Mississippi, around McComb. He shared a house with Robert "Bob" Parris Moses, hosted by a woman the organizers called Mama Cotton. One night a shotgun blast came through the bedroom lattice window and passed over Diamond's body, he notes that his frame was thin enough that the shot cleared him. The incident concretized the danger of Mississippi voter-registration work for him.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Southern University and Criminal Anarchy
clips.summary: Sent to Holly Springs, Mississippi for voter registration, Diamond detoured to Baton Rouge after Southern University president Felton G. Clark closed the school and expelled student-protest leaders. Organizing a class boycott on a campus where he was a stranger, Diamond was arrested by state and campus police and charged with criminal anarchy, attempting to overthrow the state of Louisiana, along with trespassing and disorderly conduct misdemeanors. Placed initially in the East Baton Rouge Parish general jail population with convicted murderers, then moved to solitary confinement after FBI contact via an NAACP lawyer.
related_events: Southern University Baton Rouge student protests 1961-62

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Wisconsin, Harvard, and Career
clips.summary: At a 1963 National Student Association Congress in Columbus, Ohio, Diamond was recruited on the spot to the University of Wisconsin by the student-government president, transferring from Howard and changing his major from physics to history and sociology to investigate why the emancipation-to-protest interval took a century. In 1966 he came to D.C. to work at the United Planning Organization; in 1968 he was offered three options, Marshall Islands anti-poverty administration, the Robert F. Kennedy campaign, or Harvard. RFK was assassinated; Diamond went to Harvard for graduate study in education with a thesis on race and ethnic relations. His career followed at the Department of Labor (GS-14), economic-development training, HUD, and HEW Medicaid/Medicare fraud-detection consulting.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Reflections on Progress and Loss
clips.summary: Diamond reflects on the bittersweet quality of desegregation, the loss of close teacher-student-parent relationships in small segregated towns, the depletion of HBCU athletic programs as Big Ten schools recruited Black athletes, and the persistence of a glass ceiling beneath token CEO appointments. He critiques Black Lives Matter framing as segmenting an issue he sees as universal, names the Charleston church shooting as evidence the country has not cured the problem, and warns that what is past is prologue when discussing the Trump rallies he was watching at the time of the interview.

clips.subject: Dion Diamond
clips.title: Civil Rights Act and Obama
clips.summary: Asked for his most memorable moment, Diamond names the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the election of Barack Obama as the dual highlights of his lifetime, noting that neither he nor his SNCC peers in McComb in 1961 imagined Black elected officials beyond a single commissioner. He observes that one expected John F. Kennedy to act on civil rights but never Lyndon Johnson, a Southerner, to implement it.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Earliest Memories in Montgomery
clips.summary: Juandalynn recalls police officers seizing the family's new car when she was two or three, to satisfy debts incurred by the Montgomery bus boycott, and the auctioning of family property. Donzaleigh, born after the January 1957 bombing of the Abernathy home, describes the household's atmosphere of caution and the Freedom Riders staying at their parsonage. Both sisters dispute and reconcile their memory of Juandalynn cutting Donzaleigh's hair during a Freedom Rider gathering.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Fear and Nighttime Prayers
clips.summary: Ralph III, born in 1959, describes his earliest memory as fear-of the house being bombed again and of nighttime in a room alone. He asks his mother to leave the hallway light on, and the siblings communicate by knocking on shared walls. They describe threatening phone calls during dinner, traveling the segregated back roads of Alabama and Georgia, and Juanita Abernathy's image of two angels over their beds.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Children Discuss the Movement
clips.summary: The siblings reflect on whether they spoke among themselves about the danger. Donzaleigh recalls saying goodbye to her father each Monday morning expecting he might be killed. Juandalynn states she had to set the example for her younger siblings and that the family was anchored in religion and church community. They describe the King and Abernathy children as constant companions.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Move to Atlanta
clips.summary: The siblings describe the 1961 move from Montgomery to Atlanta. Juanita Abernathy insisted her husband would not relocate without a church of his own, refusing the suggestion that he live as a paid SCLC staff member. The King family found West Hunter Street Baptist Church for him to pastor. Donzaleigh and Juandalynn describe meeting Yolanda King and the King children as their constant companions before and after the move.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Abernathy-King Partnership
clips.summary: Ralph III analyzes the partnership of Martin Luther King Jr. and Ralph David Abernathy Sr.: King delivered the philosophical 'why' of nonviolence, Abernathy delivered the strategic 'what, when, and how.' He cites his father's prior pastorates, his sociology degree from Atlanta University, his Army service to sergeant rank, and his student demonstrations at Alabama State University as the experience King depended on. Donzaleigh describes the engraved Movado watch King gave Abernathy inscribed 'To my dearest friend and closest associate.'

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Integrating Spring Street Elementary
clips.summary: Ralph III, Dexter King, Juandalynn, Donzaleigh, and Yolanda King integrated Spring Street Elementary School in Atlanta. Ralph III and Dexter played football and the team was undefeated; Donzaleigh and Juandalynn were taunted by classmates and graded harder by teachers. Donzaleigh recalls her third-grade teacher Ms. Elliott teaching her about racism, the PTA objecting to her papers placed at the top of the learning tree, and Juanita Abernathy enrolling them in afterschool tutoring at the Learning Foundation.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Children on the Front Lines
clips.summary: The siblings describe Ralph Sr.'s insistence that his children participate in marches. Donzaleigh recalls the Selma-to-Montgomery march in 1965, walking with Jim Letherer, the celebrity fundraising mass meeting with Sammy Davis Jr. and Peter, Paul and Mary, and Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'How long? Not long!' speech at the Alabama State Capitol. She also recalls being tear-gassed in Cicero in 1966 and the James Meredith march in Mississippi.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Singing, Preaching, Acting
clips.summary: Each sibling describes how the Movement shaped their adult vocations. Ralph III initially resisted preaching, served as a Georgia state senator and member of the Georgia House of Representatives, and later earned a doctorate in theology from the North Carolina College of Theology. Donzaleigh chose acting after meeting movie stars at the Selma mass meeting. Juandalynn became an opera singer, encouraged from childhood by Mahalia Jackson, who often stayed at the Abernathy home.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Ralph III Arrested on the Mule Train
clips.summary: On June 15, 1968, traveling from Marks, Mississippi to Washington, D.C. with the Mule Train of the Poor People's Campaign, nine-year-old Ralph III was arrested in Douglasville, Georgia on the order of Governor Lester Maddox. Sixty-seven marchers, including roughly thirty children, were held overnight in the National Guard Armory. The next day they reunited with Ralph Sr. and Juanita at West Hunter Street Baptist Church. Donzaleigh recalls she was barred from joining the Mule Train because she was a girl.

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Family Life with the Kings
clips.summary: Donzaleigh recalls the Abernathys and Kings traveling together to the 1964 New York World's Fair and to California, sharing hotel suites with the boys' room, the girls' room, and the parents' rooms. She recalls watching the Sixteenth Street Baptist Church bombing news at West Hunter Street Baptist Church on September 15, 1963, freezing in the alley out of fear, and her brother taking her hand to lead her through.
related_events: Sixteenth Street Baptist Church bombing September 15 1963

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
clips.summary: Donzaleigh describes the family learning of King's shooting by phone and television in Atlanta on April 4, 1968. Juanita Abernathy went to the Atlanta airport to meet Coretta Scott King, then went home with her to tell the King children before flying with her to Memphis. Ralph III recounts the account his father gave him of the Lorraine Motel: the shared room with two beds, his father's last conversation with King about cologne, the shots that followed, King dying in Ralph Sr.'s arms at the hospital. Ralph III also describes his father's succession to the presidency of SCLC.
keywords: assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
related_events: assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. April 4 1968

clips.subject: Abernathy family
clips.title: Carrying the Mantle
clips.summary: Donzaleigh argues for her father's contribution after King's death: the Poor People's Campaign in Washington, the calming of urban unrest in 1968, and the words 'You may be able to kill the dreamer, but you cannot kill the dream.' She describes her father funding Andrew Young's congressional campaign through SCLC, paying for the King grave site at Daddy King's request, and using HUD Secretary Pierce's six-million-dollar grant for Abernathy Towers to benefit West Hunter Street Baptist Church rather than the Abernathy family. She closes with her father's last days and death.
related_events: death of Ralph David Abernathy Sr. 1990

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Goldsboro Childhood and Father
clips.summary: Cotton describes 917 North Greenleaf Street in Goldsboro, North Carolina, where she was born; her mother Maggie's death when Cotton was three; her father Claude Foreman raising four girls; his third-grade education and work at a Goldsboro tobacco factory; and her mixed feelings about his strap discipline, traced through her aunt Penny's account of her father's own upbringing. Cotton recounts her father's later move to Buffalo, New York, where he worked at Bethlehem Steel, and his quiet pride at the airport.
related_events: Cotton family relocation to Buffalo NY

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Miss Rosa Gray and Shaw University
clips.summary: Cotton recalls her Goldsboro high school English teacher Rosa Gray, who cast her in lead roles in the annual play, praised her poetry recitation ('there's your ready girl'), and pleaded with Cotton's father on the porch to let her attend the junior-senior prom. Gray then telephoned Shaw University and secured Cotton's admission plus two jobs, in the dining hall and cleaning the teachers' dormitory, to fund her schooling.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Daniel Household and Marriage to George Cotton
clips.summary: Cotton describes becoming housekeeper at the Shaw University president's residence under Dr. Robert Prentiss Daniel and his wife Blanche, the role she retained when the Daniels moved to Virginia State College in Petersburg. She recounts taking Shakespeare alongside classmate Harris Sims, meeting George Cotton through Sims, and George's formal request to Dr. Daniel for her hand at the foot of the president's staircase.
related_events: Daniel's move from Shaw to Virginia State College in 1950

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Petersburg Improvement Association
clips.summary: Cotton joins Gillfield Baptist Church under Reverend Wyatt Tee Walker, who heads the regional NAACP and the Petersburg Improvement Association. She becomes secretary of the NAACP chapter and trains youth to picket Petersburg's segregated public library and the Woolworth's downtown. Walker invites Reverend Martin Luther King Jr. from Montgomery to address a mass meeting at the church; Cotton recites a poem on the program and meets Dr. King over dinner at the parsonage hosted by the Young Women's Parish Club.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Arriving at SCLC in Atlanta
clips.summary: After completing her master's degree at Boston University in summer 1960, Cotton tells her husband she will go to Atlanta for three months to help Walker; she stays twenty-three years. She finds three people in the early SCLC office on Auburn Avenue, Lily Hunter, a woman named Brown, and Ms. Ella Baker. She finds housing first with Mrs. Mosley, then briefly with Dora McDonald (who became Dr. King's secretary), then her own apartment. She begins as administrative assistant to Walker.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Highlander, Septima Clark, Esau Jenkins
clips.summary: Dr. King sends Cotton to Highlander Folk School in the Cumberland Mountains, where she meets Septima Clark and learns the program's origin: Esau Jenkins of Johns Island, South Carolina, used his school-bus route to teach Sea Islands residents about voter registration after a white man shot a Black boy with impunity. Septima Clark, fired from the Charleston school system for refusing to disavow NAACP membership, joined Highlander and worked with Jenkins on cursive-writing literacy training tied to voter registration.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Citizenship Education Program at Dorchester
clips.summary: When Tennessee confiscates Highlander's property, the Marshall Field Foundation transfers the literacy-and-citizenship training funds to SCLC. Cotton becomes Director of the Citizenship Education Program. Andrew Young secures Dorchester Academy in McIntosh, Georgia, through his Congregational Church connections. Cotton, Young, and Septima Clark travel the South recruiting people from protest hotbeds to five-day residential workshops, where Cotton learns from Albert Minnis (the 'junk man') to lead with the offer of paid travel.
related_events: Transfer of citizenship-education funding from Highlander to SCLC

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Workshop Pedagogy and 'If Your Back's Not Bent'
clips.summary: Cotton describes her Socratic workshop method: rather than lecturing, she drew out attendees' grievances and asked questions that turned victims into agents, illustrated by the woman whose well water the town clerk refused to test, whom Cotton asked, 'Why aren't you the city clerk?' She estimates between five and eight thousand people came through the five-day workshops over the years, recruited through repeat attenders like Fannie Lou Hamer from Ruleville, Mississippi. Dr. King closed one workshop with the line that became Cotton's book title: 'Nobody can ride your back if your back's not bent.'

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Gender, SCLC Staff Dynamics, and King's Inner Circle
clips.summary: Cotton reflects on being the only woman at SCLC executive staff meetings, the coffee and note-taking expectations, and Jack O'Dell's intervention telling her to stay at the table. She describes Dr. King as a listener who let debates run before speaking, naming his 'team of wild horses', Hosea Williams, C.T. Vivian, James Bevel, and others. She recounts gathering with Dr. King, Bernard Lee, and Andrew Young in Jamaica when J. Edgar Hoover called King 'the most notorious liar in the country,' and the trip to Oslo for the Nobel Prize where Alberta Williams King led singing on the airplane.
related_events: 1964 Nobel Peace Prize trip to Oslo
Hoover's 'most notorious liar' attack on King

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Vietnam, Stokely Carmichael, and Nonviolence
clips.summary: Cotton describes Dr. King coming to her Atlanta apartment, 'giggly like a child,' during the staff debate at Ebenezer Baptist Church about whether he should speak publicly against the Vietnam War. She narrates the SCLC board member Marion Logan signing a letter, Cotton believes Logan did not write it, urging King not to give the speech, and how the criticism strengthened his resolve. She recalls sitting at King's kitchen table with Stokely Carmichael, who quoted Frantz Fanon and argued for abandoning nonviolence; King replied that if he were 'the last lone voice' for nonviolence he would still speak. Cotton later challenged Carmichael at Cornell on his 'Take Up the Gun' refrain by asking him to name who he would shoot.

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: After the Assassination: Abernathy and Head Start
clips.summary: Cotton describes the three-year mourning at SCLC after King's assassination, the difficulty of Ralph Abernathy stepping into the presidency over the 'team of wild horses,' and overhearing Abernathy say on the phone, 'I would do it but I don't have anybody to help me.' She praises Abernathy's pastoral care of his Ebenezer Baptist congregation but says he was 'in over his head' leading SCLC. Andrew Young suggested she take an interim Head Start director post in Jefferson County, Alabama, and she moved to Birmingham. She later worked for Maynard Jackson in Atlanta.
related_events: Cotton's Head Start directorship in Jefferson County

clips.subject: Dorothy Foreman Cotton (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Dorothy Cotton Institute and Movement Memory
clips.summary: Cotton recounts Anke Wessels of Cornell's Center for Transformative Action proposing the Dorothy Cotton Institute, which Cotton accepted as an answer to her question of what would become of her papers and honorary degrees, including one from Ithaca College. She describes the Institute's steering committee meeting in Ithaca and her insistence that it preserve the Movement's 'spiritual quality.' She closes by criticizing the leader-centric distortion of Movement history, that Jesse Jackson and three or four others get lifted up as the Movement, and reaffirms her purpose in writing her book to document the Citizenship Education Program and the thousands who 'did something other than march.'
related_events: Founding of the Dorothy Cotton Institute at Cornell
Cotton's award from the National Civil Rights Museum

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Jewish Immigrant Childhood in Wartime New York
clips.summary: Zellner describes her January 14, 1938 birth in New York City to Lithuanian-British and Ukrainian-Jewish immigrant parents. Her dentist father and his nurse-wife operated out of their apartment; both were left-wing secular Jews. World War II, the Holocaust (she discovered The Black Book at age eight), and her father's solidarity with Spanish Civil War veterans were the defining childhood frames.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: McCarthyism and Political Awakening
clips.summary: Zellner recounts a Communist Party family arrested under the Smith Act in their apartment building in 1951, her high-school class's blocked invitation of J. Robert Oppenheimer as a 1954 graduation speaker, and the formative trip to picket the White House in 1953 during the Julius and Ethel Rosenberg execution stay. She joined the NAACP in high school and recalls meeting Paul Robeson on Second Avenue.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: CORE Workshop and New Orleans Sit-In Arrest
clips.summary: Reading the Greensboro sit-in story in February 1960 galvanized Zellner. Through a CORE contact she joined a June 1960 Miami workshop with Rudy Lombard, Hugh Murray, the Stephens sisters, and Angeline Butler. Arrested at a Miami restaurant sit-in, she then traveled to New Orleans where she canvassed a Woolworth's on Canal Street, met Father Joseph Fichter at Loyola University New Orleans, and was thrown out of Rabbi Julian Feibelman's office. Bernard Lafayette directed her to SNCC.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Southern Regional Council and FBI Firing
clips.summary: Hired by Dr. James A. Moss in June 1961 as a research associate at the Southern Regional Council, Zellner conducted the newspaper-clipping research behind SRC's 18-month sit-in report (heavily edited by Leslie Dunbar). She lived on the white side of Atlanta with Jane Bond at 2222 Tilson Street. After an FBI visit produced a photograph of her, Dunbar fired her, citing redundancy, possibly linked to her participation in a Howard Zinn-organized Cuban Missile Crisis demonstration.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Joining SNCC Under James Forman
clips.summary: Volunteering at SNCC nights from October 1961, Zellner met James Forman, who had arrived only a month earlier. After SRC fired her she joined SNCC's staff full-time. She typed field affidavits dictated by returning field secretaries, affidavits Forman sent to Robert F. Kennedy invoking the 1957 Civil Rights Act. She describes Forman as the architect of SNCC's wheel-and-hub administrative structure: Friends of SNCC support groups, campus travelers, motor pool.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Danville, Virginia and Greenwood, Mississippi
clips.summary: Zellner recounts the 1963 Greenwood, Mississippi police stop alongside Bob Dylan's folk-festival visit, and the June 1963 Danville, Virginia Dan River Mills campaign where she was beaten unconscious by a policeman's club, captured in a Danny Lyon photograph of Forman taking her affidavit. She describes Forman saving the group during a second Danville demonstration by distracting the police chief while signaling staff to retreat from imminent fire-hose-and-club violence.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: James Forman's Character and Decline
clips.summary: Zellner mounts an extended defense of Forman's reputation against historiographic portrayals of him as volatile. She describes his cultural sophistication, French fluency, ruthless press discipline, and the buying of twenty SNCC cars at $2,000 each. She traces his three psychiatric breakdowns starting in 1968, including Constance "Dinky" Romilly's role rescuing him and Elizabeth "Betita" Sutherland Martínez's role helping him write his book, through his stabilized but altered later years.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Student Voice and Press Strategy with Julian Bond
clips.summary: From the winter of 1961-62, Zellner worked with Julian Bond on SNCC communications, laying out dummies of the Student Voice newspaper, taking it to a black printer associated with the Atlanta Inquirer. They taught field staff and summer volunteers to give the press truthful counts ("forty-two, not five hundred") and trained on telephone-interview discipline. She identifies the deliberate practice of racially identifying Black people in press releases (with white as default).

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Boston Northeast Regional Office and Volunteer Vetting
clips.summary: After marrying Bob Zellner in August 1963 and moving to Cambridge for his graduate study, Zellner ran SNCC's Northeast Regional Office in Boston, emphasizing she was a salaried staff member, not a Friends of SNCC volunteer. She and others rigorously interviewed Mississippi Summer Project applicants using COFO's questionnaire, screening for adventurers and divas. She admits to letting Abbie Hoffman through (then in suit and tie), a volunteer who later publicly attacked SNCC's treatment of white staff.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: 1963 Civil Liberties Workshop and the SNCC No-Cooperation Decision
clips.summary: Zellner identifies the spring 1963 SNCC civil-liberties workshop, convened jointly by Anne Braden and Ella Baker, as a historically underdocumented turning point. Surviving McCarthyism with Carl Braden's year-long contempt-of-Congress imprisonment as personal context, SNCC voted to refuse loyalty oaths and to take the First Amendment rather than the Fifth if subpoenaed. She argues an undercover agent must have transmitted the decision to the federal government, which then refrained from subpoenaing the sit-in heroes for international-image reasons.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: The Peg Leg Bates Vote and Leaving SNCC
clips.summary: Zellner recounts the May 1967 Peg Leg Bates central-committee meeting where she drafted a proposal, presented by Bob Zellner, to retain their voting rights as SNCC staff while organizing in the white community. The proposal was rejected. She argues SNCC's collapse was not primarily caused by white staff departure but by Atlantic City disillusionment and the country's deep racism. The Zellners then joined Anne Braden's Southern Conference Educational Fund (SCEF) in New Orleans for five years.

clips.subject: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner (PARTIAL)
clips.title: Ruby Doris Smith Robinson and Mississippi Freedom Summer Reflections
clips.summary: Zellner remembers Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, SNCC's de facto second-in-command, as someone the men were afraid of, and who became gentle only during her terminal illness at New York's Beth Israel Hospital. Zellner attributes Ruby's collapse to the 30-day rock-breaking sentence at Parchman, dismissing the government-poisoning theory. She closes with reflections on Mississippi Freedom Summer's WATS-line work, the unrealized 1965 summer project, and Allard K. Lowenstein-coalition efforts to displace SNCC.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Family Roots in Bangor and the Bronx
clips.summary: Derby describes her maternal grandparents in Bangor, Maine, her grandmother an original charter member of an NAACP chapter founded in the 1920s, her grandfather a Bangor businessman, and her paternal grandparents, including her father's experience as the only African-American in his civil engineering class at the University of Pennsylvania and his refusal to enter through a service elevator to collect his awards.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Quest for Black Cultural Images
clips.summary: Derby recounts seeking positive Black images as a child, finding them in great-aunt Jesse's letters from missionary work in Monrovia, Liberia; in dance lessons at the Katherine Dunham school; and in research at the Schomburg Library in Manhattan. She describes her father's family photography practice as a precedent for her own later documentary work.
related_events: Katherine Dunham Dance school scholarship audition

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: NAACP Youth and Family Discussions
clips.summary: Derby describes joining her church's NAACP youth chapter and the dinner-table political conversations in her extended family, her father's University of Pennsylvania story, her uncle's dental training at Tufts and his eventual work as a sky cap, and her aunt Julia Green's career as W.E.B. Du Bois's executive secretary in Boston before federal recruitment to integrate the Washington DC secretarial workforce in the 1940s.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Hunter College, Nigeria, and AMSAC
clips.summary: Derby describes summer travel to a Navajo reservation in New Mexico, then a 1960 NAACP scholarship trip to Nigeria the summer before independence, with stops in Paris and Rome. She traces her involvement with Hunter College's Anthropology Club, the African Scholarship Committee supporting John F. Kennedy airlift students, and AMSAC's African-American cultural connections work, plus a Hunter visit by Malcolm X.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Summer 1962 in Albany, Georgia
clips.summary: Derby describes a planned summer 1962 trip to Mexico for anthropology study diverted into SNCC work when she stopped first in Atlanta. She met James Forman, Bob Zellner, and Ruby Doris Smith at the SNCC office, then traveled with Forman and Zellner to Albany, Georgia. She canvassed for voter registration with the Albany Movement and was eventually asked by Charles Sherrod to return to New York to raise funds and goods.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: New York SNCC Office and March on Washington
clips.summary: Derby describes returning to New York to teach school by day and run SNCC fundraising by evening through 1962–63. She partnered with Rae Brandstein on a school-bus food, clothing, and book drive for Mississippi; helped establish the New York SNCC office; co-founded New York Artists for SNCC; and joined the recruiting committee for the March on Washington under Bayard Rustin.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Bob Moses and the Move to Mississippi
clips.summary: Derby recounts Bob Moses traveling to her New York fundraising event, then repeatedly calling to recruit her to a Mississippi adult-literacy pilot project tied to SNCC voter registration. She initially declined, planning graduate study in Mexico, but the spring 1963 Birmingham violence, police dogs and fire hoses on children, prompted her to commit to Mississippi after the March on Washington.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Jackson, COFO, and Field Work
clips.summary: Derby describes arriving in Jackson in fall 1963 as a SNCC field secretary on $10-a-week payroll, housed with local families, working out of the Council of Federated Organizations umbrella office. She describes COFO's coordination of SNCC, CORE, and NAACP staff on voter registration, demonstrations, integration of facilities, and the mock gubernatorial election, alongside the dangers of integrated cars, the limits of federal protection, and the spiritual strength of community members.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Founding the Free Southern Theatre
clips.summary: Derby describes co-founding the Free Southern Theatre in October 1963 with John O'Neal and Gilbert Moses while housed on the Tougaloo College campus. The group conceived theatre as a vehicle for the other arts and a way to take plays into rural Mississippi, churches, cotton fields, grocery stores. Bill Hutchinson, a Tougaloo theatre professor, joined the effort. The first production was Purlie Victorious in spring 1964; the theatre lasted 19 years, eventually moving to Tulane University in New Orleans.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Head Start in Mississippi
clips.summary: Derby describes joining Mississippi Head Start as resource teacher at a center outside Holly Springs after a University of Michigan research project at Tougaloo, Talladega, and Tuskegee. She describes recruiting teachers from mothers and community members because regular Black teachers feared association with the program, making toys from recycled materials, recording children's responses to make custom books, and later serving as troubleshooter trainer at the Durant child development center, a building constructed by the local Black community on donated land.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Poor People's Corporation and Liberty House
clips.summary: Derby describes her work with the Poor People's Corporation as an umbrella for handcraft co-operatives across Mississippi, Louisiana, and Georgia, with Liberty House in Jackson handling raw-materials purchasing, storage, and marketing. She traveled to potential co-op groups setting minimum requirements of ten members and seed money, recruited Northern volunteer crafts teachers, learned candle-making and leather work to train co-ops in turn, and singles out the Una sewing co-operative's black rag dolls as a notable product.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Southern Media and the Photographic Record
clips.summary: Derby describes joining Southern Media in 1968, founded in 1967 by two white organizers (Bill Pell and a Midwestern colleague) to train young Black Mississippians in documentary film and photography. She describes documenting women in all walks of movement work, children, men engaged in constructive activity, the flourishing handcraft and farmers co-operatives, the Tufts–Mound Bayou healthcare partnership, and her later work with the Atlanta-based Sistography Black Women's Photography Collective.

clips.subject: Dr. Doris Derby
clips.title: Black Womanism and Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Derby describes her self-identification as a Black womanist drawing on Alice Walker's framing, a Black woman working alongside family and community to develop the community and bring Black people forward, with all ages included and political and cultural discussion not hidden from children. She links this philosophy to her family's dinner-table conversations and her continuing work starting a theatre group and an African-American filmmakers group at Georgia State.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Birth and Family in Hattiesburg
clips.summary: Holloway introduces himself as born September 26, 1952 in Hattiesburg, Mississippi to Beatrice Gibson Holloway from York, Alabama and Willie Holloway, native to Hattiesburg. He describes his father's sixty-year tenure at the Meridian Fertilizer Company (precursor to Coastal Chemicals, home office Yazoo City) beginning at one dollar a day, eventually becoming office custodian and post-retirement watchman.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Mobile Street Community
clips.summary: Holloway describes 904 Mobile Street, where his mother's Bea's Beauty Salon was attached to their home. He inventories the self-contained Mobile Street commercial district: a movie theater, twelve to fifteen restaurants and clubs, beauty salons and barbershops, a physician's office, dentist, pharmacist, haberdasheries, tailor shops, six cleaners, four churches, a watchmaker, four motels and a hotel, dance halls. The district served a thirty-mile catchment area drawing residents from McLain, Beaumont, Richton, and Wiggins every Saturday.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Schooling at W.H. Jones and Rowan High
clips.summary: Holloway attended the Maddie Robinson kindergarten, then W.H. Jones Elementary and Junior High (opened 1958), and Rowan High School on Royal Street (later renamed Martin Luther King Avenue). He describes hearing teachers and Mrs. Sip, president of the Mississippi Teachers Association, discuss the building of new Black schools such as W.H. Jones and Lillie Burney as a state strategy to forestall integration after Brown. He explains the parallel MTA structures, Mississippi Teachers Association for African American teachers, MTA for Caucasian teachers, meeting separately in Jackson on different days.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Mother's Death and Alabama Family
clips.summary: Holloway's mother died when he was eleven, after which his aunt and uncle Earl and Louise from Bessemer, Alabama (twelve miles from Birmingham) became central figures. They were 65 when they took him in, deeply pro-civil-rights and pro-education though formally uneducated. Every summer and holiday Holloway took the train to stay with them. They took him to the 16th Street Baptist Church, the Say Hey Burger restaurant, and the Birmingham Black Barons stadium associated with Willie Mays, instilling standards of pride, presentation, and self-reliance.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: N.R. Burger and Black School Equity
clips.summary: Holloway frames N.R. Burger as a visionary principal at Eureka and Rowan High School who chose Cornell University for graduate work and built a Hattiesburg Black school system focused on equal funding rather than integration. Burger's son and others later confirmed for Holloway that Burger pushed for parity in average daily attendance funding, books, and lab equipment. Holloway recalls receiving one new geometry textbook in October or November 1968, books typically arrived as hand-me-downs from white schools. Hattiesburg was one of few Mississippi places offering a twelfth-grade Black diploma in the 1940s, drawing students from a fifty-mile radius.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Father's Voting Stance and Downtown Access
clips.summary: Holloway's father Willie never voted, valuing his standing with white power-holders at the fertilizer company. Willie's network gave Holloway softer treatment at downtown Hattiesburg businesses, Sears, JC Penney, Bill Whitley, the pharmacy, the service station, where his father had accounts. Holloway recalls the wide circulation of Theron Lynd as a topic in Hattiesburg, referencing the book Count Them One by One on the voting rights effort, and the earlier group of fifteen and more who attempted to register.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Mass Meetings and Freedom Schools
clips.summary: Holloway describes near-nightly mass meetings at True Light Baptist Church, St. Paul, Mount Zion Baptist Church, and Palmer's Crossing. After his mother's death he had more freedom to attend, and one summer relatives from Muskegon, Michigan gave him additional latitude. The mass meetings were his first integrated meetings with whites. He participated in Freedom Schools at the community center on Mobile Street and at True Light Baptist Church, borrowing books and attending some classes on civics, but the Alabama family pressed self-sufficiency over accepting freedom-school food. He marched but withdrew when Klan presence with guns appeared.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Dr. Tatum's Office and Federal Filings
clips.summary: Holloway confronted Dr. A.T. Tatum's segregated medical practice in the late 1960s or early 1970s, refusing the back-door entry and filing a complaint with the Office of Civil Rights in Washington. Tatum told Holloway he could no longer return to the office. From that point Holloway received Office of Civil Rights consultative calls for twenty-five years on voting location changes and school boundary issues, and filed objections to separate medical facilities at the old Methodist Hospital in Petal, Mississippi.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Sheraton Hotel Scholarship Interview
clips.summary: Holloway recounts the Sheraton Hotel in Jackson scholarship interview where the interviewer asked his opinion of Senator James O. Eastland. Holloway answered with measured criticism of Eastland's Farm Subsidies Act work as a major landholder paid not to grow crops. The interviewer denied him the scholarship explicitly because Holloway had told the truth without accommodation. Holloway draws from this the lesson of framing and reframing to communicate honestly without being removed from the conversation.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Clyde Kennard and USM
clips.summary: Holloway recounts thirty years of conversation with Aubrey Lucas, William D. McCain, and N.R. Burger's son about Clyde Kennard's denied entry to USM. Lucas told Holloway that McCain held that Mississippi higher education should be integrated but that USM was one vote away from being closed by the state board, so integration should start at Ole Miss. Lucas also said McCain remarked it would be perfect if Ole Miss were winning the SEC the year integration occurred. Holloway hears the contested account that Burger may have negotiated state funding for the junior college at Piney Woods to keep Kennard out of USM.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: USM as Mississippi's Fourth HBCU
clips.summary: Holloway describes USM's transformation, calling it an inside joke that USM is Mississippi's fourth HBCU with roughly forty percent African American enrollment. He credits J. Ralph Noonkester at William Carey College, the first private Mississippi college to integrate, for collaborating with N.R. Burger, who selected the Rowan High students who integrated in 1965. Holloway worked with Aubrey Lucas and Joe Paul to build USM's Office of Inclusion, civility training, and Office of Student Affairs busman services for high-risk first-generation students from a 120-mile catchment area.

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: Civil Rights Commission and Museum Work
clips.summary: Holloway serves on Mississippi's Civil Rights Commission, established by legislative act to integrate civil rights content into public school curriculum, working with the William Winter Institute for Racial Reconciliation, William Winter, and Susan Glisson. He also sits on the Mississippi Coalition Against Sexual Assault. He met with the Civil Rights Museum and Mississippi History Museum planning groups four or five months before the interview. He founded Leadership Hattiesburg twenty-five years prior. He emphasizes that museum exits must leave visitors with a dream rather than only agony.
related_events: Mississippi Civil Rights Museum planning (pre-2017 opening)

clips.subject: Eddie Holloway
clips.title: The Spirit and Reflections
clips.summary: Holloway describes 'the spirit', the unidentified Hattiesburg Black-community enforcement collective that visited boycott-breakers at night, breaking windows or otherwise sanctioning those who crossed economic boycott lines. He distinguishes the spirit from the Deacons for Defense, who provided dignitary security. He references the Ladner sisters Dora and Joyce, the Brown University–Tougaloo partnership, and Joan Trumpauer Mulholland's Tougaloo work. Holloway closes by framing inside-the-system negotiation as the more effective civil rights mode in his experience, advocating institutional accommodation for students bringing academic and social deficits.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Jamaican Family and Arrival at Howard
clips.summary: Thelwell describes his father's political career in Jamaica, his mother raising three children after his father's death at 44, his brother's athletic scholarship to the University of Michigan and refusal to major in physical education, and his own arrival at Howard University in September 1959. He read John Oliver Killens' Youngblood and Frank Yerby's The Foxes of Harrow before coming, giving him a dread of the South that was confirmed when he first saw Spanish moss in Louisiana.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: NAG and Howard's Political Culture
clips.summary: Thelwell explains how the Nonviolent Action Group at Howard remained an unrecognized student organization while NAACP and language clubs were recognized, a strategy he later realized President Mordecai Johnson used to deflect Dixiecrat congressional pressure. He describes the 40-person NAG core including Courtland Cox, Charlie Cobb, Cleve Sellers from South Carolina, Hank Thomas from St. Augustine, Stokely Carmichael, Muriel Tillinghast, and Tim Jenkins, alongside faculty like Eugene Clay Holmes, Sterling A. Brown, and E. Franklin Frazier.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Women in NAG and Tom Kahn
clips.summary: Thelwell reflects on women in NAG including Mary Lovelace, who refused to straighten her hair and was nearly expelled until Dean Patricia Roberts Harris intervened, and Muriel Tillinghast's account of leadership exclusion. He describes Tom Kahn, a white socialist roommate of Bayard Rustin sent by Rustin to guide NAG toward labor-aligned nonviolence, and Bill Mahoney's first words to him about the Kantian imperative and dialectical materialism.
related_events: Dean Harris intervention for Mary Lovelace

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Malcolm X at Howard
clips.summary: Thelwell recounts the project-awareness debates that brought Malcolm X to Howard against Bayard Rustin and Herbert Aptheker, his own interview of Malcolm with white co-eds nearby that made Courtland Cox blanch, Malcolm's parables about cats kittening in ovens and bears trapping wolves, and Malcolm's wistful appearance at a SNCC demonstration toward Robert Kennedy's office. James Baldwin attended Malcolm's appearance to listen.
related_events: Malcolm X-Bayard Rustin debate at Howard
SNCC demonstration at Robert Kennedy's Justice Department

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Nonviolence as Strategy
clips.summary: Thelwell distinguishes NAG's strategic nonviolence from the religious nonviolence of John Lewis and Diane Nash, describes American Nazi Party counter-protests at D.C. demonstrations, and recounts the SNCC discussion at Amzie Moore's home in Cleveland, Mississippi, where Charlie Cobb and Mendy Samstein were asked whether to take rifles when the Klan threatened. The organization concluded that individual self-defense decisions were personal, not organizational.
related_events: Hartman Turnbow defense of his home

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: First Trip South and the Waveland Speech
clips.summary: Thelwell describes his first drive South with Cleve Sellers stopping at Sellers' parents' home in South Carolina, his terrified solo sit-in at a Southern restaurant, and his second trip South to Atlanta where James Forman tasked him with writing a speech from a tape recording of SNCC's founding history. Thelwell pre-seeded the audience with 'May the Circle Be Unbroken,' which the room spontaneously sang at the speech's close at Waveland, moving even insurgent Frank Smith to tears.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Setting Up the SNCC Washington Office
clips.summary: Thelwell describes opening the SNCC Washington office in fall 1963 with Bill Mahoney, conducting the Thanksgiving 1963 federal-programs conference where he first met Fannie Lou Hamer and heard her sing, and learning from James Forman the principle of accepting Teamsters money as a top-line sponsor. Bob Moses arrived in spring 1964 to present the Mississippi Summer Project to Washington liberals after the murder of Louis Allen, and Joseph Rauh's reported claim that MFDP could be seated at Atlantic City was decisive.
related_events: Bob Moses planning of 1964 Summer Project

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Summer Project Recruitment and Counter-Propaganda
clips.summary: Thelwell recruited volunteers and ran food-and-clothing drives with the High School Friends of SNCC including Charlene Crines.
