Ralph Featherstone left his teaching job after Thelwell warned him people might die, becoming assistant director of Freedom Schools. Thelwell describes Hazard, Kentucky miners visiting the office, the convening of a National Theatre hearing with E.W. Steptoe, Hartman Turnbow, and the Mooty Kim brothers testifying because Congress refused official hearings, and the syndicated Alsop brothers' columns claiming SNCC sought to create martyrs.
related_events: E.W. Steptoe Amite County voter registration

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Atlantic City Aftermath and Literary Patronage
clips.summary: Thelwell skipped Atlantic City because he had left the SNCC staff, spending months with a Russian-accented actress in New York. A literary agent who had represented Richard Wright offered him a path to a novel-writing career in Italy until she dismissed James Baldwin's Blues for Mister Charlie and announced the literary world was ready for a new black writer; Thelwell rejected the patronage and returned to SNCC.
related_events: Blues for Mister Charlie Broadway run

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: MFDP Office and Congressional Challenge Mechanics
clips.summary: Lawrence Guyot sent Thelwell back to Washington with $200 to open the MFDP office. Thelwell explains the procedural structure of the congressional challenge: only a fellow candidate could challenge a seat, Congress is the sole judge, and the speaker had to recognize a member raising the challenge. He recounts arranging the MFDP-loyalty posters with LBJ at the top, the Leadership Conference's hostility, and finally identifying Rep. William Fitts Ryan as the floor sponsor.
related_events: MFDP support for LBJ ticket in Mississippi
January 1965 House challenge to Mississippi delegation

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Recruiting Freshmen Congressmen and the 147-Vote Result
clips.summary: Thelwell recruited freshmen Democrats including John Conyers, Patsy Mink, and an upstate New York freshman by handing them invitations as they exited LBJ's orientation meeting. A Union of Electricians contribution of $1,000 produced Rep. William J. Green Jr.'s vote. On vote day Thelwell lined MFDP delegates silently along the Capitol tunnel platforms; 60 congressmen rose with Ryan and the roll call produced 147 votes to vacate, forcing depositions in black churches across Mississippi by attorneys including the LCDC.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Voting Rights Act Memorandum
clips.summary: Sen. Proxmire presented a trial-balloon Voting Rights Act language at the Leadership Conference allowing the president to send registrars only where less than 10% of Black citizens were registered. Thelwell's memorandum, signed by SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and MFDP, argued this normalized 91% disenfranchisement and called for street action. The Leadership Conference fractured; Clarence Mitchell Jr. had a heart attack; Walter Reuther tried to seize the vacuum. Selma occurred within two weeks, and the final Voting Rights Act of 1965 dropped the 10% threshold.

clips.subject: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
clips.title: Jesse Harris and the FBI Deportation Threat
clips.summary: Thelwell tells the story of Jesse Harris, a 17-year-old McComb local who walked into the white waiting room with the Freedom Rides and was beaten in Parchman Farm by a 'walking boss' before refusing to break, later becoming a SNCC project director. After Jesse disappeared into addiction, Thelwell included him in his Stokely Carmichael memoir; Jesse reemerged in Jackson alive. FBI agents threatened to deport Thelwell unless he was enrolled in school by September 1965; a fellowship from Sidney Kaplan at UMass Amherst was the path. Dr. King's phone call (relayed by Andrew Young) softened the chairman's resistance to a delayed start.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Tennessee Childhood and Segregated Schools
clips.summary: Howard, born in Tennessee in 1938, describes growing up without his father, raised by his mother and aunts. He attended segregated schools through the 11th grade and recalls the 1954 conversation about integration in his community, saying he had his own circle of friends and was indifferent to integrating white schools.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Air Force Service and Europe
clips.summary: Howard enlisted in the Air Force in 1956 after the Korean War, choosing it over the Army on advice from returning veterans. Stationed in Europe, he visited Paris, Stockholm, and Copenhagen, and encountered jazz musicians who had relocated for artistic freedom. He observed that European whites differed sharply from the segregationists of the South.
related_events: Black expatriate jazz scene in Europe

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Oakland, Merritt College, Meeting Newton and Seale
clips.summary: Howard discharged in Northern California, settled in Oakland, and enrolled in Merritt College on the GI Bill. He began studying Black history for the first time and met Bobby Seale and Huey Newton, with whom he held rap sessions on police repression in Oakland. The group sought a non-civil-rights, revolutionary alternative and began drafting the Ten Point Platform.
related_events: Founding of the Black Panther Party (1966)
Drafting of the Ten Point Platform and Program

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Revolutionary Influences and Police Confrontation
clips.summary: Howard describes the Party's study of Che Guevara, Mao Zedong, Kwame Nkrumah, and Malcolm X, while insisting the Party would not emulate Cuba or China. He recounts a personal incident at Don Barksdale's club in Oakland, a selective police ticket and subsequent arrest, that pushed him to commit to the BPP, including buying a legal 12-gauge shotgun after consulting Newton on the law.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Police Patrols and the Denzil Dowell Case
clips.summary: The Party began armed police patrols in Oakland, observing arrests and following members through bail. Howard recounts the Richmond shooting of Denzil Dowell, whose family told the Party Dowell could not have jumped a fence as police claimed. The case triggered the Party's first organized rally and its first mimeographed publication, the seed of the Black Panther newspaper.
related_events: BPP armed police patrols in Oakland
Denzil Dowell shooting and Richmond Courthouse demonstration

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Editing The Black Panther Newspaper
clips.summary: Howard became the founding editor of the Party newspaper at Seale's request. When Eldridge Cleaver was released from prison after publishing Soul on Ice, Cleaver took the editor title and Howard became Deputy Minister of Information. He describes the paper's role in countering the Oakland Tribune and San Francisco Chronicle and building a weekly circulation that reached 250,000 copies.
related_events: Founding of The Black Panther newspaper
Eldridge Cleaver's release from prison and BPP entry

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Producing and Distributing the Paper
clips.summary: Howard describes the difficulty of getting printers because the FBI pressured them, and how Max Scherr of the Berkeley Barb offered his presses. Emory Douglas produced the artwork. He describes the Party newspaper's national distribution by chapter members, federal interference with airline shipping, and the paper's eventual international reach into Asia, Japan, and African nations.
related_events: BPP newspaper printing through Berkeley Barb
International distribution of The Black Panther

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Richard Aoki and Coalition Building
clips.summary: Howard tells the Party assigned Richard Aoki to organize the Asian community, and Aoki worked with the Red Guard Party in Berkeley. He describes coalitions with the Young Lords (Puerto Rican), the American Indian Movement, and Latino organizations, and his rebuttal of later FBI-informant allegations against Aoki, with whom he remained a lifelong ally.
related_events: Posthumous FBI-informant allegations against Richard Aoki

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Cross-Country Legal Defense Work
clips.summary: After Cleaver took the editor's chair, Howard traveled across the country visiting jailed Party members, working with attorneys on legal defense, and organizing community defense committees. He describes survival programs, free breakfast, free clothing, free health screening for sickle-cell anemia, and liberation schools, as both community service and a strategy for sustaining support for incarcerated members.
related_events: BPP free health clinics and sickle-cell testing
Memphis Invaders becoming a BPP chapter

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Japan and European Tour
clips.summary: Howard and Roberta Alexander were sent to Japan in 1971 to speak at anti-Vietnam-War student rallies, working with filmmaker Shinsuke Ogawa and shadowed by Japanese surveillance. Howard continued to Sweden, Denmark, possibly Finland, and Germany, where he met with Black GIs on a base whose commander imposed a blackout to prevent their visit; they met instead in a nearby village.
related_events: BPP support committees in Sweden and Denmark

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: New Haven Trial and Attica
clips.summary: Recalled from Europe when Bobby Seale was extradited to New Haven on the Alex Rackley murder charge, Howard worked on the Seale-Huggins defense for the duration of the 14-to-18-month trial, drawing support from Daniel Berrigan, Dr. Benjamin Spock, and Kingman Brewster. Shortly after, Howard accompanied Seale to Attica Correctional Facility during the 1971 uprising, where Governor Nelson Rockefeller refused to negotiate and the prison was retaken by force.
related_events: Kingman Brewster's statement on Black revolutionary trials

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Sam Napier and the East-West Coast Schism
clips.summary: Howard recounts the call from New York reporting Sam Napier's murder. Napier ran the BPP newspaper distribution. Howard arrived to find women and children sheltered in a hotel and was arrested with two comrades by NYPD, jailed in the Tombs, and represented by William Kunstler. The judge dismissed charges on condition Howard leave New York. Robert Webb had earlier been shot in retaliation amid the East Coast-West Coast schism inside the Party.
related_events: Sam Napier killing in New York (1971)
Robert Webb shooting in New York

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Merritt College Parolee Program
clips.summary: Returning to Oakland, Howard was hired by Merritt College's president to run the work-study program and negotiated with the California Adult Authority to enroll parolees in college, providing GED preparation, stipends, and meal tickets. The program ended after a confrontation between Panther students and an instructor critical of the Party.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Disability Access, Rent Strikes, Leaving the Party
clips.summary: Howard describes Panther organizing of disabled wheelchair users that won curb cuts and ramps on county buildings, and rent strikes against landlords with lead-paint hazards. He attributes the end of the Party to Huey Newton's decision to dissolve all chapters and pull organizers to Oakland for the Bobby Seale mayoral campaign, leaving community programs stranded. Howard left the Party and never looked back.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Anonymity, FBI Surveillance, Coming Forward
clips.summary: Howard hid his Panther background for years, working in retail management at Kmart and Service Merchandise across the South. FBI agents had earlier visited his elderly aunt in Tennessee. He kept the past from his daughter until she was a teenager. Eventually, at Billy X Jennings's urging, he attended a Washington-DC Panther reunion and began publicly reclaiming his history.

clips.subject: Elbert "Big Man" Howard
clips.title: Sonoma County PACH and Continuing the Fight
clips.summary: Reconnecting in California with his partner Carol, Howard discovered ongoing police killings by state troopers, county sheriffs, and local police in Sonoma County, alongside ICE immigration enforcement. He co-founded PACH, a 24-hour helpline and database for documenting police abuse. He cites the Sheriff's Deputy killing of 13-year-old Andy Lopez, who was carrying a toy weapon, as a case the system refused to indict.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Alcorn to Tennessee A&I
clips.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes her undergraduate years at Alcorn in the early 1940s and her transfer to Tennessee A&I to complete a bachelor's degree. She recounts the dean at Tennessee A&I requiring her to repeat Alcorn coursework, contrasting the resource gap between Black schools and the city schools and law schools that white students attended.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Teaching in Forrest County
clips.summary: After graduating in 1947, Ellie taught home economics in Jasper County, Jones County, and then Forrest County, moving for better salary. She describes the disparity in school resources, the literacy test the white superintendent gave Black teaching applicants, and the consolidation that built Earl Travillion as the single Black school for Forrest County, after which she was named on a list of teachers not to rehire.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Meeting Vernon Dahmer
clips.summary: Ellie recounts being recruited to teach in Kelly Settlement and meeting Vernon Dahmer there. She describes his Christian practice, his ownership of land in Kelly Settlement, the family farm and sawmill operations, the grocery store on the property, and his interest in making things better for other people.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Family Farm and Children
clips.summary: Ellie describes the working farm operations: tractors replacing horses, six boys and one daughter Bettie helping in the fields, children learning to drive young. She names crops (cotton, corn, peanuts, sugar cane) and the grocery store, and notes she handled sales-tax paperwork and Social Security records for sawmill workers.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Trying to Register with Theron Lynd
clips.summary: Ellie describes her early attempts to register to vote in Hattiesburg with registrar Theron Lynd, who handed her a card with part of the Constitution and rejected her interpretation regardless of accuracy. She explains the affidavit teachers were required to notarize disclosing organizations they donated to, with NAACP a forbidden recipient.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Voter Card Buried With Him
clips.summary: Ellie states that Vernon Dahmer never had a chance to vote in his lifetime; his voter-registration card arrived from the Justice Department only in time to be buried with him. She describes her own later voting practice of going to the polling place except for one absentee vote at the courthouse after a hip replacement.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Three Terms as Election Commissioner
clips.summary: Ellie describes running for and serving three terms as election commissioner for District 2 of Forrest County roughly ten to fifteen years after Vernon's death. She recounts gathering qualifying signatures with her daughter and others, working alongside two Black commissioners and one white colleague, and following the law to ensure no one was denied the vote.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: NAACP Meetings in the Living Room
clips.summary: Ellie describes NAACP meetings held in the Dahmer living room with a lookout posted by the car, the local chief of police arriving at the house on one occasion, and a small core of roughly ten neighbors willing to be known as NAACP members. She names Mrs. Beard as the lead worker with young people in the local NAACP branch, including in connection with the Ladner sisters (Dorie Ann and Joyce).

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Living Under Threat
clips.summary: Ellie describes the climate of fear preceding the firebombing: receiving threats, hives breaking out on her neck, sleeping in shifts with the children dressed for school, sewing blackout curtains so figures could not be seen through the window, and the family keeping the front porch light off. She notes the KKK posted signs on utility poles that Vernon would tear down, and that the sheriff was complicit or related to participants.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: U.S. v. Lynd: The 18 Plaintiffs
clips.summary: Ellie identifies herself as one of the 18 plaintiffs the Justice Department forced Theron Lynd to register without requiring them to refile. She recounts the trial evidence that two Black women in the Beard family who appeared white were allowed to register without being tested, proving Lynd's intent to discriminate. She names herself, her aunt, her mother-in-law, and others as the 18.
related_events: U.S. v. Lynd federal voting-rights case

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: John Doar and the Federal Court
clips.summary: Ellie describes Justice Department attorney John Doar handling the case in a federal court hearing before a three-judge panel, distinguishing it from later proceedings before Judge W. Harold Cox. She recounts that a young Dahmer son was nearly jailed for taking a photograph during the proceedings and that Doar's strategy spared most plaintiffs from having to testify in person.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Hosting Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins
clips.summary: Ellie recounts that Vernon came back from a Jackson NAACP meeting with SNCC organizers Curtis Hayes (Curtis Muhammad) and Hollis Watkins out of McComb. The two young men lived with the Dahmer family, worked alongside the children, and after their work canvassed the community for voter registration through SNCC and COFO.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Freedom Summer Picnic
clips.summary: Ellie describes her work cooking for the Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers at a celebratory picnic on the Dahmer property: potato salad, fish, and watermelons retrieved from the field. She recounts the moment a young SNCC tractor-driver returned through the white community in Hattiesburg with the watermelon trailer behind him, an unintended danger.

clips.subject: Ellie Dahmer
clips.title: Drafted Sons and the Voting Rights Act
clips.summary: Ellie recounts that her older sons were systematically targeted for the draft as soon as they turned 18, including Alvin who was taken on crutches. Vernon Jr., Joe volunteered; Hal and Alvin were drafted. She closes with her reaction to the 1965 Voting Rights Act and the federal registrars working from a separate booth because Black registrants were not allowed inside the Forrest County courthouse.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Family and Madrona Upbringing
clips.summary: Dixon describes his Chicago and Mississippi family origins, his father's Boeing illustrator job that brought the family to Seattle in 1957, and the multicultural Madrona neighborhood the family settled into in 1960. He details cross-cultural exchanges with Jewish, Russian Orthodox, Chinese, and Scandinavian neighbors and the values his parents instilled.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Father at Boeing and Civil Rights Awareness
clips.summary: Dixon discusses his father's outgoing personality and adventurism at Boeing, his father's progressive associations including possible Communist Party members, and the television imagery of the early civil rights movement that filtered into his childhood. He recalls Medgar Evers, Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, and the racial micro-abrasions framework his colleague later articulated.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Stokely Carmichael at Garfield
clips.summary: Dixon describes his brother Aaron Dixon's earlier political awareness and the 1967 Stokely Carmichael speech at Garfield High School that catalyzed his own consciousness. He recounts the cultural analogies Carmichael used and his reflection afterward on tennis-club exclusion, his mother's job-search discrimination, and his developing racial pride.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: SNCC and the Garfield Black Student Union
clips.summary: Dixon recounts joining SNCC as Seattle chapter co-chair, voter-registration organizing, and the campaign with classmates to establish a Black Student Union at Garfield High School. He describes refusing to stand for the Pledge of Allegiance, the pep-rally Black Power chant that got him expelled, and the boycott that compelled the principal to grant the first high-school BSU on the West Coast.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Franklin Sit-In and King Assassination
clips.summary: Dixon describes his first arrest at a Franklin High School rival-school incident defending Deborah Rodriguez, his judge-assigned essay, and the larger Franklin sit-in he led with his brother Aaron Dixon and Larry Gossett to demand a BSU and reinstate an expelled student. A week later he was arrested at Garfield for unlawful assembly on the same day Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Bobby Hutton Funeral and Founding Seattle BPP
clips.summary: Dixon and his brother Aaron Dixon traveled with a Seattle BSU contingent to the West Coast BSU conference at San Francisco State, leaving early to attend Bobby Hutton's funeral in West Oakland. Viewing Hutton's body at age seventeen and hearing Bobby Seale speak that night, Dixon committed to the Party. The brothers cornered Seale, who came to Seattle within two weeks; fifty potential members gathered in the Dixon parents' home, and the chapter was established as the first BPP chapter outside California.
related_events: West Coast BSU conference San Francisco State 1968

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: What It Meant To Be a Panther
clips.summary: Dixon explains the discipline, reading regimen, and arms requirements imposed on Seattle Panthers after J. Edgar Hoover named the BPP the number one threat to U.S. internal security in 1968. He outlines the ten-point program, the Huey Newton precedent of armed police-alert patrols, the Lowndes County Freedom Organization origin of the panther symbol, and the chapter's reduction from several hundred to a hardcore group of about twenty.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: COINTELPRO Targeting of Seattle Chapter
clips.summary: Dixon describes the FBI memo identifying Los Angeles, Chicago, and Seattle as the three chapters targeted for elimination, naming Bunchy Carter and Fred Hampton among the assassinated leaders. He recounts attempts on his own and Aaron Dixon's lives, including a doctored shotgun cartridge that nearly killed Aaron at a target range, and FBI visits to his father at Boeing. He cites Fred Hampton's rainbow-coalition formulation as the origin of the phrase later associated with Jesse Jackson.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Survival Programs and Coalition Building
clips.summary: Dixon details five free breakfast programs serving over 2,000 children daily at Yesler Terrace, Holly Park, High Point, Rainier Vista, and the Atlantic Street Center, where chapter head Ike Ikeda had known the Dixon family since 1957. He describes founding the Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center with neurologist John Green, the fortified 20th and Spruce office with sandbag bunkers, and coalitions with the Peace and Freedom Party, SDS, Young Lords, Red Guard, and American Indian Movement.
related_events: Carolyn Downs Family Medical Center founding

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Oregon Imprisonment and HUAC Testimony
clips.summary: Dixon recounts his 14-month imprisonment in Salem, Oregon for an alleged theft while speaking at the University of Oregon in Eugene, the prison captain's threat to shoot him first if Attica-style unrest erupted, and his refusal of Governor Tom McCall's clemency offer. He describes his prior subpoena before the House Un-American Activities Committee, his Fifth Amendment refusal to identify his BPP membership, and the chapter's transfer to Oakland in 1971.

clips.subject: Elmer Dixon
clips.title: Ideological Split and COINTELPRO Disruption
clips.summary: Dixon describes the ideological split within the Seattle chapter between military-action advocates and survival-program proponents, the bank-robbery convictions of dissenters, and the broader national split between Huey Newton and Eldridge Cleaver factions. He traces COINTELPRO's history from its early-twentieth-century counterintelligence origins through the McCarthy blacklist era to its deployment against the BPP.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Priscilla's Plainfield and Smith Years
clips.summary: Priscilla Bassett describes her Plainfield, New Jersey elementary education in a New Deal-enlightened environment with Harold Rugg textbooks, her Republican family's reaction to Franklin Delano Roosevelt's 1944 death, her work at Life Magazine after her father lost his job, and her engagement with the progressive party at Smith College. She mentions visiting Senator Clifford P. Case and her interest in Countee Cullen.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Emmett's Henry County Childhood
clips.summary: Emmett describes his Henry County, Virginia family: his father's 1935 death when Emmett was 14, his half-brothers' education at Hampton Institute and St. Paul, his teacher Ida Dotson promoting him from fifth to seventh grade at the Chestnut Knob school, and the Bassett Furniture Industries' role in displacing his grandfather William Henry from a sawmill supervisory position under Virginia's racial restrictions on Black supervisors.
related_events: Continental Bank of Chicago furniture financing

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Family Land and the Swimming Pond
clips.summary: Emmett recounts the Tillesta furniture-company family that employed his father, the cross-racial cousinship between his mother and white neighbors that became known to him through a swimming pond his family built, and his post-University of Massachusetts visit to a white cousin of his father whom he refused to call on. Sets up the intergenerational interracial-kinship context of his later marriage.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Roadhouse Picket and Meeting
clips.summary: Priscilla and Emmett describe meeting during a late-1940s civil rights picket of a segregated roadhouse on the Smith College-Amherst road. Priscilla served as a witness at the resulting trial. Emmett was studying at the University of Massachusetts. They describe their early relationship, including a Smith house mother's reaction and Plainfield public school memories of Paul Robeson performing.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Tuskegee and George Washington Carver
clips.summary: Emmett describes his Tuskegee work-study at four o'clock in the morning milking 13 Guernsey cows for 15 to 20 cents an hour, his National Youth Administration scholarship arranged through writer Ralph Ellison, and his work at Dr. George Washington Carver's place for twelve hours a week showing visitors. Describes Carver's personal interactions including the speculation that Carver had been castrated as a young child.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Draft Board and Military Service
clips.summary: Emmett describes refusing the Tuskegee Army Air Force volunteer route on his mother's advice, his draft-board exchange with judge Whittle in Martinsville, Virginia, his service in the Quartermaster Corps Company E at Camp Lee with Black officers including Colonel Snowden, his work with attorney Robert Ming, and his refusal to be moved to the back of an all-white meeting room. Includes Petersburg, Virginia incident and Fort A.P. Hill, Virginia training.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Sharecropper-Training Refusal
clips.summary: After the war, Emmett worked as a county agricultural teacher in Henry County, Virginia for ninety dollars a month. He was offered a position teaching a Washington Virginia plantation school that would have trained returning Black veterans as sharecroppers receiving seventy-five dollars a month. Emmett refused, identifying the trap of veterans being stuck on plantations after the three-year federal subsidy expired. The encounter pushed him toward graduate study.
related_events: post-WWII Black veteran agricultural extension programs

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Columbus, UAW, and Emmett Till Fundraising
clips.summary: After the couple's marriage and brief New York period, they moved to Columbus while Emmett pursued his Ohio State University PhD. Priscilla worked at General Motors on the production line and became a UAW shop steward-she notes she was the only woman steward in that plant. She describes meeting a Black co-worker who had to work in the washroom on probation despite Curtiss-Wright wartime factory experience, then becoming active in the union. The chapter covers the Brown II decree, the late summer 1955 Till murder, and the Bassetts raising three thousand dollars for Mamie Till at a Columbus Baptist church.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Dairy Technology PhD and Industry Blacklisting
clips.summary: Emmett describes his Ohio State University PhD as the first African American to take a doctorate in dairy technology. Chicago dairy companies including Beatrice Foods refused to hire him, with one executive saying the top would come off the building if a Black PhD were hired. Southern Black colleges in Florida and Georgia offered positions Emmett could not accept under Virginia miscegenation laws prior to Loving v. Virginia 1967. The chapter also covers his postdoctoral work at Columbia University on biosynthesis of patulin and aromatic amino acids in collaboration with Rockefeller Institute researchers.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Red Scare FBI Visits
clips.summary: Priscilla describes her Women's International League for Peace and Freedom membership in Columbus and the FBI visit during Emmett's consideration for an Ohio State University assistant professorship under Dr. Gould. Dr. Gould reported FBI concerns about Priscilla's friends, including a woman who had been to Iowa House and whose husband was at the Ohio State University Hospital medical school. Emmett refused to talk to the FBI or CIA and lost the position. Priscilla also received an FBI visit while hanging clothes in the yard and declined to speak with them.
keywords: Women's International League for Peace and Freedom
related_events: Association of University Professors censure of Ohio State

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: School Integration in Washington Heights
clips.summary: After moving to New York City, the Bassetts faced housing discrimination (a landlord changed the lock on an apartment they had claimed). Priscilla met Mae Mallory, who had worked with Robert F. Williams in North Carolina, at a New York City Public School System meeting. They sent their three children to the Fieldston School in Riverdale but Priscilla and Emmett also organized for the integration of three Washington Heights junior high schools, the Freedom School at a Methodist church, and Ocean Hill-Brownsville community control. Their car gas tank was filled with sugar by opponents.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: In Friendship and March on Washington
clips.summary: Priscilla describes her work with In Friendship, the 1956-era coalition organized by Stanley Levison and Bayard Rustin in support of Martin Luther King. Bayard Rustin had gone to Africa to oppose the French bomb when she joined. At an Apollo Theater meeting, In Friendship turned the March on Washington organizing over to A. Philip Randolph once the hundred-thousand-person threshold was reached. The chapter also covers the 1957 Prayer Pilgrimage for Freedom at the reflecting pool-the first time Priscilla heard Dr. King speak.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Malcolm X and the Mitchell-Lama Co-op
clips.summary: Emmett describes attending three or four Malcolm X speeches in Harlem and a sidewalk conversation between Michael Harrington, Malcolm X, and himself. The Bassetts moved from a Mitchell-Lama cooperative in 1961 after the cooperative board voted to refuse meeting space for Upper Manhattan Committee for Equal Rights gatherings. They went to Sweden in 1964-65.
keywords: Upper Manhattan Committee for Equal Rights

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Ortho Pharmaceutical and New Jersey College of Medicine
clips.summary: After Sweden, Emmett worked two years at Ortho Pharmaceutical (a Johnson and Johnson subsidiary) but left over concerns including the company's practice of buying blood from poor communities through front groups. He was recruited to the New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry after the Newark rebellion when the institution had no African American faculty; he served on the admissions committee. Johnson and Johnson countered with offers including free tuition for his daughter but he refused to return.
keywords: New Jersey College of Medicine and Dentistry

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Uppsala and Reflections on King and Malcolm
clips.summary: The Bassetts describe their year at Uppsala, Sweden where Emmett worked in Dr. Arne Tiselius's laboratory after recommendation from Columbia colleagues. They traveled by ocean liner to Norway, visited a Rockefeller Institute friend in Trondheim, Norway, and were welcomed by a Swedish station master who took them into his house overnight. Priscilla reflects on Dr. King's Riverside Church speech, the Vietnam war's wrongness following Dien Bien Phu, Muhammad Ali's draft refusal statement, and her respect for both King and Malcolm X following Manning Marable's biography.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Interracial Marriage and Loving v. Virginia
clips.summary: Priscilla reflects on the couple's 62-year marriage. She recalls an NAACP statement (attributed in the transcript to Dr. Du Bois though this attribution is chronologically uncertain) following Loving v. Virginia that the NAACP did not oppose or support interracial marriage but believed individuals who fall in love should pursue their relationships. She emphasizes respecting cultural difference rather than racial elimination by consolidation. Their older daughter attributed their long marriage to a shared moral compass.

clips.subject: Emmett W. Bassett and Priscilla Tietjen Bassett
clips.title: Human Rights Commission and Voter Registration
clips.summary: Emmett describes his service on the New York City Human Rights Commission, including monthly meetings at the Plaza Hotel funded by the governor, monitoring housing-organizing groups in his community. The interview closes with his account of registering to vote in Henry County, Virginia around 1942 or 1943 before entering the Army: at the Field Station two miles from home he was handed a blank sheet of paper rather than an application, was coached the previous week by Jim Mullins (a Black businessman who ran the cornmeal mill), and was registered after adding Negro to the form.
related_events: pre-1965 Black voter registration in Virginia
NYC Human Rights Commission housing oversight

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Father, Aunts, and Smithfield Origins
clips.summary: Finney describes being raised by his school-teacher father and his aunts after his mother died when he was ten days old. He recalls his father as a Virginia State University graduate, a strict but fair head of household, and a teacher who moved frequently between Virginia elementary schools whenever a better job appeared.

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: From the Pentagon to Claflin
clips.summary: Finney traces his father's career from Virginia teaching to wartime work at the Pentagon, then to Morgan State University in Baltimore, then to a position as Dean of Claflin College in Orangeburg, South Carolina around 1945-46. He arrives in Orangeburg expecting lynchings from his Baltimore friends' warnings, only to find a welcoming community.
related_events: World War II domestic federal employment

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: The Weber Soda Shop and the Klan
clips.summary: Finney recalls working six to eight years as a soda jerk at the Weber family's college soda shop on the perimeter of Claflin and South Carolina State. He witnesses a Ku Klux Klan parade down the street in front of the two campuses during his first or second year at Claflin, and identifies the Trinity United Methodist Church across the street as his family's church home.
related_events: Ku Klux Klan parade past Claflin and South Carolina State

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Observing Black Lawyers in Washington
clips.summary: Finney describes how observing Black attorneys in Washington during the family's Baltimore years shaped his ambition to enter law. He names Belford V. Lawson Jr., a Washington lawyer, fraternity president, and national leader, as a figure he wanted to hitch his star to.
related_events: Observation of Black legal practice in DC

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: South Carolina State Law School
clips.summary: Finney enters South Carolina State University School of Law in fall 1952, describing small classes of about four students with dedicated faculty. He names John H. Wrighten III (likely) or John W. Wrighten as the student whose lawsuit helped desegregate the University of South Carolina, and recalls graduating and taking the bar in early summer 1954, weeks after the Brown decision. He laments South Carolina's eventual closure of the law school, contrasting it with North Carolina Central University School of Law's continued operation.
keywords: South Carolina State University School of Law
North Carolina Central University School of Law
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education decision
Desegregation of the University of South Carolina

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Conway, the Ocean Forest, and Early Practice
clips.summary: Finney describes teaching school in Conway, South Carolina, while picking up work at the Ocean Forest Hotel as a waiter. He recounts attending his first South Carolina Bar Association meeting in 1954 or 1955 as a waiter, Black bar members were excluded from the social events, and his later return to the same body's head table as Chief Justice.
related_events: 1954/55 South Carolina Bar Association annual meeting

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Move to Sumter and Civil Rights Practice
clips.summary: Finney describes how a mission encountering Matthew J. Perry Jr. and I. DeQuincey Newman around the opening of a state park stimulated his decision to leave Conway for Sumter, South Carolina and become a full-time civil rights attorney. The President of Morris College offers him a part-time South Carolina history teaching position that eases the transition. He identifies I. DeQuincey Newman as field secretary of the SC NAACP.
related_events: Transition to full-time civil rights practice

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: The Friendship Nine and Jail-Without-Bail
clips.summary: Finney recounts representing the Friendship Nine, students from Friendship Junior College in Rock Hill, South Carolina, who were connected with the Congress of Racial Equality through field secretary James T. McCain of Sumter. After his ragged car broke down on the way to court, he reached Rock Hill to learn after the convictions that the students had adopted a new jail-without-bail strategy he had not been informed of. He notes the presence of SNCC, the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, in Rock Hill at the same time.
related_events: Friendship Nine sit-in arrests in Rock Hill

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: NAACP, Civil Rights Commission, Orangeburg Massacre
clips.summary: Finney describes his closest affiliation with the Sumter NAACP chapter and the cadre of Black attorneys, mostly South Carolina State law graduates, who took non-lucrative civil rights cases. He details joining the South Carolina Advisory Committee to the US Commission on Civil Rights in 1963, naming James McBride Dabbs as an active white participant, and resigning along with Ben Payton in 1969 after the federal government refused to reinvestigate the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre.
related_events: 1969 resignation from US Commission on Civil Rights

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: State House, Black Caucus, RFK Job Offer
clips.summary: After three losing campaigns, Finney wins a seat in the South Carolina House of Representatives in 1972 and helps found the Legislative Black Caucus, prioritizing learning the chamber's rules. He recalls drawing 20-25 percent of the white vote in his Sumter elections and recounts an earlier offer from Robert F. Kennedy to join the US Justice Department, which he declined to remain in South Carolina politics. He also recalls running unsuccessfully for Congress, possibly against Albert W. Watson.
related_events: Founding of the SC Legislative Black Caucus
Robert F. Kennedy DOJ job offer

clips.subject: Ernest Adolphus Finney Jr.
clips.title: Supreme Court, Abbeville, and the Death Penalty
clips.summary: Finney describes his 1985 election to the South Carolina Supreme Court, running against a senator while he himself worked the 124-member House, and his 1994 elevation to Chief Justice. He explains the Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina decision and the structural difficulty of getting tax-base counties to share with poorer counties in the Corridor of Shame. He closes by recounting the death-penalty case as a circuit judge, a defendant who robbed a coin dealer and raped two women in Horry County, one of whom died, and the inner struggle of sentencing a human being to die despite his legislative-era opposition to capital punishment.
related_events: Abbeville County School District v. State of South Carolina decision

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Family and Farm in Wise, North Carolina
clips.summary: Terry describes growing up the youngest of twelve children on a family farm in Wise, Warren County, North Carolina. Her mother Mary Alexander, widowed by the 1918 flu epidemic from first husband Arthur Hunt, returned to Warren County with three children and married Terry's father, who raised all the children together. Her father moved from sharecropping to ownership, buying the land in 1945.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Church, School, and a Mother's Ethic
clips.summary: Terry recounts her mother's churchgoing without fanaticism, her preference for teaching over preaching, and the family rule against speaking of white people as an undifferentiated 'they.' She describes farm labor schedules that pulled children from school and her mother's compensating labor in the cotton field.
related_events: segregated schooling in 1940s rural North Carolina

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Social Security and the Path to College
clips.summary: Terry explains how the 1957 extension of Social Security to farmers, with retroactive eligibility to age 65, gave her parents a check that funded her college tuition. She describes helping neighbors document their ages through family Bibles and midwife memory when birth certificates were missing.
related_events: 1957 Social Security extension to farm workers

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Arrival at Bennett College
clips.summary: Terry describes her family's arrival on the Bennett College campus, the bond formed that day between her mother and Roslyn Smith's mother, and the mutual mothering arrangement that made Smith her lifelong closest friend. She names Theaoseus Tobias Clayton Sr as a Warren County connection.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Bennett Faculty and the English Department
clips.summary: Terry describes her Bennett English faculty: Dr. Hobart Jarrett's exacting literary training and intellectual showmanship, Dr. Crawford's grammar drills and sentence diagramming, and theater director Fred Eady, whose marriage Terry and her classmates engineered. She lived in the Honors Dorm.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Bennett's Ethic and the Segregated City
clips.summary: Terry contrasts the protected life inside Bennett's gates, where students dressed for dinner and learned which fork to use, with the segregated downtown Greensboro of white-only signs and the F.W. Woolworth lunch counter. She describes the cognitive dissonance that made student action inevitable.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Planning the Greensboro Sit-ins
clips.summary: Terry recounts the planning of the Greensboro sit-ins among Bennett sociology students Roslyn Smith, Gwen Mackel, Shirley Dismute, Gloria Brown, and others. She describes Dr. Hobart Jarrett's advice to include North Carolina A&T State University men, and Dr. Willa B. Player's counsel to wait until after Christmas vacation so participants would not scatter.
related_events: fall 1959 sit-in planning at Bennett College

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: February 1, 1960 and the Sit-ins
clips.summary: Terry describes Gwen Mackel's account of standing inside Woolworth's on February 1, 1960, having bought something so as not to be ejected, waiting as part of a planned action when the A&T four sat down. Terry herself sat at the lunch counter alongside Linda Brown and other Bennett College student activists, encountering the hatred of onlookers.

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Dr. Willa B. Player's Leadership
clips.summary: Terry describes Bennett College president Dr. Willa B. Player as a tall, quiet, dignified woman with a profound commitment to social justice. Player allowed students to march and sit-in but required them to keep their grades, drove alone to the Greensboro jail to claim arrested Bennett students during a second wave of arrests, and had a cross burned at the president's house.
related_events: second wave of Greensboro sit-in arrests (Feb-April 1960)

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: UNC-Chapel Hill Master's Degree
clips.summary: Terry describes her father's insistence that as a taxpayer his daughter could attend UNC-Chapel Hill rather than be paid by the state to go north. She recounts the housing office's surprise on arrival that she was Black, having paired her with a white roommate, and her mother's reminder that 'it's not the girls, it's the ruling.' Terry studied with C. Hugh Holman and earned the master's.
related_events: UNC-Chapel Hill graduate school integration period (early 1960s)

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Founding African American Studies at UMass Amherst
clips.summary: Terry describes arriving at UMass Amherst with Michael Thelwell, Bernard W. Bell, and her future husband Eugene Terry. Sterling A. Brown at Howard University had been redirecting graduate students to Sidney Kaplan at UMass Amherst, who had helped found The Massachusetts Review. The 1959 first issue had been dedicated to the A&T Four. Terry helped build the African American Studies department, supported by a multiracial coalition.
related_events: UMass Amherst W. E. B. Du Bois Department founding (late 1960s-early 1970s)
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party at 1964 Democratic Convention in Atlantic City

clips.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
clips.title: Return as Provost and a Charge to Students
clips.summary: Terry reflects on her 2009 return to Bennett College as Provost. She tells current students that the segregation signs are down but that economic injustice remains, and that each generation runs only its own lap in the relay. She charges Bennett women with carrying social justice forward alongside whatever discipline they pursue.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Delta Origins and Family Land
clips.summary: Simpson describes her birth in Itta Bena, Mississippi as the youngest of seven, her father's work as a butcher and her parents' sharecropping years between Greenwood and a plantation. She recounts her mother's family in Mound Bayou, her great-grandfather Jeff Davis Pearson who attended Tougaloo College in the first generation after emancipation, and how the family lost their land in the 1920s-30s.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Schooling in the Delta
clips.summary: Simpson recounts attending Ellis Rogers Elementary on the Mississippi Valley State University campus, then seventh through ninth grade in Itta Bena, and tenth grade at the newly consolidated Amanda Elzy High School built to bus Black students from across the county. The split session calendar allowed students to chop and pick cotton.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Emmett Till and Reverend George Lee
clips.summary: Simpson, age nine when Emmett Till was lynched in 1955, describes seeing the open-casket photograph in Jet magazine and her terror that any Black child could be next. She recounts her father's NAACP membership and his attendance at the funeral of Reverend George Lee, a voting-rights activist gunned down in Belzoni.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Racine, Wisconsin and Cultural Shock
clips.summary: At 14, Simpson moved to Racine, Wisconsin to live with her sister and attend Washington Park High School, where she encountered an integrated classroom for the first time. She joined the NAACP Youth Chapter there and tried to organize a Mississippi support trip. She returned to Mississippi for her senior year to graduate from Amanda Elzy in January 1963.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Movement Entry in Greenwood
clips.summary: Returning to Mississippi in January 1963, Simpson attended a SNCC mass meeting in Greenwood where she met Hollis Watkins, Lawrence Guyot, John O'Neal, Mary Lane, and her lifelong friend June Johnson. She describes her father calling Guyot to put him personally in charge of her safety, and her own commitment to voter-registration canvassing in Greenwood and Itta Bena.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: SCLC Citizenship School in Charleston
clips.summary: Annelle Ponder of SCLC selected Simpson, Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and others for the two-week Citizenship School in Charleston, South Carolina. Simpson studied with Myles Horton of Highlander, Septima Clark, Esau Jenkins, and Guy and Candie Carawan, learning the rights and responsibilities of citizenship and how to conduct voter-registration workshops back home.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Arrest and Beatings in Winona
clips.summary: On the bus trip back from Charleston in June 1963, the group was arrested at the Winona, Mississippi bus station after using the white side of the waiting room. Simpson describes the jailhouse cell-by-cell beatings of Ponder, Johnson, Hamer, and herself with a leather strap, the trustees forced to administer them, and singing 'Walk With Me' through the night with Hamer to keep her conscious as she developed a fever from her injuries.
related_events: Winona, Mississippi arrest and beatings of June 1963

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Medgar Evers and the Oxford Trial
clips.summary: Andrew Young arrived to release the group from Winona on Wednesday and informed them that Medgar Evers had been assassinated the night before in Jackson. Simpson later testified at the federal trial in Oxford, Mississippi, where she was given only a few minutes on the stand and not permitted to describe what she had heard and seen happen to the others.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Freedom Vote and Summer Orientation
clips.summary: Simpson conducted Freedom Vote workshops in Clarksdale at the church of Aaron E. Henry, the MFDP candidate for governor, demonstrating mock voting procedures. She attended part of the Freedom Summer orientation in Oxford, Ohio in 1964 but left after a few days when her mother fell ill. Word arrived that James Chaney, Michael 'Mickey' Schwerner, and Andrew Goodman had disappeared in Meridian.
related_events: Disappearance of Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: COFO Office and the WATS Line
clips.summary: Through summer 1964 Simpson worked shifts on the WATS line at the COFO office in Jackson, a 24-hour staffed phone that field workers across Mississippi used to check in and report trouble. The office held SNCC, CORE, SCLC, and NAACP staff together; Simpson met Bob Moses, Dave Dennis, Dona Richards Moses, Gwen Gillon, Worth Long, and her future husband Jesse Moore there.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: MFDP at Atlantic City
clips.summary: Simpson rode the bus to Atlantic City for the 1964 Democratic National Convention, sharing delegation badges to rotate observers into the hall. She witnessed the LBJ press conference timed to interrupt Fannie Lou Hamer's televised testimony and the internal rift when Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray rejected the two-seats-at-large compromise. Simpson sided with the rural activists who refused the offer.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Tougaloo College Years
clips.summary: In fall 1964 Simpson enrolled at Tougaloo College on a Movement-arranged scholarship alongside Gwen Gillon, Hollis Watkins, and MacArthur Cotton. She recalls Dr. Naomi Townsend's strict English class, where Simpson's afro and slacks drew rebuke, and Dr. Ernst Borinski's internationally known forums. She married Jesse Moore in early 1965 and began raising five children, attending Tougaloo for two years.

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Career in Civil Rights and Community Development
clips.summary: Simpson worked as a legal secretary for the Lawyers' Committee for Civil Rights Under Law, then at Friends of Children of Mississippi Head Start under director Fred Mangram Thomas, then managed a Voice of Calvary health clinic in Mendenhall. At Delta State she ran three AmeriCorps programs, then served as a Mid-South Delta Initiative community coach for the Kellogg Foundation, leading Mississippi-Louisiana-Arkansas delegations on three trips to Ghana.
keywords: Lawyers Committee for Civil Rights Under Law

clips.subject: Euvester Simpson
clips.title: Reflections and Family
clips.summary: Simpson assesses Mississippi's surface and structural changes: residential integration and Black elected officials versus eroded tax bases, 90-percent-Black public schools, and the rise of Black-on-Black violence absent from her Delta childhood. She closes by introducing her five children, Earl, Orlando, Omar, T., and attorney Jessica, and her second husband Les.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Danville Childhood and Family
clips.summary: Hopkins describes his 1954 birth at Winslow Hospital, the segregated Black-only facility in Danville, Virginia. He details his mother Margaret Swanson Hopkins, his father Daniel Hopkins (Dan River Mills's first Black supervisor and founder of a Danville landscape business), and his maternal grandfather, who built houses on Bruce Street and physically moved a brick house from white West Main Street back to the Black neighborhood. Hopkins frames family complexion differences as a window into internalized racial hierarchy.
related_events: Black-owned construction and business in Danville Virginia

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: 1963 Danville Demonstrations
clips.summary: Hopkins reads from his memoir to describe the 1963 mass demonstrations in Danville against segregated buses, department stores, and the city library. He recalls wounded protesters staggering into High Street Baptist Church for first aid, fire hoses and dogs deployed by police and deputized garbage men, and arrests tried before Judge Archibald M. Aiken, who placed a pistol on the bench. After courts mandated library integration, officials removed all tables and chairs to prevent Black patrons from sitting alongside whites.
related_events: Mass arrests and trials in Danville 1963

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Integrating George Washington High
clips.summary: Hopkins describes his family's decision to enroll him in newly desegregated George Washington High School in Danville rather than the distant county school. He recounts being harassed, jostled, called slurs, and once attacked from behind, and reflects in retrospect that the experience qualifies as racial bullying. He frames that period as foundational to his later rage and resistance.
related_events: Token integration of formerly white high schools

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Discovering the Panthers
clips.summary: On a 1970 trip to Washington D.C. Hopkins encountered the Black Panther Party newspaper through a cousin who sold it. He had already been wearing dashikis and an afro as part of the cultural nationalist movement, but the BPP offered him both racial and class analysis. He describes the fear permeating the Black community under Ku Klux Klan terrorism and the family practice of keeping window drapes closed.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Student Walkout at GW High
clips.summary: Hopkins recounts organizing a 1970 walkout at George Washington High School to protest the absorption of historically Black John M. Langston High School into a middle school and to demand African American studies and a new school name. The walkout was leaked a day early, drew about 150 students including white participants, and resulted in Hopkins and other organizers being suspended for three days. He links the absence of police violence partly to the white students' presence and to the Kent State backdrop.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Winston-Salem Community Worker
clips.summary: Hopkins describes moving to Winston-Salem at 17 to live with the BPP chapter as a community worker, the membership classification used after Huey Newton shut down national membership and David Hilliard managed the party. He worked sixteen-to-seventeen-hour days selling newspapers and running the free breakfast program. He recalls the dialectical materialism textbooks and the philosophical training in the party's socialist framework.
related_events: BPP membership freeze under David Hilliard
Survival programs serving the Black community

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Newton Return and Cleaver Split
clips.summary: Hopkins recounts joining the party in 1971 after Newton's prison release and the split with Eldridge Cleaver, whose New York chapter and international faction pushed armed struggle while Newton called for putting the gun away and emphasizing community survival programs. Hopkins still privately romanticized armed struggle. He frames BPP self-defense in the context of COINTELPRO and roughly thirty Panther deaths by 1971-72, including George Jackson at San Quentin.
related_events: George Jackson death at San Quentin 1971

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Oakland Newspaper Cadre
clips.summary: In August 1972 Hopkins moved to Oakland and joined the BPP newspaper editorial cadre under editor-in-chief David Graham Du Bois, son of W.E.B. Du Bois and stepson via Shirley Graham Du Bois. He worked alongside artist Emory Douglas and covered Bobby Seale's mayoral campaign and Elaine Brown's City Council race as the writer-and-sound-man for campaign tape recordings. Hopkins met photographer Stephen Shames, singer Abbey Lincoln, and filmmaker Bert Schneider, who was shooting a Seale campaign documentary.
related_events: Elaine Brown Oakland City Council campaign

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Mentorship Under Du Bois
clips.summary: Hopkins details how David Graham Du Bois, who had edited the English-language daily in Cairo for twelve years, taught him the fundamentals of journalism and the sensibilities of literary craft. Du Bois was working on a novel, And Bid Him Sing, set in Cairo around Malcolm X's hajj. Hopkins frames Du Bois as the mentor who shaped his cinematic eye, his sense of style, and his understanding of art as a vehicle for political truth.
related_events: David Graham Du Bois editorship of BPP newspaper
Publication of And Bid Him Sing

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Party Crumbling 1973-74
clips.summary: After Seale's 1973 mayoral loss in the runoff, the party began to unravel. Hopkins describes the disciplinary use of floggings, the climate of fear inside the organization, Huey Newton's increasing use of cocaine and cognac, Newton's 1977 pistol-whipping of his tailor, and Newton's later flight to Cuba after shooting a woman. Hopkins recounts stealing a car to fund bringing his pregnant fiancee from Winston-Salem to California, being disciplined within the party, and leaving Oakland the same weekend Newton fled to Cuba.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Women in the Party
clips.summary: Hopkins reflects on the BPP's stated ideal of gender equality, the estimated forty-eight percent female membership in Oakland, and women's positions of leadership on the Central Committee. He frames Black women's broader role in the civil rights movement in terms of educational attainment and exposure to white households as a route to cultural enrichment. He briefly assesses Bobby Seale's character as organizer, jazz drummer, stand-up comedian, and sheet-metal mechanic.

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: COINTELPRO and Mass Incarceration
clips.summary: Hopkins describes his post-Oakland turn toward armed robbery, a ten-year sentence, and a subsequent life sentence handed down by an all-white Danville jury for a robbery in which no one was injured. He analyzes the drug war and the cultural shift from Panther-era resistance to gang-versus-gang violence, framed via the documentary Bastards of the Party. He situates mass incarceration as a successor civil rights issue.
related_events: Hopkins's bank robbery conviction and ten-year sentence
Hopkins's life sentence in Danville Virginia

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: Heritage and Maggie L. Walker
clips.summary: Hopkins discusses cultural justice alongside economic justice, the redistribution-of-wealth ideal he absorbed in the party, and the Tea Party movement's pull on working-class whites. He describes the Maggie L. Walker Governor's School in Richmond, which now occupies the formerly segregated Black high school named for the Richmond banker who founded the Independent Order of Saint Luke and the oldest surviving Black bank. He is writing a play and possible film about Walker.
related_events: Maggie L. Walker founding of Black bank Richmond Virginia
Maggie L. Walker Governor's School Richmond

clips.subject: Evans Derrell Hopkins
clips.title: COINTELPRO Personal Targeting
clips.summary: Hopkins describes how COINTELPRO seeded distrust inside the BPP via poison-pen letters, helped imprison Elmer 'Geronimo' Pratt, and split Eldridge Cleaver from Bobby Seale. He recounts learning that COINTELPRO operatives went to Winston-Salem State University and blocked the scholarship he was up for there, citing him as a Panther. He frames the BPP as the philosophical extension of Malcolm X and closes on the power of love within the party as its enduring legacy.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Pocahontas Childhood
clips.summary: Born into a sharecropping family in Pocahontas, Mississippi, raised by her mother, grandparents, and great-grandparents after her parents separated. She attended church schools through fifth grade before busing to a Clinton district school, where seasonal field labor first surfaced her awareness of being Black and poor.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Move to Jackson and Quilting Frame Stories
clips.summary: Around age 12 her mother, a domestic worker, moved the family to Jackson and eventually built a small business. Adams-Johnson absorbed Black women's accounts of white households from sitting under the quilting frame as a child, narratives she later recognized in the film The Help.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Entry to the NAACP Through Cleveland Donald Jr.
clips.summary: At Sam M. Brinkley High School, Cold War civics lessons on 'communism' provoked her curiosity. Her god-brother Cleveland Donald Jr., whose father was an NAACP officer in Jackson, brought her to NAACP meetings at his father's house starting around 1962, where she first met Medgar Evers.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Mass Meetings: Hamer, Devine, Gray
clips.summary: At Jackson mass meetings Adams-Johnson encountered Fannie Lou Hamer, Annie Devine, and Victoria Gray. Hamer's singing and the three women's bearing modeled an unfamiliar kind of Black female authority. She specifies that Hamer's congressional challenge was later, around 1964.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Woolworth's Sit-In and May 28, 1963 Student March
clips.summary: Pearlena Lewis, a neighbor and North Jackson Youth Council leader, was among the Tougaloo students attacked at the Woolworth's lunch counter sit-in with Anne Moody and John Salter. Three days later Adams-Johnson joined the Medgar Evers-organized walkout from Sam M. Brinkley, Jim Hill, and Lanier High Schools, which was halted within a quarter mile by police trucks and garbage trucks.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Fairgrounds Detention as 'Concentration Camp'
clips.summary: Hundreds of arrested students were trucked to the Mississippi State Fairgrounds livestock pens, surrounded by armed police. Adams-Johnson attributes the 'concentration camp' framing of the detention directly to Medgar Evers's own vocabulary, recalling him liken it to a concentration camp while reflecting on the experience. Boys formed protective circles around the girls through the night.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Summer 1963 Marches and Police Violence
clips.summary: After Medgar Evers's June 12 assassination, daily marches from the Jackson Masonic Temple became her 'summer job.' At the post-assassination march she was struck across the back by a police baton while reaching for a writing pad, an incident she identifies as her break with nonviolent discipline because she had been raised to defy a tyrannical grandfather but could not strike back at a white officer.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Jesse Harris, Nonviolence Training, and SNCC
clips.summary: After Evers's death, young Jackson activists trained with Jesse Harris, who functioned as a SNCC contact for Jackson youth while most SNCC field activity ran from McComb and Greenwood through the COFO office. Adams-Johnson describes Medgar Evers as a father figure who had pleaded with older organizers to bail young people out of jail.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Family Reactions and the Meridian Bus Mob
clips.summary: Her mother and her employer warned the family against marching. Adams-Johnson attended the August 1963 March on Washington as her one out-of-state trip; on the return through Meridian, a group of white motorcycle thugs and a coordinated mob attacked the buses after the activists used the white facilities for the first time. Ministers physically intervened to prevent the young people from fighting back.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Tougaloo, SNCC Voter Canvass, and Benjamin Brown
clips.summary: She entered Tougaloo on a music scholarship in summer 1964 via the pre-freshman program, where Annie Devine's daughter was her classmate. She joined Stokely Carmichael on a Greenwood voter-registration trip and was expelled from the pre-freshman program by director Mrs./Dr. Towns for bringing her white tutor friends home. She names the Borinskis as progressive Tougaloo faculty against more conservative administrators including President George A. Owens. The 1967 Lynch Street killing of Benjamin Brown by the National Guard, after activists had been told the Guard would not fire unless fired upon, prompted her mother to send her to White Plains, New York.
related_events: May 1967 Jackson State Lynch Street unrest

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: From Harlem Streets to BPP Officer of the Day
clips.summary: In White Plains and Manhattan she first met cultural nationalists including Amiri Baraka's wife, then encountered Black Panther Party members on 125th Street and Seventh Avenue in spring 1968. With her sister she set up a White Plains BPP branch and later became Officer of the Day in the Bronx and Brooklyn, running a liberation school out of a daycare center, free-breakfast canvassing, and tenants' organizing against urban renewal displacement.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: BPP Gender Politics, Marriage, and Family
clips.summary: She describes New York BPP women as more empowered than the California chapter, where visiting California men brought a 'socialistic sex' attitude the New York women rejected. Persistent chauvinism remained, including pressure on women to be both 'guerrilla' and mother on the Algerian revolution model. She married a Panther man, watched many marriages form because Panther men were locked up, and was later called 'bourgeois' by a fellow Panther for putting her children first.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Four Years Underground
clips.summary: After the 1971 BPP split she briefly returned to Mississippi but felt she was bringing COINTELPRO surveillance home. A 1971 incident set her on the run; her youngest daughter was born underground in a hideout maintained by white women supporters. She lived underground for four years, eventually surrendered with a lawyer's help, and would have received two years' probation except that her husband's larger case extended hers to five years.

clips.subject: Frankye Adams-Johnson
clips.title: Tougaloo Archive Work and Advice to Young People
clips.summary: Returning to Mississippi she worked as an archivist organizing President George A. Owens's papers at Tougaloo, discovering a rich civil-disobedience collection kept by the publicly conservative president. She closes by urging young people to stand for something, to study the movement in its 'good, bad, and ugly' detail rather than romanticize it, and to avoid blindly following.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Greenwood Family Roots
clips.summary: Greene introduces her 1945 birth in Greenwood, Mississippi, her mother's upbringing in the same Howard Street house owned by her great-grandmother, and her father's farming background. She describes her father's house-painting business, her mother's training as a teacher at Tougaloo, and the social-security planning that would later support her father when movement involvement cost him work.
related_events: Black landownership in the Mississippi Delta

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Segregated Greenwood
clips.summary: Greene describes growing up in a fully segregated Greenwood, segregated schools using hand-me-down textbooks, no integration until 1970, and the spatial separation between the Black community on Howard Street, the antebellum white North Greenwood, the Jewish community, and Chinese-owned grocery stores. She recounts her father's protective rules after Emmett Till's body passed through a Greenwood funeral home.
related_events: Brown v. Board (post-1954 Mississippi segregation maintenance)

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Father's Protective Discipline
clips.summary: Greene describes how her father's caution about white-controlled bootlegging networks and the informant economy shaped the family's safety practices. She contrasts her parents' late-1950s voter registration, done quietly, without actually voting, with the public organizing that would follow.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Entering the Movement
clips.summary: In 1962 Greene's brother Dwight, returning from the Navy, applied to be the second Black student at Ole Miss and was summoned with their father to a meeting with the Greenwood mayor demanding the application be withdrawn. He went to work for the Mississippi Free Press in Jackson, where he met Robert Parris Moses, Sam Block, Willie Peacock, Reggie Robinson, Medgar Evers, and Amzie Moore. Sam Block and Willie Peacock arrived in Greenwood that summer, and Greene began attending SNCC mass meetings as a high school student.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Leflore Food Cutoff and Distribution
clips.summary: Greene describes the 1962 Leflore County, Mississippi termination of federal surplus commodities as retaliation against the voter registration drive, coinciding with the first season the mechanical cotton picker reduced Delta wages. She helped distribute the powdered milk, peanut butter, and grain that Dick Gregory airlifted and Ivanhoe Donaldson trucked in, working out of Wesley Methodist Church two blocks from her home.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: SNCC Workers and New Horizons
clips.summary: Greene recalls the SNCC field staff, Stokely Carmichael speaking of Trinidad and New York, Eleanor Holmes at Yale Law School, Tim Jenkins, opening a window onto a world beyond segregated Greenwood. She names the local supporters: June Johnson's mother, the Silas McGee family farm outside Greenwood, Reverend Aaron Johnson of the renamed First Christian Church, Reverend Sterling Tucker, and her own family. She describes how most Black teachers stayed out of the movement out of fear of losing their jobs.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Shotgun Attack and Going to College
clips.summary: In March 1963, after her brother George attended a SNCC office meeting in Greenwood, a car followed him home and someone fired a shotgun blast through the front of the family house. Her father interpreted the attack as confirmation that the family had to keep organizing. Greene left for Dillard University in New Orleans in September 1963, traveling by train from Winona, the only way she had ever seen the campus.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Pete Seeger and the McGee Farm
clips.summary: Greene recalls the SNCC-organized concerts on the McGee family farm outside Greenwood featuring Pete Seeger and the Freedom Singers, and the Free Southern Theater's traveling plays across Mississippi. She frames these as cultural enrichment that black Greenwood schools never offered and that the segregated public library blocked her from accessing.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Dillard and Tulane Connection
clips.summary: Greene describes the difficulty of her freshman year at Dillard, where no student movement organization existed, while the Birmingham church bombing and Medgar Evers's assassination unfolded back home. She connected with Cathy Cade or Casey Hayden at the Tulane Newman Center, navigating the strict in loco parentis rules that required parental written permission for any car ride off campus.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: McComb Summer Project 1964
clips.summary: Greene joined the 1964 Mississippi Summer Project, traveling to the Oxford, Ohio orientation and then with the Southwest Mississippi contingent to Washington to lobby congressional representatives for federal protection. The Schwerner-Chaney-Goodman disappearance reached the orientation while she was in DC. She went to McComb, Mississippi to work on voter canvassing and Freedom Schools alongside her brother George; eleven volunteers shared a freedom house that staff voted to expand against George's objection, and that night the house was bombed with dynamite at the garage door, injuring Curtis Hayes with glass fragments.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: The Heffners and McComb Isolation
clips.summary: Greene recounts how nine Black churches were burned in the McComb area that summer with no white community protest. She corrects the public version of the Heffner story: an Oregon minister-volunteer who knew Mr. Heffner from military service, paired with Stanford student Dennis Sweeney to pass as father and son, visited the Heffners, leading to the family's social ostracism and eventual departure from McComb. No white voices in McComb spoke out against the bombings.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: MFDP and Atlantic City
clips.summary: Greene canvassed and ran the precinct meetings that selected the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party delegates, then traveled to the Atlantic City Democratic Convention with the delegation. She believed the delegates would seat the MFDP. Her takeaway from the rejection: Mississippi could maintain segregation only because the rest of the country did not care, Fannie Lou Hamer's televised testimony made the national stakes visible.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Jackson Fairground Detention
clips.summary: Greene returned for a difficult second year at Dillard, then left college after her sophomore year to work full-time on voter registration in Jackson. During the summer 1965 Jackson mass demonstrations, she was arrested and held a week at the Mississippi state fairground, which had been converted into a detention site. She describes the concrete floor sleeping conditions, the tin-tray meal line of syrup and light bread for breakfast and grits for lunch, and the work of older field staff trying to maintain morale among hundreds of arrestees as days stretched on with no bail-out date.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Atlanta SNCC Office
clips.summary: James Forman recruited Greene to the Atlanta SNCC headquarters, where she rotated from the switchboard into finance work alongside SNCC bookkeeper Chessie Johnson and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson. Greene describes Forman's organizational philosophy of cycling field staff through the office and vice versa to build mutual understanding, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson's mentorship pushing her to learn finance and skills beyond her Mississippi exposure. Her brother George stayed in the field, working in the Lowndes County, Alabama and Greene County, Alabama projects.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Black Power and SNCC's Funding Collapse
clips.summary: Greene describes how the 1966 Meredith March Against Fear call for Black Power triggered an immediate and sustained drop in SNCC donations and a hostile media response. She situates the staff decision that white organizers should work in white communities while Black organizers worked in Black communities, characterizing the policy as more nuanced than the press portrayed. She left SNCC in June 1968, two months after King's assassination, as the organization's resources collapsed.

clips.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
clips.title: Stokely Carmichael as Family
