He recounts demonstrations against the California Real Estate Association and a previous Woolworths sit-in led by Dr. Jack Kimbrough.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Bank of America and SDG&E Campaigns
clips.summary: As chairman of CORE's Western Region (California, Oregon, Washington, and Colorado), Brown led statewide direct action against Bank of America and San Diego Gas & Electric to open employment to Black workers. Tactics included marches, sit-ins, mail-ins (mailing payments above or below balance owed), and coin-ins (paying in pennies at teller windows while others sang on the floor). Brown was jailed for trespassing, serving sentences on weekends while continuing to teach. He was summoned to Sacramento to defend his teaching credential.
related_events: CORE Western Region direct action campaigns

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: CORE Membership and Internal Conflict
clips.summary: Brown notes that San Diego CORE membership was approximately two-thirds white during his tenure. He describes the difficulty of recruiting Black members, attributing fear of reprisals. He details internal conflict with the peace movement and Trotskyites attempting to influence CORE's direction, accusations of communism, and what he describes as an organized effort to remove him from leadership. He resigned in 1967.
related_events: FBI infiltration of civil rights organizations

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Peace Corps in Lesotho
clips.summary: Brown describes his year as Peace Corps deputy director in Lesotho, beginning with a layover in Johannesburg where he and his wife were confined to a Blacks-only room. He recounts King Moshoeshoe II's house arrest for opposing apartheid, Prime Minister Chief Leabua Jonathan's accommodation with the regime, and the surprise of Basotho leaders when white Peace Corps volunteers arrived. He held a memorial service at an AME church in Lesotho after Martin Luther King's assassination, attended by King Moshoeshoe II. Brown returned to the United States following King's death.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Floyd McKissick Enterprises and Return
clips.summary: Unable to find a position in San Diego after Peace Corps service, Brown worked with Floyd McKissick at McKissick Enterprises in Harlem and then at Marine Midland Bank on Wall Street as a loan officer. He returned to San Diego in September 1971 when an old acquaintance recruited him as assistant to the president of San Diego State to organize what became the African American Studies program.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: African American Studies and Administrative Career
clips.summary: Brown became San Diego State's first Black administrator, hired faculty, and established a major and minor in what is now Africana Studies, securing university approval for the courses to fulfill general education requirements. He later directed campus computer services, then served 26 years as associate dean of the College of Business Administration, founding the Community Economic Development certificate program before retiring in 2004 to pursue real estate development.

clips.subject: Harold K. Brown
clips.title: Black Economic Development and Reflections
clips.summary: Brown founded the Black Economic Development Task Force in 1987 and Community Leaders Undoing Biases (CLUB) to address what he identifies as the unfinished work of the civil rights movement: economic self-determination and capital formation. He critiques the movement's omission of economic education, notes Tony Brown's KPBS programming on Black business, and reflects on what he describes as post-traumatic stress carried by civil rights veterans. He calls for Black studies to be marketed across the university curriculum.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Path to the LDF: Gellhorn's Legal Survey
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts arriving at Columbia Law School with no civil rights career in mind, taking Professor Walter Gellhorn's seminar 'Legal Survey' (a euphemism chosen to avoid faculty objections in the McCarthy era), and working on actual cases for the few civil-liberties and civil-rights lawyers then practicing. He took the seminar for four semesters and became chair of the student body enrolled in it.
related_events: Walter Gellhorn's congressional testimony on Communist Party membership
Origins of clinical legal education at Columbia

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Joining LDF in 1949
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the size of the civil rights bar in the late 1940s: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund had three or four lawyers; the ACLU had one; three Jewish organizations had one apiece. After Marian Wynne Perry left LDF to take a New York State position, Walter Gellhorn recommended Greenberg, and Thurgood Marshall hired him in March 1949.
keywords: NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund
related_events: Greenberg's March 1949 hire by Thurgood Marshall
Marian Wynne Perry's transition to New York State

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: The Marshall Team and LDF Independence
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the LDF staff he joined: Thurgood Marshall as Director-Counsel after the restrictive-covenant and voting-rights victories, Robert L. Carter as first assistant, Franklin H. Williams (later ambassador to Ghana and head of the Phelps Stokes Foundation), and a staff sociologist. He explains LDF's separation from the parent NAACP in 1957 for tax-exemption reasons and the resulting tensions with Roy Wilkins.
related_events: Franklin H. Williams' ambassadorship to Ghana

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Trying the Kansas Component of Brown
clips.summary: Greenberg and Robert L. Carter tried the Kansas school segregation statute case in Topeka. He explains the dual evidentiary strategy: documenting measurable inequalities in Black versus white schools (books, teachers' salaries, square footage, gymnasia, laboratories) while also urging the court, through expert witnesses, to find that segregation itself constituted inequality.
related_events: 1951 Topeka trial of Brown v. Board of Education

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Brown Decision and Southern Reaction
clips.summary: Greenberg recalls the long path from the 1950-51 argument through the 1952 Supreme Court appeal and two reargument cycles to the May 1954 decision. He describes the stunned reaction at LDF and the recognition that Brown swept away all state-imposed segregation, far broader than school segregation alone. He distinguishes the earlier higher-education cases (involving one or two plaintiffs) from school integration's mass-numbers implications, including the segregationist taboo against mixed-sex contact in integrated schools.
related_events: May 17, 1954 Brown v. Board of Education decision
Late-1950s Southern resistance to school desegregation

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Sit-In Defense and Race Relations in American Law
clips.summary: As Director-Counsel from 1961, Greenberg led LDF's representation of approximately 3,500 sit-in defendants, securing acquittals in all of them. He also discusses his 1959 book Race Relations and American Law, the first treatise on civil rights law since approximately 1919. The Columbia Law Review published paired pro and anti reviews, an unusual treatment Greenberg attributes to the topic's perceived controversy.
related_events: 1960 lunch-counter sit-ins beginning in North Carolina
1959 publication of Race Relations and American Law

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Critics: Herbert Wechsler and Edmond Cahn
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts the academic critique of Brown v. Board of Education by Herbert Wechsler in 'Toward Neutral Principles of Constitutional Law' and by NYU legal philosopher Edmond Cahn, who attacked Brown's use of social-science evidence. When Greenberg asked Cahn to review his book, Cahn declined on conflict-of-interest grounds related to his son's interracial marriage.
related_events: Wechsler's 1959 'Toward Neutral Principles of Constitutional Law'
Cahn's critique of Brown's social-science footnote

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Airport Desegregation and the Tureaud Story
clips.summary: Greenberg discusses the segregation of railroad and airport facilities into the 1960s, including at federal facilities north of the Mason-Dixon line. He recounts Coke v. City of Atlanta (desegregating the Atlanta airport in the home city of Coca-Cola) and his case against the New Orleans airport with co-counsel A.P. Tureaud, then the only Black lawyer in New Orleans. After winning, Tureaud took his granddaughter to the airport so she could buy an ice cream cone for the first time.
related_events: Coke v. City of Atlanta airport desegregation case

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Cooperating Attorneys Network
clips.summary: Greenberg describes the 1963 program he launched to seed Black legal practices in Southern cities that had no Black lawyers. With foundation funding, LDF gave each new attorney $10,000 to buy a typewriter, a rug, and law books. The program produced Julius Chambers in North Carolina, Mel Watt (later a Charlotte congressman), and Marian Wright Edelman, who set up practice in Columbus, Georgia. Greenberg names another beneficiary who became a Georgia congressman from Columbus.
related_events: 1963 LDF launch of Southern cooperating-attorneys grant program
Marian Wright Edelman's Columbus Georgia practice

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Civil Rights Act of 1964: Public Accommodations and Employment
clips.summary: Greenberg recounts LDF's litigation under the Civil Rights Act of 1964: public accommodations (restaurants, theaters, hotels) came easily because businesses wanted the trade, although some hotels created sham private clubs to evade the law. Title VII employment cases proved harder because labor unions, while nationally pro-civil-rights, maintained segregated white and Black locals at the local level. LDF brought some of its 'bitterest cases' against the labor unions.

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Alexander v. Holmes and the Common-Law Certiorari Writ
clips.summary: Greenberg narrates the procedural maneuver in Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education. After Senator John C. Stennis blocked the armed services appropriation bill to pressure Nixon, HEW Secretary Robert H. Finch asked the Fifth Circuit to remand the desegregation cases without ruling. LDF could not appeal because there was no final judgment, so Greenberg invoked the ancient common-law writ of certiorari (distinct from the statutory certiorari) to get the Supreme Court to take the case. The Court agreed and ruled with LDF, ordering immediate desegregation.
keywords: Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education
related_events: 1969 Alexander v. Holmes County Board of Education decision

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Swann and Burger's Vote Switch
clips.summary: Greenberg describes Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education (1971) as the first substantive case heard under Chief Justice Warren Burger, who wrote an opinion calling for quotas, busing, and teacher integration. Following the argument, the preliminary conference vote favored upholding the lower court against LDF. As conferences continued, Burger found himself outvoted and switched his vote to assign the opinion to himself. Greenberg recounts being told this by Chief Justice Earl Warren, who used the phrase 'there he stood naked.' Burger included a paragraph signaling that desegregation requirements would eventually end, a line that proved consequential 10-15 years later.
keywords: Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education
related_events: 1971 Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg Board of Education decision
Burger Court's first substantive desegregation ruling

clips.subject: Jack Greenberg
clips.title: Milliken and the Retreat from Metropolitan Remedies
clips.summary: Greenberg discusses Milliken v. Bradley (1974) and San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez (1973) as turning points marking the Court's retreat from school-desegregation enforcement. Milliken made political boundaries between city and suburbs determinative for desegregation remedies, separating families living a short distance apart by school-system jurisdiction. Greenberg characterizes the decision as 'really a political decision' rather than a doctrinal one. The interview is cut off mid-discussion of Milliken.
keywords: San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez
related_events: 1973 San Antonio Independent School District v. Rodriguez decision

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Williamsville Farm Roots
clips.summary: Jones describes his family of ten children in Williamsville, Arkansas, born to Ernest Jones (who bought his first acre for $1.25 at age 13) and Verdis 'Vic' Jones. His father raised five Routin children from his mother's first marriage alongside five Jones children; the farm anchored on land inherited from German-American maternal grandfather Frank McKinney. Discusses 4 a.m. milking, the family's discipline around school attendance, and selling fruit and vegetables to finance cotton-corn operations because the Farmers Home Administration would not lend to Black farmers.
related_events: Black exclusion from Farmers Home Administration loans

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Mother's NAACP Activism
clips.summary: Jones recounts his mother Verdis 'Vic' Jones's NAACP organizing in Magnolia, Arkansas, where she would attend meetings and return to talk to church youth about community improvement. He describes her practice of loading neighborhood children into a pickup truck to take them to meetings without asking parental permission, and her foundational counsel about never going through the back door.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Brown v. Board on the Radio
clips.summary: Jones describes his father gathering the family and notifying neighbors and teachers about Thurgood Marshall's radio broadcast asking 'every Negro in America to stand still' at noon on the day the Supreme Court would announce Brown v. Board of Education. Notes that Williamsville could pick up St. Louis radio broadcasts; his father went door-to-door at his elementary school to ensure everyone heard the announcement.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education 1954 announcement

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Armed Self-Defense at the Cotton Gin
clips.summary: Jones describes how his family weighed their own cotton at the gin under the armed protection of his mother, father, Uncle Bluford, and Aunt T, who sat with double-barreled shotguns and shells between their fingers while other Black farmers were systematically short-weighted. Recounts his brother Troy's confrontation on a train passing through McNeil, Arkansas, where Black passengers were excluded; Troy refused to disembark and stabbed multiple white attackers before being shot. The family then armed themselves against a planned white retaliation that was averted in part by Williamsville store owner Mr. Chester Waters warning the local whites away.
related_events: McNeil Arkansas train assault on Troy Jones
averted white mob attack on Jones family farm

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Arrival at AM&N
clips.summary: Jones describes arriving at Arkansas AM&N College in Pine Bluff, his preparation by Oak Grove High School (an all-Black school district built in 1929 by Quaker or similar Northern foundation), and being welcomed by Dr. Hainan, a vocational school professor from his hometown, who introduced him to housing arranged by Dr. Marshall. Describes seeing urban segregation for the first time in Pine Bluff downtown.
related_events: arrival at AM&N College Pine Bluff

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Meeting Hansen and Worth Long
clips.summary: Jones describes meeting SNCC field secretaries Bill Hansen and Worth Long, who had come to Pine Bluff after working in Little Rock. Hansen, identifying himself as a white organizer who could not lead, asked Jones to take the front. Jones recounts Bill Hansen's question about how his parents would react to his arrest, and his certain answer that they would support him -- contrasted against Little Rock students whose families opposed their involvement. Discusses encountering Brian Warmac, a white Williamsville merchant who tried to persuade his mother to retrieve him from Pine Bluff.
related_events: SNCC arrival in Pine Bluff Arkansas

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Recruiting the Townsend Park Students
clips.summary: Jones describes being directed to recruit students from Townsend Park High School by a Pine Bluff principal who said the students were 'thugs' who could be turned. Jones met with them, explained nonviolent discipline, and despite their initial resistance to passive resistance, brought them into the movement. Discusses his reasoning that nonviolent discipline could expose the moral discomfort of white attackers -- describing seeing white youth crying as they struck the demonstrators.
related_events: recruitment of Townsend Park HS students into Pine Bluff SNCC

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Woolworth Sit-in with Diane Nash
clips.summary: Jones describes the February 1963 Pine Bluff Woolworth sit-in, where Diane Nash -- in town because she was light-skinned enough to enter ahead of the demonstrators and confirm the lunch counter was open -- coordinated the action. Nash's tactical advice from her Freedom Rides experience: fall toward the white patrons so they would be pinned on the floor too. Then describes Magnolia, Arkansas as the most violent of the early actions: the hotel owner locked them in and turned on tear gas, but Jones (warned by his brother Troy that water counteracts tear gas) directed everyone to the sink.

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: AM&N Expulsion and Community Support
clips.summary: Jones describes being suspended by Arkansas AM&N College President Dr. Lawrence A. Davis Sr. for the Woolworth action, along with Joanne State, a Pine Bluff student who later became an AM&N professor, and a tall student from Texarkana. Describes the Black community response: Reverend B.E. Allen of the Methodist church (likely from out of state) intervened at a hotel demonstration and opened his church to organizing meetings; Mr. Brown the Black funeral home owner bonded out arrestees over his son's objections; Dr. Dotton, the Pine Bluff Black dentist, offered free dental care to organizers injured in actions.
related_events: AM&N College suspension of SNCC student demonstrators
Reverend B.E. Allen hosting Pine Bluff SNCC organizing meetings

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Interracial Tensions and Project Directorship
clips.summary: Jones describes growing community resentment toward white SNCC organizers as perceived movement leaders, exacerbated by Bill Hansen's interracial marriage to Ruthie in Little Rock. Discusses meetings where Hansen would address the crowd and 'it would just die there,' contrasted with response to Black organizers. Recounts his May 16, 1964 marriage to a fellow expelled AM&N organizer, and the transition of the Arkansas SNCC project directorship to him in 1964, with Hansen moving to office coordination. Discusses Hansen's reluctance to relinquish the lead role.
related_events: 1964 transition of Arkansas SNCC directorship from Hansen to Jones
Jones's May 16 1964 marriage to fellow SNCC organizer

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Delta Voter Registration
clips.summary: Jones describes pivoting Arkansas SNCC operations toward voter registration in the eastern Arkansas Delta -- Forrest City, West Helena, Marianna, areas that were 90% Black with near-zero registered Black voters. Describes the organizing methodology taught to him by 'Mrs. Dear': enter a new community through the Black barbershop and locate the local matriarch. In Forrest City, Mrs. Clay -- the funeral home matriarch -- arranged housing and convened the meeting where the local cannery factory manager was recruited to run for office. Recounts directing James Forman that he didn't need staff, only the SNCC Freedom Singers for community meetings.
related_events: SNCC Freedom Singers visit to Pine Bluff

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Georgia Nursery and SNCC's End
clips.summary: Jones describes a Georgia community organizing case where, faced with women unable to take factory work at a nearby Army facility (he says 'Navy' but the only large military facility in Pine Bluff context is the Pine Bluff Arsenal -- speaker memory error) because of childcare needs, he convinced a local gambler-warehouse-owner to donate his warehouse and a lumber-company manager to donate boards for community-built nursery. Then describes his 1965 departure from Arkansas SNCC: declining movement energy, organizers needing to make a living, and the rise of Stokely Carmichael and H. Rap Brown which he characterizes as personal ambition seeking 'a feather in the cap' rather than benefit-focused organizing.
related_events: 1966 John Lewis ousting and Carmichael ascendancy

clips.subject: James Oscar Jones
clips.title: Federation of Southern Cooperatives
clips.summary: Jones describes his post-SNCC work with the Federation of Southern Cooperatives, identifying small Black farmers in Virginia, Georgia, Florida, and South Carolina and connecting them to national chain stores that wanted fresher, closer, cheaper product. Discusses tensions inside the cooperative around organizers who wanted to attack the Farmers Home Administration and redistribute money, versus his pragmatic focus on marketing channels. Reflects on agricultural decline as children of farmers moved to Northern cities, and his skepticism that reparations payments to descendants 75 years later would compensate the actual people who did the hard work.
related_events: Jones's work with Federation of Southern Cooperatives 1970s

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Montgomery Family and Bus Line
clips.summary: Jones describes growing up in Montgomery with her mother, grandmother, sister Doris Crenshaw, and younger brother John Alan McQuirter. She and her sister rode the Montgomery bus line during school, experienced bus-driver harassment from age six, and identifies that experience as the wellspring of her connection to the Montgomery Bus Boycott.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Rosa Parks and the NAACP Youth Council
clips.summary: Jones recounts joining the NAACP Youth Council with her sister as vice president, attending meetings at Rosa Parks's home where Parks read from National Office NAACP papers, taught the youth that voting was central to their lives, and emphasized carrying themselves responsibly. Parks served Kool-Aid after meetings.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: The 381 Days Walking
clips.summary: Jones, eleven when the boycott began, describes the youth's independent organization of cross-town walking routes, her sister's refusal to take rides because they would displace adult walkers, and the joy of the boycott's conclusion. She recalls testing segregation by sitting in the bus front and her child's fascination with touching a white woman's red hair.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Forming the Montgomery Gospel Trio
clips.summary: Jones traces the origin of the Montgomery Gospel Trio at Alabama State Laboratory High School Friday talent shows, where four girls including Gladys Carter and Minnie Hendricks McCans chose freedom songs as their repertoire. The group became regulars at Montgomery teas raising money for the movement and the churches.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Carnegie Hall and the First Album
clips.summary: Jones describes Reverend Solomon S. Seay Sr.'s influence at her home church, the trio's travel to New York to perform at a Carnegie Hall fundraiser organized in part by Reverend Sheldon, and the recording of one of the first civil rights movement albums. The naming of the Montgomery Gospel Trio occurred for that album.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Receiving the Freedom Riders
clips.summary: Jones describes meeting James Bevel and Bernard LaFayette Jr. before the Freedom Rides, then going to Reverend Ralph Abernathy's house where Mrs. Juanita Abernathy reported no available drivers. Jones, Antoinette Carson, and others took Carson's grandfather's car (marking the tire positions with chalk), picked up the beaten Freedom Riders, and returned the car undetected.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: The Church Under Siege and the Doodle Bird
clips.summary: Jones recounts the mass meeting at the Montgomery church the night the Freedom Riders arrived, when armed whites on horses surrounded the building and the congregation stayed inside overnight. She relays Reverend Solomon S. Seay Sr.'s "doodle bird" parable about backing into the sand to protect one's own as the stand-your-ground response.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Freedom Singers and Harambee Singers Origins
clips.summary: Jones clarifies she was not part of the original Freedom Singers but joined Bernice Johnson Reagon in Atlanta after the Montgomery Gospel Trio. She and Matti Casey Pierce knocked on Reagon's door to continue singing, founding the Harambee Singers from the Pinnett Festival collaboration between school and community.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Highlander and the We Are Not Afraid Verse
clips.summary: Jones describes the trio's Highlander Folk School visits at age fourteen with Guy Carawan, where exposure to other singers expanded their songwriting. She recounts "Ain't Gonna Ride No Buses No More" and the night police raided Highlander, cut the lights, and her introduction of the "We Are Not Afraid" verse to "We Shall Overcome" as the song that unnerved the shaking policeman.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: College Years, Voter Registration and Sit-Ins
clips.summary: Jones describes Alabama State University college years working with SCLC organizers James Bevel and James Orange on voter registration in Kings Hill and other rural areas, and on sit-ins at five-and-dime stores. She returns to her twelve-year-old voter-registration training under Rosa Parks and Mrs. Williams, learning the 27-page questionnaire and the Preamble to the Constitution to teach adults at their homes.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Harambee Singers and Pan-Africanism
clips.summary: Jones describes the Black Power and Pan-Africanist turn in her work around 1966, including the Harambee Singers' radical repertoire ("We'll Move On Over You," "Hands Off Nkrumah") and references to the CIA in their songs. The group's repertoire never overlapped with the Montgomery Gospel Trio's; they wore Afros and addressed international as well as domestic struggles.

clips.subject: Jamila Jones
clips.title: Community Elders and the John Story
clips.summary: Jones names community figures who shaped her: Flora Grant at Atlanta Life Insurance Company, Sullivan Rogers among the first Black jockeys, and her storytelling aunt Besidee. She recounts Besidee's "the John" sharecropper-and-chicken-coop story which she later turned into her own song "Pry, boy," reflecting on how older people's impact sustained her movement work into her sixties.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Fairfield Origins and Father's Workshop
clips.summary: Lawson describes being born in 1946 in Fairfield, Alabama, a Birmingham suburb. Her father, born 1899 in Bullock County near Union Springs, left a 15-child farm family at age 15 for Birmingham, worked the coal mines, then built Lawson's Repair, a backyard mechanical-and-appliance shop. She recounts his self-taught tinkering, his cross-country travel including a much-discussed Birmingham-to-Canada drive on kerosene-converted fuel, and his insistence that she and her brothers learn motor-rewiring and basic repair work.
related_events: Tennessee Coal and Iron company-town strikes

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Segregated Fairfield and Community Library
clips.summary: Lawson describes the totally segregated geography of Fairfield and Birmingham, bus barriers, white-only signage, restaurants accessible only through back windows, libraries with seats removed to prevent integration. She recounts her family's travels to New York and Mexico as breaks from this confined world, and the community library that Mrs. Parham and other women organized in a room above Dr. Parham's pharmacy as a substitute for the closed public library.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: First Awareness of Race
clips.summary: Lawson recalls her earliest race consciousness around age five when she and Teresa Ragusa, a child from the Italian-American grocer family across the street, were told by relatives on both sides that they could no longer play together once they reached school age. She describes hearing adults discuss lynchings, including a Birmingham case of a Black man kidnapped and castrated, where his attackers' kerosene actually clotted the wound and saved his life, and her childhood knowledge of the Ku Klux Klan.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Miles College and Birmingham Black Community
clips.summary: Lawson describes Miles College as both a struggling historically-black institution and a community playground six blocks from her home. She recounts attending a community screening at Miles of the film 'Imitation of Life', banned from the segregated black Carver Theater, and the multi-generational discussion of passing that followed. She also describes the wealthier Black community on Center Street, including attorney Arthur Shores (whose home was repeatedly bombed) and the Angela Davis family.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Fairfield Industrial High School and Principal Oliver
clips.summary: Lawson describes the school's hand-me-down books and uniforms inherited from the white Fairfield High School, the Booker T. Washington industrial-education legacy embedded in its name, and the formative role of principal Imanu J. Oliver (also called E.J. Oliver). She describes Oliver running the school 'almost as if it was a private school' with strict standards and homilies including 'dare to struggle, dare to win.' Alumni reunions continue every even year on July 4th weekend.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Family Travels, Mississippi Stop, Arthur G. Gaston
clips.summary: Lawson recounts a nighttime Mississippi police stop on a westward family trip where her father deflected the officer by calling his children 'little cotton pickers', a subservience that wounded the children but defused the threat. She describes her father's political life in the Republican Party (opposed to the Dixiecrat-controlled Alabama Democrats), his reading of the Pittsburgh Courier and Chicago Defender, and his business partnership with Arthur G. Gaston, who later became one of the first Black millionaires and whose family welcomed Lawson's family to their pool.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Birmingham Demonstrations 1963
clips.summary: Lawson describes her growing awareness of the civil rights movement through the Montgomery bus boycott, the Freedom Rides arrival in Birmingham, and the 1963 SCLC demonstrations where Martin Luther King Jr. called for student participation. Principal Oliver locked the school doors to prevent her class from leaving; they defied him, walked to downtown Birmingham, and some classmates were arrested or hit by firehoses in Kelly Ingram Park. Lawson herself was not at that location during those events but participated in other demonstrations. Her mother had died by this time; her father initially sided with the principal but later joined her at mass meetings.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Hayden Planetarium and Sloan-Kettering Internships
clips.summary: Lawson describes summer 1963 and 1964 New York internships in cancer research at the Hayden Planetarium and Sloan-Kettering Memorial Center, arranged through her older half-sisters who worked there as lab technicians. She did blood-analysis lab work using a slide rule, then watched the protocol of discarding samples when a patient died. A Cornell dean among her mentors offered to help her enter Cornell's medical school. Both summers, she felt the conflict of being absent from major civil rights moments, the August 1963 March on Washington and the 1964 Freedom Summer, while in New York.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Tuskegee Entry and Gomillion Voting Rights Work
clips.summary: Lawson describes entering Tuskegee Institute on a full Gorgas Scholarship (earned via a statewide science-fair sound-wave demonstration) and quickly meeting Almena Lomax (mother of Michael Lomax) and Dr. Charles Gomillion, whose work on Macon County gerrymandering culminated in the Gomillion v. Lightfoot Supreme Court case. Working at the Tuskegee hospital lab, she quit when pressed to practice drawing blood on a woman in labor. She joined an honors program that let her take graduate-level Veterinary Medicine courses including animal parasitology, and met veterinarian Dr. Doris Mitchell, an Odetta friend with an imported sports car.
related_events: Gomillion v. Lightfoot Supreme Court case

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Mississippi 1964 Elections and SNCC Tension
clips.summary: Lawson describes volunteering for SNCC's November 1964 Mississippi voter registration push in Jackson, staying with Euvester Simpson, doing door-to-door canvassing. She recounts the campus tension at Tuskegee when Willie 'Mukasa' Ricks arrived as a SNCC representative and the local student group resisted external direction, while she increasingly identified with SNCC's broader strategic vision over the campus group's downtown-Tuskegee focus. She mentions her classmate Samuel Younge Jr.'s killing as occurring while she was still in school.
related_events: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Atlantic City challenge

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Selma-to-Montgomery and the Capitol Arrest
clips.summary: Lawson describes a Tuskegee student delegation marching with picket signs in front of the Alabama State Capitol in Montgomery during the Selma-to-Montgomery period. State troopers slapped billy clubs against their thighs while an unrobed group identified as Klan approached in cars. Troopers arrested all 40-50 demonstrators just before the Klan group reached them. Lawson and her cohort went limp per training and were taken to the Montgomery County jail for about five days, where conditions deteriorated and the group launched a hunger strike. William Kunstler arranged their release.

clips.subject: Jennifer Lawson
clips.title: Atlanta Arrest and Lowndes Freedom House
clips.summary: Lawson describes a Christmas-season arrest in Atlanta around 1967 with Karen Edmonds Spellman, Fay Bellamy Powell, and Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, by Black officers on Auburn Avenue, illustrating intra-community policing dynamics of the Black Power era, with Howard Moore Jr. handling the legal response. She describes leaving school in spring 1966 to work full-time with SNCC, first in Wilcox County (Gee's Bend's Pettway-plantation enclave), then living in the Lowndes County Freedom House, a Jackson-family shack where she slept in a sleeping bag near a pot-belly stove. Late-night conversations with Stokely Carmichael, Ralph Featherstone, Courtland Cox, Charlie Cobb, and Ivanhoe Donaldson engaged Frantz Fanon's writings and Featherstone's economic-empowerment vision, which later informed Mississippi catfish farming and the Fine Vines garment cooperative with Ed Brown and Charles Bannerman in Mississippi Action for Community Education. The transcript ends mid-sentence at 'I worked for...', the recorded interview was truncated before Lawson could complete her account of her subsequent SNCC and post-movement work.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Arlington Childhood and Georgia Roots
clips.summary: Mulholland describes being born in Washington, D.C. and raised in Arlington's Buckingham apartments among New York Jewish neighbors during the early 1940s. She introduces her father from southwest Iowa working at the Department of Labor and her Georgia-born mother, frames Arlington as segregated by law and custom, and describes annual summer visits to her grandmother in Ocoeenee, Georgia, a logging-camp company town in Wayne County.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Presbyterian Conscience and Secret Meetings
clips.summary: Mulholland recounts memorizing Bible verses and the Declaration of Independence, attending a segregated Presbyterian youth fellowship in Arlington that hosted secret integrated meetings with black YWCA students despite the American Nazi Party headquartered around the corner. She describes seeing the Niggertown school disparity in Georgia and a Spanish classmate displaced by Virginia's massive resistance school closures as formative motivations.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Duke and the Durham Sit-Ins
clips.summary: At Duke University as a 1959 freshman, Mulholland and roommate Lucia rejected sorority rush and joined the Durham sit-ins after North Carolina College students visited the Presbyterian chaplain's secret meeting. She was arrested twice, jailed under a plan exploiting North Carolina's 'others' racial category that attorney Floyd McKissick judged frivolous, and faced the Dean's locked-office demand that she telephone her segregationist mother.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: NAG, Arlington Sit-Ins, and Glen Echo
clips.summary: Mulholland dropped out of Duke, worked Capitol Hill jobs in Washington, and joined the Nonviolent Action Group at Howard, identified by NCC students as the lapsed founding-meeting affiliate. She describes integrated sit-ins at Drug Fair and Peoples in Cherrydale Arlington with American Nazi Party counter-pickets and the summer-long Glen Echo Amusement Park picket campaign supported by the Bannockburn, MD Jewish cooperative community.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Rock Hill Jail-In and Freedom Rides Origin
clips.summary: Mulholland traveled with NAG to Rock Hill, South Carolina in support of the jail-in led by Reverend C.A. Ivory, where Frank Holloway was incarcerated and reporting for the Afro-American. She traces the Freedom Ride concept to a Greyhound conversation between Tom Gaither and Diane Nash after Rock Hill, the April SNCC Charlotte meeting calling for testing the December 1960 Supreme Court ruling, and the May 1961 Aniston bus burning that triggered NAG and Nashville deployment.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Freedom Ride to Jackson and Hinds County Jail
clips.summary: Mulholland flew to New Orleans with a NAG Freedom Rider cohort, took the train to Jackson, and was arrested by Jackson Police Captain J.L. Ray on breach-of-peace charges under the Kennedy-Mississippi bargain to avoid Alabama-style violence. She describes the scripted trial, the overcrowded Hinds County women's cell where she had more in common with Southern black inmates than Northern white riders, and the Clarie Collins Harvey-founded Women Power Unlimited's church-cell supply network.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Parchman Penitentiary
clips.summary: Transferred to Parchman's death row, Mulholland describes the intake vaginal exam by a matron and a friend's 45-year scent-triggered memory of it, the segregated cell-by-cell layout that prevented seeing other riders, Rabbi Perry Nussbaum's weekly Hebrew-prayer news smuggling that she preferred over the visiting Catholic seminarians, attorneys Jack H. Young Sr. and Carsie Hall barred from bringing William Kunstler, and her decision to serve out the two-month sentence rather than bail to fill the jails.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Tougaloo College Integration
clips.summary: Mulholland enrolled at Tougaloo College in fall 1961, applying in reaction to Charlayne Hunter-Gault and Hamilton E. Holmes facing riots at Georgia. She describes Tougaloo's pre-segregation charter, Charlotte Phillips as the first white student preceding her, the dormitory girl who mistook her for a ghost, the AKA member's eventual acceptance, and her co-chairing of the campus NAACP nonviolent agitation group with Joyce Ladner.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Jackson Woolworth's Sit-In
clips.summary: On May 28, 1963 Mulholland and dorm mother Lois Chaffee began as picket-line spotters but joined Pearlena Lewis, Anne Moody, and Memphis Norman after the picket arrest. She describes Norman pulled off the stool and stomped, John R. Salter Jr. attacked with brass knuckles and mustard, three hours of mob violence with police laughing outside, Fred Blackwell's iconic photograph, Tougaloo president Adam Daniel Beittel sitting at the counter to negotiate safe passage, and the Supreme Court precedent (citing her Durham case) that prevented police from entering the store.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: March on Washington and Birmingham Bombing
clips.summary: Mulholland worked in the March on Washington press office that summer, expecting federal intervention to prevent the buses arriving and merchants boarding downtown windows against the American Nazi Party across the river. She camped on the return trip with Anne Moody and Edwin King along the Blue Ridge, attended the 16th Street Baptist Church funerals in Birmingham where she picked up glass shards and shotgun shells, and identifies that day as the worst in the movement.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Galloway Church Visits and Canton Attack
clips.summary: Mulholland describes the integrated church-visit campaign at Galloway Memorial Methodist Church, the resignation of Reverend W.B. Selah, and the post-graduation June 1964 attack near Canton on a carload of Edwin King, Hamid Kizilbash, and herself returning from a mass meeting. She identifies the attack as a planned Klan murder to stop Freedom Summer, deflected when Kizilbash was identified as Pakistani and the lead attacker pulled the others off, sending the violence instead toward Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney days later.

clips.subject: Joan Trumpauer Mulholland
clips.title: Meredith March, Stokely, and Five Decades After
clips.summary: Mulholland returned for the 1966 Meredith March, picking it up at Tougaloo and slipping the first printed 'Black Power' flyers into her New Testament. She describes Dean Branch's Tougaloo house hosting the Marlon Brando breakfast and Stokely Carmichael kneeling at a 1970s Smithsonian event to greet her young twin sons. She closes summarizing her menial post-graduation jobs, raising five sons, 30 years as a classroom teaching assistant, and her son Loki Mulholland's documentary 'An Ordinary Hero.'

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Jean's Quaker Formation
clips.summary: Jean Rosenberg describes her Philadelphia birth, adoption by a transplanted New York couple, family conversion to Quakerism at Abington meeting, and Quaker influence on her commitment to consensus decision-making and social action. She recounts her high school refusal to participate in air raid drills at Abington High School, being called a 'Communist dupe' by the principal, and being seated by the plate glass window during drills.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Earlham and Entry to the Civil Rights Division
clips.summary: Jean attends Wilmington College, transfers to Earlham College in Richmond Indiana, and graduates June 1965. Through her Earlham friend Dorothy Shelton Landsberg, whose father was an IRS lawyer in Washington, she meets John Doar at a convocation. Doar invites her to apply to the Civil Rights Division. She qualifies her loyalty oath and joins as a research analyst in June 1965 working on school desegregation, police brutality, and employment cases until spring 1970.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Refugee from Nazi Germany
clips.summary: John Rosenberg describes his October 7, 1931 birth in Magdeburg, Germany, his father's role as teacher and welfare agency staff in the Jewish community, and the family's experience during Kristallnacht when their building's adjacent synagogue was bombed. His father spent 11 days in a concentration camp; the family escaped via Holland and arrived in the United States on February 22, 1940 on one of the last ships before the war began. His father's brother, mother, and the Rotterdam relatives perished.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Growing Up Jewish in Segregated Gastonia
clips.summary: After three years in Spartanburg, South Carolina, John's family settled in Gastonia, North Carolina. His father swept floors weekdays in a textile mill and traveled weekends to alternate rabbinical duties between Spartanburg and Gastonia. John describes Gaston County's segregated schools, the Jewish community's economic prosperity but exclusion from country clubs, FBI confiscation of family binoculars and shortwave radio during wartime, and his lack of Black friends. He notes a Gaston County voting rights case reached the Supreme Court.
related_events: Gaston County voting rights Supreme Court case

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Duke, ROTC, and the Air Force Awakening
clips.summary: John attends Duke University on a tuition scholarship from Gastonia trustee C.E. Park, lost after the trustee's death. He rooms with two Jewish students under Duke's Jewish quota and joins the Jewish fraternity. As a chemistry major and ROTC participant, he enters the US Air Force as a navigator, serving three years in England flying SA-16 missions during the Cold War. The pivotal moment comes when his radar operator Abe Jenkins, returning by train from Long Island in uniform, moves to the back of the train at Washington's segregated boundary, prompting John's resolve to address racial caste.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Law School and Joining the Civil Rights Division
clips.summary: After a year at Rohm and Haas chemical company, John attends law school at Chapel Hill, becoming friendly with Julius LeVonne Chambers, the future civil rights attorney who was number one in their class. After law school, John spends a month and a half in the Antitrust Division waiting to interview with John Doar, who was traveling weekly to Mississippi for the James Meredith Ole Miss admission. John joins the Civil Rights Division in 1962 when there were only eight or nine lawyers, motivated by Holocaust history, Air Force integration experience, and the belief that as a Southerner he could make a contribution.
related_events: James Meredith University of Mississippi admission

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Voting Rights Litigation in Georgia and Mississippi
clips.summary: John describes the Division's pre-1964 work focused on voting discrimination. With Robert F. Kennedy as Attorney General and Burke Marshall, John Doar built the field-based trial approach. John's first case in Sasser, Georgia under Jerome Heilbron involved a deputy sheriff who chased Black voter-registration students from town with a pistol; the all-white jury acquitted. He transferred to Mississippi at end of 1962. The Bob Moses Leflore County arrests led to a Division lawsuit and negotiated release. John discusses photographing voting records county-by-county under FBI supervision, documenting the disparate literacy-test administration that produced the freezing-principle doctrine in Judge Frank Johnson's Macon County Alabama ruling.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: The Fannie Lou Hamer Case and the FBI Problem
clips.summary: John details the March 1963 Winona Mississippi arrest and beating of Fannie Lou Hamer and her companions after they stopped at a lunch counter. The Division filed a criminal information against the police officers but the FBI was reluctant to investigate. John located a Black jail trustee whom the FBI claimed could not be found; the trustee testified to mopping blood off the jail floor. The December 1963 all-white-jury acquittal preceded John's drive to Sunflower County to tell Mrs. Hamer they had lost. The chapter contextualizes the institutional tension between Southern-rooted FBI agents and Division lawyers, illustrated by the Harman Turnbow firebombing case in Holmes County and the Medgar Evers funeral confrontation defused by John Doar.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: After the Civil Rights Act: Public Accommodations and Employment
clips.summary: John discusses the post-1964 expansion of Division jurisdiction to public accommodations, employment, and school desegregation. He describes Greenwood theater desegregation cases including the Leflore Theatre and Paramount Theatre, the Forrest Lynd contempt case in Hattiesburg featuring Black college-faculty registration testimony chronicled in Gordon A. Martin Jr's book, the Itta Bena 'Balance Do' church mass arrest of attendees aged 15 to 80 sentenced to county-farm time, the Mississippi Burning meridian civil rights workers murder investigation under Joseph Sullivan, and the Civil Rights Division attorneys cohort.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Voting Rights Act and the 1966 Selma Election
clips.summary: John describes the Voting Rights Act of 1965 abolishing literacy tests and authorizing federal examiners. The 1966 Alabama primary was the first election under the Act; Sheriff Jim Clark faced safety director Wilson Baker. After Black precincts' boxes took until 4 AM to count under new Black voting officials, white election officials impounded and excluded five Black-precinct boxes. The Division filed suit before Judge Frank Johnson for an order preserving the ballots, then before Judge Daniel Thomas in the Southern District. Jean and Dorothy counted 17,000 ballots under observation by Clark's deputies including one who gave Dorothy a 'Never' segregationist button. Judge Thomas ordered the ballots counted. The chapter also covers the Viola Liuzzo Selma-to-Montgomery March murder and the Voting Rights Act's downstream effects.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Bogalusa, Charlotte, and Marriage
clips.summary: John spends a month in Bogalusa Louisiana with Lou Coutter supporting CORE's James Farmer-led testers and obtaining an injunction against the Ku Klux Klan from Judge John Minor Wisdom in his undershirt on a Saturday in New Orleans. Jean describes her fear on the Meredith March near Yazoo City when a car of men followed her and a colleague through dark streets. After the Lowest Reese grand mal seizure incident in Montgomery, where the first ambulance refused to transport Reese because she was Black, John was sent to Charlotte to investigate the bombing of Julius LeVonne Chambers' house. There with Nick Katzenbach, John called Jean in Philadelphia to propose marriage.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Poll Tax, James Earl Ray, and Houston Schools
clips.summary: John describes filing the Mississippi poll tax case on the Monday after the Voting Rights Act was signed and preparing the Alabama poll tax case argued by Stephen Pollak, with Governor Wallace's highway-patrol-distributed campaign brochure entered as evidence of segregationist intent. He recounts traveling to Memphis after Martin Luther King's assassination with Nick Katzenbach to gather affidavits for the James Earl Ray extradition from England. He moves to Houston for six months to prepare the dot-map school desegregation case before Judge Ben C. Connally, plus employment cases against electrical workers in Las Vegas, steam fitters in Los Angeles, and Roadway Express.
related_events: Alabama poll tax case before Stephen Pollak
James Earl Ray Memphis affidavit gathering

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Leaving Justice and Founding Appalred
clips.summary: The Rosenbergs left the Civil Rights Division in 1970 after the Nixon administration's retreat on school cases and the politicization of case-filing by congressional district. A three-month camping trip with their infant Michael led to Terry Lenzner connecting them with Charleston attorneys interested in Appalachian public-interest law. After visiting Harry M. Caudill, author of Night Comes to the Cumberlands, at Whitesburg Kentucky, they settled in Prestonsburg in fall 1970. John founded Appalred (Appalachian Research and Defense Fund) covering 37 Appalachian Kentucky counties through 11 offices, addressing broad form deed, strip mining, Black Lung, food stamps, Medicaid access, and consumer law.

clips.subject: John and Jean Rosenberg
clips.title: Building Appalachian Civic Infrastructure
clips.summary: After retiring from Appalred in 2002, John founded the Appalachian Citizens Law Center focused on coal-related issues. Jean discusses statewide education advocacy work. The Rosenbergs describe difficulty of balancing environmental regulation with coal-mining employment, the inequity of out-of-state coal ownership and the Kentucky severance tax, and their community-building work including the local science museum with planetarium and the local Job Corps center advisory board. The interview closes with Jean's reflection that they have been fortunate to keep values congruent with day-to-day work.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Harlem Childhood and Family
clips.summary: Carlos describes being born June 5, 1945, on Lenox Avenue to Earl Carlos Sr. (a Camden SC-born WWI veteran and Harlem shoemaker) and a Cuban-born mother who immigrated at 17. He grew up between the Cotton Club and the Savoy Ballroom, learned to hustle for silver dollars in front of the clubs, and was given lasting performance advice by Fred Astaire to 'give the people a good show for the money.'

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Heroin, White Flight, and Robin Hood
clips.summary: Carlos recounts the arrival of heroin in Harlem following the bootleg-liquor 'King Kong' era, the disintegration of Black family units as fathers became addicted, and the white flight he observed from a child's vantage point. He adopted Robin Hood as a personal model, distinguishing 'God's law' from 'man's law,' and began breaking into freight yards near Yankee Stadium to redistribute food and textiles to neighbors.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Swimming Dream and Pioneer Club
clips.summary: Carlos describes hearing about the English Channel swim and the Olympic Games on the radio and announcing to his father that he would be the first Black American Olympic swimmer, only to be told that segregated pools and clubs barred his path. Detectives Lester and Brown of the 32nd Precinct at 135th Street confronted him about his freight-yard raids, told him he had a gift for running, and steered him to Joseph 'Joe' Yancey at the New York Pioneer Club at the 168th Street Armory.
related_events: Carlos's recruitment into the New York Pioneer Club

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: High School and the Building of a Legend
clips.summary: Carlos describes wanting to attend a high school with a swim program, being routed instead to Adam Clayton Powell Sr. High and Haaren High, and finally transferring to the School of Machine and Metal Trades, where he ran on the track team and broke records at the 168th Street Armory. He recounts post-meet outdoor races against open-class winners that built his reputation throughout New York's PSAL.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Athletics as Platform and the Boycott Argument
clips.summary: Carlos articulates his shift in viewing track and field as a springboard for political voice rather than personal acclaim. He compares the 1968 boycott debate to embargoes that harm only the population, explains why he no longer endorses boycotts, and uses an extended train-and-consensus metaphor to describe how those who avoided the burns of 1968 cannot retroactively claim the credit.
related_events: 1968 Olympic Project for Human Rights boycott debate

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Religious and Political Influences
clips.summary: Carlos describes his religious search leading him to Adam Clayton Powell Sr.'s Abyssinian Baptist Church and his shock at Adam Clayton Powell Jr.'s appearance preaching one Sunday. He recounts studying Marcus Garvey's Back-to-Africa movement, Paul Robeson's sacrifice of an entertainment career for political voice, and finally hearing Malcolm X at the 116th Street mosque, where he became Malcolm's walking companion until the Audubon Ballroom assassination.
related_events: Audubon Ballroom assassination of Malcolm X (1965)

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Meeting Dr. King
clips.summary: Carlos describes the meeting at the Americana Hotel with Dr. Harry Edwards, Ken Noel, Andrew Young, and Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. about the proposed 1968 Olympic boycott. He recounts King's lake-and-rock metaphor for the boycott's reach, King's commitment to serve as second-in-command under Dr. Edwards upon return from Memphis, and King's answer about why he would return despite the death threats: to stand for those who could not stand for themselves.
related_events: 1968 Americana Hotel meeting on the Olympic boycott

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: East Texas State University
clips.summary: Carlos describes his recruitment to East Texas State University by Pete Peterson, his arrival to whites-only and colored restrooms at the Dallas-Fort Worth airport, and a coach who called him 'boy' and slurs in front of his wife and infant daughter. He recounts the racist coach attempting to assault him with a hammer after Carlos refused a pre-conference workout, white teammate Terry Barnett (whom Carlos places with John Brown and Peter Norman) talking him into staying long enough to win the Lone Star Conference championship, and a bar incident in Austin that crystallized his decision to leave.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: The Mexico City Victory Stand
clips.summary: Carlos describes the planning of the demonstration in the 25 minutes between the semifinal and the medal ceremony, his asking Peter Norman whether he believed in human rights, Paul Hoffman of the Harvard rowing crew throwing down the OPHR button for Norman, and the symbols carried onto the podium: black shirt covering the USA jersey, Tommie Smith's black scarf, beads for Black lynching victims and those thrown overboard in the Middle Passage, sockless feet for poverty, and the gloves themselves. He describes a childhood vision on Lenox Avenue of an arena where joy turned to venom at his raised hand.

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Aftermath and Personal Cost
clips.summary: Carlos recounts the New York papers turning him from 'great' to 'neighborhood bum' within thirty days, his father in the hospital weeping at the headlines until Carlos asked him to trust the son he knew over the paper. He describes the silence of fellow Olympians who told him he had ruined his life, his first wife's suicide, his children's school suffering, years without Christmas presents, and the unsolicited grocery delivery from Ron Freeman that he never forgot. He frames George Foreman not as antagonist but as longtime supporter against right-wing-press distortion.
related_events: Post-1968 retaliation against Carlos and his family

clips.subject: John Carlos
clips.title: Teaching and Legacy
clips.summary: Carlos describes his work as a high school teacher and his approach of melting down to a student's level rather than standing above them. He recounts being told the previous evening at New York's first PSAL track alumni reunion of the long-term effects of his one-on-one mentoring on the runners he coached, the 80-foot mural of him in Sydney, and the statues at San Jose State, with the empty slot where Peter Norman's likeness would have stood at Norman's own request.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: North Philadelphia Childhood and Sight-Saving School
clips.summary: Churchville describes growing up in North Philadelphia with extreme myopia, attending the sight-saving program at T.M. Pierce School, and confronting discrimination against sight-savers within the school's segregated structure. He recounts being elected student-council president in fifth grade and a seventh-grade bullying incident that nearly led him to use a zip gun.
related_events: Sight-saver discrimination at T.M. Pierce School
Adam Clayton Powell Sr High School enrollment

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Mother Bertha and Musical Foundations
clips.summary: Churchville credits his mother Bertha Churchville, choir director of the Village Concert Choir in North Philadelphia, as his closest influence. He describes her teaching of music, her openness to his exploration of Islam, and his own Simon Gratz High School choir direction under Joyce Smith that led to a music scholarship at Temple University.
related_events: Simon Gratz High School choir concerts

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: New York, Ahmadiyya Islam, and Malcolm X
clips.summary: Churchville leaves Temple and moves to the Lower East Side at 19, encountering Marxists, poets, and black nationalists. He recounts converting to the Ahmadiyya Muslim Community at 17 under the name Yahya Abdul Hamid, then meeting Malcolm X at Temple Number 7 in Harlem and being struck by Malcolm's statement that 'my life is irrelevant.'
related_events: Move to East Village New York
Encounter with Malcolm X at Temple Number 7

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: SNCC and the Albany Project
clips.summary: While working as a janitor at Columbia Presbyterian Medical Center, Churchville rides a bus to Georgia and joins SNCC under Reverend Charles Sherrod in the Albany Project. Assigned to Lee County and Americus, he requests a black partner to register young black voters, brokering a peace treaty between two warring teenage gangs in Americus and recruiting them to register voters.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Mississippi Freedom School and Southwest Georgia Violence
clips.summary: Churchville teaches literacy at a COFO Freedom School in Greenwood, Mississippi. He recounts the Southwest Georgia terror, FBI complicity with local sheriffs, and the case of a Lee County mother whose son the sheriff threatened to lynch after a bakery driver raped the boy's sister. He praises Diane Nash, Bernice Johnson Reagon, Faith Holsaert, Bob Mants, Dorie Ann Ladner, and John Lewis.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Nation of Islam in Atlanta and Philadelphia
clips.summary: Churchville joins the Nation of Islam mosque in Atlanta under Minister Jeremiah Pugh, organizing in Buttermilk Bottom and recruiting Atlanta University students. After Chicago headquarters under Elijah Muhammad rejects his economic-development proposal, Jeremiah is transferred to Philadelphia and Churchville returns with him, only to be denied the secretary position.
related_events: Atlanta NOI organizing in Buttermilk Bottom
Transfer of Jeremiah Pugh to Philadelphia

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Freedom Library and the OAAU
clips.summary: Churchville founds the Northern Student Movement Freedom Library at Ridge and Jefferson with a $1,200 Dolfinger-McMahon Foundation grant in 1964. After the riots that summer spare the storefront, he meets Malcolm X again and joins the Organization of Afro-American Unity. Minister Jeremiah Pugh warns his mother of the danger and orders the Fruit of Islam not to touch him.
related_events: Founding of Northern Student Movement Freedom Library

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Freedom Library Day School and Independent Black Education
clips.summary: Churchville transforms the library into Freedom Library Day School, teaching preschoolers phonics through the J.B. Lippincott Reading Series. He uses an individual-prescriptive method, has his mother join as a teacher, and operates the school for fourteen years. He critiques Amiri Baraka and Maulana Karenga's cultural-nationalist schools for prioritizing rhetoric over skills.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Black People's Unity Movement and the Rizzo Raid
clips.summary: Churchville co-founds the Black People's Unity Movement in February 1966 with Father Paul M. Washington's church, Arthur Hall, John E. Allen Jr., and David P. Richardson Jr. He recounts a police informant, Casimiro Pereira, attempting to bring M16 rifles to the school, and the August 13, 1966 police raid led by then-Commissioner Frank Rizzo connected to the SNCC dynamite case. Attorney William Lee Akers tells him the warrant was valid.
related_events: Rizzo raid on Freedom Library August 13 1966

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: CIBI and National Black Education Work
clips.summary: Churchville helps found the Council of Independent Black Institutions (CIBI) in the early 1970s, leading a two-week teacher-training institute at Freedom Library Day School. He frames his approach as revolutionary nationalism against the cultural nationalism of Amiri Baraka and Maulana Karenga, and recounts work with Chicago teachers and the post-CBS documentary speaking circuit.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Law Practice, Seminary, and Liberation Fellowship
clips.summary: Churchville practices law as a farm-worker attorney in Cumberland County NJ then in Philadelphia, surviving a 1995 car accident that redirects him toward theology. He earns degrees at Lutheran Theological Seminary at Philadelphia and Union Institute & University, taught at LTSP for one year, and in 2005 founded the Liberation Fellowship Church of Jesus with his wife Nancy and a small congregation.

clips.subject: John Churchville
clips.title: Germantown CDC and Town Hall Redevelopment
clips.summary: Churchville describes the Liberation Fellowship CDC's HIV/AIDS testing program in Vernon Park, the founding of the Greater Germantown Business Association, and the $35 million redevelopment plan for Germantown Town Hall as a green building housing STEM education, green-trades training, and a business incubator across the street from Germantown High School.
related_events: Founding of Greater Germantown Business Association

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Family Backgrounds in Kinston
clips.summary: Each of the six interviewees describes their family origins in Kinston, North Carolina, including parents' occupations in the tobacco factory, shirt factory, mortuary work, domestic service, and small business ownership. Several grew up in Mitchell Wooten Court, the local housing project. Childhoods centered on extended family caretakers, neighborhood music traditions, and a sense, across all six, that they did not know they were poor.
related_events: Mitchell Wooten Court housing project residency

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Race Relations and the Country Club
clips.summary: The interviewees recall the Kinston Country Club caddy economy, where Black boys could earn sixty-five cents per two rounds. Samuel Dove describes confronting Larry Beck, who beat Jack Nicklaus in the 1957 national juniors and was the son of the club pro, after Beck called him 'boy,' ending Dove's caddying career. Participants describe the racial limits understood at the club.
related_events: Black child labor at Southern country clubs

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Tobacco Field Labor
clips.summary: Frances Suggs and Harold Suggs describe the tobacco-field labor cycle: pickup at 5:30 a.m., field work by men cropping leaves, women handling and looping in the barn, hanging tobacco for curing, and end-of-season barbecues. Wages were three to five dollars a day; field croppers earned ten dollars at ten hours. Participants note that tobacco-field work was one of the few integrated settings in the segregated economy, including shared drinking water from a single mason jar.
related_events: North Carolina tobacco economy in the 1940s and 1950s

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Bus Segregation and Dr. Hannibal
clips.summary: John Dudley and Samuel Dove recount childhood incidents on Kinston's segregated buses. Dove, age twelve, refused to give up his seat to a white woman with the retort, 'And so is toilet paper'; Black men at the back of the bus blocked the driver from forcing him to move. Dr. Alice Hannibal subsequently filed an ICC lawsuit against the bus company, which ended the city's bus system within a year.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Segregated Retail and Drugstore Resistance
clips.summary: Eleanor Stewart describes waitressing at Standard Drug Store, where Black customers could not sit and had to take food out from the end of the counter. Samuel Dove recounts a 1960 action two weeks after the Greensboro sit-ins: he had a friend, Curtis Henderson, pose as a Ghanaian diplomat, invoking Ghana's recent independence, while Dove served as French interpreter, in order to integrate Standard Drug Number Two. The plan succeeded. Participants describe small acts of retaliation against five-and-dime store mistreatment.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Servicemen, K-Town, and Defending Sisters
clips.summary: The participants describe Kinston as 'K-Town', the entertainment hub between Camp Lejeune, Cherry Point, Fort Bragg, and Seymour Johnson Air Force Base. Touring Chitlin' Circuit acts including James Brown, Ruth Brown, Buddy Johnson, Arthur Prysock, and Larry Darnell played in town. Charles Jarmon's father ran the Tuxedo Club. The interviewees recount having to confront servicemen who pursued local young women, and the weekly cycle of soldiers killed in accidents returning to base.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Movie Theater Violence and Daily Indignities
clips.summary: Participants describe routine racial harassment as children: white kids throwing rocks at Black children walking past the white theater to reach the Black theater; Eleanor Stewart being knocked off her bicycle, spit on, and her bike taken by white boys. The accounts establish the baseline of daily intimidation that preceded the high school walkout.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Planning the Adkin Walkout
clips.summary: John Dudley narrates how the senior class of 1952, in a Wednesday civics class taught by B.C. Davis, identified eight features of an ideal school, facilities present at white Grainger High but not at Adkin. The students asked Davis to leave the room so she would not be implicated. Five seniors petitioned the school board the following Monday. The board responded that the requested items were not in the budget for ten years. The seniors then coordinated 720 students for a walkout, signaled by an announcement that 'Carolyn Colefielder has lost her red pocketbook.'

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: The Walkout and Its Aftermath
clips.summary: On Tuesday morning, 720 students rose silently from their seats, collected pre-made placards reading 'Quality Education,' and marched down Queen Street. The principal told teachers to leave the students alone. Students stayed out until the Monday after Thanksgiving. Dr. Alice Hannibal wrote letters to the school board and faced bomb threats; senior boys took turns standing guard at her house. Within eighteen months, all eight petitioned items, including a state-of-the-art gymnasium, were built.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Parallel Cases and Rosenwald Schools
clips.summary: The interviewer notes that the April 1951 Moton High School walkout in Prince Edward County, Virginia, led by Barbara Johns, had occurred seven months before the Kinston walkout, but the Kinston students were unaware of it. The five Virginia, South Carolina, Delaware, D.C., and Kansas cases consolidated as Brown v. Board; Kinston did not litigate. The participants identify Adkin as a Rosenwald school, built through the program founded by Sears chairman Julius Rosenwald at the urging of Booker T. Washington, with required industrial coursework.

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Athletic Inequity and the Gym Fire
clips.summary: Participants describe the 1953 Adkin state championship football team that played with hand-me-down equipment from Grainger High and 'Halloween helmet' headgear. The championship game was played at Grainger's stadium because Adkin had no facility. They recall basketball great Althea Gibson playing in Adkin's small gym while at Williston High in Wilmington. The student-built original gym burned after ashes fell on the floor during a winter pickup game, accelerating construction of the replacement gym that still stands.
related_events: Althea Gibson at Williston High School

clips.subject: John Dudley et al
clips.title: Dr. Hannibal's Legacy and Civil Rights Careers
clips.summary: Participants describe Dr. Alice Hannibal, born in New Jersey, married to a Jamaican physician trained at Fisk, as the first Black member of the Kinston city council. A municipal building in Kinston now bears her name. Samuel Dove recounts joining CORE in December 1960, conducting reconnaissance of segregated facilities between New York and Washington at James Farmer's request, intelligence that fed the April 1961 Freedom Rides. Dove later joined SNCC, wrote position papers under James Forman, organized across Southern states except Mississippi, and faced a Fayetteville police chief's death threat. Eleanor Stewart founded a D.C. boys' choir that has made international tours to Austria, England, China, and Italy.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Family Origins in Terrell County
clips.summary: McCullar identifies herself as born February 4, 1940, in Sasser, Terrell County, Georgia, to Clifford Brawner and Johnnie Lou Brawner. Her father's family came from Worth County; her mother's family originated in Alabama. She describes her mother as constantly working, canning, sewing, making quilts, and her father as a farmer who later worked at the Marine Base and then for 28 years at the Swift fertilizer plant in Albany.
related_events: Family migration from Worth to Terrell County

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Farm Childhood and Move to Sasser
clips.summary: McCullar describes the family farm near Doublegate on Old Dawson Road and her dislike of farm life, the animals, heat, mosquitoes, gnats, and snakes. She recounts feeding chickens, milking the cow, and picking cotton. The family moved off the farm in the 1950s to Sasser when she was about 12 or 13. She preferred Sasser primarily because there were fewer snakes.
related_events: Brawner family relocation from farm to Sasser

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Segregated Schooling
clips.summary: McCullar attended Piney Grove, a one-room school in Terrell County with about 40 students across seven grades under teacher Vanny Wakefield. She moved to Dunbar Elementary in Sasser through ninth grade, then to Carver High School in Dawson. She describes the unheated classrooms, the lack of a lunchroom, the partitioned auditorium, and the hand-me-down textbooks with torn pages, one spelling book per class, passed down from white schools.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Awareness Without Discussion
clips.summary: McCullar reflects that Brown v. Board was not discussed in school or at home, black teachers were 'told what to teach,' and her parents 'didn't know too much about segregation.' But the inequality was visible: torn pages, shared books, the dust-compound classroom-cleaning duty that fell on the students. She graduated in 1959 and sat out a year babysitting and keeping house while her older sister finished nursing school in Bibb County, Macon, Georgia.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Albany State and the First March
clips.summary: McCullar enrolled at Albany State in 1960, choosing it because her parents wanted her close given her childhood illness. She declared a science major and lived on campus. The administration ignored complaints about the food; she contracted hepatitis from cafeteria meals. In the fall of 1961, she participated in her first march downtown, recalling Bertha Gober as the only name she retained from that day. Her parents accepted her involvement after a white head cook at her mother's school made disapproving remarks.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Joining the Terrell County Movement
clips.summary: Forced home by illness in spring 1962, McCullar found her parents already attending mass meetings, first at Zion Hill Sardis Baptist Church in Dawson, then at the Brawner family home. She became secretary of the Terrell County Movement under president Lucius Holloway. The organization had begun in 1960 but she joined upon returning. She kept notes of mass meetings where SNCC field workers Charles Sherrod, Don Harris, Prathia Hall, and Ralph Allen spoke.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Voter Registration and Reprisal
clips.summary: McCullar describes attempts to register at the Terrell County courthouse, where the registrar would leave the office to avoid prospective Black registrants. When the registrar was present, applicants had to read scripture from the Bible before completing the form. After McCullar and her mother successfully registered, her mother was fired from her cooking job at the white school. The Albany employer who later hired her mother refused a call asking her to fire her, saying her registration was not her concern.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: SNCC Field Workers and Sheriff Mathews
clips.summary: McCullar describes the SNCC field workers' regular presence in Terrell County, Charles Sherrod, Don Harris, Prathia Hall, Ralph Allen, many staying at Carolyn Daniels's house and at Lucius Holloway's. Mount Olive Baptist Church hosted the Wednesday-night Terrell mass meetings, with the surrounding SW Georgia counties rotating support. Sheriff Z.T. Mathews and his deputies would enter the meetings, put their feet on the pews, and verbally harass attendees while the singing or speaking continued.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Canvassing and Restaurant Sit-Ins
clips.summary: McCullar canvassed Dawson neighborhoods after school with Gladys and Annadine Clark and other students, walking door-to-door despite locals running back into their houses calling them 'Freedom Riders.' Most refused to talk for fear of losing jobs or housing. Reverend Samuel Wells led restaurant sit-ins at a Dawson establishment, sitting at the counter, leaving when asked. McCullar describes the workers and owners cursing them and the practice of getting hamburgers through a hole-in-the-wall window because Black patrons could not enter.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Mount Olive Burned, Family Vigils
clips.summary: Mount Olive Baptist Church was burned in August 1962. McCullar describes the loss 'like a death.' The mass meetings moved temporarily to a Methodist church, then to a tent erected behind the rebuilding foundation when the Methodist congregation feared their own church would be burned next. The Brawner family began taking nightly turns watching the house for strange cars or arson attempts, with a plan to evacuate everyone before fire could be set.

clips.subject: Johnnie Ruth McCullar
clips.title: Federal Court and Awakening
