With no clerkships or government employment available to a Black attorney, he opened a one-room law office in Spartanburg, where Columbia (with three Black attorneys) was already considered "crowded."

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Spartanburg Municipal Court Tactics
clips.summary: Perry recounts a routine pattern in which the Spartanburg municipal judge would impose the maximum sentence (30 days or $100) on his Black clients while letting white-represented defendants off with $10 fines. With no separate prosecutor (the arresting officers prosecuted their own cases), Perry developed and pre-drafted a notice of appeal he could serve immediately upon conviction, eventually using the circuit court appellate process to reverse the judge and shift the local courtroom dynamic.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Union County Habeas Petition
clips.summary: Called by the Union County Voters League about a young Black man given an excessive sentence by a magistrate judge, Perry received the case on day six, past the five-day appeal window. He filed a petition for a writ of habeas corpus and obtained what he describes as his first victory in the South Carolina Supreme Court.

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: NAACP Network and the Brown Dry Run
clips.summary: Perry describes becoming a cooperating attorney with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund while also associating with the NAACP Office of General Counsel, two separate organizations whose distinction was opaque to the public. He traveled to Washington for the Howard University moot-court rehearsal of the Brown v. Board cases, where stand-in justices (Howard faculty and senior attorneys) mimicked the sitting Supreme Court justices' judicial philosophies. He names Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, Spottswood W. Robinson III, Jack Greenberg, and Constance Baker Motley.
related_events: Brown v. Board Supreme Court arguments

clips.subject: Matthew J. Perry Jr.
clips.title: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas
clips.summary: Perry recounts Sarah Mae Fleming's 1954 incident on a Columbia city bus operated by the South Carolina Electric and Gas Company, the suit brought by white attorney Phil Wittenberg (whose yard was later burned with a cross), and the case's path through the Fourth Circuit. After Wittenberg withdrew, Perry joined Lincoln Jenkins Jr. at the trial level. He emphasizes that Fleming preceded Browder v. Gayle and the Montgomery bus boycott as a federal challenge to intrastate bus segregation. The interview also references Modjeska Monteith Simkins's role in connecting Fleming with counsel.
related_events: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company (1954-1956)

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Interview Setup and Introductions
clips.summary: Interviewer David Cline of Virginia Tech, working with the Southern Oral History Program at UNC Chapel Hill, opens the December 14, 2015 session at Wesley Theological Seminary. Moore identifies himself and notes he has lived in Washington for thirty-five years.
related_events: Civil Rights History Project recording session, December 14, 2015

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Born in Petersburg, Virginia
clips.summary: Moore states he was born in Petersburg, Virginia, in 1938 and was raised there. He frames Petersburg as a Southern town in important respects and signals that being born and raised there is essential context for understanding him.

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Petersburg as Civil War and Tobacco Town
clips.summary: Moore describes Petersburg's local history: the Crater, where Union troops tunneled under Confederate works and detonated dynamite by accidental trigger, now a historic site that townspeople treated as a prominent event. He also describes Petersburg as a long-standing commercial center, a railhead and switching area for tobacco, with the early-twentieth-century establishment of Camp Lee, later Fort Lee, as an Army quartermaster depot that boosted the economy through World War II.
related_events: Establishment of Camp Lee / Fort Lee

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Methodist Family and the 1846 Schism
clips.summary: Moore describes his Methodist upbringing at Washington Street United Methodist Church, one of ten Methodist churches in a town of 35,000 with an equivalent number of Baptist and other churches, all segregated. He notes the placard on his home church marking the 1846 organization of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South, formed when Southern Methodists broke from the northern Methodist church over slavery.
related_events: 1846 organization of the Methodist Episcopal Church, South

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Call to Ministry and Methodist Student Movement
clips.summary: Moore identifies Washington Street United Methodist Church as the place he felt the call to ministry. He recounts his participation in the youth group and the Methodist Student Movement at the district level, and his election to a state-wide Methodist Student Movement office as a senior in high school.

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Segregation Unspoken at Home and Church
clips.summary: Asked whether segregation or racial issues were discussed at home or in the church, Moore says nobody addressed racial issues, probably by unspoken design. He describes himself as asleep on these matters during those years; it was simply the way of life and not on his family's radar.

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: Petersburg's Cross-Street Geography of Race
clips.summary: Moore describes the spatial organization of Petersburg, where his family lived on a main cross street. As was typical of Southern towns, the cross streets running off the main streets were where the Black population lived, less cared for than the main streets. Black neighbors were nearby but not on the main thoroughfares.

clips.subject: Maynard E. Moore
clips.title: End of Cross-Racial Childhood Friendships
clips.summary: Moore recounts playing with Black children until junior high, when his mother explained that they would no longer be welcome in the house as had previously been the case. He had invited Black friends in to watch the Howdy Doody show or share cookies and ice cream and had visited their houses; from junior high on, there was no more interaction, though some had been among his best buddies. The recorded segment ends here.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Chicago Childhood and Family
clips.summary: McCarty introduces himself, names his birthplace (Chicago, September 7, 1950), and describes growing up on the West Side after moving from the South Side at age three. He credits his mother as the greatest influence in his life, instilling a love of reading and the obligation to act when one sees something wrong.
related_events: McCarty family migration from South Side to West Side Chicago

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Cicero and Crestwood Encounters
clips.summary: McCarty describes growing up near the sundown town of Cicero, Illinois, where being Black after dark could mean harm or death. He recounts riding bicycles with friends for cycling merit badges in the Boy Scouts and being chased from a water fountain in Crestwood after a Little League crowd emptied the stands to attack him.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: South Side Beating and Police
clips.summary: In his sophomore year, McCarty and a friend went to a party in a South Side high-rise project, where a gang beat them until tired. A gunman put a pistol to McCarty's head; a girl named in the moment as 'an angel' deflected the gun and the firearm misfired. A white police officer he flagged down for help dismissed him and then drove him back to the scene before taking him to a police station where the officers laughed.
related_events: South Side Chicago gang assault on McCarty

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Saint Ignatius Black Student Organization
clips.summary: McCarty joined the NAACP Youth Council and cofounded a Black Student organization at Saint Ignatius College Prep with classmate Tyler. The school initially supported interracial discussion panels at other Catholic schools. McCarty attended Saint Ignatius from 1965 until being expelled in March 1969.
related_events: Saint Ignatius Black Student organization founding 1966-67

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Walkout, Expulsion, and 2008 Apology
clips.summary: When the school refused the Black student organization's demands for Black studies, community outreach, and more minority students, McCarty and Tyler organized a walkout that drew press coverage. McCarty's mother learned of the walkout from the evening news. Both students were expelled. Thirty-nine years later, in 2008, classmate Greg Myers organized a reunion at which the school apologized and granted McCarty and Tyler their high school diplomas.
related_events: Saint Ignatius diploma and apology ceremony 2008

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Martin Luther King in Chicago
clips.summary: McCarty recalls King's 1966 Chicago campaign. King stayed on Hamlin near Douglas Park, where McCarty's classmate Tyler greeted him in passing. McCarty quotes King's remark that he had dealt with racism throughout the South but had seen nothing like racism in Chicago. He describes a Bridgeport door-to-door summer job where a white classmate had to extract them after overhearing threats of bodily harm.
related_events: King brick attack at Chicago march

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Joining the Black Panther Party
clips.summary: After expulsion McCarty became a full-time member of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party. He had followed the Party from reading Ramparts magazine and the 1967 Sacramento Capitol protest. He attended his first political education class at the Illinois chapter office at 2350 W. Madison Street in October or November 1968, where he encountered Deputy Minister of Education Billy 'Che' Brooks, whom he had known from track at John Marshall Metropolitan High School. McCarty joined the education cadre.
related_events: Black Panther Party Sacramento Capitol protest 1967
Illinois chapter Black Panther Party founding

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Fred Hampton's Leadership
clips.summary: McCarty describes Deputy Chairman Fred Hampton, who was 21 years old when assassinated. Hampton had organized as a high school student in Maywood, Illinois to integrate the public pool. McCarty recounts Hampton's response to a friend who came to the Party wanting a gun to kill white people: Hampton refused, gave him literature, and told him to return if still interested. McCarty notes that Fred sometimes sent him in his place for debates and speeches.
related_events: Fred Hampton Maywood pool integration organizing

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Original Rainbow Coalition
clips.summary: McCarty describes the Original Rainbow Coalition that Hampton and the Illinois chapter built with the Young Patriots Organization (poor Appalachian whites) and the Young Lords (a Puerto Rican street gang that became political). Field secretaries Bob Lee and Hank 'Bull' Gaddis organized in the Appalachian-white community. McCarty references the documentary American Revolution 2 by Mike Gray showing a community meeting and a subsequent incident where Young Patriots surrounded a police car to free Bob Lee.

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: COINTELPRO and Gang Outreach
clips.summary: McCarty discusses the Black Panther Party's outreach to street gangs and meetings between Fred Hampton, Bobby Rush, and the Black P. Stone Nation. He describes COINTELPRO disinformation operations designed to disrupt the cross-racial coalition and the gang alliance, including anonymous letters and calls planted to provoke violence. He identifies William O'Neal, head of security, as the FBI plant.
related_events: COINTELPRO operations against Illinois Black Panther Party

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: The Burglary and Jean Seberg Case
clips.summary: McCarty references The Burglary by Betty Medsger, on the peace activists who broke into the FBI office in Media, Pennsylvania on March 8, 1971 and exposed COINTELPRO. He recounts the case of an unnamed Los Angeles actress (Jean Seberg) whose pregnancy J. Edgar Hoover ordered the LA FBI office to undermine through planted rumors that the baby was not her husband's; the baby died and the actress later took her own life. McCarty states the LA FBI office was compensated for the operation.
related_events: Media Pennsylvania FBI office burglary March 8 1971

clips.subject: Michael D. McCarty
clips.title: Free Breakfast and Community Programs
clips.summary: McCarty describes the Free Breakfast for Children Program and the People's Free Medical Clinics, citing J. Edgar Hoover's identification of the Breakfast Program as the most dangerous Black Panther Party activity. He frames subsequent federal nutrition programs as a government response to Panther community organizing. The chapter ends mid-sentence as McCarty begins to describe Illinois chapter programs in the context of a 40th-anniversary reunion in Oakland.
related_events: Black Panther Party Free Breakfast for Children Program
Black Panther Party 40th anniversary reunion Oakland

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Brownsville Childhood and Family Activism
clips.summary: Roxborough describes growing up in Haywood County, Tennessee (one of three Tennessee counties where Black residents did not vote) and the county seat of Brownsville. Her parents organized voter-registration campaigns and the first local NAACP branch, leveraging the three-to-one Black-to-white population ratio to push for political power.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Selling The Crisis at Age Nine
clips.summary: Roxborough recalls her parents reading Langston Hughes poems from The Crisis magazine aloud during her childhood and describes writing to Roy Wilkins (then editor of The Crisis) at age nine to ask whether she could sell subscriptions. Wilkins sent her a typed reply with his editor's signature.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Father's 1939 Beating and Dr. John
clips.summary: Roxborough recounts the night in 1939 when two sheriffs brought her father home badly beaten and told the nine-year-old to take care of her 'paw.' She later learned that the county coroner, a white physician who was her grandmother's half-brother, had intervened to prevent his arrest. Family discusses the Roosevelt-era organizing climate.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Howard University in Wartime
clips.summary: Roxborough describes attending Howard University from 1943 to 1945 during World War II, the discussions among Black students about segregated Washington theaters and restaurants, and the presence of uniformed servicemen on campus through federal training programs. She later attended NYU's Washington Square campus.
related_events: WWII federal training programs at HBCUs

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Joining the NAACP National Staff in 1953
clips.summary: Roxborough joined the NAACP national staff in fall 1953 under Gloster B. Current, Director of Branches, to run a six-week membership campaign in Baltimore under Dr. Lillie M. Jackson. Thurgood Marshall laughed at the assignment, warning her about Jackson's exacting demands. She worked alongside Juanita Jackson Mitchell on the Youth Councils program.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Portraits of Marshall, Wilkins, and White
clips.summary: Roxborough offers personal portraits of three NAACP figures: Thurgood Marshall as a profane raconteur who inspired comfort and competence; Roy Wilkins as an unpretentious, elegant writer who took the subway and demanded staff dedication; and Walter White as a sociable figure who could pass as white to investigate lynchings in Mississippi, Georgia, and Arkansas without detection.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Hoxie, Little Rock, and Daisy Bates
clips.summary: Roxborough recalls her 1954 trip to Hoxie, Arkansas (where the school board voluntarily desegregated) and subsequent return visits during the lead-up to Central High integration. She describes Orval Faubus's anti-Black segregationist posture and Daisy Bates as a charismatic, talkative organizer who reached barely-literate rural members and mobilized students across Arkansas.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Rosa Parks, E.D. Nixon, and Montgomery
clips.summary: Roxborough describes staying with Rosa Parks and her husband in Montgomery when traveling as a field secretary. She characterizes the Parkses as quietly militant, and recalls E.D. Nixon's refusal to surrender Alabama NAACP membership rolls to the state Attorney General, leading to the NAACP's banning in Alabama.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Emmett Till and Mamie Till Bradley
clips.summary: Roxborough recalls the office's reaction to Emmett Till's murder, situating it alongside the recent killing of Mississippi branch president Gus Courts. She arranged a national branch tour for Mamie Till Bradley, who grew more militant and articulate as she traveled, and supported Bradley's insistence on the open casket at the Chicago funeral.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Clarence Norris and the Scottsboro Pardon
clips.summary: Roxborough narrates the day Clarence Norris, a fugitive Scottsboro defendant, walked into the NAACP office at 1790 Broadway in the late 1960s. She brought him to Bobbie Branch and Dr. John Morsell; he asked for help clearing his name before he died. General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned attorney James I. Meyerson, and Norris received a full pardon from Alabama.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: ACT-SO and Youth Programs
clips.summary: Roxborough describes the ACT-SO program (Academic, Cultural, Technological, and Scientific Olympics; later Afro added) co-founded by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett. She traces its growth to roughly 600 units nationwide, organized through local, regional, and national competitions, awarding scholarships at the national convention.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Succession from White to Wilkins to Hooks
clips.summary: Roxborough discusses the leadership succession from Walter White (died 1955 while on leave) to Roy Wilkins, who had been serving as administrator during White's absences. She contrasts Wilkins's intellectual elegance with Benjamin L. Hooks's Baptist-minister oratorical style, noting how Hooks used 'the gospel approach' to inspire NAACP constituents.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Civil Rights Organizations Compared
clips.summary: Roxborough offers comparative assessments of SCLC, SNCC, CORE, and the Black Panther Party. She credits SCLC for mobilizing religious leaders, SNCC for student-led sit-ins that inspired older activists, CORE under James Farmer for racial integration in membership, and characterizes the Black Panther Party as starting militant but veering into a separatism she viewed as self-defeating.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: NAACP Beyond Litigation
clips.summary: Roxborough pushes back on the public perception of the NAACP as primarily a legal organization. She cites Executive Order 8802 (Roosevelt, June 25, 1941), achieved through lobbying by Walter White, Eleanor Roosevelt, and A. Philip Randolph, and the 1957 Civil Rights Act passed through Washington Bureau lobbying under Clarence M. Mitchell Jr.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Malcolm X and Discipline
clips.summary: Roxborough offers her assessment of Malcolm X, focusing on the discipline he instilled among formerly unaffiliated street-level Black men and the cohesion of his movement. She describes the impact of his murder and the way some NAACP youth saw his evolving rhetoric as a model for their own activism.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Marriage to John Roxborough and Detroit
clips.summary: Roxborough describes meeting John W. Roxborough, chairman of the Legal Redress Committee of the Detroit NAACP branch, when she went to Detroit to work with Arthur L. Johnson on the first Freedom Fund Life Membership dinner. John led housing and education cases that went to the Michigan Supreme Court and earned commendations from Thurgood Marshall.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Branch Effectiveness and Alabama Ban
clips.summary: Roxborough attributes branch strength to leadership quality. She recounts the NAACP's defiance when Alabama, Mississippi, and other states demanded membership rolls and banned the organization. Ruby Hurley, sent to organize Confederate-state branches from Birmingham, moved her office to Atlanta after the ban and continued clandestine night-time organizing despite repeated death threats.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Field Work with Medgar Evers
clips.summary: After Medgar Evers's assassination, the NAACP sent staff to Mississippi and his brother Charles Evers became state field director. Roxborough recalls her own 1960s trip with Medgar Evers through the Delta interviewing farmers facing economic reprisals for NAACP activity; she discovered her feet resting on his shotgun, and they traveled by night between Yazoo, Jackson, and Mound Bayou, Mississippi.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: The Little Rock Nine and South Carolina Educators
clips.summary: Roxborough recalls Ernest Green and Minnijean Brown among the Little Rock Nine she met through Daisy Bates's coordinating meetings during the 1955-56 lead-up to integration. She notes meeting but not knowing Septima Poinsette Clark and Bernice Robinson, and describes Ella Baker's NAACP Department of Branches work organizing membership campaigns before Baker helped found SNCC.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Women in the NAACP
clips.summary: Roxborough traces women's roles in the NAACP from the founding meeting through Daisy E. Lampkin's 1930s-40s branch-organizing travels, Althea T.L. Simmons succeeding Clarence M. Mitchell Jr at the Washington Bureau, Constance Baker Motley in the Legal Defense Fund, Francis Hooks founding WIN (Women in the NAACP), Margaret Bush Wilson and Roslyn M. Brock as board chairs, and the unnamed female researcher whose lynching report fed W.E.B. Du Bois's silent-march planning.
related_events: NAACP Silent Parade (1917, dated by speaker as 1918-1919)

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Du Bois Encounter and Reflections on Career
clips.summary: Roxborough describes briefly meeting W.E.B. Du Bois and corresponding with him to obtain a paper for five dollars. She reflects on having held roughly eight to ten jobs in nearly every NAACP department except accounting over her career, taking fundraising courses to become development director, and her sense of satisfaction at walking Hoxie children to school.

clips.subject: Mildred Bond Roxborough
clips.title: Fear, Field Travel, and Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Roxborough reflects on the absence of fear during dangerous field travel, citing her parents' counsel that fear made one one's own worst enemy. She lists El Dorado and Cotton Plant, Arkansas among the small towns where local white power structures viewed NAACP visitors as threats, and closes by emphasizing the NAACP's role as an entity people believed could help them.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Louisiana Childhood and Carter G. Woodson
clips.summary: Walter was born in 1922 in De Ridder, Louisiana, between Lake Charles and Shreveport. She attended a Beauregard Parish all-Black high school where the only new book she owned was Carter G. Woodson's history of the Negro. She describes Black teachers, the church's role in instilling dignity, and working from age seven in white homes and a beauty shop.
related_events: Long-Bell Lumber Company departure from Louisiana

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Southern University and Longview Shipyards
clips.summary: Walter enrolled at Southern University in Scotlandville, Louisiana, where she heard Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and W.E.B. Du Bois speak. To earn tuition, she traveled to Longview, Washington, in her senior year and worked the graveyard shift cleaning ships in the shipyards alongside Black and white women. She returned to graduate from Southern in 1944.
related_events: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and W.E.B. Du Bois campus visits

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Move to Los Angeles, 1944
clips.summary: Refusing to teach in Shreveport for $50 a month, Walter went to Los Angeles with her mother's blessing in 1944. After a few days on Skid Row, she met Mozelle Moore on a bus and was taken in. She was hired as a classified clerk with the Los Angeles City Schools and earned her California credential at California State University, Los Angeles before beginning to teach in 1955.
related_events: postwar Black migration to Los Angeles

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Marriage and CORE Organizing
clips.summary: Walter married Earl Walter in 1947; he had been active in CORE since 1942 and with an organization called Drew. From 1958 onward CORE Los Angeles picketed Central Avenue grocery stores and banks demanding employment of Black salespeople, box boys, and tellers, and that they be trained for management. Walter describes successful negotiations with grocers and the eventual capitulation of the banks.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Unruh Act Test Cases and CORE Allies
clips.summary: After the 1959 Unruh Civil Rights Act, CORE Los Angeles tested the law by sending Black couples to motels and hotels and following them with a white couple to document discriminatory denials. A young lawyer took cases pro bono; the ACLU was an ally. Walter notes the test cases dismantled segregated accommodations faster than the law would have done alone.
related_events: Jesse M. Unruh's 1959 California civil rights legislation

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Teaching Black Children Pride
clips.summary: Walter began teaching elementary school in Los Angeles in 1955. She put Frederick Douglass on the classroom wall alongside Washington and Lincoln for Presidents' Day, set out brown paint for children to paint themselves, and cut her hair natural in the 1950s alongside Abbey Lincoln and Odetta. She recounts a girl returning to tell another child "I'm beautiful because teacher told me so."
related_events: 1950s natural-hair movement in Black women's culture

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Becoming a Children's Book Author
clips.summary: Frustrated by the absence of positive Black representation in children's books, Walter wrote Lillie of Watts for a Los Angeles publisher who challenged her to author the books she wanted to see. She describes rejection slips, a meeting at Scholastic with a Harvard-graduate editor who took The Liquid Trap, and her long collaboration with editor Barbara Lalicki, beginning with Ty's One-Man Band, the inaugural Reading Rainbow selection.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Awards and the Mississippi Challenge
clips.summary: Walter received the Coretta Scott King Award for Justin and the Best Biscuits in the World, the Jane Addams Children's Book Award, and the Christopher Award. Her young-adult titles cover Elizabeth "Mum Bett" Freeman's freedom suit under the Massachusetts Constitution and Mississippi Challenge, which tracks the 1964 MFDP delegation including Fannie Lou Hamer through the passage of the Voting Rights Act 1965. She contributed a piece on the MFDP to The Big Book for Peace.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Housing Pickets and Nazi Counter-Protesters
clips.summary: Walter recounts CORE Los Angeles housing-tract pickets in what is now Carson, California, after developers refused to sell to Black buyers. A Jewish CORE member named Herbert, a concentration-camp survivor and Freedom Rider, was on the line when four cars of men in Nazi uniforms with swastikas arrived bearing signs reading "ovens too good for niggers." A single voice began singing "Oh, Freedom"; Walter held the line and her anger left her.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: FESTAC, Ghana, and Diaspora Identity
clips.summary: Walter traveled to Nigeria as part of a U.S. delegation of writers, artists, and sculptors for the African Festival of Culture (FESTAC '77), the only children's book writer in the group. She then went to Ghana, where market women debated her ethnicity until they heard her speak. The poet Kofi Awoonor accompanied her to Elmina Castle, where standing above the slave-ship guns she felt herself "neither African nor American" but heir to both.
related_events: FESTAC '77 (Second World Black and African Festival of Arts and Culture, Lagos)

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: Reconciliation and the Peace March
clips.summary: Walter argues that until the United States holds the kind of reconciliation discussion South Africa and Australia conducted, racial healing will not occur. She references the Kerner Commission's denied recommendations and the Greensboro Truth and Reconciliation Commission (2004-06). She recounts walking from a Soviet town toward Moscow on a peace march in the 1980s, choosing the march over flying to San Francisco to receive the Coretta Scott King Award in person.

clips.subject: Mildred Pitts Walter
clips.title: People of Color in Nursing Education
clips.summary: Walter closes by describing 1970s work with a multi-ethnic group of nurses sponsored by the Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education and led by Dr. Marie F. Branch. The group coined the phrase "people of color" to replace "minorities," revised nursing textbooks to teach how to diagnose shock and cyanosis in non-white patients (who could not turn red, white, or blue), and changed hospital visiting protocols to accommodate family presence during transition.
keywords: Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Birth and Sharecropping Childhood
clips.summary: Jones describes being born in 1914 on the Claiborne County land where the interview is conducted, the family's move from Lorman, Mississippi, and growing up as the first of his father's ten children in a sharecropping household of fifteen after his mother died and his father remarried. He details the family's obligation to raise cotton, corn, and peas under a one-fourth share arrangement, with the landowner taking a portion of every crop.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: School at Egypt and Alcorn
clips.summary: Jones recounts attending a six-month school at the Egypt Methodist church where Rev. Martin's wife taught, having to leave by age nine to work the fields, and keeping up by studying assignments his younger sister Emma brought home. He describes traveling to Alcorn (College) commencements to watch baseball and his early subscription to the Pittsburgh Courier, which his future wife later mailed to him during Navy service.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Underground NAACP, 1952
clips.summary: Jones describes joining the underground NAACP around 1952 through his friend Mr. Charlie Cook, who connected him with Rev. Martin of the Egypt Methodist church. Members met covertly on Saturday afternoons when the streets were full, kept their membership cards hidden because the Klan was active, and faced retaliation if discovered. Jones recalls hearing about freedom and the Mason-Dixon Line and wanting to join despite the danger.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Navy Service in the Pacific
clips.summary: Drafted in 1944, Jones trained at Camp Shelby and Bainbridge before deploying via San Diego and Hawaii to the Pacific. As a steward and cook he served at Majuro, near Saipan and Eniwetok, taking shelter in lagoons during air and ship raids. Jones describes participating in a three-day hunger strike of African American stewards demanding to eat with the officers, which succeeded.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Voter Registration and R.L.T. Smith
clips.summary: Jones recounts going with Mr. Ellis Collins and Mr. Griffin to register to vote, being given a section of the Mississippi Constitution to copy and interpret, and being failed twice before passing. He describes Rev. R.L.T. Smith's congressional campaign and attending a voter-registration class led by a young New Yorker, Bob Moses, after which police waited outside but had been deterred from beating the participants.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Marriage and Buying the Land
clips.summary: Jones describes meeting his future wife when she came to help his brother's wife with their first child, and marrying after he returned from the Navy. He details sending his $100 monthly Navy pay home to her, saving toward land purchase, and after discharge buying 126 acres from a seller named Mr. Mehaw for approximately $1,400.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Movement Comes to Claiborne County
clips.summary: Jones describes how the local movement gained momentum when Charles Evers, Rudy Shields, George Henry Walker, and Ms. Leesco Guster came to Claiborne County to organize voter registration on the plantations. He explains the white establishment's formation of a biracial committee of preachers and teachers chosen to slow the movement, and the Black community's resistance to that containment strategy.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: NAACP Officer and Robert Seders
clips.summary: When the NAACP needed officers Jones accepted the role openly. He describes the white-Black relationships that supported him, including superintendent Robert Seders who sat in Jones's kitchen, hired his wife to teach in the school system, and hired him to drive a school bus. Mr. Eli Ellis, the vice-president at the bank, confronted Jones about joining the NAACP but had been good to him on loans and hunting visits.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: 1966 Alcorn Tear-Gas Protest
clips.summary: Jones recounts the 1966 protest at Alcorn College over student treatment and wage issues for campus workers. He describes leading students back to the dormitory through paths he knew from hauling logs by horse, the patrolmen throwing tear gas, and the beating of June Warners's uncle and a preacher from Hattiesburg. Jones was arrested with the marchers and jailed overnight while his wife took over his school-bus route.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Boycott, Marches, and Mass Meetings
clips.summary: Jones describes mass meetings with clapping and singing of "We Shall Overcome" and "Freedom," closing marches around town, and the Port Gibson boycott focused on worker pay and conditions. He explains how many residents were afraid to participate because plantation owners controlled their housing and would evict families whose children joined, and describes the local Black-head youth group that threw rocks until adult leadership broke them up.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Black Candidates and Wife's Election
clips.summary: Jones describes the difficulty of finding Black candidates willing to risk their jobs in the 1967 Claiborne County elections, recruiting Mr. Ellis Collins among the first three. He recounts his wife's 1971 election to circuit court office, his pride at her victory, and the support he received from white friends including the school superintendent and his banker.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: A-Mart Cooperative and Claiborne Hardware
clips.summary: Jones describes founding the A-Mart cooperative grocery store around 1967 with twenty-five-dollar shares, with Rev. R.L.T. Smith as the leading shareholder, Alexander Collins second, and the Joneses third. He recounts the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware lawsuit alleging the boycott had cut white merchants' profits, which the NAACP won in 1982 after years of litigation that tied up his property without preventing him from farming with the help of white friends.

clips.subject: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
clips.title: Reflections and Family Logging
clips.summary: Jones closes with reflections on the difficulty of carrying NAACP cards in his early years (after hearing of a young man killed for having one), the importance of his organizational training, and his pride at participating openly once it became possible. He describes his large family working together logging and picking cotton on plantations, often four members harvesting over two hundred pounds in a day.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Names and Origins
clips.summary: Mtume introduces herself, explaining she was born Norma Stoker in San Diego in 1949, married into the surname Armour during her BPP years, and took the name Mtume from her second husband Omawale Mtume, who chose the Kiswahili name meaning "the sun returns" during the 1960s name-change movement.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: South Central LA Childhood
clips.summary: Mtume describes her parents, Doris Sims from Louisiana and her father from Longview, Texas, who became a librarian in San Diego, her move to Los Angeles at age four after her parents separated, and growing up poor at 21st and Naomi in South Central. She characterizes the racial geography of her neighborhood as predominantly Black with few Latinos and Asians, and describes coming to school with cardboard in worn-out shoes.
related_events: Great Migration to California war industries

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Jefferson High and Cal State LA
clips.summary: Mtume recounts her growing political consciousness in the mid-1960s as boyfriends and older neighbors were drafted to Vietnam, the 1965 Watts uprising, her graduation from Jefferson High School in 1967 on a $250 scholarship, and enrollment at Cal State LA where she joined an active Black Student Union and met Albert Armour, who later became her first husband and a Black Panther Party member.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Joining the Black Panther Party
clips.summary: Mtume describes Albert Armour's BPP involvement, Muhammad Speaks newspaper, Westwood anti-war rally, and her own awakening when police raided the Los Angeles BPP offices in December 1969, beating Armour and putting him in the hospital with a broken arm and damaged eye socket. By January 1970 she was volunteering at the 44th Street office, typing memos, working the breakfast program, and being assigned to the new clinic.
related_events: LAPD raids on BPP offices December 1969

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Running the Bunchy Carter Free Clinic
clips.summary: At nineteen, Mtume became coordinator of the Alprentice "Bunchy" Carter People's Free Clinic at 32nd and Central. She describes the storefront build-out by a lab tech student's contractor father, the three exam rooms and pharmacy area, pharmaceutical-rep donations of medication samples, and the volunteer mentorship of Marie F. Branch (RN, University of California, Los Angeles nursing instructor) and Dr. Terry Kupers (psychiatrist). All care was free; outreach went through paper sales and BPP newspaper articles.
related_events: BPP 10-point platform, point four health care

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Communal Life and Daily Routines
clips.summary: Mtume describes communal BPP life with her son and infant daughter, the 5:30 AM start for the breakfast program, the rotating clinic shifts running to 10 or 11 PM, the children's nursery and school in Oakland where kids were assigned 24-hour caretakers, political education classes, and security guard duty in response to the Fred Hampton assassination. She frames the work ethic as unsustainable but necessary.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Police Harassment and Surveillance
clips.summary: Mtume describes constant police surveillance and harassment in Los Angeles, being stopped while doing laundry by officers carrying photographic portfolios of BPP members, daily checks for unmarked cars when leaving any BPP facility, and her observation that male members were arrested and beaten as soon as they walked out the gate with newspapers. Members assessed the personal commitment required against the certainty of state violence.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Women in the Party
clips.summary: Mtume reflects on coping mechanisms, alcohol and drug use among members who had brought those patterns from the community, the involvement of Dr. Terry Kupers and Dr. Shapiro as occasional psychiatric support, the absence of regular mental-health services, and on women's experience in the BPP. She describes the principled effort to assign men to children's centers and cooking but acknowledges persistent chauvinism, eased somewhat as women rose into leadership. She characterizes the lifelong bonds between Panther women veterans.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Texas Trip and First Racism
clips.summary: Mtume recounts her father's annual driving trips to Texas with the family relying on The Negro Motorist Green Book and Black-owned motels, then a specific 1958 trip when a Black Baptist convention had filled the El Paso motels and her father had to take coffee and donuts from a restaurant's back door, the Jim Crow signage along the route, and the segregated drive-in movie theater with bleachers behind the cars. She describes this as her first conscious encounter with discrimination.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: BPP Survival Programs
clips.summary: Mtume catalogs the LA chapter's survival programs: free food bags pre-dating food banks, free clothing and coat distribution, the SAFE program (Seniors Against a Fearful Environment) escorting elders to banks and grocery stores, free busing to prisons (which her own mother used to visit Mtume's incarcerated brother at San Quentin), legal-aid coordination with pro-bono attorneys and BPP paralegals, the Oakland school and dormitory system, and outreach clinics modeled on Chinese and Cuban barefoot-doctor programs.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: US Organization Conflict
clips.summary: Mtume describes the rivalry with Ron Karenga's US Organization, the January 1969 killings of Alprentice "Bunchy" Carter and John Huggins at UCLA where Albert Armour was attending, her own assault by US Organization members while selling papers pregnant with her daughter, and her assessment that Karenga ordered the shootings in collaboration with police. She also describes the LA street-corner ecology where BPP, US Organization, Nation of Islam, and Holy Ghost Filipino Christian groups each claimed paper-selling territory.
related_events: UCLA killings of Bunchy Carter and John Huggins January 1969

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: George Jackson Free Clinic, Berkeley
clips.summary: After the 1972 call for chapters to consolidate north for political campaigns, Mtume was assigned to direct the George Jackson Free Clinic in Berkeley, named for the Soledad Brother killed at San Quentin in August 1971. She describes coalition work with the Berkeley Free Clinic, the Women's Health Collective, the Haight-Ashbury Free Clinic for substance-abuse training, Community Services United, UC Berkeley's Black Health Science Caucus, and Vietnam corpsmen who taught suture and emergency-care skills. She references discovering her own cervical cancer through clinic practice exams.
related_events: George Jackson death at San Quentin August 1971
Roe v. Wade era women's health movement

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Minister of Finance Under Elaine Brown
clips.summary: After Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale departed and Elaine Brown took leadership, Mtume was appointed BPP Minister of Finance in late 1974. She describes the tithing system (10% of any member's income), property and utilities payments, the 501(c)(3) incorporation of the school and a social-service wing that brought in law-enforcement grants and government funding to sustain the survival programs, and her shift from informal ledgers to formal accounting feeding CPA-prepared 990 tax returns.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Newton's Return, Arrest, and Prison
clips.summary: Mtume recounts the late-period crisis after Huey P. Newton's return from Cuba, Newton's order to attack a witness from his prior prostitute-shooting case, the wrong-house shooting in which a Panther died carrying ID with Mtume's communal address, the resulting police search of her house that produced weapons charges, her expulsion from the party so resources could go to Newton's defense, her two-year California State Prison sentence served at Frontera (twelve to thirteen months), her three-month reception-center hold while administrators worried about prison organizing, and her work earning an AA degree while incarcerated.
keywords: Huey P. Newton return from Cuba
related_events: Huey P. Newton's 1977 return from Cuban exile

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: SHIELDS for Families
clips.summary: After prison and degrees in healthcare administration and marriage-and-family therapy, Mtume worked at LA County Alcohol and Drug Program before co-founding SHIELDS for Families in 1990, initially under Charles R. Drew University of Medicine and Science before forming its own 501(c)(3). She describes building from $500,000 and 14 employees to $25 million and 350 employees across 28-29 programs serving women whose children, over 1,200 per year at Martin Luther King Jr.-Drew Medical Center, were born prenatally exposed to crack. Diane Watson's state legislation funded the work.
keywords: Martin Luther King Jr.-Drew Medical Center
related_events: 1980s-1990s crack epidemic in South Central Los Angeles

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Drew University Community Faculty
clips.summary: Mtume describes her post-retirement role as community faculty at Charles R. Drew University of Medicine and Science, believed to be the only such program in the country, training medical, nursing, and MPH students in the social determinants of health, working alongside University of California, Los Angeles research faculty on diversity in medical education curriculum, and addressing the gap between prescriptions written and patients' actual ability or willingness to fill them.

clips.subject: Norma Mtume
clips.title: Legacy and Coalitions
clips.summary: Mtume closes by reflecting on what she wants young people to understand about the BPP, that the armed-self-defense framing was necessary but secondary to the survival-pending-revolution programs, and that the Party worked across coalitions with the Brown Berets, with César Chávez's farm workers, the Gray Panthers, the anti-war movement, and the women's movement. She notes she did not see her trajectory in advance and credits her BPP skills for what she has built since.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Richmond Childhood and Family
clips.summary: Hill Jr. introduces his 1949 birth in segregated Richmond, the self-contained Black community structure around Virginia Union University, and the threats and cross burning his family faced because of his father's NAACP work. He describes how his parents shielded him from racism while preparing him for the role of desegregating Richmond schools.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Father's Path to Howard Law
clips.summary: Hill Jr. traces his father Oliver W. Hill Sr.'s upbringing in Roanoke and Washington, attendance at Dunbar High School, and entry into Howard Law School in the late 1920s under President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and Dean Charles Hamilton Houston. He describes Houston's social-engineer charge and Hill Sr.'s classmate relationship with Thurgood Marshall.
related_events: Howard Law School transformation under Houston

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: NAACP-LDF and Virginia Cases
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes his father's work with the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund founded in 1940 by Thurgood Marshall, the Virginia teacher salary case of the 1940s, his father's World War II service, and Hill Sr.'s 1948 election to the Richmond City Council as the first Black elected official in the Deep South since Reconstruction.
related_events: Morgan v. Virginia interstate bus segregation case

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Barbara Rose Johns and Davis Case
clips.summary: Hill Jr. recounts Barbara Rose Johns's call to his father from Farmville, the student walkout at the Black high school with tar paper shacks for overflow, and the lawyers' strategic shift from separate-but-equal to attacking segregation per se. He notes Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County became part of the Brown package, and identifies Vernon Johns as Barbara Rose Johns's uncle.
related_events: Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Massive Resistance in Virginia
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes Virginia's massive resistance led by the Byrd Machine and Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr., the pupil placement board mechanism that controlled school assignments, and his father's working relationship with school board head Lewis F. Powell Jr. He distinguishes between desegregation and integration as his father saw the difference.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Desegregating Chandler Middle School
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes entering Chandler Middle School in seventh grade as one of the first Black students to desegregate Richmond schools, the school board measuring distances by yardstick to keep Black students out, the surreal first day with a partial walkout, and the gradual social integration through football teammates over years.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Busing, White Flight, and Annexation
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr.'s busing order covering Richmond and surrounding counties, the threats and killed dog Merhige endured, William Rehnquist's non-recusal producing a 4-4 Supreme Court split that overturned the cross-county remedy, the Richmond annexation moratorium under the Voting Rights Act, and the morph of race politics into class politics.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Howard University and Inward Turn
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes attending Howard University in the late 1960s during the black power era with Stokely Carmichael and H. Rap Brown on campus, a Howard course in African history that opened him to civilizations he had never heard of, and his turn toward Eastern philosophy, meditation, and a perception course that drew him into psychology.
related_events: Howard University black power era late 1960s

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Loss of Black Teachers
clips.summary: Hill Jr. reflects on the high quality of teachers in segregated Black schools, the unintended consequence of integration that drained the Black teacher pool as the women's movement opened other careers, and the spiraling decline of schools in Petersburg where he now works with four superintendents turning over since 2005.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Recognition of His Father's Legacy
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes the Arthur Ashe statue controversy, the objection to a Lincoln statue at the Tredegar Iron Works, the Virginia Civil Rights Memorial on the Capitol grounds depicting Barbara Rose Johns alongside his father and Spottswood W. Robinson III, and the building on Capitol grounds named for his father where Harry F. Byrd Sr. had once strategized massive resistance. He notes Clinton's 1999 Presidential Medal of Freedom to his father.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Psychology Career and the Algebra Project
clips.summary: Hill Jr. describes his career at Virginia State University researching Black education and cognitive factors, the malleability of cognitive skills challenging 1950s heredity claims, and his partnership with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg. He explains Moses's approach to abstract math teaching grounded in children's life experiences and his civil-rights model of community-based school reform.

clips.subject: Oliver W. Hill Jr.
clips.title: Education as Civil Rights Battleground
clips.summary: Hill Jr. critiques Virginia's Standards of Learning curriculum-narrowing, characterizes contemporary urban schooling as a national disgrace, and frames education and mass incarceration as the continuing civil rights battleground. He references Michelle Alexander's The New Jim Crow and connects the prison-industrial complex to collapsed family structures and inner-city schools.
related_events: Virginia Standards of Learning testing regime

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Hattiesburg Childhood and Citizenship Class
clips.summary: Connor describes growing up in Hattiesburg, delivered by Dr. Charles Smith. Her seventh-grade teacher Ruby M. Tatum taught a first-semester citizenship class that emphasized registering to vote at twenty-one. Her father worked at a lumber yard and later as a chauffeur and washed windows downtown; her mother was a housewife.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Beauty Shop and Family Trade
clips.summary: Connor began shampooing hair at her aunt's beauty shop at age eleven for ten cents per shampoo. She graduated from her aunt's Franklin Beauty School in 1946 and inherited the shop when her aunt left Hattiesburg. The trade ran through her extended family, including a beauty-school operation in Houston, Texas.
related_events: Black-owned beauty-shop economy in mid-twentieth-century Mississippi

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Ten Years to Register and Poll-Tax Collection
clips.summary: Connor describes a ten-year effort to register to vote that succeeded only after a federal court order forced Theron Lynd. She paid the two-year poll tax and collected poll taxes for her beauty-shop customers, walking lists into the circuit clerk's office. She finally received her registration letter in January 1964, twelve days before Freedom Day.
related_events: Federal court order against Theron Lynd

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Family, Marriage, and Daughter's Polio
clips.summary: Connor recounts her brief marriage to an alcoholic veteran who returned changed from the Korean War, her family's separation, and her second daughter's polio illness. The child required iron-lung treatment in Big Spring, Texas, and later Laurel, Mississippi. The daughter died around 1953 from brain aneurysms secondary to polio. Connor moved back with her parents, whose father became a primary caregiver to her surviving children.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Entering the Movement Through COFO and SNCC
clips.summary: The COFO office across from Connor's beauty shop drew her attention. Guyot crossed the street and invited her to a mass meeting where Fannie Lou Hamer spoke. Victoria Gray Adams then recruited her to a non-violence workshop at the Dorchester Center taught by Dorothy F. Cotton and Andrew Young. Returning to Hattiesburg, Connor approached True Light Church and began teaching citizenship classes after Wednesday-night prayer meetings.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: NAACP and Son's Arrest
clips.summary: Connor joined the NAACP partly as insurance, when a Hattiesburg shoeshine-stand altercation led to her son's arrest, an NAACP card was required for help. Two policemen contradicted each other in court, and the judge ordered the father to take the boy out of Hattiesburg. Her son was hidden in a car trunk to Louisiana and lived in California until the 1970s.
related_events: Targeted policing of Black families in Hattiesburg

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Freedom Day and Hosting Rep. Conyers
clips.summary: Connor recalls Freedom Day in Hattiesburg, January 1964, when ministers and out-of-state workers came down in the pouring rain. Rep. John Conyers Jr. stayed at her house with FBI agents posted for security. Her father took night shifts at the door with a shotgun across his lap and a cane.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Daughter Felicia Integrates Jefferson Davis Elementary
clips.summary: Under freedom-of-choice, Connor enrolled her daughter Felicia at Jefferson Davis Elementary School on Seventh Street and Hardy. Felicia was the only Black child in her class; teachers held her at the door until all white children were seated. Connor describes the registrar asking which school and her response that it was "the one across from Hercules."

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Courthouse March and Jail Time
clips.summary: After Freedom Day, Connor joined a courthouse picket that the chief officer halted, ordering marchers back to the jail. About sixty were arrested that morning; the count grew to roughly one hundred. Connor was moved to a city farm to keep her from organizing other prisoners. They sang songs, told stories, and drank from a dipper between cells. Released after about a week when a Hattiesburg couple posted their house as $1,000 bond.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Organizing the MFDP and Atlantic City
clips.summary: Connor was elected as a delegate of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party to the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City. Three buses brought the delegation. The party had registered better than 3,000 members. Connor describes Lyndon Johnson stopping the MFDP through the two-seat compromise, the men in the delegation inclined to accept on Adam Clayton Powell Jr.'s advice, and the women, led by Fannie Lou Hamer, refusing.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Vernon Dahmer Firebombing and NAACP Tensions
clips.summary: Connor recalls Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s firebombing on January 10, 1966, visiting him in the hospital while he was still talking about trying to escape the burning house, learning of his death about thirty minutes after leaving. She states the NAACP had previously expelled Dahmer for working with SNCC. She describes the still-smoking house the morning after, and the rumors that some white Hattiesburg residents discreetly aided Movement workers, including a William Carey College professor.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Head Start, CDGM, and the Drugstore Suit
clips.summary: Connor describes her work as a system community worker on the Child Development Group of Mississippi (CDGM) Head Start program, the first such program in the United States, originally explained to MFDP delegates by Sargent Shriver in Washington. She was later moved out of an executive role, she suspects, for political activity. Connor also recounts her 1964 sit-in attempt at a Hattiesburg drugstore lunch counter and the four-year lawsuit she filed against the owner.

clips.subject: Peggy Jean Connor
clips.title: Connor v. Johnson and Movement Reflections
clips.summary: Connor explains that as MFDP executive secretary she became the named plaintiff in Connor v. Johnson, the suit that redrew Mississippi legislative district lines and produced Black-majority districts in Jackson. She describes board meetings at Rev. R.L.T. Smith's house in Jackson with New York lawyers in khaki pants. She reflects on present-day Black elected officials, the 2011 oral history that finally drew her out of shyness, and Southern Mississippi's transformation since the 1960s.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Peekskill 1949 Robeson Attacks
clips.summary: Seeger recounts the September 1949 attacks on Paul Robeson's two Peekskill, NY concerts. He argues that J. Edgar Hoover had already prepared concentration camps for roughly 12,000 people and that President Truman had sent a high official (probably the Secretary of State) to address the American Legion in July 1949 before the violence. Seeger credits public exposure of the mob attacks with derailing Hoover's mass-internment plans.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Highlander 1957 Anniversary
clips.summary: Seeger describes first visiting Highlander Folk School in 1940 with Woody Guthrie at Alan Lomax's suggestion, then returning in 1957 for the school's 25th anniversary after Zilphia Horton's death. Myles Horton invited him to sing. Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, and Rosa Parks drove up from Montgomery; the group was photographed in front of the barn. This was Seeger's first meeting with King.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: King at the UN Peace Rally
clips.summary: Seeger's second and final meeting with King occurred a year before King's assassination, at an 80,000-person peace rally outside the United Nations the day after King's Riverside Church "Beyond Vietnam" speech of April 4, 1967. Seeger recalls King's words about the United States as "the greatest purveyor of violence in the world" and speculates that Lyndon B. Johnson's reaction to this speech reopened the channel between LBJ and Hoover.
related_events: MLK Riverside Church Beyond Vietnam speech April 4, 1967

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Montgomery Bus Boycott Origins
clips.summary: Seeger describes calling E.D. Nixon at the Montgomery Improvement Association after reading a small New York Times notice about the bus boycott. He relates Nixon's account of why Rosa Parks rather than a pregnant teenager became the test case, Nixon's recruitment of the new minister King to host the meeting at his church, and King's central instruction that the boycott would succeed only through nonviolence. Seeger then turns to Eugene "Bull" Connor, whom he had heard about in 1940 while staying near Birmingham with Joe Gelders.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: We Shall Overcome Transmission
clips.summary: Seeger traces the song's path: Zilphia Horton taught him the song in 1946 in NYC, and he printed it (imperfectly) in People's Songs magazine. The breakthrough came when Guy Carawan and Frank Hamilton learned to perform it in 12/8 time, drawing on Carawan's work with gospel singing at a local Baptist church. At Highlander's 1960 "Singing in the Movement" weekend, the new rhythm became the hit; five weeks later in Raleigh, NC, SNCC adopted both the song and the cross-armed swaying tradition.
related_events: Highlander Singing in the Movement weekend 1960

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Carnegie Hall and Freedom Singers
clips.summary: Seeger recounts his 1963 Carnegie Hall concert, paid for by Columbia Records through John H. Hammond Jr., who had earlier produced the 1938 "From Spirituals to Swing" concert at Carnegie. Seeger describes meeting Bernice Johnson Reagon and Cordell Reagon in Albany, Georgia in 1962, his approach to SNCC's Atlanta office proposing the singers travel like the Fisk Jubilee Singers to raise funds, and Toshi-Aline Ohta Seeger's organizing of the Freedom Singers' first national tour using contacts from his college circuit.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Mississippi Freedom Summer
clips.summary: Seeger describes flying into Jackson, Mississippi in 1964 with William Kunstler and being confronted at the airport by a passenger who had overheard his remarks on the plane. He recounts performing in a church for 200 people when a note arrived informing him that the bodies of Andrew Goodman, Michael Schwerner, and James Chaney had been found. He sang Fred Hellerman's "Healing River" in response.
related_events: Murder of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney June 1964

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Selma to Montgomery March
clips.summary: Seeger and Toshi-Aline Ohta Seeger received a telegram from King inviting them to the March 1965 Selma-to-Montgomery march. They joined roughly 300 marchers about a mile outside Selma. Seeger remembers Harry Belafonte urging marchers on, the new way of singing "We Shall Overcome" with the "What do we want? Freedom! When do we want it? Now!" call-and-response that countered the song's "someday" softness, and helicopters watching for snipers over the cornfields.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Late 1960s and King's Final Years
clips.summary: Asked about the late 1960s, the split in SNCC, and King's expanding focus on economic and foreign-policy issues, Seeger speculates that King knew he would be assassinated and that his final speech was a charge to others to pick up the torch. Seeger reflects on his own uncertainty about humanity's chances and emphasizes the importance of small local efforts.

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Spirit of Beacon Day
clips.summary: Seeger describes a local example of organizing in Beacon, NY: a retired widow and other women created Spirit of Beacon Day after a high school race riot. The annual event closes Main Street and brings together churches, civic clubs, the Beacon Sloop Club, and the River Town Kids music ensemble Seeger taught. Two-thirds of the town now attends; the day grew from a few hundred people to roughly ten thousand.
related_events: Beacon NY high school race riot

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Obama, Hayes, and Corporate Power
clips.summary: Asked about Obama and the long arc of history, Seeger says he wishes he had told Obama in person not to plan for re-election but to do the best job possible and trust contrast with opponents. He then offers a long historical excursus on Rutherford B. Hayes, the contested 1876 election, the federal-troops withdrawal compromise, and the 1886 Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific Railroad decision, citing Hayes's later observation that the country had become "a government of corporations, by corporations, for corporations."
related_events: Santa Clara County v. Southern Pacific Railroad 1886

clips.subject: Pete Seeger
clips.title: Dairy Strike Puppets and Closing
clips.summary: Seeger describes Camp Nitgedaiget (later Camp Beacon), a 1920s radical summer camp half a mile away founded by Jewish garment workers, where he and three others developed a puppet troupe in the 1930s. Their skit on farmers underpaid for milk had already been written when the New York Dairy Farmers Union strike broke out. The interview closes with reflections on Fannie Lou Hamer and Medgar Evers as exemplars of movement heroism.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Family Migration to Cleveland
clips.summary: Hutchings describes his parents' migration to Cleveland-his father from Macon, Georgia, as part of the Northward Migrations, working as a streetcar conductor and later postal clerk; his mother from outside Memphis, Tennessee. He outlines the family's lower-middle-class status, his father's Hatch Act restrictions as a federal employee channeling political interest through the St. James Forum, and meeting Senator Robert A. Taft at age eight in 1952.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Civic Engagement in Cleveland Youth
clips.summary: Hutchings recounts his mother's run for state representative endorsed by the Cleveland Press, her later appointment as chief referee of the Civil Service Commission under Carl B. Stokes, his paper-route campaign work, and his participation in the Archegos Club, the Council on Human Relations Youth Council, and the World Council on Human Affairs at East High School. He reflects on Cleveland's geography of East Side Black neighborhoods and Shaker Heights school integration.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: NAACP Youth Council and Dime-Store Pickets
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the spring 1960 dime-store informational pickets at Woolworth, Kresge, and Nizernos along Euclid Avenue in solidarity with Southern sit-ins, and the NAACP's dissolution of his youth chapter in response. He explains how the NAACP leadership saw itself as lawyers and viewed direct action as threatening sweetheart deals with Cleveland's white power elite, then introduces the Circle Pines Club connection and the Fair Play for Cuba Committee his mother warned him against.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Howard University and the NAG
clips.summary: Hutchings describes his mother's last-minute decision to redirect him from Ohio State to Howard University, the foreign-student presence from the Caribbean, Africa, and Iran, and Kennedy's post-debate campus visit. He details the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG)-Stokely Carmichael, Courtland Cox, Ed Brown, Tom Kahn, Bayard Rustin's mentorship-and Project Awareness, which brought Malcolm X and Herbert Aptheker to campus.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: First Arrest at the White Rice Inn
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the spring 1961 sit-in at a Chinese restaurant in Baltimore under Maryland's public accommodations law, working with CORE under Julius Hobson. After initially leaving when the owner refused to call police, the group returned and sat on the steps until arrested. Hutchings characterizes the experience as a personal liberation-making a stand that placed movement work above family and career expectations.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Howard Faculty and Gender in NAG
clips.summary: Hutchings characterizes Howard as a 13th-grade colonial institution, with most faculty cowed by federal funding and the McCarthy aftermath. He names Robert Martin and a French resistance veteran named Paul as exceptions. He then addresses gender in NAG, naming Jean Bell, Bill Mahoney, Jean Wheeler, Frank Smith, and Ruth Howard as co-organizers, and explains how the group's refusal of campus recognition strengthened cross-school organizing in DC.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Mississippi Summer with MFDP
clips.summary: Hutchings describes his 1964 work in the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge under the Second Congressional District-Ruleville, where Fannie Lou Hamer organized-gathering voter-registration signatures to take out of state for the Atlantic City challenge. He observes Ivanhoe Donaldson and others doing voter work and reflects on the organizing discipline of speaking with people on their own terms without dumbing down ideas, and Hamer's use of song to capture room sentiment.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Fear, Faith, and Mississippi as Battleground
clips.summary: Hutchings rejects framing himself as a person of faith, then describes Mississippi as a war zone comparable to Vietnam veterans' descriptions-a pressure cooker where snipers and state officials made daily life precarious. He describes communal project houses, singing as collective courage, and being tear-gassed at the 1964 Cambridge, Maryland confrontation against George Wallace with Reggie Robinson and H. Rap Brown.
related_events: Bruce Berry/Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Atlantic City Aftermath and Robert F. Kennedy
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the depression that settled on the movement after the 1964 MFDP defeat at Atlantic City, and his post-summer work in the Robert F. Kennedy Senate campaign as research and Speaker's Bureau staff. He rationalizes the work as believing Kennedy understood the movement experience and could grow politically beyond his then-current position.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Newark and the SDS/SNCC Bridge
clips.summary: Hutchings describes how Tom Hayden recruited him at a party in December 1964 to SDS's Economic Research and Action Project (ERAP) in Newark as part of a planned statewide New Jersey movement. He explains being in both SNCC and SDS simultaneously, his 1964 visit to the SDS Ann Arbor conference where he met Richard Flacks, Al Haber, and David Strauss, and his 1965 organizing of the first major SDS anti-Vietnam War march in Washington with Paul Booth.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: NCUP, Poverty Program, Central Ward Power
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the Newark Community Union Project's (NCUP) shift from unemployment to housing and police-brutality organizing, the break with the Clinton Hill Neighborhood Association, and the strategic decision to take over the District 3 area board of the federal poverty program-hiring Jesse Allen as paid organizer with Bessie Smith as chairperson. He recounts the tradeoff of office-based work that drew organizers away from doorstep community contact, and his nomination as community action director defeated by United Community Corporation leadership.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Newark Allies and Black Power Turn
clips.summary: Hutchings describes confrontations with Newark Democratic Party operatives including Eulis 'Honey' Ward, the alliance with white funder Derek Winans and his community newspaper The Newark Advance, Star-Ledger reporter coverage, Donald Malafronte as Mayor Hugh Addonizio's chief of staff cast as 'arch bad guy,' and Stokely Carmichael's 1966 visit to recruit him to SNCC's Black Power direction. Hutchings shifted NCUP's Central Ward framing from interracial to Black-power-explicit organizing.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Reformist vs. Radical and Press Strategy
clips.summary: Hutchings articulates the political theory that the Democratic Party functioned as a Northern equivalent of Southern sharecropping-a bonded system catching Black initiative-and explains the United Freedom Party third-line strategy with former Newark Assemblyman George Richardson. He describes SNCC's dependence on white mainstream press to reach Black audiences for lack of independent Black media, and Carmichael's frequent misquotation in that ecosystem.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Inside the Newark Rebellion
clips.summary: Hutchings recounts being at the corner of 17th Avenue and Springfield Avenue when the July 1967 Newark Rebellion ignited at the police station holding taxi driver John Smith. He describes deciding with neighborhood figure BJ to channel crowd action only against white-owned property, being beaten by police with rubber truncheons alongside bartender Joe Whitley near the Hayes Homes after shouting 'Black Power,' and using civil rights nonviolent training to protect his head from blows.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: First National Conference on Black Power
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the political logic of moving from community to urban to national Black power, working with Nathan Wright Jr. to organize the First National Conference on Black Power after the Newark Rebellion, with LeRoi Jones bringing modern Black nationalism focused on Newark as the battleground. He frames the 1970 Kenneth A. Gibson Black and Puerto Rican mayoral coalition with Hilda Hidalgo, which won when four Italian candidates split the white vote.
keywords: First National Conference on Black Power
related_events: 1967 First National Conference on Black Power Newark

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: SNCC Chairmanship and Israel
clips.summary: Hutchings describes SNCC's financial and political costs from anti-Vietnam War positioning and the 1966 Jack Minnis research paper criticizing Zionism, which alienated liberal allies. He recounts being mobbed in New York City when carrying the SNCC newspaper with the Israel article. He frames the liberalism/radicalism rupture around Vietnam and Israel that preceded his chairmanship.
related_events: Martin Luther King Riverside Church April 1967

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: COINTELPRO and the Panther Merger
clips.summary: Hutchings describes COINTELPRO operations including the FBI dropping $100 on New Jersey SNCC organizer Irving Davis's bed for information, and the contested SNCC-Black Panther merger negotiated by Eldridge Cleaver from jail, with Carmichael as Prime Minister, James Forman as Foreign Minister, and H. Rap Brown as Minister of Justice announced at Huey Newton's February 17 1968 Oakland birthday rally. Hutchings, then chair, voted that the Panther program was good but not SNCC's, ending the merger as physical tensions rose.
related_events: February 17 1968 Huey Newton birthday rally Oakland

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Black Liberators and SNCC's Decline
clips.summary: Hutchings describes spending time in 1968 with the Black Liberators in St. Louis, getting arrested with them and acquitted after William Kunstler and Hutchings's lawyer-uncle played his speech to the jury (police used an old anti-labor 'unlawful assembly' statute unused since the 1930s). He reflects on SNCC's downward trajectory since Atlantic City, his strategy of a grassroots national network bridging SNCC with local groups like Black Liberators, JOMO in Florida, and the post-Watts LA formations.

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Cuba and the Venceremos Brigade
clips.summary: Hutchings describes the Venceremos Brigade origin: the Vietnamese delegation redirected U.S. radicals from fighting in Vietnam to working in Cuba. SNCC, SDS, the Chicano movement and others organized the first brigade of 200 mostly-white participants and a second brigade of over 800 in March 1970. He recounts staying at the Habana Libre hotel, meeting Ernesto from Angola at Isla de la Juventud, conversations with Vietnamese organizers, and Cuban discipline that recalibrated 'CP time.'

clips.subject: Phil Hutchings
clips.title: Immigration and Continuing Struggle
clips.summary: Hutchings closes with reflections on his current organizing at the Black Alliance for Just Immigration, contrasting 1960s logic-which sought federal intervention against segregationist state officials-with immigration work that confronts the federal government as the architect of NAFTA and global economic dislocation. He frames immigration as integrating race, class, and increasingly gender into the analytic frame begun in the 1960s.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Growing Up in Lincolnville
clips.summary: Conway describes his birth in 1948 and his upbringing in Lincolnville, St. Augustine's Black self-sufficient community. He was raised after age five by his great-great-grandmother, of Seminole and Spanish heritage, who taught him respect, honesty, and acknowledgment of elders. He spent his childhood at the little links (the only Black park in St. Augustine, today the Galimore Center) under instructor Mr. Eddie Lee Vickers.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Paper Boy at the Castillo
clips.summary: At age ten, Conway ventured across Washington Street into the white area to ask the St. Augustine Record for a paper-boy job. After selling poorly in Lincolnville, he discovered the Castillo de San Marcos as a sales location and outsold the white paper boys until two of them followed him and tourists began buying from them instead. He learned the geography of racial commerce in downtown St. Augustine.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: High School and Awakening
clips.summary: Conway describes challenging teachers at R.J. Murray High School over outdated textbooks and reading the medical books left by his uncle Edward Norton. He recounts the high school DCT work-study program and his anger watching white storekeepers and customers address his mother as Julia while she had to say Miss to them. His employer at an Anastasia Island grocery store refused to sign his school paperwork until he threatened to quit.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Florida Memorial Students and Nonviolent Training
