clips.summary: Conway recalls hearing male Florida Memorial College students discuss civil rights demonstration tactics at the segregated college pool, the only one open to Black youth in summers. Drawn initially to Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X, he was persuaded by the college students to embrace nonviolence. At fifteen he committed to demonstrating, telling his mother he had to do this for his future and his children's future.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Lawnmower Job and Family Mobilization
clips.summary: Conway recounts the day his mother sent him to mow a white friend's yard on Anastasia Island. The white woman served him a sandwich next to her dog's food bowl. His mother, called by Conway from the woman's phone, came to retrieve him and afterward joined the movement. Conway's father and three brothers were all arrested one night while Conway, the only one not jailed, stayed with his great-great-grandmother.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: First Wade-in at St. Augustine Beach
clips.summary: Conway recounts a wade-in at St. Augustine Beach with four other teenagers and a tall white organizer. A white mob converged from north and south. A young Black girl locked herself in the car. Conway was punched in the face by a white teenager and watched the white organizer beaten over the head with a pipe before a lone police car arrived and the mob dispersed. Hosea Williams responded with a larger second wade-in two weeks later.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Second Wade-in and the State Troopers
clips.summary: Conway describes the second wade-in two weeks later: a cordon of helmeted Florida state troopers, ordered to St. Augustine by Governor Farris Bryant, walked into waist-deep water and beat the Klan and their sympathizers out of the surf with bats and chains. Conway saw the water turn bloody. Dr. Hayling adds that the US Justice Department was also involved in pressuring the state to act.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Movie Theater Confrontation and Police Dog
clips.summary: Conway describes a young white tutor who took Black children to the downtown movie theater shortly after passage of the Civil Rights Act. When the children fled screaming and a white man on a bicycle chased them with a knife, Conway intervened. Two hulking policemen surrounded him with a German Shepherd; when his Black friends taunted the officers, the police chased them and abandoned Conway, who outran the dog back to Washington Street and hid in a church yard for two days.
related_events: Post-Civil Rights Act testing of integration

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Northern College Volunteers
clips.summary: Conway describes how Northern white college students, including Gail Blattenberg from Princeton, NJ, changed his perspective on whites. The students invited Black St. Augustine youth back to their Northern homes after the tutorial program ended. Conway's mother resisted, but the white students made picket signs in front of the Conway home until she relented. Conway spent two weeks with the Blattenberg family in New Jersey.
related_events: Northern volunteer participation in St. Augustine

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Daytona Trade School and Move North
clips.summary: Conway attended a Daytona Beach junior college trade school after high school and met his wife, who attended Bethune-Cookman. After a year and a half in a brick masonry program, two white students were enrolled and after three months given certificates while Black students were told they had to take an additional six months of carpentry class. Conway's instructor was fired for protesting. Conway refused the settlement, married, and moved to his wife's family home in New York.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: NYPD and Promotional-Exam Lawsuits
clips.summary: After five years in the New York City post office, Conway passed the NYPD exam. He describes the buddy system in NYPD promotions as a racial barrier and his role as a plaintiff in successful lawsuits against the city for discriminatory promotional exams. The sergeants test lawsuit produced over 300 minority sergeants who were called quota sergeants. Conway later filed and won a similar suit on the lieutenants exam; the criteria established in those suits is still used by police departments nationwide.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Return to Florida and Lincolnville Today
clips.summary: Conway moved back to Palm Coast, Florida. He describes seeing abandoned houses, drug damage, and what he recognizes as red-lining patterns from New York repeating in Lincolnville: banks not lending, taxes rising, gentrification displacing Black residents. His current advocacy focus is reopening the Galimore Center pool, which has been closed over two years; he petitioned the city commissioners with historical context.

clips.subject: Purcell Maurice Conway
clips.title: Parents and ACCORD Tribute
clips.summary: Conway pays tribute to his parents Julia and George Conway. He recounts how his father's white butcher coworker at the Anastasia Island grocery store threatened to quit if George was fired for marching, and how a judge greeted his father by name at a local bank during a court-ordered dispute. His mother Julia worked at Flagler Hospital training nurses and was fired before her tenth year to deny her a pension. Conway closes by thanking Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and the ACCORD organization, plus his neighbor Mrs. Barbara Vickers who organized the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers Monument.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Introductions and Free State of Jones
clips.summary: Emilye Crosby opens the December 1, 2015 interview at the USM Center for Oral History and Cultural Heritage. Smith introduces herself as born January 1, 1940 in Jones County, Mississippi, and recounts her great-great-grandmother Rachel's relationship with Captain Newton Knight, Knight's desertion from the Confederacy, and Dr. Victoria E. Bynum's documentation of the Free State of Jones lineage.
related_events: Civil War desertion from the Confederacy
Publication of Victoria Bynum's Free State of Jones

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Branch's Hattiesburg Origins
clips.summary: Branch describes her birth at 711 Whitney Street in her grandmother's three-room shotgun house, the Hattiesburg neighborhood that included Miss Emma Campbell and Mr. Kelly, and her family's land out around Prentiss, Mississippi at Mount Carmel. She recounts her great-grandparents purchasing 80 acres outright, disputing the 'forty acres and a mule' narrative.
related_events: Post-Reconstruction Black land ownership in Jefferson Davis County

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Chicago Migration and Emmett Till
clips.summary: Branch describes her father's blood disorder and the family's loss of two Chicago homes, their period of homelessness, and her mother's firing from Brock Candy Company after lunching with a Black co-worker. She recounts seeing the Jet magazine photograph of Emmett Till in his casket as her awakening to the depth of racial violence.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Cajun and Mixed-Race Lineage
clips.summary: Branch traces her maternal grandmother Phanimae Holloway's flight from Prentiss after a rape by a plantation owner, settlement in Hattiesburg, and marriage to a Cajun named Frank Elsie from Southern Louisiana. She describes the 'one drop' classification system and the social ostracism faced by her mother Alpharenzia from both Black and white communities.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Newton Knight Descendants and Protection
clips.summary: Branch recounts how her grandfather Colonel Robert Hall, a green-eyed and blonde Newton Knight descendant, was permitted by Jim Crow custom to sell guns and ammunition that Black storeowners could not. She describes her grandfather wearing Klan sheets to infiltrate a Soso meeting and force the Klan to leave Jones County alone, and the protective recognition Newton Knight's descendants received from white kin.
related_events: Ku Klux Klan terrorism in Jones and Forrest counties

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Integrating USM in 1965
clips.summary: Branch describes enrolling at the University of Southern Mississippi in September 1965 with Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong, both Rowan High School graduates. She names Vernon Dahmer, James Bourne, and Elaine Armstrong as key supporters, discusses President William D. McCain's John Birch Society membership, and contrasts the calm of USM's integration with James Meredith's experience at Ole Miss in 1962. The dormitory mirror incident with white students watching for her 'tail' is recounted.
related_events: Ole Miss integration with James Meredith (1962)

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Clyde Kennard's Framing
clips.summary: Branch and Smith give detailed accounts of Clyde Kennard's three attempts to enroll at Mississippi Southern College, the back-channel negotiations between McCain, Governor Ross Barnett, and Black Hattiesburg principals seeking a separate junior college, and the chicken-feed frame-up using the false testimony of nineteen-year-old Johnny Lee Roberts. Branch describes flying to Chicago with Mr. JC Fairley, Dick Gregory, and Charles Evers to pressure Roberts to recant, and Roberts's eventual recanting through Jerry Mitchell of the Clarion-Ledger.
related_events: Clyde Kennard's framing and Parchman Farm imprisonment

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Local NAACP and Outside Volunteers
clips.summary: Smith situates Hattiesburg as a 1964 Freedom Summer destination because of its long-standing NAACP branch (chartered 1946) and the 1950s voter-registration suit. Both women critique outside organizers and writers who took credit for local Black activism, and Branch quotes a Pennsylvania judge: 'If it's not local, it's not real.' Branch describes her segregation-era jobs as the first Black hire at the Hattiesburg telephone company and Sears, and the Big Yank garment factory permanent-press shift.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Hospital Integration and Bill Thacker
clips.summary: Smith describes her husband Dr. Charles E. Smith building a clinic in Hattiesburg, the Forrest General Hospital (then called Methodist Hospital, now Merit Health Wesley) practice of consigning Black patients to a single floor that is now a cafeteria hallway, and the basement charity-ward in Laurel. She describes Dr. Bill Thacker, a white Hattiesburg dentist who treated Black patients despite Klan threats to kidnap his daughters, and the brick wall he built around his house.

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Voter Registration with Theron Lynd
clips.summary: Branch describes registering to vote at age twenty-one in 1961 at the counter of Forrest County registrar Theron Lynd, escorted by Mr. JC Fairley, Mr. James Bourne, and Mr. Vernon Dahmer. She credits Rowan High School world history teacher Mrs. Marjorie James for her preparation on the Mississippi Constitution. She also recounts learning from an NAACP poster distributed from Washington that her grandfather Lee Val Graves had attempted to register at Lynd's counter years before.
related_events: Forrest County voter-registration suits of the 1950s

clips.subject: Raylawni G. Branch and Jeanette Smith
clips.title: Head Start and Federal Leverage
clips.summary: Branch recounts her husband's integration of Hattiesburg's medical infrastructure using Hill-Burton and Medicare compliance leverage, his eventual presidency of the local NAACP, and her own conversation with Robert Beach that produced the Greyhound-bus tactic of sending five hundred Hattiesburg children to Washington to compel Head Start funding. The chapter closes with Branch's recollection of meeting her husband at her father's church-dedication in Laurel and his subsequent treatment of her father for pneumonia.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Baltimore Childhood and Cortez Peters
clips.summary: Robinson recounts his Baltimore birth in October 1939, parents Leroy and Leovester Jenkins Robinson, his father's 39 years at Bethlehem Steel, and dropping out of Dunbar High School around tenth grade. His cousin Josephine Thorn convinces him to enroll at Cortez Peters Business College, founded by a Black typist who learned 150 words per minute and was financed by Royal or Underwood Typewriter to teach Black students government-test skills and shorthand in DC, Baltimore, and Chicago.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Civic Interest Group and Voter Registration
clips.summary: After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, Robinson is pushed by Dean Walter Dixon, simultaneously his college dean and a Baltimore City Councilman who introduced a public-accommodations bill, to represent the school in the Civic Interest Group. He becomes treasurer, runs sound-truck voter-registration drives in Clarence Mitchell Jr.'s district that register approximately 30,000 people, and learns to handle accounting under union representative Troy Brailey.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: SNCC Seat and Ella Baker
clips.summary: When Clarence Mitchell withdraws under family NAACP pressure, Robinson takes Maryland's open SNCC seat in June 1961 and travels to Louisville for the SNCC meeting wearing a black eye patch from a pretzel injury. On Anne Braden's porch, Ella Baker identifies him by tracing his mother Leovester Jenkins back to his uncle McKinley, who worked with her in New York. Baker's recognition opens him into the SNCC inner circle and a Baltimore SNCC mass meeting where the collection is taken before Wyatt Tee Walker can claim it for SCLC.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Arrival in McComb
clips.summary: Robinson travels from Baltimore through Atlanta, where SNCC executive director Ed King introduces him to a tuxedo-clad Wyatt Tee Walker, then to New Orleans and via Illinois Central to McComb. Cab driver George Hitch transports him to C.C. Bryant's house where he meets Bob Moses. With local infrastructure figure 'Super Cool Daddy' Mr. Webb, he arranges housing with Mama Cotton on Wall Street, opens a SNCC bank account, rents a mimeograph at the stationery store, and sets up the voter-registration school in the Masonic Temple over Burglund Supermarket above Burglund High School.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Magnolia Jail and the Constitution Test
clips.summary: Robinson describes the Mississippi voter-registration mechanics: registrars demanding interpretation of arbitrary sections of the state constitution, SNCC's literacy-school response, and the principle from Bob Moses that organizers must walk applicants to the registrar's office. He recounts Bob Moses's beating, Herbert Lee's killing in the cotton gin by State Representative E.H. Hurst, and his own black-suited visit to the Magnolia jail with George Hitch's concealed silver-handled pistol to see Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Baltimore Sit-Ins and Family Support
clips.summary: Robinson contrasts the relatively non-violent Baltimore sit-in tactics, in which three Renaults full of demonstrators rotated between major restaurants triggering Miranda readings and police closures, with the rougher Mississippi work. He describes the support of William 'Little Willie' Adams who underwrote bail through the Dixon bond agency, Elizabeth Murphy Oliver of the Baltimore Afro-American newspaper and her daughter Marcia, and Larry Steele, the Jet/Ebony stringer who relayed news to his mother during his Southern assignments.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: McComb High School Walkout and Protecting Diane Nash
clips.summary: Robinson narrates the day McComb students walked out of school following Herbert Lee's killing. He observed from a vantage point as police beat Bob Moses, Chuck McDew, and others, then watched from the Masonic Temple as Sherrod and Cordell Reagon were arrested. With a Detroit-based A.G. Gaston and Diane Nash inside, he led them through a black-curtained section onto the roof to evade the sergeant searching the building. Gaston jumped off the roof and was caught; Robinson got Diane Nash safely back to Wall Street.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Crisfield Arrest and Eastern Shore Maryland
clips.summary: After the retreat to Atlanta and reassignment at BB Beaman's, Robinson coordinates Route 40 desegregation with CORE in late 1961. Ruby Doris Smith Robinson directs him to a CORE group from New York including Faith Holsaert, Peggy Dammond, Angela Butler, and Bill Hansen, who travel to Crisfield, Maryland to sit in at Governor Tawes's restaurant during Christmas 1961. Robinson is arrested for the only time in his six SNCC years; the group is jailed in Princess Anne, Maryland with Juanita Mitchell as attorney and Freddie St. Clair from Cambridge providing bonds.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Cambridge and Gloria Richardson
clips.summary: Robinson and Bill Hansen organize Cambridge, Maryland with weekend mobilizations from Civic Interest Group and Nonviolent Action Group affiliates. When Freddie St. Clair steps back to run his funeral business, his cousin Gloria Richardson assumes leadership of an organization that negotiates with the National Guard and the Kennedy administration. Robinson confronts Richardson over her call to boycott the public-accommodations referendum, arguing that no SNCC affiliate could advocate non-voting while Southern workers died for the franchise.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Mississippi Reflections and John Doar
clips.summary: Robinson reflects on Amzie Moore as the post-office veteran NAACP figure who convinced the SNCC team to remain in Mississippi after Herbert Lee's funeral, when their first instinct was to flee. He recounts night affidavit-collection in an open Mississippi field with Bob Moses, John Doar, Marion Barry, and George Hitch, the rural Black farmer's fear that the white folks 'might turn green and jump on you,' and John Doar's coded refusal to acknowledge a beaten John Hardy in a jail cell to protect his cover.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Cambridge National Guard and George Gelston
clips.summary: After Robinson's Orangeburg South Carolina VEP project, the National Guard under General George Gelston enters Cambridge and becomes an unexpected ally. Gelston attempts to recruit Robinson as an officer and is surprised to confirm Robinson's 4-F mental-incompetent status (Robinson refused to identify inkblots as anything other than inkblots). When Gelston is absent, Colonel Tawes's troops shoot tear gas after Cliff Voss charges fixed bayonets; Stokely Carmichael and others are arrested. Robinson confronts Bobby Kennedy on the Cambridge water situation and on John Doar's reports.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Baltimore Advisory Network and the March on Washington
clips.summary: Robinson sketches the Baltimore adult-advisory network: August Meier teaching at Morgan State, Reverend Marion Bascom, Reverend Vernon Dobson, Reverend Frank Williams, and Catherine Adams as the advisory-group secretary to whom all records flowed. He recalls the March on Washington as a day of singing and good food, with no assigned role, and his anger at the bumping of SNCC's intended speech content. He describes meeting Diane Nash for the first time across a table in a hotel conference room with Connie Curry catching him doodling a heart between their names.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: MFDP Atlantic City and SNCC Fundraising
clips.summary: In 1964 Robinson briefly works for Senator Joseph D. Tydings before being assigned to lobby Western state delegations for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge in California and Oregon. He is then sent to Atlantic City for the convention, where with NAG's Mary Lovelace O'Neal he secures hotel rooms through Atlantic City gangster Reggie Edgel. He moves to New York for special-gift fundraising, then to the Philadelphia office until Fred Mealy disrupts the operation. Late 1965 finds him surveying SNCC's projects in disarray.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: UPO, CDGM, and Senator Stennis
clips.summary: After leaving SNCC in 1965, Robinson serves as Meg Due's second-in-command at United Planning Organization Center #1 in DC for about a year before being politically pushed out. Ed Brown recruits him as Social Service Director for the Child Development Group of Mississippi. In a meeting during CDGM's dissolution, Senator John C. Stennis repeatedly addresses Robinson's team as 'you boys'; Robinson confronts him directly, and is fired.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: OSTI, Kaiser, and the Black Panthers in Oakland
clips.summary: Through Peggy Dammond's husband, Robinson joins OSTI in Boston. After a Detroit free-Detroit-Committee community project, he is sent to Oakland to broker a 40-year land-acquisition partnership between Kaiser Engineers and the Black Panthers for the Acorn redevelopment in East Oakland. The Panthers refuse, citing his arrival in a Cadillac. Robinson switches the partnership to a community group brokered via Timothy L. Jenkins's contact Don Warden, and the West Project Equal senior-citizens housing is built. He is excluded from white planning meetings and quits.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Back Pain, Marriage End, and Memphis
clips.summary: Diagnosed with muscle-spasm back pain requiring six hours of daily medicated sleep, Robinson instead quits smoking cigarettes after three days and stops coffee. Bobby Yancey calls him to a Memphis project with A.L. Nellum & Associates organizing the first Congressional Black Caucus dinners. He returns to Boston to find his wife leaving, and relocates to DC in 1981 to take over the project there.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: DC Polling, Marion Barry, and Federal Career
clips.summary: Robinson becomes involved in the DC Charter campaign with John A. Wilson. At Afro-American Data Namix at 14th and Kenyon, on the only Black-owned DEC PDP-11/45 between DC and New York, he runs Marion Barry's first polling operation. After unemployment and Pride work, he joins DC Department of Employment Services running the youth division for two summer-jobs programs (40,000 placements one summer, 25,000 the school year), then becomes a fraud investigator, then assistant LAN administrator overseeing 100 machines. He retires on the day he files papers after a forced incentive-speaker mandatory event.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Albany Jail, Ella Baker, and Paul Robeson
clips.summary: Robinson recounts riding to Albany jail with Wyatt Tee Walker to deliver pork chops to King and Abernathy, who sat in silk pajamas while Robinson refused to bring Abernathy ice cream. He describes Ella Baker as a maternal figure who took him drunk before his wedding at Harry Belafonte's house. When Cambridge work overwhelmed him, Baker sent him via psychiatrist Joe Baker of the Medical Committee for Human Rights to Peekskill, New York for a Paul Robeson commemorative fundraiser, where he met Paul Robeson at Dr. Samuel and Helen Rosen's Katonah home and sat with him in a sauna.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: SNCC Building Strategy and the NAACP Conflict
clips.summary: Robinson articulates the SNCC organizing principle drawn from Ella Baker: enter a community to build local infrastructure and leave, never to make a home there. He plants the 'silk suits vs. denim' story with reporter Karl Rogoff to expose the NAACP's collection-taking in Cambridge, chasing them out of town. He distinguishes SNCC's pushed-envelope strategy from the NAACP's court-reliant approach and reflects on Baltimore's Freddie Gray uprising and the unchanged poverty in West Baltimore alongside Pikesville's wealth.

clips.subject: Reginald Robinson
clips.title: Black Power Reframed and Economic Strategy
clips.summary: Robinson argues that the SNCC move to Black Power was never about guns but about economic infrastructure: a financial institution on a Greenwood Mississippi corner that could loan to a laundromat, a restaurant, a computer-assembly factory leveraging tooling that no longer requires a PhD. He cites Henry G. Parks Jr's Parks Sausage Company, Red Brown's Mace denim manufacturing, and Victorine Q. Adams as Black entrepreneurial precedents. He critiques the 2014 Selma film's misrepresentation of James Forman and SNCC, and emphasizes that SNCC's enduring contribution is the leaderless-leader model.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Plantation Childhood and Schooling
clips.summary: Blake describes his parents (Mississippi-born father, Arkansas-born mother), his 1934 birth in Arkansas, and the family's early move to Louisiana. He recounts growing up on two plantations as the son of an illiterate sharecropper, attending a one-room church school where a single college-sophomore teacher covered first through seventh grade, and learning to read and write only as he approached his teenage years.
related_events: 1940s-1950s Black agricultural labor in northeast Louisiana

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Mules, Salt, and Pastoral Strategy
clips.summary: Blake recounts plowing mules in high school and deciding to teach mules his language rather than learn theirs. He explains his father's proverb that you can lead a horse to water and, if you salt his diet, he'll find water on his own. Blake credits this pedagogy as the foundation of his pastoral strategy for persuading reluctant congregations, while warning that the same technique enabled Jim Jones and David Koresh to lead followers to mass suicide.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: First Pastorates and Mount Canaan
clips.summary: Blake locates his two plantations (Ashley in Madison Parish near Tallulah; Wood Spur north of Shreveport) and describes his liberation from the plantation through college and marriage. He outlines his ministerial trajectory from rural one-Sunday and two-Sunday churches in 1958 through his 1966 call to Mount Canaan Baptist Church in the Allendale neighborhood of Shreveport, where the building was so dilapidated it later collapsed.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Sharecropper Debt and Race Relations
clips.summary: Blake describes the plantation economy as a system of perpetual indebtedness in which sharecroppers owed their housing, food, mules, and implements to the plantation commissary and were forbidden to leave without another farmer paying their debt. He explains the social compact in which white plantation owners would protect "their" Black workers from the draft and from jail provided the workers stayed within the racial caste line, comparing the system to contemporary American credit-card debt.
related_events: World War II draft board manipulation

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Registering to Vote in Caddo Parish
clips.summary: Blake describes Louisiana's voter-registration barriers: a registered voter had to vouch for the applicant (nearly impossible when only whites were registered), identification was required, and applicants had to interpret the U.S. Constitution to the registrar's satisfaction and compute their exact age in years, months, and days. Blake circumvented the residency requirement by using a friend's city address and studied the test materials to register at twenty-one.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: H.L. Whitlow and Father's Influence
clips.summary: Blake recounts his summer employment at H.L. Whitlow's general store in Dixie, Louisiana, where Whitlow practiced employment fairness uncommon for a 1950s white Southern storekeeper and was called "a nigga lover" by white peers. Blake describes negotiating his wage from $15 to $25 a week and progressing to cashier and $300/month by high-school graduation. He attributes his stance on dignity under Jim Crow to his father, who taught him that one could call a white man a dog if one knew how, without surrendering manhood.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Joining SCLC and First Arrests
clips.summary: Blake describes his attentiveness to the Baton Rouge bus boycott led by Rev. T.J. Jemison around 1953-1955, which he identifies as the model for the Montgomery boycott. He recounts reading Stride Toward Freedom and meeting Martin Luther King Jr. as his Bishop College commencement speaker. Dr. C.O. Simkins, a Shreveport SCLC board member, arranged his February 1960 interview with King in Atlanta; Blake joined SCLC's staff March 1, 1960 and was arrested that same day for "careless and reckless driving" while sitting in a parked car. An October 1960 assassination attempt fired a bullet through the windshield of his car.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Wyatt Walker Arrest and SCLC Selection
clips.summary: Blake describes a Shreveport service where Dr. King was preaching and the Ku Klux Klan protested outside. Blake and SCLC executive director Dr. Wyatt Tee Walker were ordered back inside by the chief; Walker refused, citing freedom of assembly, and the two were jailed for "mental observation," a charge Walker later joked Blake had never been cleared of. Blake notes the U.S. Civil Rights Commission rated Shreveport the most difficult city in the South, citing the plantation environment as a discipline of obedience, and recounts his family's support and his father's blessing of his Movement enlistment.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Little Union Baptist Beating
clips.summary: Blake details the September 22, 1963 memorial march planned to honor the four girls killed in the 16th Street Baptist Church bombing. Commissioner of Public Safety George D'Artois denied the permit at the last possible moment; Blake redirected the gathering into Little Union Baptist Church. After the service, Commissioner D'Artois and police entered the vestibule, beat Blake inside the church, and dragged him to the sidewalk where police struck his head with nightsticks. Believed dead, he was driven to Dallas for treatment by his family, who feared white-doctor sabotage in Shreveport.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Mount Canaan and Community Development
clips.summary: Blake describes confronting Commissioner D'Artois and the Shreveport mayor in 1966 to secure cooperation for community development at Mount Canaan. The mayor confessed that he had quietly directed police to arrest Blake's protesters away from sympathetic Black churches because he was "a politician." Blake secured eight acres of city land for $35,000, restricted to housing, recreation, education, or religious use; the church then built 120 units of low-income housing, a daycare center, and the Mount Canaan high-rise senior tower.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Integration Critique and Black Church Power
clips.summary: Blake argues that the Movement targeted immoral practices (segregated transit, lunch counters, restrooms) but that the integration outcome the South delivered was not what activists had asked for. He maintains the Movement asked for equal funding and capable teachers for Black schools, not necessarily integrated schools, and that the bussing-based integration closed Black schools while leaving curriculum disparities intact. Blake identifies the Black church as the enduring source of Black political power because Sunday gatherings provide a captive audience for community organizing.

clips.subject: Reverend Harry Blake
clips.title: Summer Boys Academy and Homeschool
clips.summary: Blake describes his current ministry focus on Black male education, prompted by statistics that prison-cell counts are projected from fourth-grade Black male performance. He recounts the Mount Canaan Summer Boys Academy, started three summers earlier, where one student advanced from second- to sixth-grade reading level. He describes the case of Walter, a twelve-year-old congregant struggling with fourth-grade material, leading the church to launch a homeschool program with a retired teacher serving five boys eight to three daily.
related_events: Mount Canaan Summer Boys Academy launch

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Tallahassee Roots and Family
clips.summary: Hayling describes his November 20, 1929 birth in Tallahassee to a Florida A&M tailoring instructor (father from Grenada) and a Creole homemaker mother from New Orleans. The four Hayling siblings, Charles Hayling Jr., Francis Hayling, Yvonne Hayling Clark, and Robert, all attended Florida A&M and earned advanced degrees.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Air Force Service and Toxicology
clips.summary: Hayling describes entering the Air Force in August 1951, basic training at Lackland Air Force Base, medical-technician training at Sheppard Air Force Base, and graduation from Officer Candidate School in December 1952. He was assigned to Wright-Patterson Air Force Base in Dayton, Ohio as an aeromedical researcher in toxicology, where he declined an early promotion to captain in order to enter dental school.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Meharry and the Looby Bombing
clips.summary: Hayling enrolls at Meharry Medical College in Nashville in September 1955 after marrying his Florida A&M sweetheart. His dental jurisprudence instructor is Z. Alexander Looby, lead attorney for Nashville school desegregation. After Looby's home is dynamited in April 1960, blowing out windows of Hayling's adjacent Meharry dormitory, Hayling joins the April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall and meets John Lewis, Diane Nash, C.T. Vivian, James Bevel, and other student-movement leaders.
related_events: April 19, 1960 silent march on Nashville City Hall

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Florida Movements and Coming to St. Augustine
clips.summary: Hayling recounts the Tallahassee movements led by Rev. C.K. Steele (bus desegregation) and Patricia Stephens Due and Priscilla Stephens (movie-theater and restaurant desegregation), including the sisters' imprisonment in a wire-fenced Tallahassee jail with no privacy. Florida's tuition-supplement program for Black dentists at Meharry, combined with the GI Bill, funded his training. He worked summers as a sky-cap at Newark Airport and a Sheepshead Bay seafood waiter, then chose St. Augustine in July 1960 over Panama City because the late Dr. Rudolph Gordon's practice was available there.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Setting Up Practice in St. Augustine
clips.summary: Hayling inherits Dr. Gordon's office and 50-60% white patient roster in late 1960. He becomes the city and county jail dentist and the state-prison-farm dentist for St. Johns County, in part because white dentists were uncomfortable with armed deputies accompanying prisoner-patients. Sheriff L.O. Davis becomes a private patient. Hayling describes the self-financed payment plans that sustained a low-cash-economy Black practice and the early absence of any racial-rights conflict.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Youth Council Advisor and the Quadricentennial
clips.summary: After Rev. Thomas A. Wright Sr. is forced out of St. Augustine and relocates to Gainesville (Mount Carmel Baptist Church), a delegation of NAACP Youth Council members asks Hayling to take over as advisor in 1963. Local NAACP president Fannie Fulwood and secretary Elizabeth Hawthorne become his stabilizers. The federal government appropriates $400,000 for St. Augustine's 400th-anniversary Quadricentennial celebrations; no Black St. Augustinians are appointed to any committee, despite Black residents comprising roughly 20% of the city. The exclusion becomes the wake-up moment that crystallizes the local movement.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The George Reedy Double-Cross
clips.summary: Hayling and his delegation write to Vice President Lyndon B. Johnson protesting the segregated Quadricentennial dinner at the Ponce de Leon Hotel. LBJ aide George Reedy visits the office repeatedly during the week before the dinner and persuades the delegation to suspend demonstrations in exchange for a meeting with the City Commission. Six or eight delegates are seated in an alcove above the Grand Ballroom, separated from the dinner. The next morning's meeting brings only the Mayor's Secretary with a tape recorder, no commissioners, no Mayor, no federal representative. The city simultaneously dresses a Black Lincolnville-community night patrolman in a uniform and parks him at the Old Slave Market to refute Hayling's claim that the force had no Black officers.
related_events: Vice President LBJ St. Augustine visit 1963

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: The September 1963 Klan Rally Beating
clips.summary: Hayling, James Jackson, James Hauser, and Clyde Jenkins drive to observe a major Klan rally outside St. Augustine. Connie Lynch of California and J.B. Stoner of Atlanta are scheduled speakers. The four are surrounded by armed Klansmen at a dug-out moat across the road, dragged to the rally stage, piled like cordwood, and beaten with ax handles and baseball bats. The rally announcer prepares the crowd to smell "a nigger burn" with gasoline. An Episcopal priest from Daytona Beach who infiltrated the rally with a personal recorder calls the Florida Highway Patrol; Sheriff L.O. Davis sends auxiliaries. The four are evacuated by E.G. Chase's hearse to Brewster Hospital in Jacksonville. Hayling loses 11 teeth and sustains right-arm damage that nearly ends his dental career. Hoss Manucy, the local Klan leader, was a convicted moonshiner deputized along with Klan members as auxiliary deputies under Sheriff Davis.
related_events: September 1963 St. Augustine Klan rally beating

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Break with NAACP, Alliance with SCLC and Yale
clips.summary: Roy Wilkins phones from NAACP New York demanding that Hayling follow national instructions or surrender the St. Augustine charter. Hayling sends the charter back by special delivery. He travels to Atlanta on weekends and meets Julian Bond, SNCC organizers, Ella Baker, and members of Ebenezer Baptist Church. Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr. of Yale Divinity School organizes a spring-break tutorial deployment in which Yale students live with Black St. Augustine families, conduct cleanup and tutoring, and return home to mount a stage drama portraying L.O. Davis and Judge Charles Mathis. Northern white supporters, Esther Burgess (wife of Bishop John Melville Burgess), Mrs. Rowe, Mrs. Campbell, and Mary Peabody (mother of Massachusetts Governor Endicott "Chub" Peabody), provide financial backing and bodies on the ground.
related_events: Yale spring-break St. Augustine tutorial program

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Refusing the LBJ Pause and Civil Rights Act 1964
clips.summary: When President Lyndon B. Johnson asks civil rights organizations for a two-week cessation of demonstrations to ease passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Dr. King and the national organizations agree. Hayling refuses on behalf of St. Augustine, "fool me once, shame on you, fool me twice, shame on me", invoking the Reedy double-cross. Mary Peabody is arrested with Hayling at the Monson Motor Lodge. James Brock, owner of the Monson, becomes infamous for the photograph of him pouring acid into the motel pool while integrated swimmers are in the water. Dr. King is in St. Augustine on June 30, 1964; the Civil Rights Act of 1964 is signed shortly after.

clips.subject: Robert Bagner Hayling
clips.title: Cocoa Relocation and ACCORD Recognition
clips.summary: After fulfilling the five-year Florida tuition obligation, Hayling relocates to Cocoa, Florida with help from mortician Rudy Stone, opening a practice next to Dr. Jerkins that serves NASA Cape Kennedy workers until LBJ moves the control center to the Johnson Space Center in Houston. Hayling returns to St. Augustine in retirement; on June 20, 2003 the city renames Scott Street as Dr. Hayling Place, championed by Commissioner Earl Jones (son of Hayling's longtime office assistant Maddie Jones). The city gives him the Award of Valor on July 2, 2011 at the ACCORD ceremony, where Dorothy Cotton speaks and the Rev. Goldie Eubanks family is honored. Hayling's daughter Robin (with husband David, from Mississippi), his younger daughter (a Yale graduate with a Stanford MBA) and her husband Chris (a Princeton and Harvard Business School graduate working for Apple Inc. in the Far East), and his grandsons Roman and Avery attend the event.
related_events: June 20, 2003 Dr. Hayling Place street naming

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: New York Origins and Columbia Law
clips.summary: Sobol describes his New York City upbringing on Manhattan's west side, his father's legal practice and mother's teaching career, his Bronx High School of Science and Union College education, and his return to Columbia Law School. He notes writing a published Law Review piece on a Supreme Court gerrymander decision in Alabama as his first civil rights exposure.
related_events: Columbia Law School Law Review publication on Alabama gerrymander decision

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Arnold, Fortas and Porter to LCDC
clips.summary: Sobol traces his career from clerking for Judge Paul R. Hays on the Second Circuit to joining Arnold, Fortas & Porter in 1963. He describes his initial expectation that the firm would handle Abe Fortas-style constitutional cases (referencing Gideon v. Wainwright and McCarthy Committee defendants) and his volunteer outreach to the Lawyers Constitutional Defense Committee through director Henry Schwarzschild, leading to his 1965 New Orleans assignment.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: First Three Weeks in New Orleans
clips.summary: Sobol arrives in New Orleans on August 1, 1965, expecting Selma or Jackson and instead finding sustained civil rights activity. He files a school desegregation case in St. Francisville, West Feliciana Parish, gets it summarily reversed at the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals after Judge E. Gordon West's dismissal, and decides Louisiana needs permanent lawyers rather than rotating volunteers. He partners with the Collins, Douglas, and Elie firm, attorneys to the Congress of Racial Equality.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Permanent Move to New Orleans
clips.summary: Sobol returns in summer 1966 as LCDC's permanent New Orleans lawyer, brings his family from Washington, and describes Don Juneau joining the office by December and growing the staff to three or four lawyers. He sketches the Dryades Street office above the CORE storefront and his early contacts with Bob Hicks, Gayle Jenkins, and A.Z. Young of the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: New Orleans Black Bar and Bogalusa Setting
clips.summary: Sobol profiles Robert F. Collins (later the first Black federal judge in the South), Nils Douglas, and Lolis Elie of the Collins-Douglas-Elie firm, and their working relationship with the LCDC office. He describes Bogalusa's hot, paper-mill atmosphere, nightly union-hall mass meetings, the Civil Rights Act of 1964 going into force, and his first appearance in Bogalusa city court where the judge slammed a crutch on the table when Nils Douglas mentioned Abe Fortas's recent Supreme Court nomination.
related_events: Title VII Civil Rights Act takes effect July 1965

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Crown Zellerbach and EEOC Charge
clips.summary: Sobol describes Crown Zellerbach as the dominant Bogalusa employer with four plants (paper mill, box plant, two bag plants) and segregated unions. He details how he revised the original July 15, 1965 EEOC charge to itemize testing, seniority, segregated locals, and other practices, replacing what he viewed as the rushed original filing. He notes meeting workers at the plants and learning the substance of their complaints.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Bogalusa Leadership and Movement Dynamics
clips.summary: Sobol describes Gayle Jenkins as the organizational spark who recruited A.Z. Young as president of the Bogalusa Civic and Voters League, with her cousin Robert Hicks as vice president. He notes the San Francisco support network for pressure on Crown Zellerbach, the Robert Hicks home as the center of out-of-town civil rights visitors (James Farmer, Lincoln Lynch of CORE, the (SNCC) Freedom Singers, the Free Southern Theater), and the post-1990s return litigation he handled for Black Bogalusa police officers under mayoral resistance.
related_events: Crown Zellerbach pressure from San Francisco

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Crown Zellerbach Trial and Judge Heebe
clips.summary: Sobol describes the consolidated trial with United States v. Local 1 in summer 1967 before Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe. He explains how his Washington antitrust-discovery experience produced a more thorough litigation pattern (interrogatories, document production, depositions before trial) than the local norm. He credits George Cooper's research on testing and DOJ Civil Rights Division attorneys Robert Moore and David Rose on seniority issues, and references Quarles v. Philip Morris and Gabrielle Kirk McDonald's role.
related_events: Crown Zellerbach Title VII trial summer 1967

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Plant Tour, Deacons Skepticism, Travel Safety
clips.summary: Sobol tours the Crown Zellerbach plants by court order and observes the simplicity of the jobs against the 25-year seniority gates. He describes the highest-paying Black job (palletizing the final product) and the segregated union structure across the box, bag, and paper plants. He notes his rule of never sleeping in Bogalusa or other small Louisiana civil rights towns, crossing into Natchez or Vicksburg instead. He expresses skepticism that the Deacons for Defense and Justice played the central role later historians attribute to them in Bogalusa, citing his contemporary observation and conversation with Lolis Elie.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Crown Zellerbach Trial Mechanics
clips.summary: Sobol describes the eight-day Crown Zellerbach trial in which George Cooper arrived with a trunk full of testing-literature books and used them to cross-examine the company's expert witnesses. He details the chart-based presentation of seniority-impact data and Robert Hicks's continuous presence at counsel table for all eight days, which he believes affected Judge Heebe's view that the case represented a genuine community rather than lawyer-manufactured claims.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Wonderlic Test and Job-Relatedness Doctrine
clips.summary: Sobol explains how Crown Zellerbach made the Wonderlic test a hiring requirement only after the Civil Rights Act passed, with no historical basis. He describes the disparate pass rates by race, the developing job-relatedness doctrine, and the cultural-bias examples (such as the RSVP question). He notes the EEOC and Department of Labor Office of Federal Contract Compliance guidelines that followed, and his subsequent Washington-area testing litigation.
related_events: Wonderlic test challenge in Crown Zellerbach litigation

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Seniority Remedies and the Harvard Law Review Article
clips.summary: Sobol details two seniority remedies: plant-wide seniority replacing job-progression seniority in segregated systems like Bogalusa, and random job assignment by lottery in the New Orleans Public Service Inc. (NOPSI) bus-driver case before Judge Heebe (Fagan v. NOPSI). He describes co-authoring the 1969 Harvard Law Review article with George Cooper on Title VII seniority, its wide circulation and influence in intermediate federal courts, and the 1977 Teamsters Supreme Court reversal that ended the article's prevailing-doctrine status. He notes that union-side Washington lawyers afterward held the article against him.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Federal Judges and Court Composition
clips.summary: Sobol reflects that civil rights outcomes turned heavily on the disposition of individual federal judges. He contrasts the open-door welcome from district judges in New Orleans (visiting Judge Herbert W. Christenberry at home on July 4, 1967) with the dismissive treatment from Judge E. Gordon West in Baton Rouge. He notes that the Kaiser aluminum Title VII case begun by Nils Douglas took 17 years to relief, and observes that contemporary New Orleans federal courts no longer welcome the same kind of civil rights litigation.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Gary Duncan, Leander Perez, and Sobol's Arrest
clips.summary: Sobol recounts representing 19-year-old Gary Duncan in Plaquemines Parish in 1967 after Duncan was charged with battery for touching a white boy's elbow during a roadside encounter. He describes Leander Perez's segregationist apparatus (with his son as district attorney), the Louisiana statutory denial of jury trial for sub-two-year offenses, Sobol's appeal to the Louisiana Supreme Court, and his arrest by Perez and a sheriff outside the judge's chambers on charges of practicing law without a license.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Sobol v. Perez Three-Judge Court
clips.summary: Sobol describes the three-judge federal court (Judge Fred J. Cassibry, Judge Frederick J.R. Heebe, Judge Robert A. Ainsworth Jr.) assembled to hear the challenge to Perez's prosecution. He details the three-week trial covered daily by the New Orleans Times-Picayune, the five-party structure (Sobol and Duncan, CORE's Isaac Reynolds, federal government as plaintiff-intervenor, Perez and Louisiana, and the Louisiana State Bar Association on Perez's side), and the court's ruling that Sobol was temporarily in the state and operating with local counsel under the Louisiana statute.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Duncan v. Louisiana at the Supreme Court
clips.summary: Sobol describes arguing the Duncan case before the U.S. Supreme Court, which ruled that the Fourteenth Amendment incorporates the right to jury trial against the states for non-minor crimes. After remand and a new trial with a jury, the parish retried Duncan, prompting Sobol to file Duncan v. Perez before Judge Cassibry, who enjoined the retrial as a malicious prosecution. Sobol describes Duncan's later role as a community leader and shrimper, still a friend.
related_events: Duncan v. Louisiana Supreme Court decision 1968

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Madison Parish Voting Rights Cases
clips.summary: Sobol describes two Madison Parish voting-rights cases under the Voting Rights Act before Judge Dawkins in Monroe. In the first, Zelma Wyche lost the police-chief race by 10 to 15 votes after the clerk of court collected approximately 200 absentee ballots from white nursing homes; the judge set aside the election under a 'discrimination in fact' doctrine. In the second, Harrison Brown's school-board candidacy was disconnected from the Democratic Party 'rooster' party-line lever; the judge ordered the election rerun and Brown won.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Hicks v. Weaver Public Housing Case
clips.summary: Sobol describes Hicks v. Weaver, 302 F. Supp. 619 (1969), brought when Robert Hicks identified Bogalusa's public-housing siting plan as placing units deep in Black neighborhoods and deep in white neighborhoods to avoid integration. The case established a private right of action under the fair housing law and resulted in an injunction halting bulldozers ready to clear the land. Sobol cites this as exemplifying Hicks's pattern-detection ability across school, employment, and housing issues including the prom-queen and football-team litigation before Judge Heebe.
related_events: Bogalusa school football and prom-queen litigation

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Welfare Cases and Pre-Batson Peremptory-Challenge Win
clips.summary: Sobol describes 1970s welfare-fraud defense work in New Orleans federal court with Anne Sobol and Lola C. Light, representing approximately twenty women indicted for fraudulent welfare receipt. He details the statistical challenge to the grand jury (food-stamp recipients underrepresented in jury pools) and the peremptory-challenge ruling by Judge Alvin B. Rubin in United States v. McDaniels that prosecutors could not strike all Black jurors. Sobol notes the Supreme Court adopted this rule nationally twenty years later in Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79.
related_events: United States v. McDaniels peremptory-challenge ruling

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Chronology: Michigan, Washington, Return to Louisiana
clips.summary: Sobol traces his career path from his August 1968 departure from full-time Louisiana work, through fall 1968 at the Institute for Policy Studies, the 1969 spring semester teaching at the University of Michigan Law School, two years at the Washington Research Project (later Children's Defense Fund) with Marian Wright Edelman, his 1971 return to Louisiana with Anne Sobol, the welfare litigation period and NOPSI bus-driver case, and his 1974 move back to Washington where he opened a law firm with Mike Trister.
related_events: Washington Research Project to Children's Defense Fund transition

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Sweet Potato Cooperative and Loving v. Virginia Marriage License
clips.summary: Sobol describes the Opelousas-Lafayette case in which a Black farmers' cooperative (Southern Consumers Cooperative), organized with CORE worker John Zippert and Father Albert J. McKnight, broke the white wholesaler monopoly on sweet potato sales. After local district attorney harassment and a police break-in seizing the cooperative's files, Sobol obtained federal injunctive relief. He also recounts compelling the Lafayette clerk of court to issue a marriage license to John Zippert and Carol Zippert under Loving v. Virginia.

clips.subject: Richard Barry Sobol
clips.title: Reflections on Movement Lawyering
clips.summary: Sobol reflects that LCDC's short-term volunteer lawyers, though not cost-effective per case, were a contagion that shifted careers across the country into civil rights and pro bono work, including law students from the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council. He explains that two years in Louisiana made it impossible to return to commercial firm life at Arnold, Fortas & Porter, despite his initial insistence on a leave of absence as security, and that union-side billing clients later sustained his Washington civil rights practice.
keywords: Law Students Civil Rights Research Council
related_events: Arnold Fortas and Porter leave of absence

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: New England Roots and a Porter's NAACP Card
clips.summary: Tuttle opens with his paternal family's arrival in New Haven in 1638 and his great-grandfather Monroe Gillette's Civil War service at Fort Wagner and Petersburg. He recounts a formative childhood memory of his father using an NAACP card to enlist Pullman porters in helping the family transfer trains in Cincinnati after the war, framing organized solidarity as the lesson that shaped his later political life.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Wesleyan and the Fraternity Desegregation Fight
clips.summary: Tuttle attends Wesleyan University beginning fall 1957 and becomes president of Alpha Chi Rho, which discovers a Christian-only clause and a hidden whites-only clause in its national charter. With Stephen S. Trott and Bruce Corwin as the precipitating members, the chapter breaks from the national after a one-year ultimatum. Tuttle takes a junior year off in 1960 to read and reflect during the sit-in wave.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: John Maguire, William Sloane Coffin, and the Freedom Rides
clips.summary: Wesleyan religion instructor John Maguire receives a phone call from Rev. William Sloane Coffin Jr after the Anniston bus burning and joins the Freedom Rides with department chair David Swift following. Tuttle and his roommate decide to go but the bus has already left. He frames the Freedom Rides as a strategic exploitation of interstate Commerce Clause jurisdiction.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: UCLA, Steve McNichols, and the Decision to Go South
clips.summary: Tuttle arrives at UCLA in 1962 to study under Progressive Era historian George Mowry. He meets Steve McNichols, NSA representative and former Houston Freedom Rider, and his circle of West Coast civil rights veterans including Bob and Helen Berry Singleton and Robert C. Farrell. A John Poppy article on SNCC's Sam Block, Charles McLaurin, and Willie Wazir Peacock in the Mississippi Delta catalyzes Tuttle's decision to go South.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: George E. Brown's Letter and Arrival in Greenwood
clips.summary: At a Hollywood Hills party celebrating the election of Congressman George E. Brown Jr and councilman Tom Bradley, Tuttle is introduced to the Californians for Liberal Representation network. Brown writes him a federal-letterhead letter to carry as a hedge against extra-legal threats. Tuttle drives to Greenwood, Mississippi with Sam Block and Charles McLaurin and lodges at 708 Avenue N near the COFO office.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Medgar Evers's Murder and the Winona Beatings
clips.summary: Lawrence Guyot leaves to bail Fannie Lou Hamer, June Johnson, and Annelle Ponder out of jail in Winona and is himself arrested and beaten. Tuttle calls Medgar Evers, who promises to call back after a meeting; Evers is murdered by Byron De La Beckwith that night. With Willie Wazir Peacock in charge, Tuttle calls Governor Ross Barnett with a scripted demand that produces the Highway Patrol's disclosure of where the prisoners are held. Andrew Young drives in with bail money.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Atlanta, Lester Maddox, and John Kifner Crosses the Line
clips.summary: After De La Beckwith threatens to kill the white boy in Greenwood, Tuttle is flown to Atlanta. He participates in SNCC-led lunch-counter sit-ins outside the local civil rights consensus, observing Lester Maddox with a pickaxe handle outside his restaurant. New York Times reporter John Kifner, a Wesleyan acquaintance present at a near-boiling-water incident, puts away his notebook and joins the movement.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Savannah Night Marches and the House of Hospitality
clips.summary: Tuttle travels to Savannah with Bruce Gordon and Joe Iyer to support Hosea Williams and Benjamin Van Clark's Chatham County Crusade for Voters. He participates in night marches where movement members surrender brass knuckles and switchblades before each action. The group is housed pro bono in a house of prostitution whose owners also put up property as bail collateral.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Chatham County Jail and the Bridge Boys
clips.summary: Police arrest Tuttle in the Chatham County Crusade office on a John Doe warrant while Andrew Young and James Bevel ease away unrecognized. Tuttle is held under Judge Victor Mulling's seven-piece-bond detention scheme with Hosea Williams and roughly 45 others. Father Crowder visits to administer communion. African-American jail trustees called bridge boys, including a Filipino-American welterweight boxer, threaten white prisoners against touching him. Bruce Gordon and Tuttle organize a freedom school inside the jail.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Aaron Buchsbaum, William Waxler, and the Communist Smear
clips.summary: Through an Anti-Defamation League network in Connecticut and a separate Cincinnati law-firm contact, attorney Aaron Buchsbaum agrees to represent Tuttle and Dr. William Waxler puts up property bond. The Atlanta Constitution runs a story linking the Chatham County Crusade office to the International Union of Mine, Mill, and Smelter Workers, framed to brand Tuttle a communist. California Attorney General Stanley Mosk and former Arkansas Governor Frank Cherry, then head of the Subversive Activities Control Board, intervene; the story dies. Tuttle's release is contingent on everyone else being released.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: UCLA Student Coordinating Committee and No on Proposition 14
clips.summary: Back in Los Angeles, Tuttle, Bob Singleton, and Russ Ellis form the UCLA Student Coordinating Committee. They mobilize over 100 students each weekend and 900 on Election Day for the No on Proposition 14 fair-housing campaign. The group functions as a Friends of SNCC chapter, lobbying Congressmen Augustus Hawkins, Phil Burton, and William Fitts Ryan to hold hearings on Mississippi. The chapter also covers the UC Berkeley Free Speech Movement connection and the RFK 1968 campaign in which Tuttle meets his first wife.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: LA City Controller: Private Clubs and South Africa Divestment
clips.summary: Tuttle serves four terms as Los Angeles City Controller beginning 1985. He leads passage of an ordinance prohibiting discrimination at the Jonathan Club, California Club, and Hillcrest Country Club, with Councilwoman Joy Picus. He requires sworn affidavits from city investment-holding companies to certify no South Africa business ties, an apartheid-divestment technique credited to Councilman Zev Yaroslavsky, Councilman Robert C. Farrell, and Mayor Tom Bradley. He extends the work to bank-merger redlining-disclosure ordinances.

clips.subject: Rick Tuttle
clips.title: Return to Greenwood, Savannah, and the Voting Rights Act
clips.summary: Tuttle and his second wife Rebecca tour the civil rights sites in 2008-09, visiting 708 Avenue N in Greenwood, Canton, Philadelphia Mississippi, the former Chatham County Jail now part of the Savannah College of Art and Design, and a 1963 photo exhibit at the Telfair Museum featuring Tuttle's own quotes. An impromptu corner-store reunion in Greenwood with a former teenage SNCC volunteer closes the loop. Tuttle frames continuing work to defend the 1965 Voting Rights Act federal preclearance provisions and to extend civil rights principles to gender and sexual-orientation equity.
related_events: Shelby County v. Holder challenge to VRA Section 5

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Grandmother and Family Roots
clips.summary: Brown describes being raised in High Point by his grandmother Nelly Marshall Brown, whose father Joseph Kimmel Sr. was enslaved in Anson County and used as forced labor on North Carolina railroad construction. His grandmother brings him as a child to the old slave mart in Fayetteville where her father was sold, and to her childhood Rosenwald school in Wadesboro. Decades later Governor Jim Martin appointed Brown president and CEO of the North Carolina Railroad Company.
related_events: Brown's NC Railroad appointment by Governor Jim Martin

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Segregated Childhood in High Point
clips.summary: Brown, born February 1935, describes segregated water fountains and toilets in Woolworth and S.H. Kress stores. His grandmother corrects him for drinking from the white fountain, telling him God will fix it. Brown frames this story as bookend to his later corporate work with F.W. Woolworth Co. on integration, with John J. McCloy and Fred Kirby of the Allegheny Corporation on the Woolworth board.
related_events: Jim Crow segregation in North Carolina retail

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: William Penn and Mary McLeod Bethune
clips.summary: Brown attends Leonard Street School through sixth grade and William Penn High School (founded by Quakers) for grades seven through twelve. The principal assigns him to escort Mary McLeod Bethune during a school visit; she counsels him for hours on hard work and life direction. Langston Hughes and Morehouse College professor Dr. Brailsford Brazeal also visit William Penn. Brown becomes a voracious reader, helped by neighbor Mrs. Loudon who brings him discarded newspapers from her white employer's home.
related_events: Brown's editorship of William Penn school paper

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Becoming a Police Officer
clips.summary: Brown attends Virginia Union for one year, returns home when his grandparents fall ill, and enrolls at North Carolina A&T. After taking the High Point police exam in 1956, Captain Johnson recruits him to the force. Brown describes arresting a white suspect in a creek branch and a public confrontation on Washington Street, breaking the unwritten rule that Black officers do not arrest white men. There were four or five Black officers in High Point at the time.
related_events: Brown's first arrests of white suspects in High Point

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Federal Agent to B&C Associates
clips.summary: Brown leaves High Point for federal law enforcement in New York City, working organized crime cases and testifying in the Vito Genovese mafia case. He resigns to pursue public relations, returning to High Point in December 1960 to found B&C Associates (initially with a partner named Campbell). Driving a 1960 Rambler, often sleeping in his car between meetings, he secures F.W. Woolworth Co. as his first major client in early 1961.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: SCLC, Woolworth, and Corporate Pressure
clips.summary: Brown meets Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and Rev. Wyatt Tee Walker through Stanley S. Scott of the Atlanta Daily World. He underwrites SCLC hotel and movement expenses by charging them to corporate clients as business development. Brown works with Dr. Sidney H. Evans, A&T economist, to develop research on Black consumer purchasing power. He brokers integration of the National Retail Association lunch counters through Woolworth's influence and tests the The Peabody Hotel in Memphis personally before SCLC events.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: 1968 and the Nixon Campaign
clips.summary: Brown describes 1968: the King assassination, escorting Coretta Scott King to Memphis to retrieve King's body, the Hyatt Regency suite the night before the funeral with Sidney Poitier and Robert F. Kennedy. Brown assists the Poor People's Campaign planning, then works with Clarence Townes under Ray Bliss at the Republican National Committee. Bob Finch recruits him to the Nixon campaign; Brown joins the last presidential whistle-stop train tour across Ohio.

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Special Assistant to President Nixon
clips.summary: After the 1968 election, H.R. Bob Haldeman summons Brown to the Pierre Hotel transition office. Nixon introduces him to Herbert Brownell Jr. and William P. Rogers as a principal White House assistant. Brown tells the story of E. Frederic Morrow's marginalization under Eisenhower and Nixon's pledge of full access. Brown attends every cabinet meeting and is never overruled during his four years and three months at the White House. He drives expansion of Black general-officer promotions through reviewing Defense Department flag-officer lists with Haldeman.
related_events: Brown's appointment as Nixon Special Assistant
Defense Department flag officer promotion reviews

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Coretta Scott King in South Africa
clips.summary: Around 1986, Coretta Scott King asks Brown to arrange a South Africa trip through the King Center. Brown secures visas through South African ambassador Piet Koornhof. After overnight consultations with Dr. Allan Boesak and other South African leaders, Mrs. King decides not to meet with President P.W. Botha; Brown delivers her letter declining the meeting to the State House. The group then travels to Soweto to meet Winnie Mandela, who asks Brown to bring the Mandela grandchildren to America for school.
related_events: Coretta Scott King meeting with Winnie Mandela in Soweto

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Meeting Mandela at Pollsmoor
clips.summary: Niel van Heerden calls Brown at his hotel granting permission to visit Nelson Mandela at Pollsmoor Prison the next day. The chief warden welcomes Brown, Zenani Mandela, and Prince Moosa Dlamini and allows a two-hour visit when prior family visits had been capped at fifteen minutes. Mandela outlines terms for peace inside the country. Brown writes a memo immediately afterward and passes it to Winnie Mandela, the ANC, Secretary of State George Shultz, and President Reagan. Boston University President John Silber agrees to fund tuition for the Mandela grandchildren, who attend school in America for seven years funded personally by Brown.
related_events: Brown's 1986 visit to Mandela at Pollsmoor Prison
Mandela grandchildren's education at Boston University

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Minority Business Enterprise
clips.summary: Brown explains the Nixon administration's minority business enterprise program: organizing the National Commission on Minority Enterprise chaired by James M. Roche of GM, expanding Black auto dealerships, funding HBCUs at unprecedented levels, expanding the EEOC under William H. Brown III, and bringing James Farmer Jr. (head of CORE) into HEW as assistant secretary. Arthur A. Fletcher developed the Philadelphia Plan at Labor. Federal purchases from Black businesses rose from $13 million to $142 million during Nixon's tenure. Brown also visited military bases unannounced to address race-relations problems, working with Admiral John S. McCain Jr. (CINCPAC) in Hawaii.
related_events: National Commission on Minority Enterprise establishment

clips.subject: Robert Brown
clips.title: Sealtest, A&P, and Closing Reflections
clips.summary: Brown recounts intervening in a Cleveland Sealtest dispute when Dr. King could not get the dairy to meet with him. Brown flies to New York, meets the A&P CEO, and asks A&P to pressure Sealtest by threatening to pull Sealtest products from all Ohio stores. Within hours Sealtest contacts Dr. King; at a mass rally at Rev. Odie M. Hoover's Cleveland church Dr. King names Brown's role. Brown closes by thanking Lonnie Bunch and the Smithsonian NMAAHC board, noting he continues sending books to South African schools and over 300 libraries the program has established.
related_events: King's mass rally at Olivet Baptist Church Cleveland

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Family Land and Birthplace
clips.summary: Clark describes being born in the house his father built approximately one hundred years before the interview, on land where his great-grandparents had been enslaved. After emancipation his great-grandfather's former owner sold him the property; Clark's father later urged him to return after he finished twelfth grade, and Clark agreed despite wanting to leave for Chicago. The interview is conducted in that same house.
related_events: Emancipation land transfer in Holmes County
Clark's return to family homestead after high school

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: 1940 Arrest at Age Eleven
clips.summary: Clark and his brother were jailed overnight with their father, who had been arrested on murder charges after a man was found dead about two miles from the family property. A white lawyer in Lexington defended the father and won acquittal on self-defense grounds. Clark says he was twelve at the time and believes the jailing of the children was intimidation.
related_events: 1940 Holmes County murder trial of Clark's father

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Growing Up on Family Land
clips.summary: Because his family owned 228 acres and never sharecropped, Clark says he never worked in a white man's cotton field, and other Black families regarded the Clarks as different. He attended a two-teacher grammar school three miles away that ran four to five months a year so children could be released to chop and pick cotton; white school buses passed throwing spitballs.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: High School and Segregation
clips.summary: Clark traveled sixteen miles to Lexington and twenty-four to Durant for high school, boarding because there were no buses for African American students; he then walked from his porch to Pickens to catch the 2 a.m. train to school. His mother and grandfather, who had been enslaved in Hinds County and was eleven at emancipation, taught Clark that he was equal to whites but had to observe Jim Crow rules.
related_events: Clark's enrollment at Holmes County Training School at Durant

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Jackson College and the Track Scholarship
clips.summary: Clark wanted to attend Tougaloo to study pre-law and become a lawyer like Perry W. Howard, but Tougaloo told him a work-aid student would take eight years to graduate. He walked to Pickens, took the Trailways bus to Jackson with less than five dollars, and was kept overnight at Jackson College (then Jackson College for Negro Teachers); Dr. Jacob L. Reddix arranged custodial and dormitory garbage work at twenty-five cents an hour to cover tuition. Clark became the first track scholarship recipient at what is now Jackson State University after the Southwestern Athletic Conference championship.
related_events: Clark's first-year enrollment at Jackson College for Negro Teachers

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Dropping Out and Becoming a Teacher
clips.summary: A dean told Clark Jackson State would not let him use teaching to become a lawyer and ordered him to leave if he wanted law school; without anyone to tell him he could practice law without a pre-law major, he stayed in teaching. When Dean H.T. Sampson would not implement a one-student health-and-physical-education program, Clark left at the end of fall quarter to teach at Williams-Sullivan rather than spend a fifth year. He later returned for his degree in 1952.
related_events: Clark's first teaching post at Williams-Sullivan School

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Fired in Louise, Mississippi
clips.summary: Teaching at Louise, Mississippi during the Brown v. Board era, Clark told the superintendent, Mr. Brown, that white and Black children should attend school together; at the end of the year he was told he was no longer needed. Emmett Till was killed soon after at the 1955 Southwestern Athletic Conference workshop. A barbershop visit in Belzoni, Mississippi placed Clark in the chair just before Rubin Lee (whom Clark identifies as a voter-registration activist) was shot dead about two hours later.
related_events: Brown v. Board of Education backlash firings

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Sovereignty Commission and Father's Firing
clips.summary: Clark describes the Citizens' Councils as ruling Mississippi above the legislature and governor during the Brown backlash, and recounts seeing his name in a Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission report in which the Humphreys County superintendent claimed he had "gotten rid of all of the troublemakers." His father was fired in Humphreys County for teaching voter registration classes, took a Rosenwald school principalship in Madison County, was fired there, and could not get hired again because the Sovereignty Commission circulated names to all superintendents. Clark says the Commission used Black informants.
related_events: Sovereignty Commission blacklisting of Black teachers
Citizens' Council formation after Brown decision

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Michigan State University
clips.summary: Mississippi had no graduate school for African Americans, so the state paid the out-of-state differential for Clark to attend Michigan State University. He chose Michigan State over Notre Dame ("taught too much religion") and the University of California (cost of travel). In a class of thirty-five he was the only African American; after two students answered a question wrong, Clark answered correctly and says he never feared being among white students again, which prepared him for the legislature.

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Entering the Civil Rights Movement
clips.summary: Teaching and coaching at Lexington High School from 1961 to 1966, Clark says he initially knew little of the movement and that some Black residents suspected the white community had sent him to spy. He entered the movement through Henry and Sue Lorenzi, the white couple who arrived in Holmes County in fall 1964. About thirty Freedom Summer volunteers worked in Holmes County, and some of his football players walked out of school and were beaten.
related_events: 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer in Holmes County

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Saints Junior College and Adult Education
clips.summary: When the Holmes County superintendent refused Clark's request to implement an adult education program (and the school board told him such a program would only happen if a superintendent requested it), Clark threatened to run for superintendent and was not rehired. Dr. Arenia C. Mallory hired him at Saints Junior College in Lexington, where he ran a six-day-a-week adult-education program reaching three hundred adults including future voter J.P. Love, who later accused Clark of forging signatures unaware that Clark's program had taught Love to read and write.
related_events: Holmes County school board adult-education refusal

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: 1967 Independent Legislative Campaign
clips.summary: After the legislature passed a local-and-private act preventing Clark from running for superintendent (later ruled unconstitutional), he ran for the Mississippi House. Holmes County had been redistricted from two seats to one; Clark ran as an independent because Black Mississippians could not be Democratic Party members. He ran against incumbent JP Love (Wilbur Hucks lost the Democratic primary to Love). The Durant vote, organized in part through the residence and community network connected to the Newman political machine, put Clark over after he trailed in early returns. JP Love contested the election; Marian Wright was Clark's lead lawyer.
related_events: Voting Rights Act enforcement in Holmes County

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Isolation in the Legislature
clips.summary: Clark was not informed he would be seated until fifteen minutes before swearing-in. As the only Black member among 121 white legislators, the Speaker had a desk moved to the back of the chamber to avoid sitting next to Clark, and for eight years no one sat beside Clark on the front row. He could not get the floor to make motions, prepared exhaustively for education legislation, and after about eight years began advising older white legislators on votes from districts including Vicksburg and Greenville.
related_events: Mississippi House seating of first Black legislator since Reconstruction

clips.subject: Robert G. Clark Jr.
clips.title: Buddie Newman and the Education Chair
clips.summary: Speaker John R. Junkin grew ill, and Buddie Newman approached Clark in the Capitol needing one more signature to secure Speaker Pro Tempore. Clark signed; Newman dropped to one knee and kissed the pen. When Newman became Speaker after Junkin's death, he made Clark Vice Chair of Education.
