She closes with reflections on continuing racism, the failures of desegregated education, teachers' unexamined assumptions about black children's capacity, and her assessment that movement-era struggles must continue under contemporary conditions.
related_events: Marcus Garvey UNIA dual citizenship advocacy

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Parents and Mississippi Origins
clips.summary: Wright describes his father Moses, a hard but fair Mississippi farmer who refused to leave the state, and his mother Elizabeth, raised in Hazlehurst and educated enough to teach school in Memphis. The parents met at the Churches of God in Christ headquarters in Memphis and married in Sumner, Mississippi in 1925, where Elizabeth's family already lived near what would later become the Emmett Till trial venue.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Writing Simeon's Story
clips.summary: Wright explains that watching documentaries containing what he describes as inaccurate accounts of the Till case led his wife to suggest he write his own book. He identifies specific recurring inaccuracies he sought to correct, including the claim that the cousins dared Till to speak to Carolyn Bryant and the account that Moses Wright escaped Mississippi in a coffin.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Emmett's Arrival in Mississippi
clips.summary: Wright recounts the anticipation of cousin Emmett Till and Wheeler Parker Jr. arriving from Chicago in late August 1955, just before cotton harvest. He describes Till as a storyteller who told them about Lincoln Park and Riverview Park in Chicago. They shared a bedroom and a bed; Till tried picking cotton on Monday but could not tolerate the heat.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Bryant's Store Wednesday Night
clips.summary: On Wednesday August 24, 1955, after a full day of cotton picking, six boys drove to Money. Wheeler Parker Jr. and then Till entered Bryant's Store; Maurice sent Simeon in behind Till. Wright states Till completed his purchase without incident, but as Carolyn Bryant walked to her car outside, Till whistled at her. The boys ran to the car and left town within minutes.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: The Kidnapping
clips.summary: After returning from Greenwood late Saturday night, Roy Bryant and J. W. Milam came to the Wright house around 2 a.m. on Sunday August 28, 1955. They first entered Wheeler Parker's bedroom, declared him the wrong boy, then took Till from the bed he shared with Wright. Milam ordered Wright back to sleep, threatened Moses Wright with death if he reported it, and a woman's voice outside confirmed Till as the right one before they drove away.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Body Found and Trial
clips.summary: Till's body was found in the Tallahatchie River about twenty miles north of the Wright home. Leflore County Sheriff George Smith intervened to stop a hasty burial. Wright identified Till's ring as a material witness in the witness room at the Sumner trial. He describes Sheriff Strider's later testimony for the defense regarding the body's identification.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Moses Wright's Testimony and Move to Chicago
clips.summary: Moses Wright testified against Milam and Bryant despite neighbors warning he would be killed, with Medgar Evers and the NAACP providing protection. After the not-guilty verdict, Moses told the family they had to leave Mississippi. The family relocated to Argo Summit, Illinois, where the elder Wright could only find restaurant cleanup work; older brother James provided rent-free housing in his four-flat.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: King in Chicago and Religious Conversion
clips.summary: Wright initially rejected Martin Luther King's nonviolent message during the 1966 Chicago campaign at Marquette Park, unwilling to refuse to retaliate against violence. At age twenty-four in February 1967, sitting in a tavern, Wright heard an inner voice and within two weeks quit drinking, smoking, and committed his life to Christ, after which he embraced nonviolence.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Working Life and Reverend Parker
clips.summary: Wright describes his work history from shining shoes to bowling-alley pinsetter to Dearborn Glass to Reynolds Metals, where he completed the Local 597 pipefitting apprenticeship in 1968 and worked until retiring in 1994. He describes Wheeler Parker Jr.'s influence drawing him toward Christ and notes Parker is now his pastor while Wright serves as deacon and teaches second and third grade Sunday school.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Federal Reopening 2004-2005
clips.summary: Filmmaker Keith Beauchamp persuaded Wright to talk on camera, then collaborated with Alvin Sykes to find federal violations justifying reopening. U.S. Attorney Greenleaf in Mississippi promised reinvestigation. Till's body was exhumed at Burr Oak Cemetery, with Wright as the DNA donor; the testing confirmed identity and refuted claims of castration and missing teeth. Prosecutor Joyce Childs ultimately declined to indict Carolyn Bryant, citing insufficient evidence despite a witness placing her in the truck.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Till Bill and Correcting the Record
clips.summary: Wright describes traveling to Washington with his wife to watch debate on the Till Bill, federal legislation that set aside funding for civil-rights-era cold cases. He critiques James Hicks's account of his father saying "Thar he" in court (Wright cites the trial transcript reading "There he is") and Hicks's coffin-escape story. He dismisses William Bradford Huie's Look magazine piece, including the claim Huie interviewed the family in Argo and the fifty-cents-store-credit story about Maurice Wright in Emerge Magazine.

clips.subject: Simeon Wright
clips.title: Forgiveness, Legacy, Closing
clips.summary: Wright explains forgiveness of Bryant and Milam as a religious imperative that does not preclude justice but releases him from vengeance. He compares broken hearts to scar tissue, citing Jeremiah 18 on the potter's house. He assesses the case's legacy: federal law has changed, hearts have not; racism is less overt than under Jim Crow but persists. He closes with advice to young people: go to school, respect elders, do the time if you do the crime.
